This wasn't conquest or corruption for its own sake—it was Vaelar's attempt to save what he could from what he saw as inevitable destruction. In his mind, he was the hero of this story. Which made him infinitely more dangerous than any simple villain could ever be. "The ritual begins now," he announced, raising his hands toward the perfectly aligned celestial bodies above. "With or without your cooperation, the transformation will proceed." But as power began to flow from the stars themselves into the twisted stone circles, Trink's voice echoed across the Grove from somewhere in the darkness: "Hey, Vaelar! Catch!" The distraction lasted only a split second, but a split second was all we needed. The final battle for Unu's soul had begun.

Chapter : The Auction Block (Revised) [First Person - Rawn] The Sale I woke to the sound of chains. Not the metaphorical chains of debt and desperation we'd been carrying, but actual iron shackles being fitted around my wrists. In the dim pre-dawn light of our putrid cabin, I could make out three figures in black leather—bounty hunters, their faces hidden behind masks that made them look like carrion birds. "Easy there, hero," one of them said as I started to struggle. "No point making this harder than it needs to be." Beside me, Amicae was already awake, her green eyes blazing with fury as another hunter secured her bonds. A third was approaching Rowetha, who clutched Izzy with the desperate strength of a child who'd lost too much already. "Don't touch her," I snarled, trying to rise despite the chains. "Contract says alive and unharmed," the lead hunter said with professional indifference. "Kid's part of the package. Dog too." Red was already on his feet, his renewed strength evident in the way he held himself. But even with his transformation, he was outnumbered and our captors were prepared. One of them held a crossbow trained on Rowetha while the others worked. "Grolk!" I shouted toward the door. "This wasn't our deal!" Laughter echoed from the deck above—both Grolk's gravelly chuckle and Ursa's higher-pitched cackle. "Deal changed!" Grolk called down. "Got a better offer!" The cabin door opened to reveal our former "hosts," both grinning with the satisfaction of a profitable transaction. Behind them stood a figure that made my blood run cold—a thin man with dead eyes and the bearing of someone who enjoyed his work. "Captain Korven," the lead bounty hunter reported. "Package secured as requested." "Excellent," Korven said, his voice carrying the cultured tones of minor nobility. "Lord Vaelar will be most pleased." The Betrayal Revealed "You sold us to Vaelar," Amicae said, her voice flat with disbelief. "Sold you to the highest bidder," Ursa corrected cheerfully. "Ain't personal, just business. Five hundred gold each, they offered. Plus a bonus for quick delivery." "That's more than we made all last year," Grolk added, as if this explained everything. "Nothing personal, you understand. Just couldn't pass up that kind of coin." I stared at them, these creatures who'd spent days bleeding us dry with petty fees and invented charges, who'd listened to us explain how the world would end if we failed, who'd taken our money and our desperation and turned it into profit. "The world is ending," Rowetha said quietly, tears streaming down her young face. "Everyone will become monsters, and you helped make it happen." "Not our problem," Ursa said with a shrug. "We got ours. That's what matters." "Besides," Grolk added, "way I figure it, if the world's ending anyway, might as well go out rich." Captain Korven stepped forward, his pale eyes studying us like specimens in a collection. "Lord Vaelar sends his regards. He's quite eager to resume your... conversation." The thought of facing Vaelar again, of seeing that false concern mask his corruption, made my stomach churn. But worse was the knowledge of what this meant for our mission. Every hour we spent in his custody was another hour for the corruption to spread. "The king's death," I said desperately. "People need to know the truth about what happened." "The truth?" Korven's smile was razor-thin. "The truth is that a group of dangerous cultists murdered our beloved sovereign and fled justice. Fortunately, loyal citizens helped track down these terrorists before they could strike again." "That's not—" Amicae started. "That's the official truth," Korven interrupted. "And since Lord Vaelar is now serving as regent until a proper succession can be arranged, his truth is the only one that matters." Regent. Of course. With Theron dead and no clear heir, Vaelar could claim temporary authority over the kingdom. How long before "temporary" became permanent? "Move them topside," Korven ordered. "We have a schedule to keep." The Floating Market What I saw on deck made our situation infinitely worse. Grolk's Fortune had moored alongside a much larger vessel—a converted warship that flew no flag but carried the distinctive black sails I'd seen before in the war's aftermath. Slavers. The deck was crowded with cages, and the stench of human misery hung in the air like a fog. "Welcome to the Crimson Tide," Korven said pleasantly, as if he were giving a tour of a pleasure craft. "Captain Vex runs the most... efficient... operation on the eastern waters." As we were prodded across a makeshift bridge between vessels, I got a clear view of the larger ship's cargo. Dozens of people crammed into iron cages, their faces hollow with despair and resignation. Men, women, children—all reduced to merchandise in someone else's profit scheme. "You're slavers," Amicae said, horror evident in her voice. "We prefer 'alternative labor contractors,'" said a new voice. A woman emerged from the ship's cabin, her elaborate coat decorated with gold trim and her smile sharp enough to cut glass. "Slavery has such negative connotations." Captain Vex, I realized. Everything about her radiated the confidence of someone who'd turned human suffering into a profitable enterprise. "Call it what you want," I said. "It's still evil." "Evil?" Vex laughed, the sound like breaking glass. "My dear man, I'm a businesswoman. I provide a service—connecting people who need workers with people who need employment. Admittedly, the employment terms are somewhat... inflexible... but that's just market efficiency." She gestured to the caged humans with the pride of a merchant displaying fine wares. "Each of these individuals will find meaningful work in mines, plantations, or private households. They'll have food, shelter, and the satisfaction of contributing to society." Red growled low in his throat, but one of the bounty hunters jabbed him with a shock rod. The dog yelped and stumbled, his enhanced strength meaning nothing against prepared enemies. "Don't hurt him!" Rowetha cried out, trying to reach Red despite her chains. "The dog's part of the special order," Korven said, consulting his paperwork. "Lord Vaelar has... plans... for magical creatures." I didn't want to think about what those plans might involve. The Auction What followed was a nightmare beyond anything I'd experienced, even during the worst days of the Sunding War. We were dragged before a crowd of buyers—representatives from mines, plantations, private collectors, and worse things I didn't want to identify. Captain Vex served as auctioneer, her voice carrying easily across the deck as she described our "features" to potential purchasers. "Lot number seven!" she announced, gesturing to Amicae. "Elven female, early middle years, scarred but structurally sound. Demonstrated magical abilities make her ideal for arcane labor or personal service. Bidding starts at fifty gold!" "I am not merchandise!" Amicae snarled, her bonds crackling with barely contained water magic. "Spirited!" Vex noted approvingly. "Some buyers prefer that. Shows there's still fight left in them. Sixty gold from the gentleman in blue!" The bidding continued, each raised hand reducing Amicae to a commodity, a thing to be bought and used and discarded. I watched in helpless rage as strangers argued over her value like she was livestock. The Mindreaver stirred, showing me visions of what awaited her—arcane experiments, magical slavery, worse things that made my fire burn cold with fury. "Sold for one hundred twenty gold to Master Grimwald!" Master Grimwald proved to be a corpulent man in expensive robes, his smile revealing teeth capped with gold. "Excellent! I've been needing a new arcane assistant. The last one was so... fragile." "No," I said, struggling against my chains hard enough to draw blood. "You will not take her." "Lot number eight!" Vex continued cheerfully. "Human male, artificially youthened, military bearing. Note the sword—clearly a warrior of some skill. Perfect for gladiatorial entertainment or high-risk security work. Starting bid, sixty gold!" They were selling us separately. The cruelest possible outcome—not just slavery, but separation from each other, from any hope of coordinated escape or resistance. "Seventy gold!" called a voice from the crowd. "Eighty!" called another. I found myself staring into the faces of my potential owners—a gladiator master who measured me like meat, a mine foreman who calculated my worth in tons of ore, a private collector whose interest felt distinctly unwholesome. "One hundred fifty gold!" The winning bid came from a woman in black leather, her face hidden behind a mask that made her look like some predatory bird. "I have immediate need for someone with his... qualifications." "Sold to Madame Corvina!" The Child's Fate But the worst was yet to come. "Lot number nine!" Vex's voice carried a particularly vile enthusiasm. "Human child, approximately twelve years. Unblemished, well-trained, shows signs of unusual magical sensitivity. Perfect for specialized training programs or private collection." "NO!" The word tore from my throat with such force that several buyers stepped back. "She's a child! You cannot—" "The child comes with a companion animal," Vex continued, ignoring my protests. "Magical cat, excellent for arcane research or exotic entertainment. Starting bid for the pair, one hundred gold!" I watched in growing horror as hands raised around the deck. These weren't labor contractors or even traditional slavers—these were collectors of the worst kind, people who bought children for purposes that didn't bear thinking about. "One hundred fifty!" "Two hundred!" "Two fifty from Lord Blackthorne!" Rowetha stood frozen in terror, clutching Izzy so tightly the cat could barely breathe. Her eyes found mine across the crowded deck, wide with the kind of fear that children should never have to experience. "Please," she whispered, the word barely audible over the bidding. "Please don't let them take me." I pulled against my chains until I felt bones crack, but the iron held. Beside me, Amicae was doing the same, her water magic flaring and dying as shock collars delivered punishing jolts. "Three hundred gold!" The winning bid came from a figure so thoroughly concealed I couldn't determine gender or even species. "I'll take both pets." "Sold!" The casual way this creature referred to a child and her Guardian companion as "pets" made my vision red with rage. But rage meant nothing against iron chains and shock collars. The Separation They dragged us in different directions, despite our struggles and protests. Professional slavers, I realized—they'd done this before, knew how to handle resistance, how to break bonds between people to make them easier to manage. "Amicae!" I called out as they hauled her toward one section of the ship. "I'll find you!" she called back, her voice already growing distant. "Whatever happens, I'll find you!" But we both knew the truth. Once we were sold, once we were scattered across different owners in different places, the chances of ever seeing each other again approached zero. Rowetha's sobs echoed across the deck as her new "owner" examined her like a prized horse. The figure in concealing robes ran gloved hands over her arms, her hair, checking her teeth like she was livestock. "Excellent bone structure," the figure mused in a voice like rustling leaves. "She'll do nicely for the collection." "Don't touch her!" I roared, but the shock collar around my neck delivered a jolt that dropped me to my knees. "Now, now," Captain Vex said pleasantly. "No need for dramatics. This is just business, after all. Supply and demand. Market forces." "This is evil," I gasped through the pain. "This is profit," she corrected. "Evil is just a matter of perspective." They separated us into different holding areas—metal cages barely large enough to stand in, positioned so we could see each other but not communicate easily. The psychological torture of watching your loved ones suffer while being unable to help was just another tool in their arsenal. Red was taken to a special cage designed for magical creatures, lined with symbols that suppressed his Fire Essence. The proud dog who'd regained his youth and strength was reduced to a whimpering animal, his power drained by arcane bindings. The Depths My new owner, Madame Corvina, examined me with the clinical interest of someone appraising a tool. "Remove the shirt," she ordered her assistants. "I want to see the full extent of his conditioning." They stripped me to the waist, and Corvina ran her fingers along my arms and chest, testing muscle tone and flexibility. Her touch was clinical but somehow more violating than outright brutality would have been. "Excellent preservation," she mused. "The artificial aging reversal was very well done. Muscle memory intact, reflexes enhanced..." She paused at a scar on my shoulder. "This is from the Sunding War. You really are him, aren't you? The Scorch of Eryndor." "I was," I said through gritted teeth. "Perfect. My clients pay premium rates for authentic historical figures." Her smile was visible even through her mask. "You'll be quite the attraction in the fighting pits." Fighting pits. Of course. My new life would be as gladiatorial entertainment, fighting for the amusement of people wealthy enough to buy human beings. "The dog comes with you," Korven added, consulting his paperwork. "Lord Vaelar was quite specific—the animal and the warrior are not to be separated." "Even better," Corvina said. "Beast-fighting events are very popular. Nothing like watching a man and his loyal companion fight for their lives against impossible odds." The casual way she discussed our deaths, as if we were characters in a play rather than living beings, made my stomach turn. The Transport As evening fell, they began loading their new "merchandise" onto transport vessels. Smaller boats that could navigate rivers and coastal waters, each carrying a few dozen slaves to different destinations. I found myself chained in the hold of a sleek cutter, surrounded by other prisoners who'd given up hope. Their eyes held the hollow look of people who'd already died inside, who were just waiting for their bodies to catch up. "Where are they taking us?" I asked the man chained next to me. "The Crimson Islands," he said in a voice like dead leaves. "Private fighting leagues. Rich folks bet on how long we'll last." "And the others? The woman, the child?" He shrugged. "Who knows? Mines, brothels, private collections, research labs..." He looked at me with something that might have been pity. "Best not to think about it. Makes the chains heavier." Through a small porthole, I could see other boats departing with their human cargo. Somewhere out there, Amicae was being taken to arcane slavery. Somewhere else, Rowetha was being delivered to collectors who saw children as objects to be owned. And Red... Red was trapped with me, destined to die for entertainment in some rich sadist's fighting pit. This was worse than death. Death would have been mercy compared to what awaited us. The Darkest Hour As the boat carried us away from any hope of rescue, I finally understood what true despair felt like. We'd failed completely. The corruption would spread unchecked, the world would fall to undeath, and we'd be scattered to the winds as slaves and entertainment. Vaelar had won. Not just the battle, but the entire war. The Mindreaver's whispers rose to a roar, showing me every failure, every wrong choice, every moment where I could have acted differently. I'd been a fool to trust the stranger's gift, a fool to believe in second chances, a fool to think love could triumph over corruption. "Should have stayed dead," I muttered to myself. "Should have died an old man in his bed." Red whined softly from his cage, and I saw my own despair reflected in his enhanced intelligence. He understood what was happening, understood that this was the end of everything we'd fought for. Hours passed. The boat rocked gently on calm waters, carrying us toward a future of pain and exploitation. I'd stopped struggling against the chains—what was the point? Even if I could break free, where would I go? We were surrounded by water, hundreds of miles from any friendly shore, and the entire kingdom thought we were murderers. It was over. Everything was over. The Miracle I was dozing fitfully when I heard it—a sound so faint I thought I was dreaming. A buzzing, like an insect might make, coming from somewhere above deck. Other prisoners stirred restlessly, but I was the only one who recognized that particular pattern of wing beats. "Impossible," I breathed. The buzzing grew louder, more insistent. Then I heard voices from above—guards cursing, sounds of a scuffle, someone crying out in surprise. A few minutes later, the hatch to our hold creaked open. Torchlight spilled down the ladder, silhouetting a small figure that made my heart stop. Trink. He was alive, but barely. One wing was clearly broken, his left arm hung useless at his side, and his usually vibrant green coloring was mottled with bruises and scorch marks. But his eyes still held that familiar mischievous spark. "Evening, mates," he said, his voice weak but unmistakably his own. "Sorry I'm late. Traffic was murder." "Trink!" I stared at him in disbelief. "How—" "Long story, short version," he said, buzzing unsteadily down to my level. "Vaelar's magic hurt but didn't kill. Spent the last week playing dead and listening to very interesting conversations." His grin was pained but genuine. "Turns out our false lord has been quite chatty about his plans." "What plans?" "The Fall of Eldhaven," Trink said, his expression growing serious. "It's happening in ten days. Vaelar's going to tear reality apart and let every nightmare from the Nemesis realm pour through." My blood ran cold. "The whole city?" "The whole kingdom. Maybe the whole world, if he gets his ritual completed." Trink landed heavily on my chains, his broken wing making flight difficult. "But there's more. He's been gathering power from every slave sale, every act of cruelty, every moment of despair. The corruption feeds on suffering." "Then we have to—" "We have to get you out of here first," Trink interrupted. "Can't save the world from a fighting pit." He fumbled with something at his belt—a set of lockpicks that looked impossibly delicate in his tiny hands. "Good thing I kept my burglary skills sharp." As he worked on my shackles, I felt something I'd thought was dead forever stirring in my chest. Hope. "How did you find us?" I asked. "Followed the money trail. Corruption always leaves a paper trail, and I know how to read ledgers." The first lock clicked open. "Plus, Captain Vex isn't as careful with her manifests as she thinks." "Amicae? Rowetha?" "Different boats, different destinations. But I know where they're going." Another lock clicked. "We get you out, we get them back, then we save the world." "Just like that?" Trink's grin was fierce despite his injuries. "Just like that. After all, we're the good guys. We're supposed to win eventually." As my chains fell away, I felt strength returning to limbs that had known only despair moments before. Trink was right—we were the good guys. And maybe, just maybe, it was time for the good guys to start fighting back. "What's the plan?" I asked, flexing my freed hands and feeling Fire Essence respond for the first time in hours. "First, we steal this boat," Trink said, moving to the next prisoner's chains. "Then we rescue your lady and the little one. Then we stop the apocalypse." "Sounds simple when you put it like that." "The best plans usually are," he said. "It's the execution that gets tricky." As more chains clicked open and hope spread among the prisoners, I allowed myself to believe that maybe—just maybe—we still had a chance. The world might be ending in ten days, but we weren't finished yet. Not by a long shot.

Chapter 3: The Burned Woman By the Oak Tree I sat against my favorite oak tree, still reeling from what had happened to my body. The transformation had left me weak as a newborn foal, but at the same time thrumming with energy I didn't understand. Red lay at my side, though he kept shifting nervously, whining softly as if my scent had changed in ways that confused him. "Well, fellow, another day arrives for us," I said, though the words felt hollow. This wasn't the same 'us' that had sat here countless mornings before. I looked down at my hands—smooth, unmarked by the liver spots and scars of age. These weren't my hands. These belonged to a young man, strong and capable, with none of the tremor that had plagued me for years. The red cloth from the stranger's bottle lay beside me, empty now, its purpose served. What am I? I wondered, flexing fingers that moved with precision I'd lost decades ago. What did that potion do to me? The morning sun felt different on my skin—not the gentle warmth I'd grown accustomed to, but something that seemed to feed a fire burning low in my chest. When I breathed, I could swear I tasted smoke and lightning. It was disturbing, this sensation of power coiled within me like a sleeping dragon. I'd spent forty years learning to live without the strength of my youth, accommodating the limitations of age, finding peace in the slower rhythms of an old man's life. Now that peace was shattered. Red whined again, pressing closer despite his obvious discomfort. Through some new awareness I couldn't explain, I felt his simple confusion: Pack leader smells wrong. Looks wrong. But still pack leader. "I know, old friend," I murmured, scratching behind his ears with hands that felt alien to me. "I don't understand it either." The strangest part wasn't the physical transformation—though that was unsettling enough. It was the way my memories felt different now, as if I were viewing them through the wrong end of a spyglass. The aches and pains I'd carried for decades were gone, but so too was the hard-won wisdom that came from accepting limitation. My mind still held fifty years of experience, but my body hummed with the restless energy of a man in his prime. Was this how I felt during the war? I wondered, trying to remember the sensation of youth from the inside rather than the outside. This constant awareness of power held in check? I flexed my hand and watched small flames dance between my fingers—not the labored sparks I'd been able to manage in recent years, but honest fire that responded to my will like a well-trained hound. The sight should have thrilled me. Instead, it filled me with dread. I remembered what I'd been capable of when fire flowed freely through my veins. The villages I'd burned to save them from the Lich Emperor's corruption. The soldiers I'd led into infernos, telling myself their deaths served the greater good. The weight of command that had slowly crushed the young idealist I'd once been until only a hollow, efficient killer remained. I became a farmer to escape that man, I thought, watching the flames dance. I chose this quiet life, chose to let my power fade. I chose to be small and safe and harmless. Red shifted beside me, his discomfort growing. I could feel something through our bond—not words, but emotions. He sensed the change in me went deeper than mere appearance. The old man who'd sat here yesterday had been worn smooth by time and loss, his edges dulled by grief and acceptance. This new creature wearing his shape was all sharp edges again, a blade newly forged and hungry for purpose. The stranger knew, I realized with growing unease. He knew exactly what he was giving me back. But why? What does he want from me? The grey-eyed man had vanished without explanation, leaving only this impossible gift and the terrible question of what price it would demand. In my experience, gifts of power always came with obligations. The gods didn't hand out miracles without expecting service in return. I'd been contemplating the long road of my life—the war, the choices, the weight of command that had never really left my shoulders. Red and I were supposed to be in the twilight of our days, waiting for that final rest together. We'd earned our peace through blood and loss and the slow healing that came with time. Instead, I felt like I'd been thrust back into the prime of my life, carrying an old man's memories in a young man's body. It was a cruel joke—to have the strength to act but the wisdom to know the cost of action. To feel the fire burning bright within me while remembering exactly how much pain that fire could cause. This isn't natural, I thought. This isn't right. No power comes without a price, and I haven't paid mine yet. The wind stirred the oak's leaves above me, and for a moment I could swear I heard something like laughter carried on the breeze. Not malicious, exactly, but amused. As if somewhere, someone was watching this struggle between my restored youth and hard-won wisdom with great interest. Red's ears suddenly perked up, and he lifted his head to stare toward the treeline. His nervous whining shifted to something more alert, more focused. That's when I heard the footsteps.

Document.Title: Front Matter
Document.Notes:
Note that in the “Metadata” pane of the Inspector, under “Section Type”, the “Default Subdocument Type” is set to “Front Matter”. This setting causes all subdocuments of this folder to use the “Front Matter” section type by default (that is, when “Structure-Based” is selected as the section type).
This saves us from having to manually assign the “Front Matter” section type for each document we place into this folder.
During Compile, documents assigned the “Front Matter” section type will be laid out differently from documents in the main body of the manuscript.

Document.Text:
The Storm of Delfaen The Ritual In the fetid necropolis of Delfaen, capital city of the Dead Kingdom of Necropolis, a storm was brewing. High up in the ancient tower of Loth, Ithek Acolytes were deep into an evil ritual—one last performed on Unu thousands of years in the past. Strapped to a sacrificial altar lay the naked, quivering form of a young elf maiden. A high priestess busied herself brushing runes onto the maiden's pale skin using black, ichorous blood. The maiden was extraordinarily beautiful, even in the throes of mind-numbing terror. To the left of the altar lay a circle of summoning. Three acolytes stood within, chanting in the necromantic tongue—a most foul language to behold. It reverberated with death and pestilence, all intent focused upon creating a portal to another plane of existence. A plane so vile and evil that the entities dwelling there called themselves The Nemesis, the destroyer of worlds—beings of unspeakable power bent on destruction. The ritual had been recently discovered on stone tablets excavated from a long-extinct civilization. The storm above seemed to intensify with the increased fervor of the ritual. The acolytes increased their tempo and vocal power. "Gaist Gau Porta Mae Geni Nonigu!" they chanted. The priestess raised a black dagger above the maiden's naked breast. With ferocity, she slammed it down through the maiden, embedding the blade deep into the wood of the altar table. The elf maiden gasped one last breath. A portal swirled into existence. One of the acolytes clutched at their head as a picture formed of a grey landscape. Then the acolyte collapsed, and the vision blacked out. A catastrophic failure occurred in the complex, dangerous spell. White radiant light flashed through the tower and out into the night for many miles. An epic explosion tore through the tower, pulverizing stone in undulating pulses down to the ground below. As the tower collapsed, two otherworldly intruders fled. One ascended into the storm as a noxious, swirling miasma. The other crept away into the stormy night. The Village of Crygill As the powerful thunderstorm reached the tiny, quiet village by a sinuous old river, the winds began howling through the quaint little community. Immense lightning streaked across the ominous dark night sky, illuminating the small cottages far below. Thunder shook the sleepy little village of Crygill. Crygill was a fishing village of Tifflings that hugged the southern banks of the Orkael River. The village sat on the furthest northern border of the kingdom Kleinae. In the loft of a fisher's cottage lay a frightened, sleepless little Tiffling maiden. She clutched an old, raggedy doll to her breast. "This storm is so spooky, Betsy," she whispered to her doll. "Shhh, did you hear that? It sounded like screams." BOOM! Thunder shook the old cottage to its foundation. "Goddess protect us," the young maiden said. "That was frightfully close, Betsy. Listen—do you hear that, Betsy? It sounds like people screaming. Goddess, please make this horrible storm end soon." The Old Soldier's Cottage My bones are aching—an ache that reaches deep down into the marrow. A storm approaches, and I can tell it's going to be a whopper. There's an electric charge to the air. The hairs on my arms are standing on end, and the wind is howling. This is only the edge of the storm; the heart has yet to reach my quaint cottage. At my side lies my only companion these many years. As ancient as I, he whines at the maelstrom occurring outside these four walls. "Hush now, we've been through storms before," I said, reaching down to pat his grey head. "If it does take us, at least we go together." Red is all that remains of my family. My wife passed quietly in her sleep some ten years back. Our son passed long before that to a fever. After my service to the Seven Kingdoms, I had wished for a quiet life. We found an out-of-the-way area with a spot of land and farmed. We had been content. As I stare into the warm, glowing flames of the fireplace, I contemplate my long life. Sitting and remembering seems to be my only pastime nowadays. My life had been adventurous, full of triumphs and failures alike—even devastating loss on occasion. Full of choices that had to be made under the direst of circumstances. What I had been scared of most was the politics. The Stranger's Gift The Unexpected Visitor Outside, the storm increased its intensity. Suddenly there came a knock at the door. "Red, is that the door?" I said. "Who would be out in this weather?" Red chuffed in response. Boom! Boom! Boom! "Confounded visitors in this mess," I muttered. Grunting, I rose to my feet and shuffled toward the door. I lifted the bar and turned the knob, and the door swung open. Before me stood a man of average stature with dark features. His gray eyes pierced into mine. "Yes, young man, what can I do for you?" I said. "So sorry, old master, but may I come out of this rain and find succor?" he asked. "Aye, I imagine you do. Alright, come in," I said. "I won't leave you out in this mess." He stepped inside, and I closed and barred the door behind him. "Sir, this is frightful weather. Thank you for your kindness," he said. "Are you hungry?" I asked. "Yes, sire, thank you." "Alright, let me fetch a bowl." An Evening's Hospitality The stranger and I settled down to cheese, bread, and lamb stew. Red took his customary position at my feet, hoping for a dropped morsel or two. Then the strangest little squeak and squeal escaped Red's old gray backside—it was a right stinker. "Sorry, the dog is old and my only companion," I said apologetically. The stranger laughed. "It's his home. I am but a visitor." I laughed and smiled. I thought I might like this fellow. "What brings you out in this miserable weather?" I asked. "I was passing through when this storm struck. Yours is the only place I saw," he said. "It was providence." "Yes, indeed." We finished our fare, and I offered him some wine. "I'm old and achy—you may take the bed tonight. I've grown accustomed to sleeping in this old rocking chair by the fire to keep my old bones warm." "That is very kind of you. Thank you," he said. "Sleep well," I said. "Sleep well, sire," he responded. The Morning After I awoke to the glare of Unu's double suns pouring their light through my kitchen window. I rose on creaky knees, causing me to groan. I looked and saw I had awakened the stranger. "Care to breakfast with me?" I asked. "That would be great," he said. I tottered over to the fireplace to start preparing a meal. I fried salted pork along with fresh eggs, broke out some cider and day-old bread. I set the table, and we sat down to eat. I flipped an occasional morsel to Red under the table as the stranger and I ate in companionable silence, enjoying the sounds of a spring morning. Birds were singing, and my old rooster cut loose with a crow. We finished eating, and the stranger stood. "Sir, you have shown a great deal of kindness to me. I wish to repay you. Take this tonic—it will help with the feebleness and aches of age," he said, producing a bottle wrapped in red cloth. "You owe me nothing, young man," I protested. "Rawnae Lupio, you are a good man. You deserve this simple gift," he said. Then he walked to the door, lifted the bar, opened it, and exited the cottage. Red moaned and trudged out the door after him. "Alright, I'm coming," I said to Red's retreating form. The Mysterious Departure I walked out into the early morning glare of Soli and Sola, Unu's double suns. I looked around, but I could not see the stranger anywhere. "Strange—he couldn't have gotten too far. He was a pleasant young man, though," I said to myself. I inhaled the fresh, clean spring air. It smelled of recent rain and wildflowers. The morning bustled with the activities of nature. I looked at my hand, clutching a satiny red cloth wrapped around a bottle with clear liquid inside. Red trudged from around the side of the house. I waddled my way to my favorite oak tree to lean against it. Red and I had sat here many times over the years. We would sit in this spot for hours, watching the comings and goings of the local townsfolk and farmers—most of which were Tiffling folk. I lived in the Kingdom of Kleinae. This road connected Kleinae and Mannesket, so occasionally we would see humans as well. Alaina and I had chosen to live in Kleinae because the Tifflings were respectful and private folk. They never meddled in anyone's business, yet they were kind and friendly. Contemplation and Decision I bent down and settled against the large oak tree. Red took his spot at my side, taking his sweet time to get comfortable. When he finally settled right against my hip, he let out a long sigh. I reached down to scratch his ears. "Well, fellow, another day arrives for us," I said. I started contemplating again, remembering a war that had nearly been the ending of all life on Unu. Remembering hundreds of acts of selfless heroism—men and women who had sacrificed themselves for the good of all. Red groaned. "Yes, old friend. I understand," I said. Red and I were at the stage of life where we were just waiting for the sweet release of death—the end of a full life, our final reprieve. Neither of us was what we once were. We were mere shells of the strength and vigorous bodies we had once possessed. "A tonic to remove the feebleness and aches of age, he said," I mused, examining the bottle. I reached down again, scratching along Red's neck. "You want me to take it, old boy?" Red groaned in response. "Well, if it kills me, that will remove the feebleness and aches of age too," I said with dark humor. Red whined. "Shh, stop, old boy. I was just making an observation." I continued my reflections. My Alaina had been gone now for ten years. I missed her daily. She had been a good, caring woman, though she was never the same after we lost our boy. I had never felt more helpless than when I watched him burn with fever in his bed. He was only ten summers at the time and should have had so many more. I examined the contents of the vial. Not a single thing about it seemed remarkable—clear as water, and when I pulled the cork, odorless. "Well, if it kills me, you'll have the whole place to yourself, Red," I said. Red whined again, then laid his head down and sighed. "Alright, old fellow—bottoms up!" I said, and quaffed the contents of the vial. Red raised his head and whined pitifully. The Transformation My vision exploded into a rainbow of bright colors, and I felt a pain unlike any I had ever experienced in my life. I felt like my entire body had separated into the tiniest fragments of existence. I was simultaneously freezing and burning up. I was lightning and volcanic eruptions, the mightiest storm and the deepest abyss. I found myself begging to either die or pass out. Finally, a peaceful darkness encompassed me.
Rowetha's Morning Last night, a storm of nightmares had struck our little village. I barely slept, spending the night clutching my old doll Betsy—something I hadn't done in some time, ever since Ma told me it wasn't proper for girls of my age to clutch dolls during the night. Last night I couldn't help it. I felt so alone and scared. I said my morning prayer to Anna Perenna for a good, fruitful day, as I always did. I dressed and headed to the kitchen, where I found Ma over the stove like every morning since I could remember. "Morning, sweetheart. Did you sleep any?" Ma asked. Ma always knew. I looked down bashfully and responded, "Some. I was frightened." "Oh, child, it was just a storm. See? The suns rose this morning. All is well," she said, smiling at me. "Where is Da?" I asked. "He went to check the river. Fast-flowing water fouls his nets," Ma said. Then my twelve-year-old brother bounced into the kitchen like a ball of energy. "Ma, I'm hungry!" he announced. Ma and Da had named him Otec Jr., but I just called him Bubbee. Like all younger siblings, he was a pampered baby. "Hold your chickens, mister!" Ma said. "I'm working on it now." The door suddenly swung open, and the familiar sound of Da's boots clopped on the floor. "The Dorn River is flowing way too fast," Da said with frustration. "Sorry, hun. I'm sure you'll find something to occupy your time today," Ma said. "I have a list of things that need to be done." Da groaned. "Yes, dear." "Sit, all of you. Breakfast is ready," Ma announced. The Revelation A Changed Man I felt incredibly energetic. My movements were quick and painless. I was having a tough time coming to grips with the overwhelming changes my body had just gone through. I chopped vegetables with lightning speed, delighting in working faster than I had in years as I prepared a stew. I glanced at the Elven maiden who watched me silently prepare the meal. Her cowl hid her face, but not her womanly form. It had been a long time since I'd cared to notice such things. I scowled at myself for doing so now. You're an old man! What do you think you're doing? I thought. Soon the stew was ready, needing only time to cook. The stew bubbled in the hearth, filling my Kleinae cottage with a savory aroma that mingled uneasily with the storm's damp musk from last night. I ladled the vegetable-heavy concoction into two wooden bowls, my hands—youthful, unblemished, and still a stranger's—moving with unnerving speed. Across the table, the Elven maiden sat with her cowl casting shadows over her face, her posture rigid as a deer scenting a wolf. Red sprawled at my feet, his old bones creaking as he sniffed for scraps, oblivious to the tension coiling tighter than a bowstring. First Contact I sat across the table from my guest and set a bowl before her, the clink of wood on wood sharp in the silence. "I'm Rawn," I said. "Eat, lass. It ain't much, but it's hearty." My voice carried a young man's roughness, harsher than I'd intended. Her eyes, hidden beneath the cowl, flicked to the stew, then to me, their glint green as the Eldertree's heart. She didn't touch the spoon. Her hands, gloved in fine leather, rested in her lap, fingers twitching like they yearned for a weapon. "Thank you, sir," she murmured, her voice a melodic whisper like water over riverstones, but edged with caution. "Your hospitality is... unexpected." She tilted her head, the cowl shifting, and I caught a glimpse of her jaw—smooth, then marred by a jagged line of puckered flesh. A spark from the hearth flared, its Fire Essence crackling, and she flinched, tugging the fabric tighter, her breath hitching as if burned anew. I looked away, busying myself with my bowl, though my stomach churned more from her fear than hunger. "Storm brought all sorts of strays to my door," I said, aiming for levity. "You're the prettiest, mind, but don't tell Red." The dog huffed, tail thumping. Her lips twitched—a ghost of a smile—but her shoulders stayed hunched, guarding that hidden face. Probing Questions I took a spoonful of stew, the heat grounding me, though my new body thrummed with restless energy. "You said you heard screams," I ventured, recalling her words under the oak. "Miles off, you claimed. Elven ears, eh? Or something else?" I kept my tone light, but my eyes searched hers, probing. She'd seen my transformation—multicolored mist spewing from my mouth, old flesh turning young. That wasn't idle curiosity. Her gloved hand tightened on the table's edge, knuckles whitening. "Elven ears, aye," she said, voice low. "And a sensitivity to Essence. Your change was no small thing, sir. It shook the air, like a wound in the world. And... something darker stirs nearby, in villages like Crygill. A taint, like rot in the Essence." Her gaze sharpened. "What was that potion? No alchemist's brew could do such a thing." I grunted, stirring my stew, the memory of cosmic pain—fire, ice, lightning—flashing through me. "A gift from a storm-sodden stranger," I said, offering a half-truth. "Said it'd ease my aches. Did more than that, it seems." I flexed my hand, the skin smooth and unscarred, a mockery of the decades I'd carried. "Didn't ask for it, mind. Reckon I'd rather have stayed old and creaky." Hidden Identities Her head tilted, the cowl slipping to show a lock of silver hair, stark against her shadowed cheek. Her eyes flicked to Vulcanspine, leaning against the hearth, its runes glinting faintly. "You jest," she said, almost accusingly. "Who'd choose age over youth? Power over frailty?" Her voice trembled, carrying a weight deeper than my flippant words—pain, loss, a mirror's cruel judgment. I met her eyes, or tried to, through the cowl's gloom. "An old man who's seen too much," I said, quieter. "Youth's a burden when you've naught left to live for." My thoughts drifted to Alaina, her laughter gone ten years, and my boy, fever-stolen. The Mindreaver's whispers stirred, conjuring their faces—violet-eyed, flesh splitting, clawing at me as Fleshreavers. I shook it off, focusing on her. "You're young, lass. What's got you so skittish?" Her breath caught, and she leaned back, the cowl a shield. "Not so young," she whispered. "And... I've seen my share of scars." Her gloved hand brushed her hood, a reflex, then dropped. "You're kind, sir, but you know not who I am. Nor I you... Or so I thought." Her tone shifted, probing, testing a blade's edge. I leaned forward, spoon forgotten. "Then speak plain, lass. You're no mere wanderer, not with that voice, that poise. Aethelwood, I'd wager. A Weaver, too, by your talk of Essence." Her flinch confirmed it. "I'm just Rawn, a farmer now, whatever I was before. But you saw something in me out there. What's your game?" The Recognition She hesitated, then pushed her bowl aside, untouched. "No game," she said, voice steadying. "I sought the source of that Essence surge. I found... you." Her eyes narrowed, lingering on Vulcanspine's runes, then meeting mine with sudden certainty. "You wielded Fire in the war, didn't you? Long ago. The Scorch of Eryndor." Her words were a whisper, but they struck like thunder. I froze, hearing the name of a ghost I'd buried. "That man's dead," I growled, guilt clawing up—burning villages, screams of the innocent. "Don't dig up graves, lass." Red whined, nudging my knee. I forced a breath, steadying. "How'd you know?" Her gloved hand trembled, but her voice held firm. "Tales of the Sunding War reached Aethelwood. A Fire Bladeweaver who turned battles with Vulcanspine. Few could wield such power." Her gaze softened, almost pitying. "And fewer still would hide from it, as you do. Yet here you are, reborn by some divine hand." I scowled, hating her truth. "And you? Hiding under that cowl like a thief. What's your tale, princess?" The title slipped out, half-guess, half-taunt, but her sharp intake of breath told me I'd hit true. The Unveiling She stood abruptly, chair scraping, her cowl slipping to reveal half her face—beautiful, Elven-sharp, but marred by jagged burns snaking from cheek to neck. Her green eyes blazed with shame and fury. "Do not mock me," she hissed, voice breaking. "You know nothing of my loss, General." I rose, hands raised, guilt twisting my gut. "I ain't mocking, lass. I'm sorry." My voice softened, seeing Alaina in her pain, my boy in her defiance. "Your scars ain't what I see. It's your strength. You're here, fighting whatever haunts you." I gestured to the table, the stew. "Sit. Tell me, or don't. But you're safe here." She stood frozen, her breath ragged, then sank back into the chair, tugging her cowl lower. "Safe," she echoed, bitter. "No one is safe, not with Ithek Acolytes stirring, tainting the Essence in places like Crygill." Her gaze met mine, raw, unguarded. "I am Amicae, seventh of House Draks. I seek those who burned my soul at the Eldertree. Will you help me, Rawn Mortisimus? Or will you run from your fire again?" The storm's echo rumbled outside, and the Mindreaver's whispers swelled, conjuring her scars as my failures, her attackers as my war's victims. I gripped Vulcanspine's hilt by the hearth and nodded. "I'll help, princess. But fire burns all it touches. You sure you want that?" Her smile was grim, a warrior's edge. "Let it burn." Amicae's Tale She paused, her gloved hand trembling, then spoke, voice a whisper that cut deeper than any blade. "You deserve my tale, Rawn, if we're to walk this path." I leaned forward, spoon forgotten, Red's soft whine the only sound save her words. "I'm listening," I said, meeting her eyes through the cowl's gloom, bracing for the weight of her pain. Amicae's fingers brushed her cowl, a reflex, then stilled. "Six moons past, I walked the Eldertree's glade, its Water Essence my solace. I am seventh-born, low in Aethelwood's line, free to wander where my siblings scheme in court. The Eldertree... it sang to me, its roots deep as Unu's heart, feeding my Weaver's craft." Her voice softened, then hardened. "That night, they came—Fire Weavers, cloaked in shadow, their blades etched with Ithek runes. I sensed their Essence, foul, like ash in a stream, but I was too late." Her hand clenched, leather creaking. "They bound me with flames that bit like vipers, laughing as I screamed. Their leader... his eyes gleamed red, not Elven, not human. He spoke of a ritual, of 'feeding the Breach.' They tore my circlet, burned my face, my neck—" Her voice broke, a sob swallowed. "They... took more than my skin, Rawn. They stole my soul, left me to die in the glade's ash." I gripped the table, knuckles white, seeing Alaina in her pain, my boy in her defiance. The Mindreaver's whispers swelled, conjuring a vision: Amicae's attackers, their faces melting into Fleshreavers, their laughter a gurgling rasp. I shook it off, bile rising. "How'd you survive, lass?" I asked, voice rough, guilt clawing at me for every fire I'd ever wielded. Survival and Purpose Her eyes glistened, but her jaw set. "The Eldertree's Essence. It poured into me, a tide of life, knitting my wounds enough to crawl away. I hid, shamed, my face a ruin. My court... they'd pity me, or worse, use me. My brother Vaelar—" She stopped, voice catching, a flicker of betrayal in her eyes. "He's a Fire Weaver, ambitious, whispering with those who court the Ithek. I cannot trust him, not now." She tugged her cowl, hiding her scars. "I heard tales of a Fire Bladeweaver, the Scorch, who could burn armies. I sought you, Rawn, after your Essence surge shook the air. I thought... if anyone could face these monsters, it's you." Her voice dropped, fierce. "They're tied to the Ithek, to a darkness spreading through Kleinae, tainting the land. I'll kill them, or die trying. But I need your fire." The hearth flared, its spark catching my eye, and Amicae flinched, her gloved hand rising as if to shield her scars. I cursed myself, damping the Fire Essence in my blood. "I'm no hero, Amicae," I said, meeting her gaze. "The Scorch burned villages, innocents, to end a war. My fire's a curse, same as your scars. But if it's vengeance you want, I'll wield it—for you." Her eyes searched mine, raw with pain but alight with resolve. "Not just vengeance," she whispered. "The Ithek's taint grows, Rawn. I've felt it, a rot in the Essence, like a storm's heart. They'll burn more than me if we don't stop them." She leaned forward, voice steadying. "Will you face that darkness with me, Scorch of Eryndor?" The Mindreaver's whispers hissed, conjuring my boy's face, flesh oozing, begging me to die. I gripped Vulcanspine's hilt, its runes warm under my touch. "Aye, princess," I said, voice grim. "We'll face it. But don't expect me to thank Aeloria for dragging me back to this fight." Her lips curved in a faint, broken smile. "Nor I, for surviving." Outside, the storm's rumble answered, a warning of the darkness we'd hunt.

Document.Text:
After lunch, Bubbee and I played hide-and-seek in the yard while Ma mended clothes and Da worked on repairing a fishing net that had been torn in the storm. Everything was perfectly, beautifully normal. Just another ordinary day in our little village by the river. The afternoon sun climbed higher, warming the damp earth and coaxing steam from the puddles left by last night's rain. I was winning our game handily—Bubbee always chose the same hiding spots behind Ma's herb garden—when Izzy appeared from nowhere and wound herself around my legs. "Where have you been, silly cat?" I asked, scooping her up. She was trembling slightly, her green eyes wider than usual. "Did something frighten you?" Izzy mewed softly and pressed her small head against my chin. For a moment, I felt something strange pass between us—not quite a thought, but a feeling of unease that made my stomach flutter. I pushed it aside. Cats were always nervous about something. "You're just being dramatic," I told her, though I held her a little tighter. The rest of the afternoon passed in pleasant routine. Ma sent me to gather wild herbs from the meadow beyond our cottage, where the storm had washed everything clean and bright. The familiar task of filling my basket with mint and chamomile was soothing, and by the time I returned home, the strange feeling from earlier had faded to nothing. Da came in as the afternoon shadows grew long, shaking his head ruefully. "River's still running too wild for nets, but I managed to patch the worst holes," he announced. "Tomorrow should be better for fishing." "Good," Ma said, not looking up from her mending. "We could use some fresh trout for supper tomorrow night." Bubbee bounded over to Da's chair, full of the boundless energy that made sitting still impossible for him. "Da, can I come fishing with you? I promise I'll be quiet this time!" "We'll see, little man," Da laughed, ruffling Bubbee's dark hair. "You weren't exactly quiet last time. Scared away half the fish in the river with your chatter." "I was excited!" Bubbee protested. "The fish should understand excitement!" I giggled at his earnest expression. Everything was so normal, so perfect. Our little cottage felt like the safest place in all the world, filled with warmth and laughter and the comfortable presence of family. If only I had known that safety was an illusion, fragile as morning mist.
The Visitor It was near evening when the knock came at our door. I was playing with Izzy on the kitchen floor, trailing a piece of yarn while she pounced and tumbled. Ma was preparing our evening meal, humming softly as she worked, and Da sat by the fire with Bubbee on his lap, telling stories about the old days when river sprites played tricks on fishermen. Rap, rap, rap. Three firm knocks, polite but insistent. "Wonder who that could be," Da said, gently setting Bubbee aside. "Not many folks venture out after a storm like that." I looked up from my game with Izzy, curious but not concerned. Neighbors often stopped by to check on each other after bad weather. It was one of the things I loved about our village—everyone looked out for everyone else. But Izzy had gone perfectly still, her small body tense as a bowstring. Her green eyes were fixed on the door, and a low, almost inaudible growl rumbled in her throat. "Izzy?" I whispered, reaching for her. "What's wrong?" The kitten's fur was standing on end now, making her look twice her normal size. She pressed herself against my leg, still staring at the door with an intensity that made my skin prickle. Da reached the door and lifted the heavy wooden bar. "Mr. Klisternok!" he said warmly as the door swung open. "What can we do for ye today?" I relaxed slightly. Mr. Klisternok was our neighbor, a kind man who lived with his daughter Alyona just two cottages down. He'd always been friendly to our family, and his daughter was one of my closest friends. There was nothing to worry about. But Izzy's growl grew louder. "Morning, Otec. Fine day, now that that awful storm's behind us," came Mr. Klisternok's familiar voice. Something about his tone made me look up from the floor where I sat with Izzy. From my position, I could only see his feet and legs through the doorway, but something seemed... different. He stood too still, too perfectly balanced, like a statue rather than a living person. "Aye," Da replied. "River's running too strong to fish, but no damage here. How did you folk fare?" "Oh, we managed well enough," Mr. Klisternok said, and now I could definitely hear something wrong in his voice. It was his voice, certainly, but underneath it was something else—a hollow quality, like an echo in an empty well. "Where's your lovely wife?" A chill ran down my spine at the way he said it. Not the warm, neighborly interest I'd heard from him a hundred times before, but something hungry and calculating. Ma appeared at the door, wiping flour from her hands on her apron. From my spot on the floor, I could see her expression change from cheerful welcome to puzzled concern as she saw our visitor. "Good evening, Mr. Klisternok," she said carefully. He didn't return the greeting. Instead, he stepped closer to the threshold, and I finally got a clear look at his face. It was Mr. Klisternok's face, certainly—the same weathered features, the same grey-streaked beard, the same brown eyes I'd known since childhood. But the smile was wrong. It stretched too wide, showing too many teeth, and it never wavered or changed. His eyes held no warmth, no life—they were flat and cold as winter pond ice. "I've a message for the both of you," he said in that hollow, echoing voice. Ma and Da leaned in to listen, their faces trusting and open. They had no reason to suspect anything was amiss. This was their neighbor, their friend, standing at their door in broad daylight. But Izzy knew better. The kitten's entire body was rigid with terror now, her claws digging into the wooden floor as if she were trying to anchor herself against some terrible force. "Don't," I whispered, though I didn't know why. "Ma, Da, don't—" The Corruption That's when Mr. Klisternok opened his mouth. It opened too wide—wider than any human mouth should open, stretching until his jaw seemed to unhinge like a snake's. A horrible gurgling noise bubbled up from somewhere deep in his throat, wet and wrong and utterly inhuman. Then came the mist. It poured from his gaping mouth like smoke from a fire, but smoke was never that color—dark and oily, shot through with veins of sickly purple light that pulsed like a heartbeat. The stuff moved like it was alive, slithering through the air with sinuous purpose, reaching toward my parents with greedy tendrils. "Ma!" I screamed, but it was too late. The mist rushed up Ma's and Da's noses before either of them could react, flowing into them like water into a dry well. They went rigid, their eyes widening in shock and terror for just a moment before going completely blank. It was over in seconds. Mr. Klisternok's mouth snapped shut with an audible click, his face settling back into that horrible, too-wide smile. He straightened, gave a polite nod as if he'd just delivered a pleasant bit of gossip, and turned to walk away. "Good evening," he said in his normal voice, as if nothing at all had happened. Ma and Da stood in the doorway for a long moment, staring into the distance with vacant eyes. I watched in growing horror as something shifted behind their familiar features—something cold and alien that looked out through eyes that were slowly, horribly, turning purple. When they finally moved, it was with that same unnatural smoothness I'd noticed in Mr. Klisternok. They turned and walked back into the cottage without a word, without even acknowledging that I was there. "Ma?" I whispered, my voice barely audible. "Da?" They didn't respond. They moved past me as if I were invisible, going about their evening tasks with mechanical precision. Ma resumed cooking, her movements oddly stilted. Da sat back down by the fire, but he didn't call Bubbee back to his lap. He just stared into the flames with those terrible purple eyes. And Bubbee— My little brother stood frozen in the middle of the kitchen, his face pale with confusion and fear. He looked from Ma to Da to me, his young mind struggling to understand what had just happened. "Rowetha?" he whispered. "What's wrong with Ma and Da?" I scooped up Izzy and got to my feet on shaking legs. The kitten was still trembling, her green eyes bright with an intelligence that seemed far beyond any normal cat's. "I don't know," I said, my voice barely steady. "But we need to hide." Outside, I could hear voices calling from other cottages—neighbors greeting each other with that same hollow echo I'd heard in Mr. Klisternok's voice. The purple mist was spreading, cottage by cottage, family by family. And somewhere in the distance, I could swear I heard laughter that sounded like breaking glass. The storm was over, but something far worse had taken its place. Our quiet little village by the river would never be the same again.

Chapter : The Spreading Shadow
The Refugee Crisis Begins The road to Ashfall was clogged with the desperate and the dying. We'd expected to find empty countryside as we approached the corrupted city, but instead discovered a human river flowing away from the purple-shrouded settlement. Hundreds of refugees stumbled along the main road, their faces hollow with hunger and horror, carrying whatever possessions they could manage in their flight from the spreading darkness. "Sweet Anna Perenna," Amicae breathed as we crested a hill and saw the full scope of the exodus. "There must be thousands of them." I counted the camps scattered across the valley below—hastily erected shelters, cooking fires sending smoke into the grey sky, children crying for parents who would never answer. The sight brought back memories of the Sunding War's aftermath, when entire populations had been displaced by necromantic devastation. "Not just from Ashfall," I realized as we got closer. "Look at their clothing, their accents. They're from dozens of different settlements." Rowetha pressed closer to my side, clutching Izzy tight. The kitten's fur was standing on end, her Guardian senses overwhelmed by the sheer amount of human suffering concentrated in one place. "Why aren't they helping each other more?" she asked, pointing to where different groups of refugees had formed separate camps, eyeing each other with suspicion despite their shared misery. "Fear," I said simply. "When people lose everything, they start seeing threats everywhere. Even in other victims." We made our way through the refugee camps, offering what aid we could—Amicae purifying tainted water sources, me using controlled fire to light cooking hearths, Rowetha's Guardian magic easing the nightmares of traumatized children. But it was like trying to empty the ocean with a teacup. "Twenty-three settlements," reported Marcus Ironwood, a refugee leader we met near the largest camp. He'd been a merchant before the corruption came, organized the survivors from his village of Millbrook when the purple mist started rising from their wells. "Twenty-three towns and villages gone dark in the past month alone. And it's spreading faster each day." "What about help from the capital?" I asked. "Surely Eldhaven is mobilizing aid." Marcus's laugh was bitter. "Eldhaven's got its own problems. Word is Lord Vaelar's declared martial law, says the refugee reports are 'exaggerated by panic.' Claims we're just spreading fear to destabilize the kingdom." My blood ran cold. Vaelar was using his position as regent to control information, downplaying the crisis to maintain his grip on power. Worse, he was probably using the confusion to accelerate the corruption's spread. "The other kingdoms?" Amicae asked. "Kleinae, Aethelwood, the Northern Reaches?" "Don't want to hear it," Marcus said with a shrug. "Got official responses from three different royal courts. All basically said the same thing—'Mannesket's problem, not ours.'" "They think the corruption will stop at their borders?" I asked incredulously. "They think it's not real," Marcus corrected. "Think we're making it up to get aid money or military support. One ambassador actually suggested we were staging the whole thing as some kind of elaborate hoax." I stared at him, trying to process that level of willful blindness. The evidence was right here—thousands of displaced people, detailed accounts of systematic corruption, physical samples of the taint itself. How could anyone dismiss it? "People believe what they want to believe," Amicae said quietly, reading my expression. "And nobody wants to believe their world is ending." International Denial That evening, we sat around a campfire with a group of refugees from different settlements, listening to their stories and trying to piece together the pattern of the corruption's spread. What we learned was worse than I'd feared. "Started small," said Elena, a former innkeeper from Riverside. "Just a few people getting sick, having strange dreams. We thought it was spoiled grain or bad water. Then the dreams got worse, and people started... changing." "The messengers we sent for help never came back," added Thomas, a blacksmith from Ironholt. "Not a one. Found some of their horses wandering loose, but the riders..." He shook his head. "Gone." "We tried warning the neighboring villages," said Sarah, clutching her infant son close. "But they thought we were mad. Said we were trying to steal their trade, drive down property values. Some even accused us of spreading the sickness deliberately." Rowetha had been quietly drawing in her journal, but she looked up at this. "People don't want to see monsters until the monsters are eating them." "Aye, lass," Thomas said grimly. "That's about the size of it." As the night wore on, the stories painted a picture of systematic denial and willful ignorance. Villages refusing to believe their neighbors' warnings. Regional governments dismissing reports as hysteria. Trade guilds more concerned about profit margins than survival. Most disturbing were the accounts of active obstruction—messengers intercepted, warning letters confiscated, witnesses discredited or disappeared. Someone was working very hard to keep the truth from spreading as fast as the corruption itself. "Vaelar," I said, putting the pieces together. "He's not just controlling the narrative in Mannesket. He's got agents in other kingdoms, people working to suppress information." "But why?" Amicae asked. "What does he gain from letting the corruption spread unchecked?" "Power," I said simply. "The more chaos and fear there is, the more people will accept authoritarian control. Classic strategy—create the crisis, then position yourself as the only solution." "But the corruption will kill him too, eventually," Rowetha pointed out. "Will it?" I asked. "We've seen that some people can be compromised rather than consumed. Maybe he thinks he can control it, use it as a weapon while remaining immune himself." The thought chilled me more than the night air. If Vaelar had found a way to harness the Ithek corruption without being destroyed by it, then he wasn't just a traitor—he was something far more dangerous. The First War Council Three days later, a royal messenger found our camp with news that changed everything. King Theron—or rather, the regency council acting in his name—had called for an emergency meeting with representatives from all the neighboring kingdoms. "Lord Vaelar requests your immediate presence at Ravenstone Keep," the messenger said, his face carefully neutral as he delivered what was clearly a summons rather than an invitation. "As an expert on the... unusual circumstances... affecting the region." I almost laughed at the euphemism. "Unusual circumstances." As if the systematic corruption of multiple settlements was just a weather phenomenon or trade dispute. "It's a trap," Amicae said once the messenger had left. "He's luring us out of hiding, into a place where he can control the narrative." "Maybe," I agreed. "But it's also an opportunity. If there's even a chance of convincing the other kingdoms to take this seriously..." "You think they'll listen?" I looked around at the refugee camps, at the thousands of displaced people whose warnings had been ignored for weeks. "They'll have to. The evidence is overwhelming." How wrong I was.

Document.Title: Scene2
Document.Text: Chapter: Shadows at the Threshold The dawn’s gray light bled through my farmhouse shutters, the storm’s damp musk thick as a grave’s breath. I’d slept on a wolf pelt by the hearth, giving Amicae and Rowetha my creaky bed, their soft breaths a fragile peace against Crygill’s taint. Red, my hound, stirred at my feet, his snores a comfort drowned by the Mindreaver’s whispers—Alaina’s face, oozing, my boy’s violet eyes clawing. Rowetha’s tale of her parents, turned monstrous by Klisternok’s black vapor, echoed Amicae’s scars, burned by Ithek Acolytes at the Eldertree. We’d planned to head west to Mannesket, seeking High King Theron’s aid, but the Varkis figure I’d glimpsed in the mist—his brooch gleaming—meant trouble had found us first. I’d finished morning chores, feeding chickens, goats, and cows, my young body too swift, Aeloria’s potion a curse of vigor. A violet-eyed rat in the barn, its acrid rot lingering, confirmed Amicae’s warning: the Mindreaver’s taint was in Kleinae. Now, inside, Amicae stirred porridge by the hearth, her cowl hiding scars, while Rowetha clutched Betsy, her doll, Izzy’s green eyes fixed on the door. My warning—“Somethin’s off outside”—had sharpened Amicae’s gaze, her whisper of “Mindreaver” chilling the air. A rustle beyond the shutters snapped Red’s head up, Izzy hissing. I peered through a crack, spotting a cloaked figure in the mist, Varkis colors bold, too tall for a Tiffling. Amicae’s breath caught. “Varkis,” she whispered, voice grim. “They’re tied to the Ithek, Rawn. They want Rowetha silenced—her tale exposes Crygill’s taint.” Rowetha whimpered, hugging Betsy, as I gripped Vulcanspine, my young body taut, guilt and duty burning. “Stay with the lass,” I told Amicae, stepping toward the door. “Let’s see what this bastard wants.” The Mindreaver’s whispers swelled, conjuring Alaina’s oozing face, Rowetha’s parents clawing, Amicae’s scars as my war’s ash. I’d burn that darkness, or it’d burn me. I flung the door open, Vulcanspine’s runes flaring, Red snarling at my side. The Varkis figure stood twenty paces off, mist curling around him, a longsword sheathed at his hip. Beside him, an Ithek Acolyte, cowled, eyes red as blood, clutched a rune-etched dagger, black vapor trailing his breath. Two Fleshreavers shambled behind, once Tifflings, now monstrosities—skin splitting, violet eyes glowing, claws dripping ichor. The storm’s rumble masked their rasps, but the rot stench choked me, Crygill’s plague come to my doorstep. “Rawn Mortisimus,” the Varkis man called, voice cold as iron. “The Scorch of Eryndor, reborn by divine jest. House Varkis offers amnesty for your war crimes—surrender the Tiffling girl, and walk free.” His eyes flicked to the cottage, where Amicae’s Mistveil Cloak spell wove fog, shielding Rowetha. “Burn your amnesty,” I growled, Vulcanspine steady. “Rowetha’s under my roof. You want her, you’ll bleed.” Guilt surged—villages I’d razed, screams I’d ordered—but Rowetha’s fear, like my boy’s, steeled me. The Mindreaver hissed, conjuring their faces, melting. The Ithek Acolyte stepped forward, vapor coiling. “The Breach hungers,” he rasped, voice a serpent’s hiss. “The girl’s blood will feed it, as the Elven whore’s did.” Amicae’s gasp cut the air, her Aqua Lash snapping from the doorway, water coiling like a noose. “Speak again, and you drown,” she hissed, cowl slipping, scars bared, her green eyes blazing. The Varkis man sneered, drawing his sword. “Kill them,” he barked, and the Fleshreavers lunged, claws raking. I unleashed Scorchfang Slash, Vulcanspine igniting, divine flames cleaving a Fleshreaver’s chest, its ichor sizzling. The stench—rot and charred flesh—hit hard, and Amicae recoiled, her lash binding the second Fleshreaver, but its claws grazed her shield, Essence Chill numbing her hands. “Rawn!” she cried, fog thickening. Rowetha screamed, “Izzy!” as her cat leapt, claws raking the Ithek Acolyte’s face, drawing blood. The Acolyte snarled, dagger slashing, but Izzy darted, and Rowetha threw Betsy, hitting his arm, buying time. I charged, Emberclad Ward flaring a heat shield, deflecting the Varkis man’s sword. “Get to the cart!” I roared, Red snapping at a Fleshreaver’s leg, slowing it. Amicae grabbed Rowetha, pulling her to the cart, supplies loaded from dawn. “We can’t hold them!” she shouted, her Surging Aegis shielding Rowetha as the Ithek Acolyte chanted, black vapor rising—Mindreaver’s breath. I spun, Ashen Purge burning the vapor, flames licking the cottage’s edge. Amicae flinched, fire too close to her scars, but her Tide’s Caress healed a claw wound on my arm, her touch trembling. The Varkis man laughed, sword slashing, grazing my side. “You’re no general now, Scorch,” he taunted, but I countered with Blazecharge, a fiery dash knocking him back, Overburn searing my chest. Red yelped, a Fleshreaver’s claw raking his flank, and Rowetha sobbed, “Red!” Izzy hissed, guarding her. “We run—west!” Amicae yelled, cutting the cart’s horse free, mounting with Rowetha and Izzy. I grabbed Red, blood slick, and leapt behind, urging the horse into a gallop. The Varkis man’s horn blared, more Fleshreavers rasping in the mist, but Amicae’s Mistveil Cloak cloaked us, fog swallowing the farmhouse. My side bled, Overburn throbbing, as we raced along the Orkael, Mannesket’s hills a faint promise. The Mindreaver’s whispers surged, conjuring my boy’s oozing face, Amicae’s attackers, Rowetha’s parents clawing. “Hold on, lasses,” I growled, Red panting in my arms, Izzy’s claws digging into Rowetha. The horn’s echo faded, but a violet glow pulsed behind, the Ithek’s taint trailing. We’d escaped—barely—but Mannesket’s court, Theron’s justice, held new dangers, and Crygill’s heart still burned. Chapter: Embers of Recovery The Orkael’s mist clung to us like a shroud as we rode west, Mannesket’s hills a faint promise beyond the storm’s gray veil. My side throbbed, the Varkis blade’s graze a burning reminder of yesterday’s ambush, my young body—Aeloria’s cursed gift—straining under the weight of guilt and duty. Red limped beside the horse, his flank clawed by a Fleshreaver, but his tail flicked, loyal as ever. Amicae rode ahead, her cowl hiding scars, her arm bandaged from the Ithek Acolyte’s dagger, her Tide’s Caress spell a faint glow healing us all. Rowetha clung to me, her doll Betsy lost, Izzy’s claws digging into her lap, her sobs quieter now but raw as the Crygill taint she’d fled—her parents’ violet eyes, flesh oozing, Klisternok’s black vapor the root. We’d barely escaped, Vulcanspine’s Ashen Purge burning the Mindreaver’s vapor, Amicae’s Mistveil Cloak cloaking our flight. The Varkis horn’s echo haunted me, their demand for Rowetha—“Surrender the girl, and walk free”—tied to their Ithek allies, who spoke of a “Breach” to feed with blood. My grip on Vulcanspine yesterday, stepping toward that Varkis bastard, had been a vow to burn that darkness, though the Mindreaver’s whispers—Alaina’s oozing face, Rowetha’s parents clawing, Amicae’s scars as my war’s ash—nearly broke me. Now, midmorning, we halted in a copse by the Orkael, its waters murmuring like the storm’s dying breath. I dismounted, wincing, and tied the horse to a willow. “Rest here,” I growled, voice rough, my young chest tight with Overburn from yesterday’s Blazecharge. Amicae slid down, her Tide’s Caress flaring to ease Red’s wound, then mine, her gloved hand trembling, fire’s memory in her flinch. Rowetha sat on a log, Izzy curling beside her, green eyes scanning the mist. “We’re alive,” Amicae said, voice a melodic whisper, sitting beside Rowetha. “But Mannesket’s no haven. Theron’s court is split—Varkis coin buys loyalty, and Vaelar’s spies, my kin, may hunt us.” Her green eyes met mine, fierce despite her scars’ shadow. “Rowetha’s tale exposes Crygill’s taint, but we need proof—an Ithek artifact, or the red-eyed leader’s head. Without it, Theron may turn us away.” Rowetha’s lip quivered, stroking Izzy. “What’s in Mannesket, miss? Can the king save Ma and Da?” Her voice, small but steady, hit me like my boy’s fevered plea, stirring the Mindreaver’s hiss: their faces, oozing, clawing. I knelt by her, side aching. “Theron’s a hard man, lass, but if anyone can rally against this taint, it’s him. Your tale’s our blade—keep it sharp.” I glanced at Amicae, her cowl low. “What’s our path, princess? Crygill’s lost, but the Ithek’s Breach is growin’. Eldertree after Mannesket?” Amicae nodded, her circlet glinting. “Mannesket first, for aid. Then the Eldertree, to trace the Ithek’s Essence. But Varkis tracks us, and the Mindreaver’s taint spreads—yesterday’s Fleshreavers weren’t the last.” Her voice dropped, raw. “Your fire, Rawn… it’s our edge, but it’s dim. I saw your Overburn. Can you wield it as you did in the war?” I scowled, standing, Vulcanspine’s weight heavy at my hip. “That Scorch is dead,” I muttered, guilt clawing—villages burned, screams I’d ordered. My new body thrummed, but my Fire Essence felt sluggish, decades of disuse dulling it. “I pushed too hard yesterday. Need to balance it, reconnect.” I stepped to a clearing, Red watching, and drew Vulcanspine, its runes faint. “Forms,” I said, voice grim. “Old Bladeweaver drills. Slow work, not a day’s fix.” I began the Emberflow Kata, Vulcanspine tracing arcs, my young muscles fluid but clumsy, like a lad with a new sword. The first form, Flame’s Ascent, sparked faint embers, warming my blood, but Overburn lingered, my side stinging. The second, Pyre’s Spiral, faltered, flames flickering, my focus split by the Mindreaver’s vision: Alaina, flesh splitting, begging me to die. I growled, pushing through, sweat beading, Red’s tail thumping encouragement. Amicae watched, her flinch subtle but clear, fire’s glow too close to her scars. “Careful,” she warned, voice tight, her Aqua Lash coiling reflexively. “Your Essence is raw. Overburn could cripple you.” Rowetha’s eyes widened, Izzy hissing, sensing my strain. “Gotta try,” I panted, shifting to Cinder’s Veil, a defensive form, flames weaving a faint shield. The Essence stirred, sluggish but alive, a spark of the Scorch I’d buried. I stopped, chest heaving, Vulcanspine lowered. “It’s comin’ back, princess, but slow. Won’t burn armies today.” Amicae’s gaze softened, almost pitying. “Time’s not our ally, Rawn. The Ithek’s Breach grows, and Varkis won’t stop. We need you whole.” She stood, her Tide’s Caress flaring again, easing my Overburn, her touch cool but haunted. “Rest now. We ride soon.” Rowetha offered a shy smile, holding out a strip of dried meat. “For you, sir. My da… he’d say keep your strength.” Her voice broke, but Izzy nudged her, purring, and I took the meat, nodding, my boy’s face in her courage. By noon, we rode on, the Orkael’s banks fading as Mannesket’s border loomed—a wooden palisade, Theron’s raven banner flapping. The first town, Eldhaven, sprawled just beyond, its smoke stacks curling like wary fingers. Guards in leather armor, pikes gleaming, eyed us at the gate. “State your business,” one grunted, his gaze lingering on Amicae’s cowl, Rowetha’s trembling hands. “Travelers seekin’ shelter,” I said, voice steady, Vulcanspine hidden under my cloak, Red at my side, blood crusted but alert. “Fleein’ trouble east.” The guard’s eyes narrowed, but he waved us through, muttering of Varkis patrols nearby. Eldhaven’s streets buzzed—merchants hawking bread, smiths hammering, children dodging carts—but an undercurrent of fear lingered, whispers of “strange folk” vanishing. Amicae’s voice dropped as we dismounted at an inn, the Rusty Crown. “Varkis spies are here,” she murmured, her Mistveil Cloak spell faint, shielding Rowetha. “And the taint… I feel it, faint, in the Essence. We’re not safe.” I gripped Vulcanspine’s hilt, my young body taut, the Mindreaver’s whispers hissing: Rowetha’s parents, violet-eyed, Amicae’s scars burning, Alaina’s plea. “Then we move quick,” I said, meeting her eyes. “Theron’s court next, or the Eldertree. But first, we heal—and I train.” Red nudged my leg, Izzy’s eyes on the shadows, as Eldhaven’s pulse hid a darker heart. Chapter: The Pixie’s Plunge Eldhaven’s midday bustle clung to us like the storm’s damp musk, the Rusty Crown inn a brief refuge after our market trek. Amicae had led us to a contact—a weaver named Lyssa, who loathed Varkis’s taxes and whispered of ranger allies—but her promise of aid came with a warning: Varkis spies prowled Eldhaven, and a taint, faint in the Essence, soured the market’s air. My side ached from the Varkis blade’s graze, though Amicae’s Tide’s Caress dulled it, her own arm bandaged from the Ithek Acolyte’s dagger. Rowetha, clutching Izzy, her cat, bore no scars but Crygill’s weight—her parents’ violet eyes, flesh oozing, Klisternok’s black vapor the root. Red, my hound, trotted beside, his new pearly teeth and clear eyes, a gift of Aeloria or the storm, mirroring my cursed youth. I’d trained at dawn, Emberflow Kata stirring my Fire Essence, but Overburn and decades of disuse left it weak, Vulcanspine’s runes flickering.
