Rorik, the tanner, had vowed ranger aid if we brought proof of the Ithek’s Breach, but the barmaid’s gurgling cough last night, her eyes flickering violet, hinted the Mindreaver’s taint was here. Amicae’s warning—Varkis’s sway in Theron’s court, Vaelar’s spies hunting us—kept us wary, Rowetha’s tale our blade against the Ithek’s darkness. We approached the Rusty Crown, its sign creaking, Eldhaven’s smoke curling like a wary beast. I opened the door for Amicae. She got halfway in the doorway and started up a cussing storm. I rushed around to see a Dragonfly pixie male clutching Amicae’s dress at her cleavage and spewing copious amounts of frothy swill down her appreciable cleavage. “You nasty little flyin’ creatin’! By the nine hells, get this maggot off me!” she screamed. I felt a tingle of arcane power begin to ramp up. I clutched her arm. “No, Amica!” I said. Then there appeared a sketchy-looking man reaching out his nasty hand toward Amicae’s chest. Red was there instantly, his rumbling so low you could feel it in your chest. Amicae was stompin’ and flailin’ around, tryin’ to throw the offendin’ up-chucker from her person. Finally, the flyin’ water spigot shut off. “Aaagh, you flyin’ rat! You despicable, repugnant little swine!” Again, a spark of the arcane tingled my scalp. “Amicae, no!” I said. (hiccup) “Oh, pardon me, madame, I sss-eem to have accidentally flown into your sweater puppies. I cc…commend you on their obedience trainin’. They do a marvelous job o’ sittin’ pretty.” Amicae turned an even darker shade of red. The pixie, wings buzzing, hovered unsteadily, his iridescent vest stained with ale, green eyes bleary but glinting mischief. No taller than a handspan, he was a rapscallion, reeking of swill and trouble. “Name’s Trink!” he slurred, bowing midair, nearly crashing into Rowetha, who yelped, Izzy hissing, claws swiping. The sketchy man—leather apron, reeking of fish—laughed, his hand still outstretched, until Red’s snarl froze him. I grabbed his wrist, my young grip iron, guilt and fury burning. “Back off, filth,” I growled, Vulcanspine’s hilt warm under my cloak. Amicae’s Aqua Lash coiled, water dripping, her scars hidden but her fury bared. “You’ve one breath to explain, pixie, ‘fore I drown you,” she hissed, voice a melodic blade, her cowl damp with swill, arcane spark fading but poised. The common room stilled, patrons eyeing us, some Varkis spies, I’d wager. Trink hiccupped, wings faltering. “No harm meant, lady! Jus’ celebratin’—pinched a Varkis purse, see?” He flashed a coin pouch, Varkis colors bold, then tucked it away, grinning. “Heard you lot talkin’ ‘bout Crygill, taint, Ithek. I know things—ranger routes, Varkis hideouts. Help me, I help you!” His gaze flicked to Rowetha, softening. “Poor lass, seen monsters, eh?” Rowetha clutched Izzy, eyes wide but curious. “You know Ma and Da’s sickness?” she asked, voice small, her Crygill tale—Klisternok’s vapor, violet eyes—stirring Trink’s nod. The Mindreaver’s whisper hissed, conjuring her parents, clawing, Alaina’s oozing face, Amicae’s scars as my war’s ash. I released the fishmonger, shoving him back. “Speak, Trink,” I said, Red’s rumble steady, his new teeth a threat. “What’s your game? Varkis bait, or just a drunk fool?” My Fire Essence flickered, weak from yesterday’s Overburn, but Vulcanspine’s weight grounded me. Trink landed on a table, swaying. “No bait! I’m a Varkis thorn—steal their coin, dodge their blades. Know a ranger, Kalia, hates ‘em too. She’s in Eldhaven tonight, at the Thorned Fang tavern. Wants Ithek dirt, like you.” He winked at Amicae, reckless. “Sorry ‘bout the dress, lady. Mighty fine—” “Enough!” Amicae snapped, her Tide’s Caress cleaning the swill, her face flushed, scars glimpsed by patrons before her cowl fell back. “You’ll lead us to Kalia, pixie, or my water’s your grave.” Her fire fear lingered, my presence a spark she dreaded, but her resolve held, eyes on Rowetha’s hope. I glanced at the fishmonger, now slinking off, likely to Varkis ears. “We’re watched,” I muttered, meeting Amicae’s gaze. “Kalia sounds good, but Trink’s a risk. Trust him, or ditch him?” Rowetha stroked Izzy, whispering, “He’s funny, sir, like Bubbee,” her lost brother’s memory a knife in my gut. Amicae’s voice was grim. “We need rangers. Trink comes, but one jest.” and “he’s leashed.” She glared at him, arcane tingling. “Move, pixie. Thorned Fang, now.” Trink saluted, wings buzzing, and I grabbed Vulcanspine, Red’s new strength at my side, his growl a warning to the room. We stepped into Eldhaven’s dusk, the market’s din fading, smoke curling like a noose. Trink darted ahead, unsteady but swift, leading us to the Thorned Fang. A barmaid’s cough echoed from the Rusty Crown, wet, gurgling, her eyes flickering violet. Amicae’s hand tightened, sensing the taint, and Red’s hackles rose, his pearly teeth bared. The Ithek’s Breach loomed, Varkis spies closing, and Kalia’s aid was our spark in Eldhaven’s darkening heart.

Chapter 1: The Storm's Wake The Horror in Crygill Three days after the Storm of Delfaen The first thing Rowetha noticed was that Mr. Klisternok's smile had gone wrong. She'd been sitting behind the barn with Izzy, the black kitten with white paws who'd adopted her a week ago, watching the morning sun burn away the last of the storm clouds. The great tempest that had raged three nights past was finally breaking up, though the air still crackled with strange energy that made Izzy's fur stand on end. "Shhh, Izzy. It's just Mr. Klisternok," she whispered as the kitten began to hiss, but something deep in her chest whispered that nothing was 'just' anything anymore. Mr. Klisternok walked into their yard from the tail path that ran along the river, but he moved wrong—too smooth, like he was floating just above the ground instead of walking on it. He'd always been a kind neighbor, and his daughter Alyona was one of her best friends at school. But this wasn't the Mr. Klisternok who'd taught her to bait fishing hooks and told funny stories about the river sprites. This thing wearing his face didn't even glance in her direction as it walked straight to their front door. "Mr. Klisternok, what can we do for ye today?" Da answered the knock, his voice warm with the easy friendship of neighbors who'd known each other for years. "Morning, Otec. Fine day, now that that awful storm's behind us," the thing said, smiling in a way that made Rowetha's stomach turn cold. "Aye," Da replied. "River's running too strong to fish, but no damage here." The false Mr. Klisternok nodded, that horrible smile never fading. "Where's your lovely wife?" Ma appeared at the door, wiping flour from her hands on her apron. "Good morning, Mr. Klisternok." He didn't return the greeting. "I've a message for the both of you." Ma and Da leaned in to listen—and that's when everything turned wrong. Mr. Klisternok opened his mouth... too wide. A horrible gurgling noise bubbled out as thick, oily vapor—dark and smoky and shot through with veins of purple light—poured from his mouth like it was alive. It slithered through the air and rushed up Ma's and Da's noses before either of them could move. It was over in a second. Mr. Klisternok straightened, turned around, and walked away like nothing had happened, that terrible smile still fixed on his face. Ma and Da just stood there for a moment, staring blankly into the distance with vacant eyes. Then something shifted behind their familiar features—something cold and hungry that looked out through eyes that were slowly, horribly, turning purple. When they shook their heads and went back inside, they moved with that same wrong smoothness she'd seen in Mr. Klisternok. Rowetha sat frozen behind the barn, Izzy trembling in her arms, as the morning sun painted everything in cheerful gold light that felt like a mockery. The storm was over, but something worse had taken its place. Something that wore the faces of everyone she loved. * The Last Peace Three days after the Storm of Delfaen The twin suns of Unu crested the eastern hills as I shuffled from the cottage, my walking stick tapping against the worn stone path. Each step sent a familiar ache through my knees, a morning greeting I'd grown accustomed to over the past decade. The pain started sharp and settled into a dull throb--my body's way of reminding me that another night of restless sleep on aging bones had passed. Three days had passed since the great storm—the most violent tempest I'd seen in decades—and the morning air still held an electric charge that made my old war wound ache and set Red's hackles on edge. That storm had been unnatural, I was certain of it. The lightning had struck in patterns too precise to be random, and the thunder had carried undertones that sounded almost like voices. When the worst of it passed, I'd found strange scorch marks in my garden—not from lightning, but from something else. Something that had touched the earth and left it subtly wrong. Red emerged from the cottage behind me, moving with the same careful deliberation that marked us both as creatures whose best days lay behind them, but his behavior had been odd since the storm. His ears constantly swiveled toward the north, as if he could hear something in the distance that escaped my aging senses. His claws clicked against the stones with each measured step, a sound that had grown more pronounced as arthritis stiffened his joints. We moved like a pair of old clockwork toys, our springs wound too loose, our gears grinding with each motion. "Come on then, old fellow," I wheezed, my voice carrying the gravel of too many years and too many battles. The words emerged with a puff of vapor in the cool morning air, and I had to pause to catch my breath after just those few syllables. "Eggs won't gather themselves." But even as I spoke, part of me wondered if gathering eggs would matter for much longer. The storm had brought changes I could feel in my bones—a sense that the peaceful life I'd built was balanced on the edge of a knife, ready to tip into chaos at any moment. Red's tail managed a weak wag as he fell into step beside me, his grey muzzle nearly white now, his brown eyes clouded with the milky film of age but still bright with the affection that had sustained us both through the long years since Alaina's passing. But those eyes held worry too, the kind of tension I'd seen in old soldiers who could smell battle on the wind. We made quite a pair--two old warriors marking time until the final retreat, finding purpose in the smallest of daily rituals. The chicken coop sat nestled against the cottage's eastern wall, positioned to catch the morning warmth of Soli's first light. I'd built it there fifteen years ago when Alaina and I first settled in this quiet corner of Kleinae, back when my hands were steady and my back straight. She'd wanted chickens, said they'd make the place feel more like home after the nomadic years following the war. "A proper farm needs proper animals," she'd insisted, her eyes twinkling with the same mischief that had first caught my attention in a tavern in Aethelwood. The memory brought its familiar pang of loss, dulled by time but never quite absent. I could still see her standing in this very spot, scattering grain to the birds while humming some half-remembered lullaby from her childhood. The chickens had loved her, clustering around her feet like feathered courtiers seeking favor from their queen. Now they barely tolerated me. "Easy there, ladies," I murmured to the hens as they squawked their displeasure at my intrusion. The largest of them--a bad-tempered brown bird I'd named Fury for obvious reasons--fixed me with one beady eye and clucked what could only be described as criticism. My movements were slow, careful--not just from courtesy to the birds, but from necessity. These old hands shook more each morning, a tremor that had started as barely noticeable and grown over the years into something I had to actively compensate for. The wooden latch on the coop door required three attempts to open. My fingers, once capable of the delicate work required to carve spell-runes into sword steel, now struggled with simple tasks. The arthritis was worst in the mornings, before movement and warmth loosened the joints enough for basic function. Inside the coop, the familiar smell of straw and chicken droppings mixed with the sweeter scent of fresh eggs. It was a honest smell, one that spoke of simple work and simpler rewards. Nothing like the acrid smoke and burning metal that had filled my nostrils during the war years. The first egg nearly slipped from my fingers. I caught it against my chest, feeling my heart skip with the small exertion--another reminder that this body was failing in ways both large and small. There was a time when I could have caught a dozen falling eggs without thought, when my reflexes were quick enough to snatch arrows from the air or parry blade strikes too fast for normal eyes to follow. Now I celebrated not dropping breakfast. I placed the precious egg in my worn wicker basket, noting how the simple woven container had darkened with age and use. Alaina had made it herself from reeds gathered along the Orkael River, her nimble fingers weaving the strands with a skill I'd never possessed. Like so many things in our home, it carried her memory in its every fiber. Red lowered himself gingerly to the ground nearby, his old bones protesting the movement with a soft grunt that echoed my own morning symphony of aches. He watched me work with the patient attention of a creature who understood that everything took longer now, that rushing only led to mistakes and spilled breakfasts. His breathing was audible--not quite labored, but carrying an effort that hadn't been there in his younger years. "Remember when you used to chase these birds?" I asked him, moving carefully to the next nest. My lower back sent up its familiar complaint as I bent down, and I had to grip the wooden wall for support. "Alaina would scold you something fierce, but I think she enjoyed watching you play. Said it reminded her of the pups she'd had as a girl." Red's ears perked slightly at Alaina's name, though the movement was less pronounced than it once would have been. His hearing wasn't what it used to be either. Even after ten years, he remembered her, just as I did. Some losses carve themselves too deep to ever truly heal, becoming part of the landscape of the heart. The second egg came easier, the hens having settled into their routine grumbling. Fury had positioned herself where she could continue to glare at me, but the others went about their business with the resigned air of creatures who knew resistance was futile. I moved from nest to nest, each step measured, each reach calculated to avoid the sharp complaint of my lower back. The third nest was the furthest from the door, requiring me to navigate carefully around the birds. My walking stick helped, but some mornings I wondered if it wouldn't be easier to simply stay in bed and let the world continue without me. The thought came more frequently these days--not from despair, but from a bone-deep weariness that seemed to grow heavier with each passing season. But Red depended on me, and I on him. We were each other's anchor to purpose, two old souls keeping each other tethered to life through sheer stubborn loyalty. The fourth egg was smaller than the others, probably from the young hen I'd been nursing back to health after a fox attack. She'd been laying irregularly ever since, but at least she was alive. I'd always had a soft spot for wounded creatures trying to heal--a trait that had served me well in the aftermath of battles, less well in the politics that followed. "Six eggs today," I announced, showing the basket to Red as if he could count and cared about our small triumph. "That's two better than yesterday. The brown hen's finally earning her keep." Red's tail thumped once against the ground--approval, or perhaps just acknowledgment that I'd spoken. After forty years together, the distinction hardly mattered. A distant rumble of thunder made us both look toward the northern sky. Dark clouds gathered on the horizon like an approaching army, their edges lit by flickers of lightning still too far away to hear. Though Sola had yet to fully rise and join her partner in their daily dance, the air already held that electric tension that spoke of a significant storm approaching. My old war wound--a scar along my left thigh where an Ithek blade had found its mark--began to throb with the barometric pressure change. Something about those clouds troubled me. They moved with purpose, following patterns that seemed too deliberate for natural weather. And the color was wrong—not the honest grey-black of normal storm clouds, but something darker, touched with an oily sheen that made my skin crawl. "Looks like we'll have weather tonight," I told Red, who had struggled to his feet with obvious effort. The sight of him fighting his own body's betrayals never failed to twist something in my chest. "Best get the animals secured." * The Choice Crygill Rowetha's first thought wasn't to run—it was Bubbee. She crept closer to the cottage, Izzy trembling in her arms, trying to peer through the windows without being seen. Inside, she could hear voices—Ma and Da talking in those wrong, layered tones that made her skin crawl. But no sign of her little brother. "Bubbee?" she whispered, so quietly the word was barely breath. "Bubbee, where are you?" A small sound from the root cellar behind the cottage made her heart leap. The wooden doors were slightly ajar, and she could see a flash of movement in the darkness below. He was hiding. Smart boy. She started toward the cellar, but Izzy's claws suddenly dug into her arms, the kitten's eyes wide with terror. Following the cat's gaze, Rowetha saw them emerging from other cottages now—neighbors walking the village paths with that same horrible smoothness, all wearing those patient, hungry smiles. Purple mist was beginning to rise from several chimneys. Mrs. Tibble from the bakery moved past her garden gate with mechanical precision, her usual warm greeting replaced by an empty stare. Young Henrik, who'd taught Rowetha to whittle, stood in his doorway with purple light flickering behind his eyes. Even old Grandmother Morra, who'd always moved so slowly with her cane, now glided between the cottages like she was floating just above the ground. More were coming. And if she tried to reach Bubbee now, they'd see her. The hardest choice of her young life tore at her heart. Stay and try to save her brother, or flee and hope she could come back for him later. Every instinct screamed at her to run to the cellar, to grab Bubbee and get them both to safety. But Izzy's terror was real, and the kitten had known about Mr. Klisternok before anyone else. "Stay hidden, Bubbee," she whispered toward the cellar, tears streaming down her face. "Stay hidden until I can come back for you." She couldn't save him if she was caught too. She had to be smart, had to survive, had to find help. Someone, somewhere, must know how to stop this thing that was stealing her family, her friends, her entire world. Then she ran for the old oak grove beyond the village, her heart breaking with every step, Izzy's frightened mewing the only honest sound left in a world gone mad. * The Morning Routine Rawn's cottage We made our way slowly to the small barn where Bessie, my ancient cow, stood waiting for her morning attention. Like everything else on this little farm, she was past her prime but still serving faithfully. Her brown and white hide showed patches of grey that matched the silver in my own hair, and her udder had never quite recovered its former productivity after a difficult calving three years past. I set the egg basket carefully on a fence post--another motion that required conscious effort to execute smoothly--and reached for the milking stool. The three-legged seat was worn smooth by years of use, its surface polished by countless mornings just like this one. The simple act of sitting required a moment's pause to let my knees settle into position. The joints protested the change in angle, sending sharp reminders up my legs that I was no longer the man who could spend days in the saddle without complaint. Red positioned himself nearby, close enough to offer comfort but far enough to avoid a kick from Bessie should she grow restless. "There's a good girl," I murmured, beginning the familiar rhythm of milking. My hands moved slowly but steadily, coaxing streams of warm milk into the wooden bucket. The motion itself was soothing, a meditation of repetition that connected me to countless generations of farmers who had performed this same simple task. But even this had grown harder over the years. My grip wasn't what it once was--fingers that had wielded Vulcanspine with precision now struggled to maintain pressure for extended periods. My shoulders protested the repetitive motion, the right one especially where an old wound had never quite healed properly. The muscles in my forearms burned with fatigue after just a few minutes of work. Bessie shifted restlessly, sensing my discomfort. Animals always knew when their humans were struggling, and she'd grown increasingly patient with my limitations as the years passed. Sometimes I wondered if she understood that we were all growing old together, all slowing down in harmony. The Mindreaver stirred then, as it sometimes did in quiet moments when my guard was down. Whispers of memory, faces of men I'd failed to save, villages that had burned because my fire had raged beyond control. The corruption that had spread through Ithek-touched lands, turning fertile fields to ash and clean water to poison. The weight of command decisions that had saved kingdoms but cost individual lives. I pushed the dark thoughts aside with practiced ease, the same mental discipline that had kept me sane through the war years. Those days were long past. I was a farmer now, a keeper of chickens and cows, a man whose greatest battle was against the daily deterioration of his aging body. The Scorch of Eryndor was dead, buried beneath years of peaceful routine and deliberate obscurity. "Nothing wrong with growing old, is there, Red?" I said, though I wasn't entirely convinced. The words felt like a prayer I wasn't sure I believed. "It's natural. Expected. Better than the alternative, as they say." Red's answering whine suggested he wasn't entirely convinced either. Or perhaps he was simply responding to the note of uncertainty in my voice that I'd tried to hide. With the milking finished, I struggled to my feet, muscles protesting the change in position like rusty hinges forced to move. The bucket felt heavier than it should, and I had to grip it with both hands to ensure I didn't spill our breakfast. The walk back to the cottage would test my already-tired legs, but there was no choice but to manage it. Red watched with patient understanding as I shuffled toward the cottage, moving at the pace my body demanded rather than the speed my mind might prefer. He understood limitation in a way that younger creatures couldn't--understood that strength was finite and had to be parceled out carefully throughout the day. The path from barn to cottage had never seemed longer. Each step was a negotiation between will and flesh, between the desire to move normally and the reality of joints that ground against each other with every motion. Halfway there, I had to pause to catch my breath, setting the bucket down and leaning heavily on my walking stick. The sun had climbed higher now, both Soli and Sola visible in the eastern sky, their combined light warming the morning air and painting the world in shades of gold and silver. On a younger man's day, this would have been the time for heavier work--tending the larger garden, repairing fences, perhaps riding to the village for supplies. For me, it meant rest and recovery from the simple task of gathering eggs and milk. I settled onto the wooden bench outside the cottage door, placing the bucket and basket carefully beside me with the exaggerated care of someone who couldn't afford to waste anything through clumsiness. The bench was another of my own constructions from those early years, built sturdy enough to support two people watching sunsets together. Now it held only me and my accumulated aches. Red joined me with his own careful movements, lowering himself with a sigh that perfectly matched my own feelings. We sat in comfortable silence, two old companions sharing the warmth of morning sun and the satisfaction of small tasks completed. The simple pleasure of sitting still, of not asking our bodies to perform any additional tasks, felt like luxury. "Peaceful, isn't it?" I said, scratching behind Red's ears with fingers that trembled slightly from the morning's exertions. His fur was softer now than it had been in his prime, and thinner, revealing the pink skin beneath in places. "No battles to fight, no impossible choices to make. Just chickens and cows and the slow passage of time." But even as I spoke the words, I wondered if peace was the same as contentment. There were moments--more frequent lately--when I felt like I was simply marking time, waiting for death with the patience of someone who had already lived his allotted span. The thought didn't frighten me, exactly, but it left me feeling oddly hollow, as if I were already fading away by degrees. * The Hiding The oak grove, Crygill Rowetha pressed herself against the massive trunk of the oldest oak, Izzy clutched tight against her chest. From here, she could see the entire village spread below, and what she saw made her stomach turn. Purple mist rose from every cottage now, twisting in the still air like smoke from funeral pyres. The things that had been her neighbors moved through the streets in coordinated patterns, searching. Their movements were too precise, too purposeful—like pieces on a game board controlled by a single mind. They were looking for her. She could feel it, the weight of their attention like cold fingers on her neck. "We have to be quiet, Izzy," she breathed into the kitten's fur. "We have to be so, so quiet." But even as she spoke, she could hear them calling her name. Not with the voices of people who loved her—Ma's warm tone that had sung lullabies, Da's gentle rumble that had told bedtime stories—but with something wearing their voices like masks. "Rowetha," the thing with Ma's face called from the cottage doorway. "Come home, little star. We have wonderful news to share." "Rowetha," echoed the Klisternok-thing from the river path. "Your friend Alyona is asking for you. She's eager to play." More voices joined the chorus—Mrs. Tibble promising fresh honey cakes, Henrik offering to teach her a new carving technique, Grandmother Morra saying she had stories to tell. A symphony of familiar tones twisted into something hungry and wrong. They knew she could hear them. They knew she was close. But they couldn't find her. Not yet. Izzy's purr vibrated against her chest—not the content rumble of a happy cat, but something deeper, more purposeful. The kitten's eyes glowed with faint silver light in the growing darkness, and Rowetha felt a strange warmth spread through her body. Hidden, a voice whispered in her mind—not words, but meaning flowing directly into her thoughts. Protected. Guardian watches. Rowetha didn't understand what was happening, but she trusted Izzy. The kitten had known something was wrong with Mr. Klisternok before anyone else. Maybe she knew how to keep them safe too. Below in the village, the purple-eyed things continued their search, but they passed by the oak grove without stopping, as if it simply wasn't there. Whatever power flowed through Izzy was hiding them both, wrapping them in a cloak of invisibility that even those hungry, seeking eyes couldn't penetrate. As night fell and the village lights turned from warm yellow to sickly purple, Rowetha held her breath and waited for dawn—for a chance to escape, to find help, to somehow save Bubbee and everyone else who'd been stolen by the thing that smiled with Mr. Klisternok's face. * The Approaching Storm Rawn's cottage, evening The storm clouds continued to build throughout the afternoon, and by evening, the first drops of rain had begun to fall. I secured the animals with movements that grew more labored as fatigue accumulated, brought in firewood with careful attention to my failing grip, and prepared for a night of wind and weather. Red settled by the hearth as thunder began to rumble in earnest, his instincts telling him to seek shelter and warmth. Neither of us knew that this would be the last ordinary evening of our old life together. That by morning, everything would have changed in ways I couldn't have imagined. That the peaceful farmer I'd become was about to be transformed back into something I'd thought was dead and buried. My bones are aching--an ache that reaches deep down into the marrow. The second storm of the day approaches, and I can tell it's going to be a whopper. There's an electric charge to the air. The hairs on my arms are standing on end, and the wind is howling. This is only the edge of the storm; the heart has yet to reach my quaint cottage. At my side lies my only companion these many years. As ancient as I, he whines at the maelstrom occurring outside these four walls. "Hush now, we've been through storms before," I said, reaching down to pat his grey head. "If it does take us, at least we go together." Red is all that remains of my family. My wife passed quietly in her sleep some ten years back. Our son passed long before that to a fever. After my service to the Seven Kingdoms during what they called the Sunding War, I had wished for a quiet life. We found an out-of-the-way area with a spot of land and farmed. We had been content. As I stare into the warm, glowing flames of the fireplace, I contemplate my long life. Sitting and remembering seems to be my only pastime nowadays. My life had been adventurous, full of triumphs and failures alike--even devastating loss on occasion. Full of choices that had to be made under the direst of circumstances. What I had been scared of most was the politics. Outside, the storm increased its intensity. Suddenly there came a knock at the door. "Red, is that the door?" I said. "Who would be out in this weather?" Red chuffed in response. Boom! Boom! Boom! "Confounded visitors in this mess," I muttered. Grunting, I rose to my feet and shuffled toward the door. I lifted the bar and turned the knob, and the door swung open. Before me stood a man of average stature with dark features. His gray eyes pierced into mine with an intensity that made me pause. "Yes, young man, what can I do for you?" I said. "So sorry, old master, but may I come out of this rain and find succor?" he asked. "Aye, I imagine you do. Alright, come in," I said. "I won't leave you out in this mess." He stepped inside, and I closed and barred the door behind him. "Sir, this is frightful weather. Thank you for your kindness," he said. "Are you hungry?" I asked. "Yes, sire, thank you." "Alright, let me fetch a bowl." * An Evening's Hospitality Rawn's cottage The stranger and I settled down to cheese, bread, and lamb stew. Red took his customary position at my feet, hoping for a dropped morsel or two. Then the strangest little squeak and squeal escaped Red's old gray backside--it was a right stinker. "Sorry, the dog is old and my only companion," I said apologetically. The stranger laughed. "It's his home. I am but a visitor." I laughed and smiled. I thought I might like this fellow. "What brings you out in this miserable weather?" I asked. "I was passing through when this storm struck. Yours is the only place I saw," he said. "It was providence." "Yes, indeed." We finished our fare, and I offered him some wine. "I'm old and achy--you may take the bed tonight. I've grown accustomed to sleeping in this old rocking chair by the fire to keep my old bones warm." "That is very kind of you. Thank you," he said. "Sleep well," I said. "Sleep well, sire," he responded. It was a tradition Alaina had started, insisting that we had room for one more at our table and a dry corner for anyone in need. She'd possessed an endless capacity for generosity that had shamed me during our early years together, before I learned to accept kindness as strength rather than weakness. Even now, a decade after her passing, I honored her memory by keeping an open door for travelers caught by weather or circumstance. The cottage grew quiet save for the storm raging outside and Red's occasional snore from his spot by the dying fire. I dozed fitfully in my chair, as had become my custom—the bed felt too large, too empty without Alaina's presence, and the chair allowed me to tend the fire through the night when my old bones protested too much to find sleep. But something about this storm felt different. More charged with purpose than mere weather should be. The lightning seemed to follow patterns, and the thunder carried echoes that reminded me unpleasantly of battle-cries from the war. I found myself checking the bar on the door more than once, though what I thought might be seeking entry, I couldn't say. * The Dawn of Change Crygill, the next morning By dawn, the entire village of Crygill would be transformed. Purple mist rose from every chimney, and the things that had once been her neighbors moved through the streets with coordinated purpose, still searching for the one who had escaped their net. But Rowetha remained hidden in the oak grove, wrapped in Izzy's protective magic, watching her world die and praying to gods who seemed very far away that someone, somewhere, would know how to make it right again. She didn't know that fifty miles to the south, that someone was about to be reborn. * The Transformation Begins Rawn's cottage, dawn I awoke to the glare of Unu's double suns pouring their light through my kitchen window. I rose on creaky knees, causing me to groan. I looked and saw I had awakened the stranger. "Care to breakfast with me?" I asked. "That would be great," he said. I tottered over to the fireplace to start preparing a meal. As I moved about the kitchen, I became aware of the stranger watching me with those piercing grey eyes--not in judgment, but with something that felt almost like... understanding. "You move like a soldier," he observed quietly. "Even with age, there's purpose in your steps. Economy of motion." I paused in my stirring of the porridge. Few people noticed such things. "Once upon a time, perhaps. Long ago." "Not so long that it's forgotten." He leaned forward slightly. "The Sunding War?" My hand stilled on the wooden spoon. "How did you--" "The way you positioned your chair last night. Back to the wall, clear view of the door. The sword by the hearth--that's no farmer's tool." His voice was gentle, not probing, as if he were simply stating facts he already knew. "Vulcanspine, if I'm not mistaken." I turned to face him fully, suspicious now. "Who are you?" "Someone who knows what it means to carry the weight of necessary choices," he said simply. "Someone who understands that the hardest battles are fought long after the war ends." There was something in his tone--a depth of weariness that matched my own. I found myself setting down the spoon and really looking at him. His face was unlined, but his eyes... his eyes held the same exhaustion I saw in my own mirror. I fried salted pork along with fresh eggs, broke out some cider and day-old bread. I set the table, and we sat down to eat. I flipped an occasional morsel to Red under the table as the stranger and I ate in companionable silence, enjoying the sounds of a spring morning. Birds were singing, and my old rooster cut loose with a crow. We finished eating, and the stranger stood. "Sir, you have shown a great deal of kindness to me. I wish to repay you. Take this tonic--it will help with the feebleness and aches of age," he said, producing a bottle wrapped in red cloth. "You owe me nothing, young man," I protested. "Rawnae Lupio, you are a good man. You deserve this simple gift," he said. Then he walked to the door, lifted the bar, opened it, and exited the cottage. Red moaned and trudged out the door after him. "Alright, I'm coming," I said to Red's retreating form. * The Mysterious Departure Rawn's cottage I walked out into the early morning glare of Soli and Sola, Unu's double suns. I looked around, but I could not see the stranger anywhere. "Strange—he couldn't have gotten too far. He was a pleasant young man, though," I said to myself. I inhaled the fresh, clean spring air. It smelled of recent rain and wildflowers. The morning bustled with the activities of nature. I looked at my hand, clutching a satiny red cloth wrapped around a bottle with clear liquid inside. Red trudged from around the side of the house. I waddled my way to my favorite oak tree to lean against it. Red and I had sat here many times over the years. We would sit in this spot for hours, watching the comings and goings of the local townsfolk and farmers—most of which were Tiffling folk. I lived in the Kingdom of Kleinae. This road connected Kleinae and Mannesket, so occasionally we would see humans as well. Alaina and I had chosen to live in Kleinae because the Tifflings were respectful and private folk. They never meddled in anyone's business, yet they were kind and friendly. I bent down and settled against the large oak tree. Red took his spot at my side, taking his sweet time to get comfortable. When he finally settled right against my hip, he let out a long sigh. I reached down to scratch his ears. "Well, fellow, another day arrives for us," I said. I started contemplating again, remembering a war that had nearly been the ending of all life on Unu. Remembering hundreds of acts of selfless heroism—men and women who had sacrificed themselves for the good of all. Red groaned. "Yes, old friend. I understand," I said. Red and I were at the stage of life where we were just waiting for the sweet release of death—the end of a full life, our final reprieve. Neither of us was what we once were. We were mere shells of the strength and vigorous bodies we had once possessed. "A tonic to remove the feebleness and aches of age, he said," I mused, examining the bottle. I reached down again, scratching along Red's neck. "You want me to take it, old boy?" Red groaned in response. "Well, if it kills me, that will remove the feebleness and aches of age too," I said with dark humor. Red whined. "Shh, stop, old boy. I was just making an observation." I continued my reflections. My Alaina had been gone now for ten years. I missed her daily. She had been a good, caring woman, though she was never the same after we lost our boy. I had never felt more helpless than when I watched him burn with fever in his bed. He was only ten summers at the time and should have had so many more. I examined the contents of the vial. Not a single thing about it seemed remarkable—clear as water, and when I pulled the cork, odorless. "Well, if it kills me, you'll have the whole place to yourself, Red," I said. Red whined again, then laid his head down and sighed. "Alright, old fellow—bottoms up!" I said, and quaffed the contents of the vial. Red raised his head and whined pitifully. My vision exploded into a rainbow of bright colors, and I felt a pain unlike any I had ever experienced in my life. I felt like my entire body had separated into the tiniest fragments of existence. I was simultaneously freezing and burning up. I was lightning and volcanic eruptions, the mightiest storm and the deepest abyss. I found myself begging to either die or pass out. Finally, a peaceful darkness encompassed me. And so began the transformation that would change everything—the corruption spreading north in places like Crygill, and the power that would be needed to fight it meeting in a moment that would reshape the fate of two worlds. The storm's wake had delivered its gifts: horror to some, hope to others, and change to all. In the oak grove of a dying village, a child and her Guardian cat waited for salvation. By an ancient oak in a peaceful kingdom, an old soldier was reborn as something that might provide it. The real war was about to begin.

Recognition "Do you?" she asked, her voice barely above a whisper. "Look closer, Rawnae. Look past the scars and the years." My blood turned to ice at the sound of my full name. "How do you—" "Rawnae Lupio Mortisimus," she continued, tears beginning to stream down her scarred cheeks. "The Scorch of Eryndor. The man who burned away the Lich Emperor's necromantic fog at Vyrn's Pass and saved the Seven Kingdoms." Red's ears flattened against his head, and he whined in confusion. That name—he remembered that name, remembered when it had been spoken with reverence and fear. "That man's dead," I said harshly. "Has been for forty years." She laughed, but there was no humor in it—only pain and desperate recognition. "Dead? You look remarkably alive to me. Young, even." Her green eyes blazed with emotions I couldn't name. "Though I suppose that's impossible, since I've been watching you grow old and grey for decades." Understanding hit me like a cavalry charge. Those eyes, that musical voice, the way she carried herself despite her injuries... "Amicae," I whispered. "Seventh of House Draks." "The very same." She smiled through her tears, a expression that was heartbreaking to see on her scarred face. "Hello, Rawnae. It's been a long time." The implications crashed over me like a tidal wave. She'd found me. Years ago, decades ago. She'd been watching from the shadows while I lived and loved and mourned, never knowing she was there. "Why didn't you—" "Come to you?" Her voice turned bitter. "I did find you, eventually. Found you with your wife, with your son. You had built the life you always claimed to want—peace, love, family. What right did I have to disrupt that happiness?" Red rose slowly to his feet, tail wagging uncertainly. Some part of him recognized her now, remembered the young elf who had served alongside his master in the war. But she was changed, scarred, and he couldn't reconcile the memories with reality. "Amicae..." I started, but she held up a hand to stop me. "Tell me about the potion," she said, clearly trying to regain her composure. "This transformation is impossible through normal means. Who was this stranger? What exactly did he give you?" I described the grey-eyed man, his knowledge of my full name, his mysterious disappearance in the morning. As I spoke, I watched her face grow increasingly troubled. "He knew who you were," she said when I finished. "Knew your true name, your history. And he had the power to reverse decades of aging..." She shook her head. "This goes beyond any magic I understand." "The question is why," I said. "Why find me? Why transform me? What does he want?" Before she could answer, a new sound reached us—the high, crystalline chime of a message sprite approaching at speed. The tiny creature of living light zipped through the trees like a falling star, its urgent tone cutting through the morning air. "Message for the fire-mage of Kleinae!" it called out. "Urgent news from Brewis in Thornwick!" I held out my hand reluctantly, and the sprite settled on my palm, its light pulsing as it delivered its burden: "Sir, if you're the warrior folk have been whispering about, we need help. Bad. A boy came running from Crygill this morning, half-mad with terror. Says purple mist took his village, changed everyone into something else. Says they're building something in the village square—some kind of gateway. The corruption is spreading, and we're next in line. Please come if you can help us." The sprite's light faded, leaving us in heavy silence. Amicae's scarred face had gone pale. "Crygill. That's barely fifty miles north of here." "What did he mean, 'building something'?" I asked, though something cold in my gut suggested I already knew. "A Breach Gate," she said with certainty that chilled me. "They're opening permanent doorways to the Shadowlands, creating stable passages for invasion forces." Her hands clenched into fists. "Rawnae, if they succeed in opening even one gate..." I felt Red's simple understanding through our strange new connection: Danger to pack. Must fight. "Who are 'they'?" I asked, though I was beginning to suspect the answer. Amicae's green eyes blazed with hatred and pain. "The same ones who gave me these scars. The same ones who killed Lyralei. The Ithek are back, Rawnae. And this time, they're not content to serve anyone's empire." The weight of it settled on my shoulders like a familiar cloak—the burden of command, the necessity of action, the terrible knowledge that innocent people were dying while I sat beneath my tree. "This time," she continued, "they're building their own."

Chapter 1: The Stranger's Gift Two months before the Fall of Eldhaven The Last Ordinary Morning The twin suns of Unu crested the eastern hills as I shuffled from the cottage, my walking stick tapping against the worn stone path. Each step sent a familiar ache through my knees, a morning greeting I'd grown accustomed to over the past decade. Red emerged from the cottage behind me, moving with the same careful deliberation that marked us both as creatures whose best days lay behind them. "Come on then, old fellow," I wheezed, my voice carrying the gravel of too many years and too many battles. "Eggs won't gather themselves." Red's tail managed a weak wag as he fell into step beside me, his grey muzzle nearly white now, his brown eyes clouded with age but still bright with affection. We made quite a pair—two old soldiers marking time until the final retreat. The chicken coop sat nestled against the cottage's eastern wall, positioned to catch the morning warmth of Soli's first light. I'd built it there fifteen years ago when Alaina and I first settled in this quiet corner of Kleinae. She'd wanted chickens, said they'd make the place feel more like home. The memory brought its familiar pang of loss, dulled by time but never quite absent. "Easy there, ladies," I murmured to the hens as they squawked their displeasure at my intrusion. My movements were slow, careful—not just from courtesy to the birds, but from necessity. These old hands shook more each morning, and I'd learned to compensate with patience rather than struggle against the tremor. The first egg nearly slipped from my fingers. I caught it against my chest, feeling my heart skip with the small exertion. There was a time when I could have caught a dozen falling eggs without thought. Now I celebrated not dropping one. Red lowered himself gingerly to the ground nearby, his old bones protesting the movement with a soft grunt that echoed my own morning symphony of aches. He watched me work with the patient attention of a creature who understood that everything took longer now, that rushing only led to mistakes and spilled breakfasts. "Remember when you used to chase these birds?" I asked him, placing the precious egg in my worn basket. "Alaina would scold you something fierce, but I think she enjoyed watching you play." Red's ears perked slightly at Alaina's name. Even after ten years, he remembered her, just as I did. Some losses carve themselves too deep to ever truly heal. The second egg came easier, the hens having settled into their routine grumbling. I moved from nest to nest, each step measured, each reach calculated to avoid the sharp complaint of my lower back. The walking stick helped, but some mornings I wondered if it wouldn't be easier to simply stay in bed and let the world continue without me. But Red depended on me, and I on him. We were each other's anchor to purpose, two old souls keeping each other tethered to life. "Six eggs today," I announced, showing the basket to Red as if he could count. "That's two better than yesterday. The brown hen's finally earning her keep." A distant rumble of thunder made us both look toward the northern sky. Dark clouds gathered on the horizon, though Sola had yet to fully rise and join her partner in their daily dance. The air held that electric tension that spoke of a significant storm approaching. "Looks like we'll have weather tonight," I told Red, who had struggled to his feet with obvious effort. "Best get the animals secured." We made our way to the small barn where Bessie, my ancient cow, stood waiting for her morning attention. Like everything else on this little farm, she was past her prime but still serving faithfully. I set the egg basket carefully on a post and reached for the milking stool. The simple act of sitting required a moment's pause to let my knees settle into position. Red positioned himself nearby, close enough to offer comfort but far enough to avoid a kick from Bessie should she grow restless. "There's a good girl," I murmured, beginning the familiar rhythm of milking. My hands moved slowly but steadily, coaxing streams of warm milk into the wooden bucket. This too had grown harder over the years—my grip wasn't what it once was, and my shoulders protested the repetitive motion. But the routine was soothing, a connection to the simpler life I'd chosen after the war. The Mindreaver stirred then, as it sometimes did in quiet moments. Whispers of memory, faces of men I'd failed to save, villages that had burned because my fire had raged beyond control. I pushed the dark thoughts aside with practiced ease. Those days were long past. I was a farmer now, a keeper of chickens and cows, a man whose greatest battle was against the daily deterioration of his aging body. "Nothing wrong with growing old, is there, Red?" I said, though I wasn't entirely convinced. "It's natural. Expected. Better than the alternative, as they say." Red's answering whine suggested he wasn't entirely convinced either. With the milking finished, I struggled to my feet, muscles protesting the change in position. The bucket felt heavier than it should, and I had to grip it with both hands to ensure I didn't spill our breakfast. Red watched with patient understanding as I shuffled toward the cottage, moving at the pace my body demanded rather than the speed my mind might prefer. The sun had climbed higher now, both Soli and Sola visible in the eastern sky, their combined light warming the morning air. On a younger man's day, this would have been the time for heavier work—tending the larger garden, repairing fences, perhaps riding to the village for supplies. For me, it meant rest and recovery from the simple task of gathering eggs and milk. I settled onto the wooden bench outside the cottage door, placing the bucket and basket carefully beside me. Red joined me, lowering himself with a sigh that perfectly matched my own feelings. We sat in comfortable silence, two old companions sharing the warmth of morning sun and the satisfaction of small tasks completed. "Peaceful, isn't it?" I said, scratching behind Red's ears with fingers that trembled slightly from the morning's exertions. "No battles to fight, no impossible choices to make. Just chickens and cows and the slow passage of time." But even as I spoke the words, I wondered if peace was the same as contentment. There were moments—more frequent lately—when I felt like I was simply marking time, waiting for death with the patience of someone who had already lived his allotted span. The storm clouds continued to build on the horizon, promising an eventful evening. I hoped any travelers would find shelter before the worst of it hit. The road past my cottage saw occasional traffic, and I'd always been willing to offer hospitality to those caught by weather or circumstance. "Come on then," I said to Red, using my walking stick to lever myself back to my feet. "Let's get these inside before the weather turns." We made our way slowly into the cottage, my sanctuary these past fifteen years. Everything in it spoke of the quiet life I'd built with Alaina—simple furniture, practical decorations, the accumulated comfort of years spent in peaceful routine. Vulcanspine leaned against the hearth where it had rested for decades, more decoration than weapon now, its runes dim and cold. I set about preparing a simple breakfast, moving carefully around the kitchen that had been designed for two but now served one. Red positioned himself in his favorite spot by the fire, watching with the patient attention of a creature who had learned that his human needed time to accomplish even simple tasks. The eggs sizzled in the pan, their aroma filling the small space with homely comfort. I ate slowly, partly from necessity—my teeth weren't what they once were—and partly from having nowhere else to be. Time had become something to fill rather than something to race against. After breakfast, I settled into my favorite chair by the window, Red at my feet, and watched the world outside. A few travelers passed on the distant road, their figures small and hurried as they raced against the approaching storm. Birds called to each other from the trees, and somewhere in the distance, a neighbor's dog barked its morning greeting to the world. This was my life now—quiet observation, gentle routine, the slow and patient progression toward an end I faced without fear. I had served my time, fought my battles, known love and loss in equal measure. What remained was this gentle decline, this gradual fading that seemed natural and right. Or so I told myself. Deep in my chest, beneath the aches and the careful movements and the acceptance of age, something stirred occasionally—a restlessness I tried to ignore, a sense that perhaps there was still something left undone. But such feelings belonged to youth, to men who still believed they could change the world. I was past such delusions. Wasn't I? The storm continued to build throughout the afternoon, and by evening, the first drops of rain had begun to fall. I secured the animals, brought in firewood, and prepared for a night of wind and weather. Red settled by the hearth as thunder began to rumble in earnest. Neither of us knew that this would be the last ordinary evening of our old life together. That by morning, everything would have changed in ways I couldn't have imagined. That the peaceful farmer I'd become was about to be transformed back into something I'd thought was dead and buried. The storm was coming, and with it, a stranger who would offer me a gift I'd never asked for—and didn't know I needed. The Approaching Storm My bones are aching—an ache that reaches deep down into the marrow. A storm approaches, and I can tell it's going to be a whopper. There's an electric charge to the air. The hairs on my arms are standing on end, and the wind is howling. This is only the edge of the storm; the heart has yet to reach my quaint cottage. At my side lies my only companion these many years. As ancient as I, he whines at the maelstrom occurring outside these four walls. "Hush now, we've been through storms before," I said, reaching down to pat his grey head. "If it does take us, at least we go together." Red is all that remains of my family. My wife passed quietly in her sleep some ten years back. Our son passed long before that to a fever. After my service to the Seven Kingdoms during what they called the Sunding War, I had wished for a quiet life. We found an out-of-the-way area with a spot of land and farmed. We had been content. As I stare into the warm, glowing flames of the fireplace, I contemplate my long life. Sitting and remembering seems to be my only pastime nowadays. My life had been adventurous, full of triumphs and failures alike—even devastating loss on occasion. Full of choices that had to be made under the direst of circumstances. What I had been scared of most was the politics. The Unexpected Visitor Outside, the storm increased its intensity. Suddenly there came a knock at the door. "Red, is that the door?" I said. "Who would be out in this weather?" Red chuffed in response. Boom! Boom! Boom! "Confounded visitors in this mess," I muttered. Grunting, I rose to my feet and shuffled toward the door. I lifted the bar and turned the knob, and the door swung open. Before me stood a man of average stature with dark features. His gray eyes pierced into mine with an intensity that made me pause. "Yes, young man, what can I do for you?" I said. "So sorry, old master, but may I come out of this rain and find succor?" he asked. "Aye, I imagine you do. Alright, come in," I said. "I won't leave you out in this mess." He stepped inside, and I closed and barred the door behind him. "Sir, this is frightful weather. Thank you for your kindness," he said. "Are you hungry?" I asked. "Yes, sire, thank you." "Alright, let me fetch a bowl." An Evening's Hospitality The stranger and I settled down to cheese, bread, and lamb stew. Red took his customary position at my feet, hoping for a dropped morsel or two. Then the strangest little squeak and squeal escaped Red's old gray backside—it was a right stinker. "Sorry, the dog is old and my only companion," I said apologetically. The stranger laughed. "It's his home. I am but a visitor." I laughed and smiled. I thought I might like this fellow. "What brings you out in this miserable weather?" I asked. "I was passing through when this storm struck. Yours is the only place I saw," he said. "It was providence." "Yes, indeed." We finished our fare, and I offered him some wine. "I'm old and achy—you may take the bed tonight. I've grown accustomed to sleeping in this old rocking chair by the fire to keep my old bones warm." "That is very kind of you. Thank you," he said. "Sleep well," I said. "Sleep well, sire," he responded. The Morning Connection I awoke to the glare of Unu's double suns pouring their light through my kitchen window. I rose on creaky knees, causing me to groan. I looked and saw I had awakened the stranger. "Care to breakfast with me?" I asked. "That would be great," he said. I tottered over to the fireplace to start preparing a meal. As I moved about the kitchen, I became aware of the stranger watching me with those piercing grey eyes—not in judgment, but with something that felt almost like... understanding. "You move like a soldier," he observed quietly. "Even with age, there's purpose in your steps. Economy of motion." I paused in my stirring of the porridge. Few people noticed such things. "Once upon a time, perhaps. Long ago." "Not so long that it's forgotten." He leaned forward slightly. "The Sunding War?" My hand stilled on the wooden spoon. "How did you—" "The way you positioned your chair last night. Back to the wall, clear view of the door. The sword by the hearth—that's no farmer's tool." His voice was gentle, not probing, as if he were simply stating facts he already knew. "Vulcanspine, if I'm not mistaken." I turned to face him fully, suspicious now. "Who are you?" "Someone who knows what it means to carry the weight of necessary choices," he said simply. "Someone who understands that the hardest battles are fought long after the war ends." There was something in his tone—a depth of weariness that matched my own. I found myself setting down the spoon and really looking at him. His face was unlined, but his eyes... his eyes held the same exhaustion I saw in my own mirror. The Morning After I awoke to the glare of Unu's double suns pouring their light through my kitchen window. I rose on creaky knees, causing me to groan. I looked and saw I had awakened the stranger. "Care to breakfast with me?" I asked. "That would be great," he said. I tottered over to the fireplace to start preparing a meal. I fried salted pork along with fresh eggs, broke out some cider and day-old bread. I set the table, and we sat down to eat. I flipped an occasional morsel to Red under the table as the stranger and I ate in companionable silence, enjoying the sounds of a spring morning. Birds were singing, and my old rooster cut loose with a crow. We finished eating, and the stranger stood. "Sir, you have shown a great deal of kindness to me. I wish to repay you. Take this tonic—it will help with the feebleness and aches of age," he said, producing a bottle wrapped in red cloth. "You owe me nothing, young man," I protested. "Rawnae Lupio, you are a good man. You deserve this simple gift," he said. Then he walked to the door, lifted the bar, opened it, and exited the cottage. Red moaned and trudged out the door after him. "Alright, I'm coming," I said to Red's retreating form. The Mysterious Departure I walked out into the early morning glare of Soli and Sola, Unu's double suns. I looked around, but I could not see the stranger anywhere. "Strange—he couldn't have gotten too far. He was a pleasant young man, though," I said to myself. I inhaled the fresh, clean spring air. It smelled of recent rain and wildflowers. The morning bustled with the activities of nature. I looked at my hand, clutching a satiny red cloth wrapped around a bottle with clear liquid inside. Red trudged from around the side of the house. I waddled my way to my favorite oak tree to lean against it. Red and I had sat here many times over the years. We would sit in this spot for hours, watching the comings and goings of the local townsfolk and farmers—most of which were Tiffling folk. I lived in the Kingdom of Kleinae. This road connected Kleinae and Mannesket, so occasionally we would see humans as well. Alaina and I had chosen to live in Kleinae because the Tifflings were respectful and private folk. They never meddled in anyone's business, yet they were kind and friendly. Contemplation and Decision I bent down and settled against the large oak tree. Red took his spot at my side, taking his sweet time to get comfortable. When he finally settled right against my hip, he let out a long sigh. I reached down to scratch his ears. "Well, fellow, another day arrives for us," I said. I started contemplating again, remembering a war that had nearly been the ending of all life on Unu. Remembering hundreds of acts of selfless heroism—men and women who had sacrificed themselves for the good of all. Red groaned. "Yes, old friend. I understand," I said. Red and I were at the stage of life where we were just waiting for the sweet release of death—the end of a full life, our final reprieve. Neither of us was what we once were. We were mere shells of the strength and vigorous bodies we had once possessed. "A tonic to remove the feebleness and aches of age, he said," I mused, examining the bottle. I reached down again, scratching along Red's neck. "You want me to take it, old boy?" Red groaned in response. "Well, if it kills me, that will remove the feebleness and aches of age too," I said with dark humor. Red whined. "Shh, stop, old boy. I was just making an observation." I continued my reflections. My Alaina had been gone now for ten years. I missed her daily. She had been a good, caring woman, though she was never the same after we lost our boy. I had never felt more helpless than when I watched him burn with fever in his bed. He was only ten summers at the time and should have had so many more. I examined the contents of the vial. Not a single thing about it seemed remarkable—clear as water, and when I pulled the cork, odorless. "Well, if it kills me, you'll have the whole place to yourself, Red," I said. Red whined again, then laid his head down and sighed. "Alright, old fellow—bottoms up!" I said, and quaffed the contents of the vial. Red raised his head and whined pitifully. The Transformation My vision exploded into a rainbow of bright colors, and I felt a pain unlike any I had ever experienced in my life. I felt like my entire body had separated into the tiniest fragments of existence. I was simultaneously freezing and burning up. I was lightning and volcanic eruptions, the mightiest storm and the deepest abyss. I found myself begging to either die or pass out. Finally, a peaceful darkness encompassed me.

"Sir, this is frightful weather. Thank you for your kindness," he says. "Are you hungry?" I ask. "Yes, sire, thank you." "Alright, let me fetch a bowl." The stranger and I settle down to cheese, bread, and lamb stew. Red takes his customary position at my feet, hoping for a dropped morsel or two. As we eat, I notice the stranger watching me with those piercing gray eyes—not in judgment, but with something that feels almost like understanding. "You move like a soldier," he observes quietly. "Even with age, there's purpose in your steps. Economy of motion." I pause in my eating. Few people notice such things. "Once upon a time, perhaps. Long ago." "Not so long that it's forgotten." He leans forward slightly. "The Sunding War?" My hand stills on the wooden spoon. "How did you—" "The way you positioned your chair. Back to the wall, clear view of the door. The sword by the hearth—that's no farmer's tool." His voice is gentle, not probing. "Vulcanspine, if I'm not mistaken." I turn to face him fully, suspicious now. "Who are you?" "Someone who knows what it means to carry the weight of necessary choices," he says simply. "Someone who understands that the hardest battles are fought long after the war ends." There's something in his tone—a depth of weariness that matches my own. His face is unlined, but his eyes hold the same exhaustion I see in my own mirror. It was a tradition Alaina had started, insisting that we had room for one more at our table and a dry corner for anyone in need. Even now, a decade after her passing, I honor her memory by keeping an open door for travelers caught by weather or circumstance.

“Ma, I’ll be out doing my chores,” I said as I stepped outside into the fresh morning air. The storm had passed, but the clouds still hung heavy in the sky. I walked toward the barn, grabbed a bucket, and filled it with grain. The chickens in the coop saw me coming and broke into a noisy fuss. I smiled at their routine clamor. As soon as I poured the grain into their feeder, they quieted down, too busy pecking at breakfast to complain any longer. “Meow, meow.” A black cat with two white socks brushed against my leg, begging for attention. “Oh, hi Izzy. Still hanging around?” I chuckled. “You must like my company.” She purred and followed me as I grabbed more grain and headed toward the goat pen. The goats, already expecting me, began bleating and rattling the fence. One of them was pregnant, which was good—more milk once her kids arrived. After I poured their feed and shut the pen behind me, Izzy weaved around my legs, slowing me down. “All right, all right. Let’s find you something to eat,” I said, scooping her into my arms. She squirmed like a bundle of nerves. “Calm down, Izzy.” I carried her to the smoke shed, doing my best to keep hold of her while reaching up for the smallest dried fish hanging from the rafters. Before I could even step outside, she scrambled up my arm and latched onto the fish, chewing like she hadn’t eaten in days. “Izzy, you crazy girl,” I laughed, finally setting her down outside. She growled contentedly as she devoured her prize. With my chores done and Izzy fed, I wandered over to my favorite sitting spot behind the barn—an old log near the garden where the river breeze made everything feel calm. Izzy trotted after me, fish still clenched in her mouth. She dropped beside me and continued her feast. “You’re lucky my Da’s a fisherman,” I told her. “You’ll always have fish to eat if you stick with me.” She finished, then ran over and started swatting at my fingers, full of playful energy. We’d only known each other for a week, but it already felt like she’d always been here. We played for a while—until something shifted. Izzy suddenly froze. Her back arched, fur bristling. She let out a low, warning growl. I looked up. Mr. Klisternok was walking into our yard from the tail path that ran along the river, connecting all the fishing cottages in the village. “Shhh, Izzy. It’s just Mr. Klisternok,” I whispered, trying to soothe her. He’d always been a kind neighbor, and his daughter Alyona was one of my best friends at school. But Izzy wouldn’t stop hissing, her eyes locked on him. “Honestly, Izzy, what’s gotten into you?” But something wasn’t right. Mr. Klisternok didn’t greet me or even glance in my direction. He walked straight up to our front door and knocked. Da answered it. “Mr. Klisternok, what can we do for ye today?” “Morning, Otec. Fine day, now that that awful storm’s behind us,” Mr. Klisternok said, smiling in a way that made my stomach turn. “Aye,” Da replied. “River’s running too strong to fish, but no damage here.” Mr. Klisternok nodded, that strange smile never fading. “Where’s your lovely wife?” Ma appeared at the door and greeted him warmly. “Good morning, Mr. Klisternok.” He didn’t return the greeting. “I’ve a message for the both of you,” he said. Ma and Da leaned in to listen—and that’s when everything turned wrong. Mr. Klisternok opened his mouth… too wide. A horrible gurgling noise bubbled out as a thick, oily vapor—dark and smoky—poured from his mouth like it was alive. It slithered through the air and rushed up Ma’s and Da’s noses before either of them could move. It was over in a second. Mr. Klisternok straightened, turned around, and walked away like nothing had happened. Ma and Da just stood there, staring blankly into the distance with vacant eyes. Then, as if nothing had occurred, they shook their heads, closed the door, and disappeared inside. I sat there frozen. What did I just see? Izzy hissed behind me, low and angry. She hadn’t calmed at all. Neither had I.

Chapter 8: Royal Justice The Celebration The great hall buzzed with relief and celebration such as I hadn't seen since the end of the Sunding War. Nobles who'd spent months walking on eggshells around Varkis agents now spoke freely, wine flowed like water, and for the first time in ages, hope filled the air like music. Trink had discovered the royal wine cellars—a development that filled me with equal parts amusement and dread. The dragonfly pixie hovered unsteadily above the high table with a goblet nearly as large as himself, his iridescent vest already stained with wine. "Friends! Companions! Fellow heroes!" he announced, nearly tumbling into a nobleman's soup. "Allow me to pro-pro-propound a toast!" His speech carried the careful precision of the very drunk trying to appear sober. I buried my face in my hands as Trink gestured grandly, splashing wine across several startled courtiers. "And to the beautiful, the talented, the absolutely spectacular Lady Amicae!" Trink spun in midair, nearly colliding with a chandelier. "Whose scars tell tales of heroism, and whose... whose other attributes tell tales of..." He hiccupped. "Where was I going with this?" "Nowhere good," Amicae muttered, though she was fighting a smile. Beside me, Rowetha giggled—the first genuine laughter I'd heard from her since arriving at court. The girl had been quiet during the trial, but seeing Trink's ridiculous antics was drawing her out of her shell. "And to little Rowetha!" Trink declared, attempting a midair bow that sent him spiraling toward the table. "Bravest little warrior in all the kingdoms!" The toast continued with increasingly creative descriptions of our various heroic qualities, punctuated by Trink's aerial acrobatics and the occasional crash into serving platters. Even King Theron was chuckling by the time the pixie finally exhausted himself and settled onto a chandelier to recuperate. Through it all, I found myself watching Vaelar work the crowd with diplomatic skill. He moved between conversations with practiced ease, building relationships and gathering information like the accomplished statesman he appeared to be. Growing Doubts Later in the evening, as the formal celebration gave way to smaller conversations, I noticed details that my wartime paranoia couldn't ignore. Vaelar positioned himself with clear sightlines to all exits. His hand never strayed far from his sword hilt. He asked careful questions about kingdom defenses and magical capabilities. Professional habits, or something more? "You're brooding," Amicae observed, settling beside me with a goblet of wine. Her scars were partially hidden by the formal gown King Theron had provided, but she wore them with growing confidence. "Just thinking," I said, not wanting to voice suspicions I couldn't articulate. "Your brother's arrival was remarkably well-timed." "You sound like you think that's a bad thing." I studied Vaelar more closely as he conversed with Chancellor Aldwin about kingdom military capabilities. "How did he know to come here? How did he know you were alive, that we were working together?" Amicae frowned. "Elven communication networks are extensive. Word travels fast through the right channels." "Fast enough to reach Aethelwood, mobilize a diplomatic mission, capture Joren Varkis, and arrive here within days of our own departure from the Golden Mare?" She was quiet for a moment, considering. "When you put it like that..." "And his intelligence about Ithek operations was remarkably detailed. Not the kind of information you gather through distant monitoring." "You think he's been more involved than he's admitted?" "I think someone's been watching this situation very closely. Question is why, and for how long." Across the hall, Vaelar raised his goblet in a toast to King Theron. His smile was perfect—warm, grateful, entirely appropriate for the occasion. But there was something about his eyes that reminded me of certain officers during the war. The kind who calculated acceptable losses with disturbing ease. A Moment Alone Later, as the celebration wound down, Amicae and I found ourselves on one of the palace's balconies. The night air was cool against my skin, carrying the scent of jasmine from the royal gardens and the distant sound of Eldhaven's nightlife. "Hard to believe it's over," she said softly, leaning against the stone railing. "Is it?" I asked. "Varkis might be in chains, but they were just one part of a larger problem. The Emberwraith, the corruption spreading to other kingdoms—those didn't disappear with Varren's arrest." She turned to face me, moonlight playing across her scarred features. "You really don't trust easily, do you?" "Old habit from the war. Trust has to be earned through shared blood and common sacrifice." "Even with me?" The vulnerability in her voice made my chest tighten. "You're different. You've bled beside me, saved my life more times than I can count. That builds trust." "And my brother hasn't done the same?" I thought about it, trying to separate legitimate caution from paranoia. "He's done everything right. Brought perfect evidence, arrived at the perfect moment, offered exactly what we needed. Maybe that's what bothers me." "You think good timing is suspicious?" "In war, when everything goes according to plan, it usually means someone else wrote the plan." We stood in comfortable silence, watching the lights of Eldhaven twinkle below us. Despite my doubts about Vaelar, being here with Amicae felt right in a way I hadn't experienced since Alaina's death. "What happens now?" she asked quietly. "When the corruption is cleansed, when the kingdom is safe... what then?" The question hung between us, heavy with possibility and forty years of separation. "I don't know," I admitted. "I never expected to have a 'what then.' I was an old man waiting to die, remember?" "And now you're young again, with a lifetime ahead of you. You could go anywhere, do anything." I stepped closer, close enough to see the uncertainty in her green eyes. "Is that what you think I want? Glory? Adventure?" "I don't know what you want," she whispered. "I barely knew forty years ago." I reached up and cupped her scarred cheek, feeling her lean into the touch. "Forty years ago, I wanted to end the war and build something peaceful. I found that with Alaina, for a time." Her eyes closed, pain flickering across her features. "But now?" I continued. "Now I want something I thought I'd lost forever. I want to wake up next to someone who understands the weight I carry. Someone who's seen darkness and chosen light anyway." Her eyes opened, bright with unshed tears. "Rawnae..." "I want you, Amicae. Not the memory of who we were, but who we are now. Scarred, seasoned, wise enough to know what we're choosing." She leaned into my touch, her hand covering mine. "I've wanted that for forty years. Watched you from afar, convinced myself I was being noble by staying away, but really I was just afraid." "Afraid of what?" "Of this moment. Of finding out if what we had was real or just... battlefield passion. Fear and adrenaline dressed up as love." I leaned closer, our foreheads almost touching. "And what do you think now?" Her smile transformed her scarred features into something luminous. "I think we're about to find out." Our kiss tasted of wine and possibility, of old love rekindled and new love discovered. It was gentle and desperate all at once, forty years of separation compressed into a single moment of connection. When we finally parted, I rested my forehead against hers. "So," I said, my voice rough with emotion. "What then?" "Then we build something together," she whispered. "Something worth all the pain it took to get here." The Morning After I woke to find Amicae already awake beside me, her scarred face peaceful in the morning light filtering through the guest chamber windows. After forty years apart, sharing a bed again felt both natural and miraculous. "Good morning," she murmured, green eyes warm with contentment. "Good morning," I replied, still marveling at the simple joy of it. "Sleep well?" "Better than I have in decades. No nightmares about burning trees. No dreams of you growing old while I watched from the shadows." A knock at the door interrupted our quiet moment. "Rawn? Amicae?" Rowetha's voice carried through the wood. "King Theron wants to see everyone. He says it's important." "Coming, lass," I called back, reluctantly leaving the warmth of the bed and Amicae's embrace. As we dressed, I noticed Red stretching in his corner of the room. The change in him had become even more pronounced—his grey muzzle now showed distinct patches of his original red-brown coloring, and he moved with an energy I hadn't seen in years. "He's getting younger," Amicae observed, watching him bound toward the door with almost puppyish enthusiasm. "The magical bond," I said, scratching behind his ears as he pressed against my leg. "Somehow my transformation is affecting him too." Whatever the explanation, it was good to see the old boy full of life again. We'd need all our strength for whatever came next. Seeds of Concern The morning audience with King Theron focused on practical matters—organizing the cleansing expeditions, coordinating with Aethelwood, and ensuring that no traces of corruption escaped detection. Vaelar's knowledge proved remarkably detailed. He knew the locations of Ithek cells, their communication methods, even their operational preferences. When pressed for sources, he credited Aethelwood's extensive intelligence network. But something about his answers bothered me. Too precise, too complete, as if he'd been gathering this information firsthand rather than through distant monitoring. "The cell near Millhaven is particularly dangerous," he explained, pointing to locations on the kingdom's map. "They've been working on some kind of permanent gateway—a structure that would allow massive numbers of entities to enter our realm." "How do you know about their construction projects?" I asked.
