As they drew closer to the mill's dock, the survivors could see that the expedition included individuals whose dress and bearing marked them as nobility or high-ranking officials. One figure in particular stood out—a tall woman whose expensive robes bore the unmistakable hawk crest of House Varkis. "Lady Myrra herself," Torren breathed, recognition and disgust mingling in his voice. "Lord Varren's sister and the family's chief intelligence coordinator. If she's here personally, this operation has support from the very top of the Varkis hierarchy." Through Trink's reconnaissance and their own careful observation, they were able to piece together fragments of conversation that painted a chilling picture of deliberate treachery. "The acceleration is proceeding ahead of schedule," Lady Myrra reported to her companions as they disembarked. "Integration of the local population is complete, and the entity reports readiness to begin phase two operations." "What about containment?" asked one of her subordinates, a man whose armor bore the insignia of a captain in the Varkis personal guard. "The Rangers have been asking questions about the communication disruption." "Contained for now," Myrra replied with cold satisfaction. "The entity's expansion along the river system will be gradual enough to avoid triggering widespread alarm until it's too late for effective resistance. By the time King Theron realizes what's happening, half his kingdom will already be part of the network." The implications were staggering. This wasn't just a single attack on an isolated village—it was the opening move in a campaign to overthrow the entire kingdom. "The investment in the Delfaen expedition has exceeded all expectations," Myrra continued as her group approached the mill. "The entity's capabilities far surpass what the original summoners intended. Instead of a simple weapon of destruction, we have acquired a tool of transformation that can deliver the kingdom intact but under our control." She speaks of the entity as if it were a servant, Glimmer observed, her mental voice carrying worry that touched all their minds. But that's not how these beings operate. It may be serving their purposes now, but entities of this type have their own agendas. They use allies until those allies are no longer useful. As if summoned by Lady Myrra's arrival, the mill's activity intensified. Corrupted villagers emerged to greet the visitors with the kind of coordinated precision that suggested the entity was directly controlling their movements. But more disturbing was the change in their appearance—several showed signs of physical modification that went beyond simple possession. One figure that had once been the village blacksmith now sported arms that terminated in tool-like appendages perfectly adapted for metalworking. A woman who had been a seamstress exhibited fingers that had elongated into needle-sharp points capable of incredibly delicate manipulation. Most horrifying was what had once been the village leader, whose mouth had been restructured to allow him to speak in harmonic frequencies that carried multiple conversations simultaneously. "The integration process continues to exceed expectations," the modified leader reported to Lady Myrra, his voice carrying overtones that suggested multiple consciousnesses speaking in perfect unison. "Local magical traditions have been analyzed and incorporated. Expansion capabilities have been enhanced through study of captured artifacts. We are ready to begin systematic acquisition of target settlements." Lady Myrra's smile was coldly satisfied. "Excellent. House Varkis is pleased with the entity's progress. The agreed-upon payment has been transferred to the designated accounts, and additional resources will be provided as expansion milestones are achieved." "Payment?" Rawn whispered, his Fire Essence flaring with indignation. "They're paying it? What could an entity like that possibly want that humans could provide?" The answer came from the entity itself, speaking through its various corrupted hosts in overlapping voices that created a harmony of wrongness: "Knowledge. Magical artifacts. Access to sealed archives. Most importantly—permission to operate without interference from established authorities." "The Varkis family has considerable influence over kingdom policies," Lady Myrra confirmed. "We can ensure that official investigations are delayed or misdirected until the network has achieved sufficient scope to make resistance meaningless." This is worse than simple corruption, Glimmer's mental voice carried ancient fear. They've made the entity into a partner rather than a tool. It's not just serving their immediate goals—it's building toward something larger. The Impossible Choice As they watched the meeting between the Varkis agents and the entity, the survivors faced a terrible realization: they were witnessing the early stages of what could become the complete transformation of their civilization. But the forces arrayed against them were overwhelming—not just the entity and its corrupted servants, but a major noble house with resources that included trained soldiers, political influence, and apparent access to magical knowledge that should have been forbidden. "We can't fight this alone," Torren said quietly, his soldier's assessment of the situation brutally realistic. "Even if we could somehow defeat the entity, Varkis would just find another way to achieve their goals. They have the resources to keep trying until they succeed." "Then we expose them," Amicae replied, but her voice carried uncertainty. "We gather evidence and present it to the king." "Evidence of what?" Rawn asked grimly. "A fantastic story about otherworldly entities and impossible corruption? Who would believe us? And even if they did, how many people would die while we were trying to convince them?" Red's mental voice cut through their debate with simple clarity: Pup is trapped. Pack fights or pup dies. No other choices. The dog's assessment referred to Rowetha, but it applied to the entire situation. They could try to flee and seek help elsewhere, but the entity's expansion would continue while they traveled. They could attempt to gather allies, but the corruption was spreading faster than any conventional response could be organized. There is another way, Glimmer said quietly, her mental voice carrying the weight of terrible knowledge. Guardian magic can seal a Breach, permanently severing the entity's connection to this realm. But the process requires a willing sacrifice—someone must serve as an anchor point while the sealing takes place. The small group fell silent as the implications sank in. Victory was possible, but it would cost them one of their own. "How do we know it would work?" Amicae asked. Because I have done it before, Glimmer replied simply. Long ago, in a place where similar corruption threatened to consume an entire forest. The Guardian way is to give our light so that others may keep theirs. Rowetha's grip on the cat tightened as she realized what Glimmer was suggesting. "No," she whispered. "There has to be another way. There has to be." Perhaps, Glimmer said gently. But if there is not, I am prepared to pay the price. That is what Guardians do. "Then we'd better find that other way," Rawn said finally, Fire Essence beginning to coil around Vulcanspine's blade with deadly purpose. "Because I'll be damned if I let that thing and its Varkis sponsors destroy everything we're trying to protect." The time for observation was over. One way or another, they were going to have to act. The Assault Begins They struck at midday, when the twin suns of Unu blazed directly overhead in perfect convergence. The timing was carefully chosen—the intense combined light would strengthen Fire Essence while creating harsh shadows that could provide concealment for their approach. Rawn had insisted on leading the assault himself, not out of ego, but because he was the only one among them with the raw power to carve through the entity's defenses. His transformation had given him capabilities that went far beyond his considerable skills as the Scorch of Eryndor, and the corruption's presence seemed to make his Fire Essence burn even hotter with righteous fury. Torren flanked left, his ranger training evident in how he used the mill's modified architecture to his advantage. The entity's additions to the building had created numerous blind spots and concealment opportunities that a skilled infiltrator could exploit. Amicae took the right approach, her Water Essence flowing in protective barriers that could turn aside the supernatural attacks of the corrupted defenders. But more importantly, her magic could disrupt the entity's control signals, temporarily freeing the minds of some corrupted villagers. Trink served as their eyes and ears, his tiny size allowing him to scout ahead and provide real-time intelligence about enemy positions and capabilities. His running commentary proved invaluable as the situation developed. "Five corrupted guards at the main entrance, but they're not paying attention to the roof access," he reported through a communication spell that Amicae had established. "The entity's primary consciousness is definitely in the grinding chamber, but it's got secondary nodes throughout the building. You'll need to hit multiple targets simultaneously to prevent it from just shifting to backup locations." The first contact came as they approached the mill's perimeter. The corrupted guards moved with mechanical precision, but their human-based reflexes were no match for supernatural speed and mystical fire. Rawn's Scorchfang Slash cut through three of them before they could raise an alarm, his blade burning away the corruption that animated them and leaving only ash. But their deaths triggered some kind of network response. Throughout the mill, corrupted servants began moving with coordinated purpose that spoke of central control. Worse, the entity began making modifications to its defenders in real-time, adapting them for combat efficiency. "The blacksmith's getting some kind of upgrade," Trink reported urgently. "His tool-hands are shifting into weapon configurations. And the seamstress is sprouting additional arms with cutting implements." The battle that followed was unlike anything they had experienced. The entity's corrupted servants fought with perfect coordination but also with constantly evolving capabilities. Each time they developed a successful tactic, the enemy adapted to counter it. The entity learns from every exchange, Glimmer observed as her Guardian magic created protective barriers around Rowetha. It's not just fighting us—it's studying us. Every technique we use teaches it new capabilities. The breakthrough came when Amicae realized that the entity's greatest strength was also a vulnerability. Its network-based consciousness required constant communication between nodes, and water magic could disrupt those signals. "I can jam its coordination," she called out as her Aqua Lash wrapped around the mill's modified water wheel. "But only for short periods. When I do, its servants will be confused and vulnerable." "Do it," Rawn shouted back, carving through another wave of attackers with blazing steel. The effect was immediate and dramatic. As Amicae's Water Essence flooded the mill's magical infrastructure, the coordinated movements of the corrupted villagers dissolved into chaos. They stumbled, looked around in confusion, and for brief moments their original personalities seemed to reassert themselves. "Now!" Rawn commanded, and their assault reached its climax. The Heart of Darkness The mill's grinding chamber had been transformed into something that defied geometric logic. The original machinery remained, but it had been integrated with structures that seemed to exist in more dimensions than human minds could process. At the center of this impossible space, the entity manifested as a swirling mass of energy and matter that constantly shifted between states of existence. The creature that emerged to defend its sanctum had once been the mill's owner, but the entity's modifications had turned him into something that was more weapon than man. His body had been restructured for maximum combat efficiency, with redundant limb systems and sensory organs that allowed him to fight with superhuman coordination. "Welcome, mortals," he said, his voice carrying harmonics that suggested multiple consciousnesses speaking in unison. "You have been brave, but ultimately futile. Join us. Become part of something greater than your limited individual existence." The entity's lieutenant moved with inhuman speed, his transformed claws carving gouges in stone when they missed their intended targets. But more disturbing than his physical capabilities was the way he seemed to anticipate their attacks, responding to techniques before they were fully executed. He's reading our minds, Red warned through his mental link with Rawn. Pack leader's thoughts, female-pack's thoughts. Knows what we plan before we do it. The realization forced them to fight purely on instinct, abandoning planned tactics in favor of spontaneous reactions that couldn't be predicted. It was exhausting and dangerous, but it prevented the entity from gaining the overwhelming advantage that its precognitive abilities would otherwise have provided. Torren took a glancing blow that sent him tumbling, his armor smoking from acidic burns that spoke of the entity's corrosive nature. But he rolled with the impact and came up firing, his crossbow bolts enhanced with Essence that could disrupt magical defenses. Rawn met the creature head-on, Vulcanspine blazing with Fire Essence as he drove it back step by step. But the thing was strong—stronger than any human should be—and behind it, the entity's primary manifestation was beginning to take notice of their intrusion. "The Breach!" Amicae called out as the air in the chamber began to twist with reality distortions. "It's destabilizing! If we don't seal it soon, the damage could become permanent!" Now! Glimmer's voice rang in all their minds with desperate urgency. I can close it, but you must give me time! And someone must be ready to anchor the sealing when the moment comes! The small gray cat leaped from Rowetha's arms, landing in the center of the mill's impossible machinery. Light began to emanate from her tiny form—not the harsh glare of fire or lightning, but something softer and infinitely more powerful. Guardian magic, the force that had protected reality's boundaries since the beginning of time. The corrupted miller turned toward this new threat, recognizing the danger to his master's plans. He abandoned his fight with Rawn and lunged toward Glimmer with desperate fury, his transformed claws extending toward the cat's fragile form. "No!" Rowetha screamed, her young voice carrying more pain than any child should ever have to experience. But Rawn was already moving, Pyroclasm Burst erupting from Vulcanspine as he intercepted the creature's charge. The impact drove him to his knees, but it bought Glimmer the crucial seconds she needed to complete the initial phase of the sealing. However, as the Guardian magic intensified, the entity's primary consciousness finally turned its full attention to their assault. Dark tendrils of pure malevolence lashed out from the Breach itself, seeking to overwhelm Glimmer's protective light through sheer force. The cat's cry of pain echoed in all their minds as corruption struck her small form. But she didn't stop—if anything, her light burned brighter as she poured her life force into the sealing ritual. Tell Rowetha, her voice was fading now, growing distant as her essence merged with the magical forces she was manipulating. Tell her that some lights never truly go out. They just... change form. With a sound like thunder that shook the foundations of reality itself, the Breach collapsed. The mill's walls cracked and crumbled as the entity's anchor point was severed. The sweet-sick smell of corruption was swept away by clean river air that carried the promise of natural order restored. But the victory came at the cost they had all feared. Glimmer's light flickered once, then went out forever, leaving only a small gray form that would never again speak words of wisdom or offer comfort to a frightened child. The Price of Victory In the immediate aftermath of the mill's collapse, they gathered around the small gray form that had saved not just their lives, but potentially the entire kingdom. Rowetha wept openly, her tears falling onto Glimmer's still fur while the adults struggled with their own grief for a creature they had known only briefly but who had shown them the true meaning of heroic sacrifice. "She knew," Torren said quietly, his voice hoarse from the battle's exertions. "She knew from the beginning what it would cost, and she chose to pay it anyway." The Guardian's way, came a new voice in their minds—faint but unmistakably present. A tiny black kitten with white paws picked its way through the rubble, drawn by invisible threads of connection to Rowetha's grief. We give our light so others may find theirs. But light itself is never truly destroyed—only transformed. "Izzy," Rowetha whispered, scooping up the kitten with desperate hope. It wasn't Glimmer—would never be Glimmer—but it was continuation. A new beginning born from the ashes of sacrifice. I carry echoes, the small cat explained, her mental voice carrying distant harmonics of the Guardian who had come before. Not memories, but... purposes. Protections. The hope that was freely given. As they mourned their loss and tended their wounds, the full scope of what they had prevented became clear. With the Breach sealed and the entity banished back to its original realm, the corruption that had been spreading through the water system began to dissipate. The river's natural flow gradually washed away the taint, carrying clean water to the settlements downstream that would never know how close they had come to sharing Crygill's fate. But their victory was incomplete. Lady Myrra and her Varkis agents had fled when the mill collapsed, taking with them whatever evidence might have connected the entity's presence to their conspiracy. Without proof of House Varkis's involvement, they had no way to prevent future attempts at similar sabotage. "What will you do now?" Amicae asked Rowetha gently, placing a scarred hand on the girl's shoulder. "Your village is gone, your family..." She couldn't finish the sentence, but the implication was clear. "Come with us," Rowetha said simply, her voice steady despite the tears that continued to stream down her face. "Izzy showed me things before... before Glimmer went away. This isn't over. There are more places where the corruption spreads, more people who need protection. You'll need help." "A child?" Torren protested, though his tone carried more concern than dismissal. "This kind of work is dangerous beyond measure. She's already seen too much horror for someone her age." "I'm not just a child," Rowetha interrupted with surprising firmness that reminded them of Glimmer's quiet authority. "I'm a witness. I've seen what this evil does to people, to families, to entire communities. And I remember what it was like before the corruption came." She looked down at Izzy, whose purr seemed far too strong and purposeful for such a small creature. "She's a Guardian too. Young and still learning, but growing stronger every day. The light finds a way to continue, even when it seems like everything is lost." Rawn exchanged a meaningful look with Amicae. The girl was right—they would need all the help they could get if there were more supernatural threats backed by political conspiracies. And perhaps keeping her close was better than leaving her to face a dangerous world alone. "Then we'd better move quickly," he said finally, his transformed body already recovering from the battle's toll. "If there are more Breaches, we need to find them before they grow as strong as this one. And if House Varkis is behind these attacks, we need evidence that can't be dismissed or suppressed." Trink landed on his shoulder, the pixie's usual grin subdued but present. "Where to, General? Because my intelligence network has been picking up some very interesting rumors about activities near Eldhaven. Seems our noble friends have been very busy with projects that don't appear in any official records." Setting the Course As they prepared to leave Crygill's ruins, each member of their unlikely group carried not just their personal belongings, but the weight of knowledge that would shape everything that followed. They had proven that the supernatural corruption could be fought and defeated, but they had also learned that their enemies possessed resources and influence that extended to the highest levels of the kingdom's government. Torren shouldered his pack with the practiced efficiency of someone who had spent decades moving from one crisis to another. "My contacts in the Rangers have been reporting strange happenings throughout the northern provinces. Coordination problems, supply disruptions, and communication failures that don't fit any normal pattern. If Varkis is behind this corruption, they're operating on a much larger scale than just isolated attacks." "Which means we need more than just tactical victories," Amicae added, her Water Essence still probing the local magical currents to ensure that the corruption was truly dispersed. "We need to understand their ultimate objectives and find a way to expose their conspiracy before they can achieve whatever they're really planning." "The capital's too dangerous," she continued, pulling her cowl lower to hide her scars. "If Varkis agents are embedded in the government..." "Then that's exactly where we need to be," Rawn said firmly, his Fire Essence flickering with determination. "Running from the enemy never solved anything. We gather allies, collect evidence of their involvement, and present it to King Theron himself if necessary." Red padded beside his master, the dog's transformation now nearly complete. His eyes held intelligence that went far beyond normal canine awareness, and his mental voice carried strategic thinking that would serve them well in the challenges ahead. Wolf-pack is large, but pack-leader knows their scent. Better to face known enemy than let them choose when and where to attack. Rowetha looked back at the destroyed village one last time, Izzy purring softly in her arms. The kitten's presence seemed to provide comfort not just to the girl, but to all of them—a reminder that hope could survive even the worst catastrophes. "Will it hurt?" she asked quietly, the question carrying layers of meaning that touched on everything from physical danger to emotional trauma. "Fighting more of these things?" "Yes," Rawn said, refusing to offer false comfort to someone who had already seen too much of the world's cruelty. "But some fights are worth the pain. Some lights are worth protecting, no matter what it costs." And some lights burn brightest when darkness seems strongest, Izzy's small voice added, her Guardian magic already beginning to bloom under Rowetha's care. Teacher-who-was showed me. Light doesn't fight darkness by being louder or stronger. It fights by being present, by refusing to go out. As they set out along the river road that would eventually take them toward Eldhaven and whatever confrontation awaited them there, the group of five survivors began to understand that they had become something more than just allies of convenience. They had become a family forged by shared loss and common purpose—people who would stand together against whatever forces threatened the innocent. Behind them, Crygill's ruins already showed signs of natural reclamation. Plants began to grow in patterns that followed healthy rather than corrupted principles. The river ran clear and clean, carrying away the last traces of the entity's influence. Even the mill's collapsed stones seemed to be settling into arrangements that would eventually support new growth rather than supernatural horror. But ahead lay challenges that would test not just their courage and abilities, but their understanding of the very nature of the conflict they had chosen to join. The Emberwraith had been banished, but the forces that had summoned it remained active. House Varkis continued to pursue objectives that threatened the stability of the entire kingdom, and somewhere in the shadows, other entities waited for their chance to enter the world. The war for Unu's soul was just beginning, and the twin suns that painted the evening sky in shades of amber and gold illuminated a future where every victory would come at a price, and every step forward would reveal new depths of the conspiracy they faced. Red's enhanced senses carried warnings of other troubles stirring in distant places—corruption that wore different faces but served similar masters, political maneuvering that prepared the ground for supernatural influence, and ancient powers that had been awakened by the growing chaos. Long hunt ahead, his mental voice carried certainty mixed with anticipation. But pack is strong. Pack will endure. As darkness fell and the first stars appeared in Unu's sky, they made camp in a grove that showed no signs of taint or supernatural influence. Around their fire, they shared stories and plans, gradually building the bonds of trust and understanding that would sustain them through whatever trials lay ahead. The real war was just beginning.

Chapter 2: The Mindreaver's Whisper The Village Dies Crygill, dawn after the corruption Rowetha woke to the sound of her own name being called in Ma's voice, but wrong—layered with harmonics that made her skin crawl and her bones ache with wrongness. "Rowetha, little star. Come home now. We have such wonderful things to show you." She pressed herself deeper against the ancient oak's massive trunk, Izzy trembling in her arms like a small, warm heartbeat against her chest. The kitten's silver eyes reflected more than just the dawn light—they held an intelligence that seemed far older than her few weeks of life. The purple mist had thickened overnight, and now it moved with purpose, reaching between the oak trees like grasping fingers. Where it touched the grass, the green blades withered to black stalks that crumbled at the slightest breeze. Where it brushed against the smaller trees at the grove's edge, their leaves turned the color of old bruises before falling in silent, oily rain. But it couldn't reach her. Not here, in the heart of the grove where the oldest oak's roots went deepest. Izzy's purr had become something more than sound—a vibration that seemed to flow through the earth itself, creating a barrier the mist couldn't cross. Protected, came that whisper in her mind, not quite words but meaning flowing directly into her thoughts. Ancient roots. Deep magic. Guardian's blood runs strong here. Rowetha didn't understand what Izzy meant by Guardian's blood, but she could feel the truth of the protection. The air around them shimmered faintly, like heat-waves but cooler, and within that shimmer she felt safe. Below in the village, Crygill was dying. She could see it all from her hiding place—every cottage, every path, every life being stolen breath by breath. Mrs. Tibble's bakery door stood open, purple mist pouring from within like smoke from a forge. The cheerful yellow curtains in her windows had turned grey-black, hanging limp as burial shrouds. Henrik's woodcarving shop showed no signs of life, but she could see a figure moving inside with mechanical precision—too tall to be Henrik, too fluid in its movements. The thing that had been her neighbor was doing something to the beautiful wooden figures he'd carved, running its hands over them as purple light flickered between its fingers. One by one, the carved animals began to move, their wooden eyes taking on the same violet glow. Even the river was wrong now. The Orkael's clear water had turned dark and sluggish, and things moved beneath its surface that had never been fish. When they breached the water, she caught glimpses of forms that might once have been the village cats and dogs, but stretched and twisted into shapes that hurt to look at. "Little star," Ma's voice called again, closer now. "We know you're frightened. But you don't need to be. Come to us and we'll make the fear go away forever." The thing wearing Ma's face appeared at the edge of the village square, moving with that horrible floating smoothness. It wore Ma's blue dress—the one she'd sewn herself for harvest festivals—but the fabric hung wrong, as if the body beneath wasn't quite the right shape anymore. Purple light leaked from its eyes like tears, and when it smiled, its teeth seemed too sharp, too many. Behind it came Da, and her heart broke all over again. He moved like a puppet with invisible strings, his familiar fisherman's clothes intact but his movements foreign. The hands that had taught her to bait hooks and mend nets now twitched with predatory intent. And with them came others. Grandmother Morra, who had always moved so slowly with her cane, now glided between the buildings like she was floating inches above the ground. Young Alyona, her best friend, whose laughter had filled so many afternoons—now silent and calculating, her head turning in bird-like jerks as she searched for something. They were all searching for her. Sister-friend, Izzy's mental voice carried both comfort and urgency. Cannot hide forever. Bad-things learn. Bad-things adapt. Must be clever. Must be strong. "I know," Rowetha whispered into the kitten's fur. "But I don't know how to be strong enough for this." The corrupted things that had been her family, her friends, her entire world, formed a loose circle around the oak grove. They couldn't enter—the ancient protection held them at bay—but they could wait. And they did wait, with the patient hunger of predators who knew their prey had nowhere to run. "We can wait as long as you can, little star," the Ma-thing called. "Time means nothing to us now. We have all the time in the world. But you... you'll get hungry. Thirsty. Tired. And when you do, we'll be here to welcome you home." As if responding to its words, Rowetha's stomach cramped with hunger. She'd eaten nothing since yesterday's breakfast, and the terror of the day before had burned through what little energy she'd had. Izzy was warm against her chest, but even the kitten's protective magic couldn't feed her. Root cellar, she thought desperately. Bubbee's still in the root cellar. He'll be so scared. But even as the thought formed, she saw movement at the cottage. The thing that wore Da's face was walking toward the cellar doors with purposeful strides. It knelt down—a grotesque parody of gentleness—and spoke in Da's voice toward the hidden space below. "Otec Jr., boy. Come out now. Your ma and I have something wonderful to show you." The cellar doors remained closed, but she could see them trembling slightly. Bubbee was still alive down there, still himself, still terrified and hiding just as she was. The thought filled her with desperate relief and terrible guilt in equal measure. Cannot save brother now, Izzy's mental voice was gentle but firm. Save self first. Get help. Come back strong. "But he's just a little boy," she whispered. Little boy is clever. Little boy hides well. Bad-things cannot reach him yet—cellar has old protections, different magic. Time enough to save. But only if sister-friend survives to try. The wisdom in the kitten's words was undeniable, but it felt like betrayal. How could she abandon Bubbee when he needed her most? How could she run when everyone she'd ever loved was trapped in some purple-eyed nightmare? Because staying would mean becoming one of them. And then there would be no one left to remember who they'd really been. Around the grove's perimeter, the corrupted villagers settled in to wait. Some sat on the ground with unnatural stillness, their purple eyes never blinking. Others stood like statues, their heads turned toward her hiding place with mechanical precision. A few wandered in slow circles, always keeping the grove in sight, always listening for any sound that might reveal her exact location. The sun climbed higher, both Soli and Sola painting the world in their combined radiance, but even the twin suns' light seemed dimmed by the purple mist that hung over Crygill like a funeral shroud. And in that dimmed light, Rowetha began to plan her escape. * The Awakening Rawn's cottage, the same dawn When my eyes opened, both Soli and Sola were blazing in perfect convergence overhead—that rare moment when Unu's twin suns aligned to create the most intense light of the day. I was drenched in sweat, my body soaked from whatever hell I'd just been dragged through. But my vision—gods above, it was sharper than I'd known in years. Every blade of grass stood crisp and vivid, each one touched with the golden-silver radiance that only came during high convergence. Every bird's call rang with startling clarity, and I could hear the distant hum of Essence flows through the earth itself. I glanced down at my hands and my blood went cold. These weren't my hands. Gone were the liver spots, the wrinkles, the tremor that had plagued me for years. My skin was smooth, unmarked—like that of a man thirty years younger. I touched my throat, hardly recognizing the strong voice that would come from it now. What was I? Some cursed thing wearing a dead man's flesh? The sound of careful footsteps made me turn. Red was approaching, but not with his usual eager shuffle. Instead, he moved with the cautious, measured gait of a dog encountering something unknown. His ears were pinned back, his brown eyes wide with uncertainty as he sniffed the air around me. "Red?" I called, and immediately regretted it. My voice was wrong—too strong, too clear, carrying none of the weathered gravel he'd known for decades. "It's me, old boy. It's—" Red stopped three feet away, whining low in his throat. His hackles weren't raised, but his entire body was tense with confusion. He tilted his head, studying my face with an intensity that broke my heart. I could see him searching for something familiar in features that were no longer mine. "Please," I whispered, extending my hand slowly. "I know I look different, but it's still me. Still your pack leader." Red's nostrils flared, taking in my scent. For a terrifying moment, I thought he might bolt—might run from the stranger who claimed to be his master. But then something shifted in his expression. His ears pricked forward slightly, and he took a tentative step closer. Pack leader? The thought wasn't quite words, more like an emotion pressed directly into my mind. I stared at Red in shock, wondering if the transformation had addled my wits along with my body. "Yes," I said aloud, tears starting in my eyes. "Yes, it's me." Red crept forward another step, then another, until he was close enough to press his grey muzzle against my palm. The contact sent a strange warmth through both of us—not quite magical, but more than mere physical touch. His tail gave a tentative wag, then stronger movements as recognition flooded through him. Smells different. Looks different. But feels... same, came that strange mental whisper, and I realized with growing wonder that some bond had formed between us during my transformation. Still pack. Still family. "Oh, Red," I breathed, gathering him into my arms despite the awkwardness of this too-strong body. He was heavier than I expected—or rather, I was stronger than I had any right to be. "I'm sorry, old boy. I'm so sorry you had to see me like that." Red pressed against me, his familiar warmth an anchor in a world that suddenly felt alien. But I noticed something else—his fur felt different under my fingers. Coarser in some places, softer in others, and beneath it his skin seemed to radiate a faint heat that matched the strange energy crackling through my own transformed flesh. Change, his mental voice whispered. Pack leader changes. Red changes too. Before I could fully process what that meant, another voice interrupted our reunion. But worse than the physical change were the whispers that had begun—dark thoughts that weren't quite my own, visions of faces I'd failed twisted into something monstrous. Alaina's eyes, violet-tinted, her flesh splitting as she reached for me. My boy's fevered face, transformed into something hungry and wrong. No, Red's mental voice cut through the dark visions with surprising force. Not real. Bad thoughts from old battles. Pack leader is pack leader. His simple certainty helped ground me, pushing back against the whispers that wanted to drag me into despair. That's when I heard the footsteps. * The Hunt Intensifies The oak grove, Crygill By midday, the corrupted villagers had organized themselves into something that made Rowetha's blood run cold. They weren't just waiting anymore—they were hunting with systematic precision that spoke of a single intelligence directing their actions. The thing that wore Henrik's face had positioned itself at the grove's northern edge, while the Grandmother Morra-thing guarded the south. Others spread out in a careful perimeter, each one perfectly placed to watch for any attempt at escape. But worst of all, some of them had begun to work together in ways that showed they retained their human knowledge while losing everything that made them human. The corrupted baker had brought tools from her shop—not the cheerful implements of her trade, but things twisted by the purple mist into instruments of capture. Nets woven from blackened dough that moved like living things, rolling pins that had sprouted thorns along their length, and mixing bowls that whispered with voices of all the bread they'd once held. The fishermen among the corrupted had brought their own equipment, but changed. Nets that writhed with their own malevolent life, hooks that gleamed with purple light, and lines that seemed to stretch impossibly long as they cast them experimentally toward the grove's edge. Each time one of these tainted tools touched the barrier around the ancient oaks, it recoiled with a sound like sizzling fat. Bad-things learning, Izzy's mental voice carried worried observations. Testing protection. Looking for weak spots. Guardian magic strong, but not perfect. Not eternal. Rowetha could feel the truth of it. The shimmer in the air around them seemed slightly less bright than it had at dawn, and the warmth that flowed up from the great oak's roots felt diminished. Whatever power protected this place, it wasn't limitless. "How long do we have?" she whispered. Izzy's purr shifted into a more complex rhythm, and Rowetha felt the kitten's senses extending outward, reading the flows of magic that protected them. Until true night, came the answer. When both suns set, protection strongest. But also when bad-things strongest. Must choose—stay and hope protection holds, or run when suns give most strength. The decision seemed impossible. Stay and risk the barrier failing when darkness came, or run and face certain capture by the things that had once been her neighbors. But then she saw something that made the choice for her. More figures were approaching Crygill from the north—not corrupted villagers, but travelers on the river road. A merchant's cart pulled by two tired horses, with what looked like a family of Tifflings aboard. They were heading straight toward the village, unaware of what waited for them. "No," she breathed, scrambling to her knees. "They don't know. They can't see what's happened from that angle." The merchant cart was still half a mile away, but Rowetha could make out details with startling clarity—a middle-aged Tiffling man driving, a woman beside him, and two small shapes that had to be children. Just a family traveling the trade routes, expecting to find a friendly fishing village where they could water their horses and perhaps buy some of the smoked fish Crygill was famous for. Instead, they would find purple-eyed horrors that would add four more voices to the hunting chorus. The corrupted villagers had noticed the approaching travelers too. The thing that wore Da's face stood up from where it had been crouched by the cellar doors, its head turning toward the road with predatory interest. The Ma-thing issued some silent command that sent half the watchers gliding toward the village's northern entrance. They were setting a trap. Cannot save them, Izzy's mental voice was gentle but firm. Too many bad-things. Too far away. Warning would reveal sister-friend's position. "I have to try something," Rowetha whispered back. "I can't just watch." But even as she spoke, she knew Izzy was right. Any attempt to warn the travelers would give away her position, and then both she and they would be doomed. The corrupted villagers would have her, and the travelers would walk into the trap anyway. Unless... "The church bells," she said suddenly. "If I could reach the bell tower, ring the alarm... travelers might turn back." Bell tower far from grove. No protection there. Bad-things would catch sister-friend before first step. Rowetha's heart sank as she watched the merchant cart draw closer to the village. The horses were tired, their pace slow but steady. In perhaps ten minutes, they would reach the village square where the corrupted villagers waited with patient hunger. She closed her eyes and tried to think. There had to be something, some way to— The solution came from an unexpected source. Izzy suddenly tensed in her arms, her silver eyes fixed on something at the grove's edge. Following the kitten's gaze, Rowetha saw a small shape moving through the undergrowth—a rabbit, but wrong. Its fur was patchy and grey, its eyes held a dim purple glow, and it moved with the same unnatural smoothness as the corrupted villagers. But it was moving away from the grove, toward the village center. One of many small creatures that had been changed by the purple mist, now serving as extensions of whatever intelligence controlled the corruption. Scout, Izzy's mental voice carried sudden inspiration. Bad-thing sends scouts. But scouts work both ways. Before Rowetha could ask what she meant, Izzy was moving. The kitten's eyes blazed with silver light that was suddenly much brighter than before, and that strange purr became something that resonated through the earth itself. The corrupted rabbit stopped mid-stride, its purple glow flickering. For a moment, it stood perfectly still. Then its eyes began to change, the purple light fading as silver brightness took its place. Guardian magic, Izzy explained as the rabbit began moving with new purpose. Can touch corrupted creatures if they small enough, weak enough. Rabbit will warn travelers. Will run to cart, show danger. Rowetha watched in amazement as the transformed rabbit bounded toward the approaching cart with desperate speed. Even from this distance, she could see it darting back and forth across the road in front of the horses, its behavior so frantic and obviously wrong that the driver pulled up short. The Tiffling family clustered together on their cart seat, clearly confused by the rabbit's strange behavior. The children pointed and chattered while their parents exchanged worried glances. After a moment's consultation, the driver turned the cart around and headed back north at a much faster pace than they'd arrived. The corrupted villagers who'd been positioning themselves for ambush emerged from their hiding places with obvious frustration. The thing that wore the baker's face actually stamped its foot—a grotesquely childish gesture from something that had once been a warm, grandmotherly woman. Saved them, Izzy's mental voice carried weary satisfaction. But cost much strength. Guardian magic not meant for such work. The kitten slumped in Rowetha's arms, her silver eyes dimming to their normal brightness. The effort of reaching out to touch and purify the corrupted rabbit had clearly drained her, and Rowetha could feel the protective barrier around the grove wavering in response. "You did good, Izzy," she whispered, stroking the exhausted kitten's fur. "You saved them." But the rescue had cost them. The shimmer in the air was noticeably weaker now, and the corrupted villagers had noticed. The circle around the grove was tightening, and purple eyes studied the wavering barrier with renewed hunger. Must go, Izzy's mental voice was barely a whisper. Soon. While protection still holds enough for escape. Cannot wait for true night—barrier will fail before then. Rowetha nodded, her heart heavy with the knowledge that leaving meant abandoning Bubbee to whatever fate awaited him. But staying meant becoming just another purple-eyed thing stalking through the ruins of her home. "When?" she asked. When suns begin descent. Late afternoon. Bad-things will be sluggish as light changes. Best chance. That gave her perhaps four hours to prepare for the most terrifying run of her life. Four hours to say goodbye to everything she'd ever known and loved. Four hours to become brave enough to face a world that had suddenly become a place where neighbors could be monsters and safety was always temporary. She settled back against the ancient oak's trunk and began to plan, while around the grove's perimeter, purple eyes watched and waited with the patience of the dead. * A cloaked figure stepped into view Rawn's cottage A cloaked figure stepped into view, and by her graceful movements and the musical quality of her voice, she was clearly an elf. "Greetings, stranger," she said, her voice like delicate bells but edged with caution. "I heard your screams from miles away. When I found you, multicolored smoke poured from your mouth, and you changed from a withered old man into—" She stopped mid-sentence, stepping closer. I saw her hands tremble through her gloves, saw the way her cowl shifted as if she were studying my face with desperate intensity. "No," she whispered, the word barely audible. "It cannot be." Red positioned himself between me and the stranger, not growling but alert, protective. Through our strange new connection, I felt his assessment: Female. Sad. Dangerous but not enemy. Knows pack leader. "I'm sorry, lass, but I don't know—" "Rawnae." The name fell from her lips like a prayer, like a curse. "Rawnae Lupio Mortisimus." My blood turned to ice. No one had called me by my full name in decades. No one except... "Who are you?" I demanded, reaching instinctively for Vulcanspine's hilt where it leaned against the cottage wall. With shaking hands, she pushed back her cowl. The Recognition Half her face was beautiful—sharp Elven features, green eyes bright with tears and disbelief. But the other half... jagged burns snaked from her left cheek down her neck, disappearing beneath her clothing. The scars were old but cruel, speaking of tremendous suffering. Despite the scars, despite the decades that should have aged her, I knew that face. "Amicae," I breathed. She-elf from old memories, Red's mental voice carried surprise. Pack leader's old pack. She stood frozen, tears streaming down both the scarred and unscarred sides of her face. "You remember." "How could I forget?" The words came out rougher than I'd intended. My mind reeled—memories of the war flooding back. Amicae Draks, seventh daughter of House Draks, Water Weaver extraordinaire. We'd fought side by side during the darkest days of the Sunding War, her water magic complementing my fire. And we'd been more than companions. "But you... you haven't aged a day," I said, staring at her. "And those scars..." "Elven blood runs slow," she whispered. "And these marks are recent. Six moons past." Her hand rose unconsciously to touch the damaged flesh. "But you... you were old, Rawnae. Grey and bent and..." Her voice broke. "I came here to find the source of that magical surge, and instead I find you. Young again. As you were when..." "When we served together in the war," I finished quietly. "When we were both young and foolish and thought we could save the world." Old friend returns, Red settled beside me, his presence a steadying influence as emotions threatened to overwhelm me. Through our bond, I felt his simple acceptance: Pack grows. She nodded, unable to speak for a moment. I looked at her—really looked. The memories came flooding back, clean and sharp despite the Mindreaver's whispers trying to corrupt them. The night before Sylvar's Bridge when I couldn't sleep. Her steady presence when the weight of command threatened to crush me. The way she'd understood the terrible choices I had to make. "I thought you were dead," she said, her voice raw. "After the war ended, you just... vanished. I searched for years, Rawnae. Years. Then I heard rumors of a farmer named Rawn in Kleinae, but by the time I found you..." Understanding hit me like a physical blow. "I was married. To Alaina." She nodded, fresh tears spilling over. "You had a new life. A family. You looked... content. Peaceful. I couldn't... I wouldn't destroy that. So I stayed away." The weight of it nearly drove me to my knees. She'd found me. She'd seen me with Alaina, seen our happiness, and walked away rather than complicate my life. "You watched me grow old," I said, wonder and horror mingling in my voice. "From a distance. Making sure you were safe, that you were happy." She laughed bitterly. "Pathetic, wasn't it? An elf maiden pining after a human who'd moved on with his life." Not pathetic, Red's mental voice carried gentle correction. Loyal. Like pack. "Amicae..." "But now you're young again." She stepped closer, her eyes searching my face. "It's really you. The same eyes, the same stubborn set to your jaw when you're thinking too hard. But how? What happened to you?" The Stranger's Gift "A traveler," I said, still reeling from seeing her again. "During the storm last night. He gave me a potion, said it would help with the aches of age." I gestured helplessly at my transformed body. "It did more than that." Red pressed closer to my leg, and I realized he was trembling slightly. The transformation that had changed me was affecting him too—I could feel it through our new bond, a slow alteration of his very essence. "Magic like this... it's beyond anything I've ever seen. The power required..." She shook her head. "Who was he? This stranger?" "I don't know. Grey eyes, dark hair, average build. Called himself grateful for my hospitality." I paused, remembering. "He knew my full name. Called me 'Rawnae Lupio,' just as you did." Her eyes widened. "He knew who you were? Knew what you'd been?" "Seems so. Though how, I can't fathom." We stood in awkward silence, forty years of separation and unspoken conversations hanging between us like a blade. Red whined softly, pressing against my leg, sensing the tension but offering what comfort he could. Pack leader sad, his mental voice carried concern. She-elf sad too. Why sad when pack reunites? The innocence of the question nearly undid me. How could I explain to my loyal companion the complexity of human emotions, the weight of choices made and unmade? "Young lady," I said finally, falling back on old courtesy to fill the silence, "I find myself famished. Would you care to join me for an early lunch?" She glanced toward my cottage—the home I'd shared with Alaina, where I'd built a life she'd been too noble to interfere with. I saw the pain flicker across her features. "I... I should go. This is too much. Seeing you like this, after all these years..." "Please." The word came out more desperate than I intended. "Stay. We have much to discuss, and I..." I looked down at my alien hands. "I could use a familiar face. Even if it brings complicated memories." Yes, Red's tail thumped once against the ground in agreement. She-elf stays. Pack needs all family together. She hesitated, then nodded slowly. "Very well. But just for a little while." * The Darkening Grove The oak grove, Crygill - Late afternoon The moment came sooner than Rowetha had hoped but later than she'd feared. As the twin suns began their slow descent toward evening, casting longer shadows across the corrupted village below, she felt Izzy stir in her arms with renewed alertness. Time, the kitten's mental voice carried quiet urgency. Barrier weakest now, but bad-things also weakest. Must go. Must be brave. Rowetha's stomach clenched with terror that had nothing to do with hunger, though she was desperately thirsty and had eaten nothing all day. The thought of leaving the grove's protection, of stepping out into the open where those purple-eyed things could see her, made her legs feel like water. But she'd been watching the corrupted villagers' patterns all day, learning their movements. As the light changed, they did indeed seem to grow more sluggish. Their heads turned more slowly, their reactions seemed delayed, and several had begun to sway slightly as if fighting off drowsiness. Bad-things are day-hunters now, Izzy explained. Purple mist makes them strong in bright light, weak when suns are low. Night will change that—then they become different kind of strong. Must run during weakness time. "Which way?" Rowetha whispered, studying the perimeter of watchers. The kitten's eyes blazed silver as she extended her senses, reading the magical currents that flowed through the land. South path. Toward big river. Bad-things fewest there, and water will mask scent-trail. Rowetha nodded, though the thought of crossing the corrupted Orkael made her shudder. But Izzy was right—the corrupted villagers seemed to be avoiding the river, clustering more heavily around the northern and eastern approaches to the grove. Izzy goes first, the kitten continued. Small, fast, harder to see. Will draw attention. Sister-friend waits three heartbeats, then runs different direction. When bad-things chase Izzy, sister-friend circles back toward river path. "No!" Rowetha clutched the kitten tighter. "I won't let you sacrifice yourself for me." Not sacrifice, Izzy's mental voice carried a mixture of affection and exasperation. Clever hunting. Izzy faster than bad-things, can escape easy. Guardian magic protects from corruption-touch. But sister-friend needs head start to reach water safely. The plan made sense, but it still felt like abandonment. How could she let this brave little creature risk herself? Because Guardian protects pack, Izzy answered her unspoken thought. Is what Guardians do. Is what Guardians are for. Before Rowetha could protest further, Izzy was moving. The kitten slipped from her arms with fluid grace and bounded toward the grove's eastern edge, where three corrupted villagers stood watch. As she ran, her fur began to glow with that subtle silver light, making her clearly visible to the watchers. The effect was immediate. Purple eyes snapped to focus on the small, bright shape, and the three corrupted things began moving to intercept. Their movements were still sluggish, but purposeful, and Rowetha heard the Ma-thing's voice calling from across the grove: "There! The little beast! Catch it! Where the pet goes, the child follows!" More corrupted villagers converged on Izzy's position, but the kitten was already dancing away from them, staying just barely out of reach. Her silver glow pulsed in complex patterns, and Rowetha realized it was some kind of Guardian magic—confusing the purple-eyed things, making them focus only on catching her while forgetting about watching for other movement. Now! Izzy's mental voice rang with urgent command. Run, sister-friend! Run toward water! Rowetha ran. Her legs were cramped from sitting motionless all day, and terror made her clumsy, but desperation gave her speed. She burst from the grove's protection and sprinted toward the southern edge of the village, where a narrow path led down to the river. Behind her, she could hear the corrupted villagers crying out as they spotted her—but their voices were confused, distant, as if Izzy's Guardian magic was still affecting their ability to focus. The kitten had bought her precious seconds of confusion. The path to the river seemed endless, though it couldn't have been more than two hundred yards. Every step took her further from the grove's protection and deeper into the corruption's domain. The very air felt thick and oily, and with each breath she could taste the wrongness that had infected her home. But she could also smell water ahead—clean water, flowing fast, carrying away the taint instead of holding it. The Orkael might be darkened by corruption upstream, but here at the village's edge it still ran swift enough to cleanse itself. Almost there, she told herself. Almost safe. That's when she heard footsteps behind her. * Inside the Cottage Rawn's cottage The stew bubbled in the hearth, filling my cottage with a savory aroma that mingled uneasily with the storm's damp musk from last night. I ladled the vegetable-heavy concoction into two wooden bowls, my hands—youthful, unblemished, and still a stranger's—moving with unnerving speed and precision. Across the table, Amicae sat with her cowl partially lowered, revealing half her face while casting shadows over the worst of her scars. Her posture was rigid as a deer scenting a wolf, her gloved hands resting carefully in her lap. Red sprawled at my feet, his old bones creaking as he settled into position, oblivious to the tension coiling tighter than a bowstring but somehow sensing the emotional undercurrents through our new bond. I sat across from my guest and set a bowl before her, the clink of wood on wood sharp in the silence. "I'm Rawn," I said, though we both knew it was a pale echo of the truth. "Eat, lass. It ain't much, but it's hearty." My voice carried a young man's roughness, harsher than I'd intended. Her eyes, hidden beneath the cowl, flicked to the stew, then to me, their glint green as the Eldertree's heart. She didn't touch the spoon. Her hands, gloved in fine leather, remained in her lap, fingers twitching like they yearned for a weapon. "Thank you, sir," she murmured, her voice a melodic whisper like water over riverstones, but edged with caution. "Your hospitality is... familiar." She tilted her head, the cowl shifting, and I caught a glimpse of her jaw—smooth, then marred by a jagged line of puckered flesh. A spark from the hearth flared, its Fire Essence crackling, and she flinched, tugging the fabric tighter, her breath hitching as if burned anew. I looked away, busying myself with my bowl, though my stomach churned more from her pain than hunger. "You kept the cottage simple," she observed, studying the modest furnishings. "Just as you always preferred." "Alaina wanted it homey but not cluttered." Her name felt strange on my tongue with Amicae sitting here. "She had good taste." Good alpha female, Red's mental voice carried a warm memory. Kind hands. Smelled like bread and flowers. Amicae's smile was sad but genuine. "I'm sure she did. You always chose well." The comment hung between us, loaded with meaning I wasn't ready to unpack. "Tell me about her," Amicae said softly. "Your wife. Your family." So I did. I found myself speaking of Alaina—her laugh that could chase away the darkest war-dreams, her gentle way with wounded animals that reminded me why life was worth protecting, her patience with my nightmares when I'd wake screaming about burning villages. Of our son, bright and curious as a magpie, taken too young by fever while I held him helpless in my arms, watching his small body burn with heat I couldn't fight. Red settled deeper into his spot by the fire, his presence a comforting constant as I navigated painful memories. Through our bond, I felt his own grief—he'd loved them too, in his simple, devoted way. But there was also something else: a sense that this reunion with Amicae was necessary, that our small pack was incomplete without her. Amicae listened without jealousy, her expression soft with genuine sympathy for losses that might have caused another woman to feel relief or satisfaction. "She sounds wonderful," Amicae said when I finished. "I'm glad you were happy, Rawnae. Truly." "And you? All these years..." She pulled her cowl lower, shadowing her scars. "I wandered. Served my house when duty called, but mostly wandered. The seventh daughter has few obligations." Her voice turned bitter. "Until six moons ago, when duty found me at the Eldertree." "The scars," I said gently. Her face went distant, pain flickering in her green eyes like candleflame in wind. "Ithek Fire Weavers. They came in the night, speaking of rituals and feeding something called 'the Breach.' They... they didn't just burn my flesh. They burned pieces of my soul." Red's hackles rose slightly, a low growl rumbling in his throat as he sensed the darkness in her memories. Through our bond, I felt his instinctive hatred for anything that would harm a member of our pack. The Mindreaver stirred at her words, showing me twisted visions of her attackers' faces, feeding on her pain like a parasite. "I sought you after your Essence surge shook the air," she finished. "If anyone could face these monsters, it's the Scorch of Eryndor." The old title hit me like a blow. "That man's dead, Amicae. Has been for decades." "Has he?" She leaned forward, firelight playing across her scars. "Because the man I see before me looks remarkably like the young warrior who burned away the Lich Emperor's necromantic fog at Vyrn's Pass." "The Scorch burned villages," I said bluntly. "Innocent people died because I couldn't control it." Protected more than harmed, Red's mental voice carried firm disagreement. Saved pack. Saved all packs. "And now more innocents will die if you don't embrace what you once were." Her green eyes blazed with desperate intensity. "The Ithek taint grows, Rawnae. I've felt it, a rot in the Essence, spreading through the northern villages. They'll burn more than me if we don't stop them." I looked at Vulcanspine where it leaned against the hearth, its runes warming slightly as if responding to my thoughts. The familiar weight called to me, but it also reminded me of all the blood on these hands. Sword remembers, Red's mental voice carried certainty. Sword wants to protect again. "I'm no hero, Amicae. Never was. Just a soldier who learned to burn hot enough to end things quickly." "Then be that soldier again," she whispered. "Not for glory. Not for kingdoms. For the people who'll suffer if we don't act." * River's Edge The Orkael River, Crygill The footsteps behind Rowetha were gaining ground, but they weren't the smooth gliding of the fully corrupted. These steps stumbled and lurched, as if whoever followed was fighting against the purple mist's control. She reached the riverbank as the sun's light grew dimmer, both Soli and Sola sinking toward evening. The Orkael ran dark but swift here, and she could see that the water itself seemed to resist the corruption—purple tendrils of mist recoiled when they touched the flowing current. Water cleanses, she thought desperately. Water always cleanses. "Wait!" The voice behind her was familiar but wrong—young Tam from the blacksmith's shop, but his words came out in a strange harmony of normal speech and something else underneath. "Please, Rowetha! Don't run!" She turned to see Tam stumbling down the path after her, but something was clearly wrong with his transformation. His eyes flickered between their normal brown and the purple glow of corruption, and his movements jerked like a marionette with tangled strings. "Tam?" she whispered. "I... I'm fighting it," he gasped, collapsing to his knees at the water's edge. "The purple stuff, it got in me, but I keep pushing it back. Been hiding in the forge—the fire burns it, hurts it. But it keeps trying to take over." Steam was rising from his skin, and she could see why. Purple veins pulsed under his flesh, but around each vein was a network of tiny burn marks, as if something inside him was literally fighting fire with fire. "The forge-fire," he continued, his voice fluctuating between his own and something alien. "It's not normal fire anymore. Ever since the storm, it burns different. Burns the purple stuff. But I can't stay there forever, and it's getting stronger." Rowetha's heart clenched with hope and terror in equal measure. If Tam could fight the corruption, maybe others could too. Maybe Bubbee, safe in his cellar, could resist whatever tried to change him. "How?" she asked. "How are you fighting it?" "Don't know exactly. But the forge-fire, it's not just heat now. It's... angry. Like it hates the purple stuff as much as I do." His eyes flickered to full purple for a moment, and his voice became layered with harmonics: "Come back to the village, little star. We have such gifts to show you." Then the brown returned, and Tam shook his head violently. "No! Get away from me, Rowetha! I can't hold it much longer!" Behind them, more corrupted villagers were approaching the river path, drawn by the sound of voices. The Ma-thing led them, its purple eyes blazing with frustration as it saw its prey so close to the water. "Cannot cross running water," it called in Ma's stolen voice. "Cannot follow where the current cleanses. But you cannot run forever, little star. And your friend there... he will be ours soon enough." Tam's form was already beginning to change, the brown in his eyes fading as whatever force let him resist finally weakened. "Go!" he gasped. "Cross the water! Find help! Tell them about the forge-fire—tell them some fires can fight back!" Rowetha looked at the dark water, then at Tam's desperate face, then at the approaching crowd of purple-eyed horrors. There was only one choice. She jumped. The Orkael was shockingly cold and deeper than she'd expected. The current caught her immediately, dragging her downstream and away from the village. She fought to keep her head above water, struggling against the powerful flow while behind her the corrupted villagers gathered at the bank, watching her escape with inhuman patience. Good, came Izzy's mental voice, weaker now but still present. Sister-friend crosses threshold. Water protects. Current carries away from danger. "Where are you?" Rowetha gasped between mouthfuls of river water. Safe. Escaped bad-things. Moving toward river downstream. Will meet when current brings sister-friend to slow waters. The river carried her past the village and into wilder country, where overhanging trees created a tunnel of darkness. As the twin suns sank lower, the water grew colder, but it also grew cleaner. The corruption that had tainted the river upstream was being diluted and cleansed by the swift current and natural purification. By the time she managed to drag herself out onto a sandy bank a mile downstream, full darkness was approaching and her lips were blue with cold. But she was alive, she was free, and somewhere in the darkness ahead, a small silver-eyed guardian was waiting for her. Behind her, the purple glow of corrupted Crygill painted the northern sky like a malevolent aurora. Ahead lay the unknown—dangers she couldn't imagine, but also the possibility of finding help, of saving Bubbee, of making the wrongness right again. She wrapped her arms around herself for warmth and began walking south, toward whatever hope the wider world might hold. * The Days That Followed Rawn's cottage Amicae didn't leave. The first morning after our reunion, I woke expecting to find her gone—returned to her wandering, taking our shared history with her. Instead, I found her sitting by the cold hearth, staring into the ashes with an expression I couldn't read. The cottage's crystal windows were beginning their slow transition from night-opacity to the greater transparency that would welcome the double-dawn. Red was curled near her feet, which surprised me. He'd always been polite to visitors but rarely offered such trust so quickly. She smells like old pack, his mental voice explained when he noticed my attention. Safe. Sad, but safe. "Couldn't sleep?" I asked, moving to rekindle the fire. My transformed body made the task effortless, but I still missed the familiar morning ache of old joints. "Too many memories," she said softly. "Being here, seeing you like this... it's like the past forty years were just a dream." I knew the feeling. Having her here made my cottage feel different, charged with echoes of who we'd been. But it also highlighted how foreign this young body felt, how disconnected I was from my own life. "You don't have to stay," I said, though the thought of her leaving made my chest tighten. "I know this is difficult." Should stay, Red's mental voice carried disagreement. Pack stronger together. "Where would I go?" She pulled her cloak tighter around her scarred neck. "I've been searching for purpose since the Eldertree. Maybe this is it—helping an old friend find his way again." Old friend. The careful distance in those words spoke volumes. The Simple Things Gone Wrong That first week was a series of small disasters that made me feel like a stranger in my own life. When I tried to milk Bessie, my grip was too strong, causing the old cow to kick and low in distress. The wooden bucket split in my hands like kindling—a bucket that had been crafted by a Tiffling woodworker using traditional techniques, its wood treated with gentle Earth Essence to make it nearly unbreakable under normal use. Red immediately moved to calm the agitated animal, his presence somehow soothing to all the farm creatures. The transformation affecting him had deepened his natural connection to the living world, making him something like a bridge between human concerns and animal instincts. Through our bond, I felt his understanding: the familiar scent and presence remained the same, even if the hands were different. "Your strength isn't the same," Amicae observed, helping me calm Bessie with gentle Water Essence that flowed in soothing spirals around the cow's legs. "This body has power you're not used to." "Forty years of knowing my limits," I muttered, staring at the destroyed bucket. Its destruction felt like a small violation of the harmony that pervaded everything in Kleinae—the careful balance between human needs and natural systems that made Tiffling communities so remarkably stable. Learning, Red's mental voice carried patient acceptance. Pack leader learning new body. Red learning too. She began teaching me control—not magical techniques, but simple awareness of my transformed physical capabilities. How to judge grip strength, how to move without the careful compensation for age I'd developed over decades. "Remember when you first learned to channel Fire Essence?" she asked as I practiced handling eggs without crushing them. These weren't ordinary eggs—the hens in Kleinae were all touched by Earth magic, producing shells that were both stronger and more nutritious than normal. "You burned through three practice dummies before you learned restraint." "I was twenty then. Young enough to adapt quickly." "You're young now too," she pointed out gently. "Your mind might be old, but your body is learning fresh. Give it time." But time felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Every moment in this stolen flesh felt like a betrayal of Alaina's memory, of my boy's short life. They'd grown old and died while I got to play at youth again. Red seemed to sense my emotional turmoil. He began staying closer, offering wordless comfort through our bond. More remarkably, I noticed changes in him too—his movements were becoming more fluid, his eyes brighter, as if proximity to my transformed state was slowly revitalizing his aging body. Pack leader's fire shares, he explained when I asked about it mentally. Red stronger. Feels good. The Market Incident When market day came, I debated whether to attempt it. Amicae settled the question by offering to accompany me. "Two are less threatening than one mysterious stranger," she said, pulling her cowl up to hide her scars. "And if there's trouble, I can help." Red insisted on coming too, despite his age making the journey taxing in the past. But now he moved with surprising vigor, his step lighter than it had been in years. Protect pack, his mental voice carried determination. Show village Red knows pack leader. The walk to Grenhall took half the usual time, my transformed body covering ground with effortless strides. The road itself was a marvel of Tiffling engineering—not paved with dead stone, but grown from living rock that had been coaxed upward by Earth Weavers generations ago. Grenhall's market square was a testament to Tiffling architectural philosophy—a space that had been coaxed from the living bedrock rather than built upon it. The ground itself formed natural amphitheater-style terraces where vendors could display their wares, while overhead, carefully trained vines created a canopy that provided shade without blocking the essential flow of Essence from above. The square buzzed with typical Tiffling activity until I appeared. Vendors tended stalls filled with Essence-touched goods—preserves that would never spoil thanks to Earth magic, tools that stayed sharp through embedded Air Essence, clothing woven from fibers that adapted to weather changes. Then the whispers started, heads turning, conversations dying into uncomfortable silence. But Red's presence seemed to help. Many of the villagers recognized him, and his obvious comfort with me went a long way toward easing their fears. "That's his dog, sure enough," I heard Marta whisper to another vendor. "Red knows him. Dogs always know." Good observation, Red's mental voice carried satisfaction. Humans trust dog judgment. Holdwick approached us, his weathered face creased with suspicion but less hostile than I'd feared. "You again. And you've brought... company." "My niece," I said quickly, hoping the lie would hold. "She's visiting from... distant parts." Amicae gave a small curtsy, keeping her cowl positioned to hide her scars. "Uncle Rawn speaks of you often, Master Holdwick. Your kindness to him over the years." Her musical voice and gracious manner seemed to ease some of Holdwick's suspicion, but he still studied my face with uncomfortable intensity. "Family resemblance is strong," he admitted grudgingly. "But this is still... unusual." Red chose that moment to perform one of his most endearing tricks—sitting up on his hind legs to beg for attention. It was something he'd done since he was a pup, a gesture so distinctly his that several villagers smiled despite their unease. Good strategy, I thought to him. Pack leader taught Red. Now Red teaches pack leader. Yet even in the ordinary rhythm of trade, something felt wrong. At Marta's stall, a basket of apples gleamed with unnatural luster. When one split under her hand, its flesh was veined black and reeking of rot. She muttered, cursing, and swept it away as if it were nothing more than bad luck. Across the square, a woman spoke of her husband's lingering cough, but as she did, a sulfur tang rose in the air—sharp, acrid, unmistakable to my heightened senses. No one else seemed to notice, yet it clung to me like smoke. The atmosphere remained tense, whispers following us wherever we went. Constable Brewis intercepted us at the edge of the square, his official staff in hand. "A word, if you please." The confrontation played out much as I'd feared—questions I couldn't answer honestly, suspicions I couldn't allay. But throughout the tense conversation, Red remained calm and alert at my side, his presence a visible testimony that I was still the man he'd always known. "Your... niece... she's Elven," Brewis observed. "Half-Elven," Amicae corrected smoothly. "My mother was human. From Mannesket." "And she just happened to arrive when you were... recovering from your illness?" "Family takes care of family," I said firmly. "Surely you understand that, Brewis." Red added his own commentary by moving to sit directly between Brewis and me—not aggressively, but with clear protective intent. Brewis noticed the gesture and seemed to relax slightly. "Your dog's loyal, I'll give you that. Always has been." "Loyalty earned over fifteen years," I said. "Some bonds don't break, no matter what changes come." He didn't look convinced, but couldn't find grounds for official action. We left under a cloud of suspicion that had only deepened with my return. * On the Road South Two days after escaping Crygill Rowetha had never been more than a few miles from her village, and the world beyond Crygill's borders seemed impossibly vast and dangerous. She followed the trade road south toward the larger settlements, staying hidden during the day and traveling mostly at night when fewer people were about. Izzy had found her again the morning after her escape from the river, appearing like a small silver shadow while she huddled in the ruins of an abandoned mill. The kitten was thinner than before, her fur damp and bedraggled, but her eyes still glowed with that protective intelligence. Sister-friend safe, Izzy's mental voice carried exhausted relief. Water-crossing worked. Bad-things cannot follow running water, cannot track scent through flowing current. "Are you hurt?" Rowetha asked, gathering the kitten close. Tired. Guardian magic costly. But regenerates with rest and sunlight. Izzy's purr was weaker than usual, but warming. And sister-friend's presence helps. Pack-bond strengthens Guardian power. They'd been traveling together for two days now, and Rowetha was learning things about the wider world that her sheltered village life had never taught her. The corruption from Crygill wasn't spreading randomly—it followed specific patterns, like water flowing downhill or fire following fuel. Streams and rivers that ran swift and clean seemed to resist the taint, which explained why the corrupted villagers couldn't cross running water. But standing pools, wells, and slow-moving waterways all showed signs of infection—an oily film on their surfaces and a sulfurous smell that made both her and Izzy gag. Plants told their own story. Healthy forests with deep, strong root systems showed resistance to the spreading blight. But fields and meadows, gardens and orchards—anywhere humans had shaped the land and weakened its natural defenses—the corruption took hold more easily. Bad-magic follows human-paths, Izzy explained as they skirted around a farm where purple mist leaked from the barn doors. Roads, trade routes, settlements. Uses human networks to spread faster than natural magic can adapt. That explained why they were seeing more and more refugees on the road. Families fleeing their homes with whatever they could carry, speaking in frightened whispers of neighbors who'd changed overnight, of livestock that had turned violent, of wells that bubbled with purple foam. Most of these refugees ignored a travel-stained girl with a kitten, but some tried to help. A Tiffling trader shared bread and cheese when he saw how thin she was growing. A human merchant's wife gave her a worn but warm cloak when the spring nights proved colder than expected. A group of shepherds warned her away from the northern roads, where they'd seen things moving that weren't quite animal or human anymore. Good people still exist, Izzy observed after a particularly kind family had shared their evening meal with them. Corruption spreads fear, but also brings out protective instincts.
