Pack-bonds grow stronger when pack is threatened. But not everyone was kind. Some refugees looked at her small size and obvious youth and saw opportunity rather than someone to help. Twice now, groups of desperate people had tried to rob her of what little she carried, assuming a child traveling alone would be easy prey. They'd learned otherwise when Izzy's eyes blazed silver and their would-be attackers found themselves overcome with paralyzing terror—not magical compulsion, but the sudden, bone-deep certainty that they faced something far more dangerous than a little girl and her pet cat. Guardian magic, Izzy explained after the second such incident. Not harm—just truth. Show them what they really face. Most humans afraid of things they cannot understand. Rowetha was beginning to understand that Izzy was far more than just a magical cat. The term "Guardian" clearly meant something specific, something important, but the kitten was reluctant to explain in detail. Time will teach, was all she would say. Sister-friend grows stronger every day. Will understand when ready. What Rowetha did understand was that they were moving toward something important. Not just fleeing the corruption, but being drawn toward some place or person who could help them fight back. South and west, Izzy's mental voice carried certainty whenever Rowetha asked about their destination. Following warm currents in the magic-flow. Something awakens there. Something that burns clean. The third day of their journey brought them to a crossroads where multiple trade routes converged. A large inn called the Golden Mare sat at the intersection, its courtyard bustling with travelers, merchants, and refugees. The building itself was clearly Tiffling-built—grown rather than constructed—with living wood walls and a roof garden that bloomed despite the early spring season. Here, Izzy said suddenly, her mental voice sharp with attention. This place important. Information here. Maybe allies. Rowetha looked at the crowded inn with apprehension. She had no money for a room or even a meal, and her travel-stained appearance would mark her immediately as just another refugee child. Trust, Izzy's mental voice carried gentle encouragement. Guardian magic opens doors when they need opening. Sister-friend has important story to tell. People here need to hear truth about northern corruption. Taking a deep breath for courage, Rowetha walked toward the inn's main entrance, trying to project confidence she didn't feel. Inside, she might find help, information, or even someone who knew how to fight the purple-eyed horrors that had stolen her family. Or she might find more danger than a twelve-year-old girl and her Guardian cat could handle. But staying on the road wouldn't save Bubbee or anyone else who needed saving. Time to be brave. * The Call to Action Rawn's cottage The next morning brought disturbing news, delivered in a way that showcased Kleinae's remarkable communication network. A Tiffling messenger arrived at dawn—young Pip, Brewis's grandson, but he wasn't alone. Perched on his shoulder was a crystal-bright hummingbird whose artificial nature was immediately apparent—a message sprite, one of the living constructs that carried urgent communications throughout the kingdom. The sprite's eyes glowed with stored Essence as it spoke in Brewis's own voice: "Refugees coming down from Crygill. Something's wrong there. People with purple eyes. Sick, they say. Or worse." Red was immediately alert, positioning himself between the messenger and the cottage door. Through our bond, I felt his assessment: Young male. Frightened. Brings bad news. Magic bird carries death-scent. "How many refugees?" Amicae asked, studying the message sprite with professional interest. Pip swallowed hard, clearly uncomfortable with his role as bearer of dire tidings. "Dozen so far. More expected. They're talking about... about family members who changed. Who stopped being people." His voice dropped to a whisper. "The message sprite says Constable's asking anyone with fighting experience to consider helping. Even..." He looked at me uncertainly. "Even people who might not be what they seem." The sprite's stored message continued: "The corruption spreads faster than natural disease. We need all the help we can get." Around us, the cottage itself seemed to respond to the news. The breathing stones in the walls subtly shifted their patterns, as if the dwelling's living elements could sense the wrongness that was approaching. Red's hackles rose as he caught scent of more than just the boy's fear. Through our enhanced bond, I felt his awareness of something deeper, a taint in the very air, corruption carried on winds that had blown over afflicted lands. Corruption smell, his mental voice carried certainty. Evil magic. Old enemy. "Tell Brewis we'll be there by midday," I said firmly. The message sprite's eyes flared brighter as it recorded my response, then took wing with a high, chiming sound that would guide it back to its creator. Pip nodded and ran off, clearly relieved to be away from us and whatever danger we represented. "The corruption has reached Crygill," Amicae said unnecessarily, but her words carried the weight of someone who had seen such taint before. "Then it's time." I reached for Vulcanspine, its familiar weight grounding me. The blade's runes responded to my touch, beginning their slow transition from dormant steel to awakened fire. "Time to see if an old warrior in borrowed flesh can still make a difference." Not borrowed, Red's mental voice carried certainty. Earned. Given for purpose. Pack leader ready. Around us, the cottage continued its morning routines, but there was a subtle shift in its rhythms. The living wood of the walls seemed to pulse with tension. The moss gardens on the roof oriented themselves differently, as if preparing for some fundamental change. Even the Essence flows through the building's magical infrastructure felt altered—still harmonious, but tuned to a martial frequency that hadn't been present since my warrior days. "We'll make a difference," Amicae corrected. "The Scorch of Eryndor and the Tide Walker, together again." I looked at her—scarred but determined, her green eyes blazing with purpose. Then at Red, whose aging body was showing new vitality, whose eyes held intelligence that went beyond normal canine awareness. Finally, I gazed around the cottage that had been my peaceful refuge but was now transforming itself into something like a war council chamber, its living elements preparing for battle. For the first time since my transformation, I felt like I knew exactly who I was supposed to be. "Aye, princess," I said, letting Fire Essence flow through Vulcanspine's runes. "Together." The blade ignited with steady flame, and the cottage's crystal windows immediately adjusted their transparency to contain the light while the breathing stones shifted to absorb any excess heat. Even my home understood that the Scorch of Eryndor had awakened, and it was adapting to serve a warrior once more. Red's approval hummed through our bond, warm and fierce. Whatever power had transformed me, it had also begun awakening something in my oldest friend. We would face this darkness as we always had—as pack, as family, as companions bound by loyalty stronger than any magic. Outside, the twin suns continued their eternal dance across Unu's sky, but their light now revealed a world at war with forces that threatened not just life, but the very nature of existence itself. Our quiet days were over. The fight for Unu's soul was about to begin. And somewhere to the north, a brave young girl and her Guardian companion were also heading toward their own confrontation with the spreading darkness. Though we didn't know it yet, our paths were converging—the old warrior reborn, the child who'd witnessed the corruption's true face, and the ancient powers that would be needed to save them all. The Mindreaver's whispers grew stronger as I contemplated the battles ahead, but Red's loyal presence and Amicae's determined smile pushed back the darkness. Whatever came next, we would face it together. Just as we always had.

Document.Title: Scene 3
Document.Text: My body feels incredible. I am lost in thought as to how this could be. I honestly just wanted the tonic to kill me. Instead, I am entirely reinvigorated. I am not sure just how I feel about it. The Elf maiden who I woke up to observing me as I transformed is very quiet. She is very graceful with her movements. Like her kind is want to be. I am surprised she even accepted my invitation. Elves are very slow to trust. When we reach my cottage door, I open it and usher her to enter before me. “Please excuse the mess. I was an old man who had been living with just his dog for many, many years. This old cottage hasn’t felt a woman’s touch in decades.” I said. Her mannerisms were regal and graceful. I began to recognize that this young Elf was likely of High blood. It reminds me of a time past. A time when Koric, the Elven envoy to the King of the Elven Kingdom, Sylvantus pushed me so very hard to get involved in Mannesket politics. “Sit wherever you please, I’ll get a start on something to eat,” I said.”Thank You.” She said. She takes a seat at the small kitchen table. She continues wearing her cloak up. Elves are strict about their etiquette. So, this made me look her over more closely. What is she hiding? Yet, I do not challenge her on her breach of etiquette. I put my old pot over the fire, half full of water, and stoked the fire. I have ingredients for a hearty stew already laid out earlier for my meal today.

Document.Title: Standard Manuscript
Document.Text:
Andrew Nonymous 700 words. Your manuscript should have your address and contact details on the title page only.
If you have an agent, your agent's address can be included beneath.
NOVEL STANDARD MANUSCRIPT FORMAT by Andrew Nonymous
Nonymous / MANUSCRIPT FORMAT / 1
Chapter One
Standard manuscript format is often required for novels so that work can easily be read by editors. It allows editors to estimate word count and determine roughly how many pages will be required to produce a book using the page size, style and font of their house. This PDF file was generated via Scrivener's compile feature and was created from the "Novel" project template.
Manuscripts for full-length novels require a separate title page. The author's name, address, telephone number and e-mail address should be shown in the upper-left corner of the title page, single-spaced. The title of the story should appear in all caps about halfway down the page. The author's name follows the title on the by-line as the author wants it to appear when published. A real name or pseudonym may be used and may include initials, actual given name or professional designation.
All text should be double-spaced and left-justified with a ragged right margin. Paragraphs should be indented by about five
Nonymous / MANUSCRIPT FORMAT / 2
spaces (half an inch) and not separated by an additional blank line. (It used to be traditional to separate each sentence with two spaces, but in these days of word processors this is now less common.) A 12-point font such as Courier, Times New Roman or Arial should be used throughout. In Scrivener, however, you can write using any font and formatting you choose--the options in compile can be used to change the formatting in the compiled document. In the "Novel" project template, the compile settings will deal with changing the font and formatting to those required for standard manuscript format.
Top, bottom, left and right margins should all be approximately one inch, not allowing for the page header. Each page except for the title page should include a header comprising the author's real surname, the title of the work (or a key word from the title) in capitals, and the page number. In Scrivener, all of this is handled for you by the "Manuscript" compile formats.
# Scene breaks are indicated with the hash character. Do not simply add an extra line space as this can be missed by the typesetter. The "Separators" tab of Scrivener's compile format designer can handle this for you if you are writing each scene as a separate text document. In general, this will be handled by compile in Scrivener, however, so that you can either leave empty lines in the text (and Scrivener will insert the hash character
Nonymous / MANUSCRIPT FORMAT / 3
during compile) or use different documents for different sections (and Scrivener will add the hash between them).
Some editors and publishers prefer emphasis to be indicated by underlining rather than italics. In Scrivener, this conversion can be handled by compile. The "Manuscript (Courier)" compile format will convert italics to underlines, whereas the "Manuscript (Times)" format will not.
Nonymous / MANUSCRIPT FORMAT / 4
Chapter Two
Begin each new chapter on a new page like this with the chapter number about a third of the way down the page. In Scrivener, this can all be dealt with in the compile settings. In Scrivener, all of this is handled automatically by the "Manuscript" compile formats.
Although it is often said that writers should not use the word-count features of their computers to determine the number of words in a manuscript, most publishers and editors will happily accept a word processor word count these days. In Scrivener, you can insert a word count of your manuscript by using Edit > Insert > Draft Word Count (for a manuscript, you may wish to have the word count rounded to the nearest 100, in which case select Edit > Insert > Draft Word Count > Rounded To Nearest 100--this feature is used on the title page of this manuscript, in fact).
Note that the actual word count will not be inserted, but a tag that will be substituted for the word count upon export. In
Nonymous / MANUSCRIPT FORMAT / 5
reality, the formatting of a manuscript is often only really crucial when submitting directly to a publisher--first time novelists will usually submit to an agent first, and agents are often less picky about format and will point you in the right direction before submitting your work to a publisher.
# Much of the information contained in this document is based on a description of novel manuscript format by Matt Carless for the BBC Writers' Room website: http://www.bbc.co.uk/writersroom
# # # # #

Chapter: The Hawk’s Claws Dawn’s gray veil cloaked Eldhaven’s outskirts, the Orkael’s murmur a faint echo of the storm as we slipped from the Thorned Fang, Torren’s ranger cloak leading the way. Rowetha’s tale last night—her parents’ violet eyes, flesh oozing, Klisternok’s black vapor turning Crygill to nightmares—had steeled Torren’s vow to get us to High King Theron, my identity as the Scorch of Eryndor buried. My side ached, Varkis blade’s graze dulled by Amicae’s Tide’s Caress, my Fire Essence weak from Overburn, Emberflow Kata’s spark not yet a blaze. Amicae’s cowl hid her scars, her Aqua Lash poised, fire fear straining near my warmth. Rowetha clutched Izzy, her courage a blade sharper than Vulcanspine. Red, his pearly teeth and Fire Essence glow Aeloria’s gift, padded beside, while Trink, the pixie rapscallion, scouted ahead, his Varkis purse theft and Crygill sympathy proving his worth beyond his swill-soaked jests at the Rusty Crown. Torren’s plan—a hidden trail to Vaelhold, Theron’s keep, posing us as wanderers with Rowetha’s testimony—hinged on dodging Varkis’s eyes. House Varkis, crimson hawk sigil blazing, loomed larger now: nobles who’d turned Mannesket’s trade to poison, funding Ithek Acolytes to spread Mindreaver taint, silencing Rowetha to hide their rot. Their lord, Varren, schemed for Theron’s throne, his sister Myrra’s spies—like the fishmonger Trink crossed—weaving a net across Eldhaven. My return as the Scorch, hero of the Sunding War, could rally Theron’s loyalists, but Varkis would twist my name into a rebel’s banner, sparking chaos to seize power. Trink’s wings buzzed, darting back from a copse ahead. “Trouble, mates!” he hissed, green eyes sharp despite last night’s ale. “Varkis Talons—three, with a prisoner. Near the old mill, blockin’ our trail!” Torren cursed, hand on his sword, while Amicae’s Mistveil Cloak spell wove faint fog, shielding Rowetha. Red’s growl rumbled, his new teeth bared, sensing the taint’s rot in the air. We crept to the mill’s ruin, its wheel creaking, the Orkael’s mist thick with Varkis’s stench—blood and ore. Three Crimson Talons, leathers etched with hawks, stood over a bound ranger, her face bruised, a raven badge torn. “Kalia,” Torren whispered, fury in his eyes. A Talon, towering with a scarred cheek—Captain Drenn, I’d wager—held an Ithek artifact, a black orb pulsing violet, its vapor curling like Crygill’s plague. “Talk, ranger,” Drenn snarled, “or the Emberwraith takes your soul.” Rowetha gasped, Izzy hissing, her small hand gripping my sleeve. “That’s like Klisternok’s vapor,” she whispered, her tale’s echo chilling me. I gripped Vulcanspine, my young body taut, guilt burning—hero’s crown or no, I’d failed villages like Crygill. “Stay low,” I growled, nodding to Torren. “We free Kalia, learn Varkis’s game.” Amicae’s Aqua Lash coiled, her voice steel. “Drenn’s got an Ithek orb—proof for Theron. We take it, but Varkis’ll hunt us harder.” Her fire fear flickered, my warmth a spark she dreaded, but her eyes held Rowetha’s hope. Trink buzzed, grinning. “I’ll nick that orb, mates! Talons hate pixies!” His worth shone, no mere drunkard. Torren and I charged, Red leaping, his Fire Essence flaring. My Scorchfang Slash ignited Vulcanspine, cleaving a Talon’s arm, ichor sizzling, while Torren’s blade parried another. Red’s teeth sank into a Talon’s leg, pearly whites fierce, as Amicae’s Surging Aegis shielded Rowetha, her Aqua Lash binding Drenn’s wrist. Trink darted, snatching the orb, its violet pulse stinging his wings, but he cackled, tossing it to Amicae. Drenn roared, breaking free, his Fire Bladeweaving flaring—a Blazeclaw Rend grazing my side, Overburn searing. Kalia, freed by Torren, grabbed her dagger, stabbing a Talon. “Varkis funds Ithek rituals!” she gasped, voice raw. “Varren’s gold buys taint—Crygill’s just the start!” Rowetha’s sob cut through, her tale’s truth bared—Klisternok was Varkis’s pawn, the Emberwraith their Ithek master. The Mindreaver’s whispers swelled—Alaina’s oozing face, Rowetha’s parents clawing, Amicae’s scars as my war’s pyres—urging me to burn Varkis’s rot. Drenn retreated, blowing a horn, more Talons’ boots echoing. “Run!” Torren barked, supporting Kalia. Amicae’s Mistveil Cloak cloaked us, the orb in her pack, proof for Theron. We fled down the trail, Red limping but fierce, Trink’s wings singed but buzzing. Rowetha clung to Izzy, her testimony now Varkis’s bane, her courage my boy’s echo. Vaelhold loomed, Theron’s court a crucible, and Varkis’s hawk circled, its claws sharp with Ithek fire.

Red's tail managed a weak wag as he fell into step beside me, his grey muzzle nearly white now, his brown eyes clouded with age but still bright with affection. We made quite a pair—two old soldiers marking time until the final retreat. The chicken coop sat nestled against the cottage's eastern wall, positioned to catch the morning warmth of Soli's first light. I'd built it there fifteen years ago when Alaina and I first settled in this quiet corner of Kleinae. She'd wanted chickens, said they'd make the place feel more like home. The memory brought its familiar pang of loss, dulled by time but never quite absent. "Easy there, ladies," I murmured to the hens as they squawked their displeasure at my intrusion. My movements were slow, careful—not just from courtesy to the birds, but from necessity. These old hands shook more each morning, and I'd learned to compensate with patience rather than struggle against the tremor. The first egg nearly slipped from my fingers. I caught it against my chest, feeling my heart skip with the small exertion. There was a time when I could have caught a dozen falling eggs without thought. Now I celebrated not dropping one. Red lowered himself gingerly to the ground nearby, his old bones protesting the movement with a soft grunt that echoed my own morning symphony of aches. He watched me work with the patient attention of a creature who understood that everything took longer now, that rushing only led to mistakes and spilled breakfasts. "Remember when you used to chase these birds?" I asked him, placing the precious egg in my worn basket. "Alaina would scold you something fierce, but I think she enjoyed watching you play." Red's ears perked slightly at Alaina's name. Even after ten years, he remembered her, just as I did. Some losses carve themselves too deep to ever truly heal. The second egg came easier, the hens having settled into their routine grumbling. I moved from nest to nest, each step measured, each reach calculated to avoid the sharp complaint of my lower back. The walking stick helped, but some mornings I wondered if it wouldn't be easier to simply stay in bed and let the world continue without me. But Red depended on me, and I on him. We were each other's anchor to purpose, two old souls keeping each other tethered to life. "Six eggs today," I announced, showing the basket to Red as if he could count. "That's two better than yesterday. The brown hen's finally earning her keep." A distant rumble of thunder made us both look toward the northern sky. Dark clouds gathered on the horizon, though Sola had yet to fully rise and join her partner in their daily dance. The air held that electric tension that spoke of a significant storm approaching. "Looks like we'll have weather tonight," I told Red, who had struggled to his feet with obvious effort. "Best get the animals secured." We made our way to the small barn where Bessie, my ancient cow, stood waiting for her morning attention. Like everything else on this little farm, she was past her prime but still serving faithfully. I set the egg basket carefully on a post and reached for the milking stool. The simple act of sitting required a moment's pause to let my knees settle into position. Red positioned himself nearby, close enough to offer comfort but far enough to avoid a kick from Bessie should she grow restless. "There's a good girl," I murmured, beginning the familiar rhythm of milking. My hands moved slowly but steadily, coaxing streams of warm milk into the wooden bucket. This too had grown harder over the years—my grip wasn't what it once was, and my shoulders protested the repetitive motion. But the routine was soothing, a connection to the simpler life I'd chosen after the war.

Chapter 6: Royal Justice The Celebration The great hall buzzed with relief and celebration such as I hadn't seen since the end of the Sunding War. Nobles who'd spent months walking on eggshells around Varkis agents now spoke freely, wine flowed like water, and for the first time in ages, hope filled the air like music. But I'd been to enough victory feasts to know how quickly they could turn sour. Trink had discovered the royal wine cellars—a development that filled me with equal parts amusement and dread. The dragonfly pixie hovered unsteadily above the high table with a goblet nearly as large as himself, his iridescent vest already stained with wine. "Friends! Companions! Fellow heroes!" he announced, nearly tumbling into a nobleman's soup. "Allow me to pro-pro-propound a toast!" His speech carried the careful precision of the very drunk trying to appear sober. I buried my face in my hands as Trink gestured grandly, splashing wine across several startled courtiers. "And to the beautiful, the talented, the absolutely spectacular Lady Amicae!" Trink spun in midair, nearly colliding with a chandelier. "Whose scars tell tales of heroism, and whose... whose other attributes tell tales of..." He hiccupped. "Where was I going with this?" "Nowhere good," Amicae muttered, though she was fighting a smile. Beside me, Rowetha giggled—the first genuine laughter I'd heard from her since Glimmer's death. The girl had been quiet since arriving at court, but seeing Trink's ridiculous antics was drawing her out of her shell. "And to little Rowetha!" Trink declared, attempting a midair bow that sent him spiraling toward the table. "Bravest little warrior in all the kingdoms!" The toast continued with increasingly creative descriptions of our various heroic qualities, punctuated by Trink's aerial acrobatics and the occasional crash into serving platters. Even King Theron was chuckling by the time the pixie finally exhausted himself and settled onto a chandelier to recuperate. But through it all, I found myself watching Vaelar. Growing Doubts Amicae's brother held court near the throne, surrounded by Elven diplomats and human nobles eager to curry favor with Aethelwood. He was everything an aristocratic lord should be—gracious, intelligent, well-informed about the corruption threat. So why did something about him set my teeth on edge? "You're brooding," Amicae observed, settling beside me with a goblet of wine. Her scars were partially hidden by the formal gown Theron had provided, but she wore them with growing confidence. "Just thinking," I said, not wanting to voice suspicions I couldn't articulate. "Your brother's arrival was... fortuitous." "You sound like you think that's a bad thing." I studied Vaelar more closely, noting how he positioned himself with clear sightlines to all exits, how his hand never strayed far from his sword hilt. Old habits from my military days, or something more? "How did he know to come here?" I asked. "How did he know you were alive, that we were working together?" Amicae frowned. "Elven communication networks are extensive. Word travels fast through the right channels." "Fast enough to reach Aethelwood, mobilize a diplomatic mission, capture Joren Varkis, and arrive here within days of our own escape from the Thorned Fang?" She was quiet for a moment, considering. "When you put it like that..." "And another thing—how did he intercept Varkis correspondence? Those documents were fresh, not months old. Someone would have had to be monitoring their communications in real time." "You think he's been spying on Varkis?" "I think someone's been spying on someone," I said carefully. "Question is who, and for how long." Across the hall, Vaelar raised his goblet in a toast to King Theron. His smile was perfect—warm, grateful, entirely appropriate for the occasion. But Rowetha was right about his eyes. There was something behind them that didn't match the expression on his face. A Moment Alone Later, as the celebration wound down, Amicae and I found ourselves on one of the palace's balconies. The night air was cool against my skin, carrying the scent of jasmine from the royal gardens and the distant sound of Eldhaven's nightlife. "Hard to believe it's over," she said softly, leaning against the stone railing. "Is it?" I asked. "Varkis might be in chains, but the corruption itself is still out there. The Emberwraith, the Breaches—those didn't disappear with Varren's arrest." She turned to face me, moonlight playing across her scarred features. "You really don't trust my brother." It wasn't a question, so I didn't treat it like one. "I don't trust anyone completely. Old habit from the war." "Even me?" The vulnerability in her voice made my chest tighten. "You're different. You've bled beside me, saved my life more times than I can count. That builds trust." "And Vaelar hasn't done the same?" I thought about it, trying to separate legitimate concern from old paranoia. "He's done everything right. That's what bothers me. In my experience, when something seems too good to be true..." "It usually is," she finished. "I know. I've learned that lesson myself." We stood in comfortable silence, watching the lights of Eldhaven twinkle below us. For all my doubts about Vaelar, being here with Amicae felt right in a way I hadn't experienced since Alaina's death. "What happens now?" she asked quietly. "When the kingdom is safe, when the corruption is cleansed... what then?" The question hung between us, heavy with possibility and forty years of separation. "I don't know," I admitted. "I never expected to have a 'what then.' I was an old man waiting to die, remember?" "And now you're young again, with a lifetime ahead of you. You could go anywhere, do anything." I stepped closer, close enough to see the uncertainty in her green eyes. "Is that what you think I want? Glory? Adventure?" "I don't know what you want," she whispered. "I barely knew forty years ago." I reached up and cupped her scarred cheek, feeling her lean into the touch. "Forty years ago, I wanted to end the war and build something peaceful. I found that with Alaina, for a time." Her eyes closed, pain flickering across her features. "But now?" I continued. "Now I want something I thought I'd lost forever. I want to wake up next to someone who understands the weight I carry. Someone who's seen darkness and chosen light anyway." Her eyes opened, bright with unshed tears. "Rawnae..." "I want you, Amicae. Not the memory of who we were, but who we are now. Scarred, seasoned, wise enough to know what we're choosing." She leaned into my touch, her hand covering mine. "I've wanted that for forty years. Watched you from afar, convinced myself I was being noble by staying away, but really I was just afraid." "Afraid of what?" "Of this moment. Of finding out if what we had was real or just... battlefield passion. Fear and adrenaline dressed up as love." I leaned closer, our foreheads almost touching. "And what do you think now?" Her smile transformed her scarred features into something luminous. "I think we're about to find out." Our kiss tasted of wine and possibility, of old love rekindled and new love discovered. It was gentle and desperate all at once, forty years of separation compressed into a single moment of connection. When we finally parted, I rested my forehead against hers. "So," I said, my voice rough with emotion. "What then?" "Then we build something together," she whispered. "Something worth all the pain it took to get here." The Morning After I woke to find Amicae already awake beside me, her scarred face peaceful in the morning light filtering through the guest chamber windows. After forty years apart, sharing a bed again felt both natural and miraculous. "Good morning," she murmured, green eyes warm with contentment. "Good morning," I replied, still marveling at the simple joy of it. "Sleep well?" "Better than I have in decades. No nightmares about burning trees. No dreams of you growing old while I watched from the shadows." A knock at the door interrupted our quiet moment. "Rawn? Amicae?" Rowetha's voice carried through the wood. "King Theron wants to see everyone. He says it's important." "Coming, lass," I called back, reluctantly leaving the warmth of the bed and Amicae's embrace. As we dressed, I noticed Red stretching in his corner of the room. The change in him had become even more pronounced—his grey muzzle now showed distinct patches of his original red-brown coloring, and he moved with an energy I hadn't seen in years. "He's getting younger," Amicae observed, watching him bound toward the door with almost puppyish enthusiasm. "The magical bond," I said, scratching behind his ears as he pressed against my leg. "Somehow my transformation is affecting him too." Whatever the explanation, it was good to see the old boy full of life again. We'd need all our strength for whatever came next. The Announcement The throne room hummed with anticipation. Theron had summoned not just our small group, but representatives from across the kingdom—nobles, merchants, guild leaders, even common farmers who'd fought corruption in their villages. Trink perched miserably on a windowsill, still nursing his legendary hangover but present out of duty. His usual vibrant green had returned, though he moved with the careful deliberation of someone trying not to jar his aching head. "Citizens of Mannesket," Theron began, his voice carrying the authority of absolute conviction. "Yesterday, we uncovered a conspiracy that reached into the very heart of our realm. House Varkis, trusted allies for two decades, had allied themselves with forces of corruption and darkness." Murmurs rippled through the assembled crowd. Many had suspected something was wrong, but the scope of the betrayal still shocked them. "But we have triumphed," the king continued. "Through the courage of heroes—the returned Scorch of Eryndor, Lady Amicae of Aethelwood, and their brave companions—we have exposed this threat and brought the traitors to justice." Applause filled the throne room. I felt Amicae's hand slip into mine, both of us still adjusting to being celebrated instead of hunted. "Furthermore," Theron said, raising his voice above the crowd, "I am pleased to announce a formal alliance with Aethelwood. Lord Vaelar has graciously agreed to remain as our liaison, helping to coordinate the cleansing of corruption from both our realms." Vaelar stepped forward, resplendent in ceremonial Elven robes of silver and green. He bowed formally to the king, then turned to address the crowd with practiced ease. "The bonds between Aethelwood and Mannesket run deep," he said, his voice carrying easily through the hall. "In this dark time, we must stand together against the forces that would corrupt our very souls." More applause, even more enthusiastic than before. An alliance with Aethelwood was a significant coup—the Elven realm's magical expertise would be invaluable in dealing with any remaining corruption. But as I watched Vaelar work the crowd with diplomatic skill, that nagging feeling returned. Everything was proceeding too smoothly, too perfectly. And in my experience, when everything seemed perfect, it usually meant you were walking into a trap. Seeds of Doubt During the private audience that followed, I found myself studying Vaelar more intently. He answered questions with easy confidence, provided detailed intelligence about remaining Ithek cells, and offered resources with the kind of generosity that should have been reassuring. Instead, it made me more suspicious. "Your network of informants must be extensive," I observed during a lull in the conversation. "To gather this much intelligence so quickly." Vaelar's smile was perfect—confident without being arrogant, grateful for the opportunity to serve. "Aethelwood has many resources for gathering information. We've been monitoring the corruption threat for months." "Monitoring," I repeated. "Not acting?" "Intelligence gathering requires patience," he said smoothly. "Act too soon, and you alert your enemies. Act too late..." He gestured to the documents spread across the table. "Well, we've seen the consequences." It was a reasonable explanation. Logical, even. But something about his tone, the way his eyes never quite met mine when he spoke, kept my suspicions alive. Rowetha, who'd been quietly drawing in her journal, looked up suddenly. "Lord Vaelar, how did you know about the Thornwood Grove attack before it happened?" The question hung in the air like a blade. Vaelar's expression didn't change, but something flickered behind his eyes—too quick to identify, gone almost before I noticed it. "Intelligence networks," he said smoothly. "We'd been monitoring Ithek communications for months." "But the attack was planned in secret," Rowetha pressed, her young voice carrying an odd certainty. "Glimmer showed me things before she died. The corruption-bringers were very careful about their plans." "Glimmer was a Guardian," Vaelar said, his tone becoming slightly patronizing. "Their visions aren't always literal, child. Symbols and impressions, not detailed intelligence." Amicae straightened beside me. "Glimmer's visions were always accurate," she said quietly. "And Rowetha is right—the Ithek cells compartmentalized their operations. How would you have intercepted communications about a specific attack?" The room grew tense. King Theron looked between the siblings, sensing something building beneath the surface. For just a moment, Vaelar's perfect diplomatic mask slipped. I saw something cold and calculating underneath, quickly hidden behind polite concern. "Sister," he said gently, "you've been through tremendous trauma. The attack at the Eldertree, weeks of fighting corruption—it's natural to see threats where none exist." "Don't patronize me," Amicae said, her voice hardening. "I may bear scars, but my mind is clear. Answer the question." The mask slipped further, revealing something I recognized from the war—the look of someone who'd been caught in a lie and was deciding whether to double down or cut his losses. But before Vaelar could respond, a commotion erupted outside the throne room. Shouts, running footsteps, the sound of steel on steel. "What in the seven hells—" Theron began, but his words were cut short as the doors burst open. Royal guards stumbled in, their armor smoking, their faces pale with terror. Behind them came something that had once been human but was human no longer. Purple eyes blazed in a face that writhed with shadow and corruption. When it spoke, its voice was the whisper of grave earth and rotting dreams. "The Emberwraith sends greetings," it rasped, raising hands that ended in claws dripping with acidic fluid. "Did you truly think capturing a few pawns would end our great work?" As chaos erupted around us, I saw Vaelar's expression shift one final time. The diplomatic mask was gone entirely now, replaced by something that looked almost like... relief? As if a performance he'd grown tired of maintaining was finally over.

Chapter 1: The Storm's Wake The Horror in Crygill Three days after the Storm of Delfaen The first thing Rowetha noticed was that Mr. Klisternok's smile had gone wrong.Mr. Klisternok used to joke with everyone, but since the storm he barely spoke. Ma said some people just 'went quiet' when the magic felt wrong, like they'd forgotten how to be themselves. She'd been sitting behind the barn with Izzy, the black kitten with white paws who'd adopted her a week ago, watching the morning sun burn away the last of the storm clouds. The great tempest had drained the local Essence wells—Da said the fishing nets felt 'magically dead' and wouldn't hold properly anymore. The storm had brought more than rain—something in the water tasted wrong now, and the local Essence wells felt tainted. "Shhh, Izzy. It's just Mr. Klisternok," she whispered as the kitten began to hiss, but something deep in her chest whispered that nothing was 'just' anything anymore. Mr. Klisternok walked into their yard from the tail path that ran along the river, but he moved wrong—too smooth, like he was floating just above the ground instead of walking on it. He'd always been a kind neighbor, and his daughter Alyona was one of her best friends at school. But this wasn't the Mr. Klisternok who'd taught her to bait fishing hooks and told funny stories about the river sprites. This thing wearing his face didn't even glance in her direction as it walked straight to their front door. "Mr. Klisternok, what can we do for ye today?" Da answered the knock, his voice warm with the easy friendship of neighbors who'd known each other for years. "Morning, Otec. Fine day, now that that awful storm's behind us," the thing said, smiling in a way that made Rowetha's stomach turn cold. "Aye," Da replied. "River's running too strong to fish, but no damage here." The false Mr. Klisternok nodded, that horrible smile never fading. "Where's your lovely wife?" Ma appeared at the door, wiping flour from her hands on her apron. "Good morning, Mr. Klisternok." He didn't return the greeting. "I've a message for the both of you." Ma and Da leaned in to listen—and that's when everything turned wrong. Mr. Klisternok opened his mouth... too wide. A horrible gurgling noise bubbled out as thick, oily vapor—dark and smoky and shot through with veins of purple light—poured from his mouth like it was alive. It slithered through the air and rushed up Ma's and Da's noses before either of them could move. It was over in a second. Mr. Klisternok straightened, turned around, and walked away like nothing had happened, that terrible smile still fixed on his face. Ma and Da just stood there for a moment, staring blankly into the distance with vacant eyes. Then something shifted behind their familiar features—something cold and hungry that looked out through eyes that were slowly, horribly, turning purple. When they shook their heads and went back inside, they moved with that same wrong smoothness she'd seen in Mr. Klisternok. Rowetha sat frozen behind the barn, Izzy trembling in her arms, as the morning sun painted everything in cheerful gold light that felt like a mockery. The storm was over, but something worse had taken its place. Something that wore the faces of everyone she loved. * The Last Peace Three days after the Storm of Delfaen The twin suns of Unu crested the eastern hills as I shuffled from the cottage, my walking stick tapping against the worn stone path. Each step sent a familiar ache through my knees, a morning greeting I'd grown accustomed to over the past decade. The pain started sharp and settled into a dull throb--my body's way of reminding me that another night of restless sleep on aging bones had passed. Three days had passed since the great storm—the most violent tempest I'd seen in decades—and the morning air still held an electric charge that made my old war wound ache and set Red's hackles on edge. That storm had been unnatural, I was certain of it. The lightning had struck in patterns too precise to be random, and the thunder had carried undertones that sounded almost like voices. When the worst of it passed, I'd found strange scorch marks in my garden—not from lightning, but from something else. Something that had touched the earth and left it subtly wrong. Red emerged from the cottage behind me, moving with the same careful deliberation that marked us both as creatures whose best days lay behind them, but his behavior had been odd since the storm. His ears constantly swiveled toward the north, as if he could hear something in the distance that escaped my aging senses. His claws clicked against the stones with each measured step, a sound that had grown more pronounced as arthritis stiffened his joints. We moved like a pair of old clockwork toys, our springs wound too loose, our gears grinding with each motion. "Come on then, old fellow," I wheezed, my voice carrying the gravel of too many years and too many battles. The words emerged with a puff of vapor in the cool morning air, and I had to pause to catch my breath after just those few syllables. "Eggs won't gather themselves." But even as I spoke, part of me wondered if gathering eggs would matter for much longer. The storm had brought changes I could feel in my bones—a sense that the peaceful life I'd built was balanced on the edge of a knife, ready to tip into chaos at any moment. Red's tail managed a weak wag as he fell into step beside me, his grey muzzle nearly white now, his brown eyes clouded with the milky film of age but still bright with the affection that had sustained us both through the long years since Alaina's passing. But those eyes held worry too, the kind of tension I'd seen in old soldiers who could smell battle on the wind. We made quite a pair--two old warriors marking time until the final retreat, finding purpose in the smallest of daily rituals. The chicken coop sat nestled against the cottage's eastern wall, positioned to catch the morning warmth of Soli's first light. I'd built it there fifteen years ago when Alaina and I first settled in this quiet corner of Kleinae, back when my hands were steady and my back straight. She'd wanted chickens, said they'd make the place feel more like home after the nomadic years following the war. "A proper farm needs proper animals," she'd insisted, her eyes twinkling with the same mischief that had first caught my attention in a tavern in Aethelwood. The memory brought its familiar pang of loss, dulled by time but never quite absent. I could still see her standing in this very spot, scattering grain to the birds while humming some half-remembered lullaby from her childhood. The chickens had loved her, clustering around her feet like feathered courtiers seeking favor from their queen. Now they barely tolerated me. "Easy there, ladies," I murmured to the hens as they squawked their displeasure at my intrusion. The largest of them--a bad-tempered brown bird I'd named Fury for obvious reasons--fixed me with one beady eye and clucked what could only be described as criticism. My movements were slow, careful--not just from courtesy to the birds, but from necessity. These old hands shook more each morning, a tremor that had started as barely noticeable and grown over the years into something I had to actively compensate for. The wooden latch on the coop door required three attempts to open. My fingers, once capable of the delicate work required to carve spell-runes into sword steel, now struggled with simple tasks. The arthritis was worst in the mornings, before movement and warmth loosened the joints enough for basic function. Inside the coop, the familiar smell of straw and chicken droppings mixed with the sweeter scent of fresh eggs. It was a honest smell, one that spoke of simple work and simpler rewards. Nothing like the acrid smoke and burning metal that had filled my nostrils during the war years. The first egg nearly slipped from my fingers. I caught it against my chest, feeling my heart skip with the small exertion--another reminder that this body was failing in ways both large and small. There was a time when I could have caught a dozen falling eggs without thought, when my reflexes were quick enough to snatch arrows from the air or parry blade strikes too fast for normal eyes to follow. Now I celebrated not dropping breakfast. I placed the precious egg in my worn wicker basket, noting how the simple woven container had darkened with age and use. Alaina had made it herself from reeds gathered along the Orkael River, her nimble fingers weaving the strands with a skill I'd never possessed. Like so many things in our home, it carried her memory in its every fiber. Red lowered himself gingerly to the ground nearby, his old bones protesting the movement with a soft grunt that echoed my own morning symphony of aches. He watched me work with the patient attention of a creature who understood that everything took longer now, that rushing only led to mistakes and spilled breakfasts. His breathing was audible--not quite labored, but carrying an effort that hadn't been there in his younger years. "Remember when you used to chase these birds?" I asked him, moving carefully to the next nest. My lower back sent up its familiar complaint as I bent down, and I had to grip the wooden wall for support. "Alaina would scold you something fierce, but I think she enjoyed watching you play. Said it reminded her of the pups she'd had as a girl." Red's ears perked slightly at Alaina's name, though the movement was less pronounced than it once would have been. His hearing wasn't what it used to be either. Even after ten years, he remembered her, just as I did. Some losses carve themselves too deep to ever truly heal, becoming part of the landscape of the heart. The second egg came easier, the hens having settled into their routine grumbling. Fury had positioned herself where she could continue to glare at me, but the others went about their business with the resigned air of creatures who knew resistance was futile. I moved from nest to nest, each step measured, each reach calculated to avoid the sharp complaint of my lower back. The third nest was the furthest from the door, requiring me to navigate carefully around the birds. My walking stick helped, but some mornings I wondered if it wouldn't be easier to simply stay in bed and let the world continue without me. The thought came more frequently these days--not from despair, but from a bone-deep weariness that seemed to grow heavier with each passing season. But Red depended on me, and I on him. We were each other's anchor to purpose, two old souls keeping each other tethered to life through sheer stubborn loyalty. The fourth egg was smaller than the others, probably from the young hen I'd been nursing back to health after a fox attack. She'd been laying irregularly ever since, but at least she was alive. I'd always had a soft spot for wounded creatures trying to heal--a trait that had served me well in the aftermath of battles, less well in the politics that followed. "Six eggs today," I announced, showing the basket to Red as if he could count and cared about our small triumph. "That's two better than yesterday. The brown hen's finally earning her keep." Red's tail thumped once against the ground--approval, or perhaps just acknowledgment that I'd spoken. After forty years together, the distinction hardly mattered. A distant rumble of thunder made us both look toward the northern sky. Dark clouds gathered on the horizon like an approaching army, their edges lit by flickers of lightning still too far away to hear. Though Sola had yet to fully rise and join her partner in their daily dance, the air already held that electric tension that spoke of a significant storm approaching. My old war wound--a scar along my left thigh where an Ithek blade had found its mark--began to throb with the barometric pressure change. Something about those clouds troubled me. They moved with purpose, following patterns that seemed too deliberate for natural weather. And the color was wrong—not the honest grey-black of normal storm clouds, but something darker, touched with an oily sheen that made my skin crawl. "Looks like we'll have weather tonight," I told Red, who had struggled to his feet with obvious effort. The sight of him fighting his own body's betrayals never failed to twist something in my chest. "Best get the animals secured." * The Choice Crygill Rowetha's first thought wasn't to run—it was Bubbee. She crept closer to the cottage, Izzy trembling in her arms, trying to peer through the windows without being seen. Inside, she could hear voices—Ma and Da talking in those wrong, layered tones that made her skin crawl. But no sign of her little brother. "Bubbee?" she whispered, so quietly the word was barely breath. "Bubbee, where are you?" A small sound from the root cellar behind the cottage made her heart leap. The wooden doors were slightly ajar, and she could see a flash of movement in the darkness below. He was hiding. Smart boy. She started toward the cellar, but Izzy's claws suddenly dug into her arms, the kitten's eyes wide with terror. Following the cat's gaze, Rowetha saw them emerging from other cottages now—neighbors walking the village paths with that same horrible smoothness, all wearing those patient, hungry smiles. Purple mist was beginning to rise from several chimneys. Mrs. Tibble from the bakery moved past her garden gate with mechanical precision, her usual warm greeting replaced by an empty stare. Young Henrik, who'd taught Rowetha to whittle, stood in his doorway with purple light flickering behind his eyes. Even old Grandmother Morra, who'd always moved so slowly with her cane, now glided between the cottages like she was floating just above the ground. More were coming. And if she tried to reach Bubbee now, they'd see her. The hardest choice of her young life tore at her heart. Stay and try to save her brother, or flee and hope she could come back for him later. Every instinct screamed at her to run to the cellar, to grab Bubbee and get them both to safety. But Izzy's terror was real, and the kitten had known about Mr. Klisternok before anyone else. "Stay hidden, Bubbee," she whispered toward the cellar, tears streaming down her face. "Stay hidden until I can come back for you." She couldn't save him if she was caught too. She had to be smart, had to survive, had to find help. Someone, somewhere, must know how to stop this thing that was stealing her family, her friends, her entire world. Then she ran for the old oak grove beyond the village, her heart breaking with every step, Izzy's frightened mewing the only honest sound left in a world gone mad. * The Morning Routine Rawn's cottage We made our way slowly to the small barn where Bessie, my ancient cow, stood waiting for her morning attention. Like everything else on this little farm, she was past her prime but still serving faithfully. Her brown and white hide showed patches of grey that matched the silver in my own hair, and her udder had never quite recovered its former productivity after a difficult calving three years past. I set the egg basket carefully on a fence post--another motion that required conscious effort to execute smoothly--and reached for the milking stool. The three-legged seat was worn smooth by years of use, its surface polished by countless mornings just like this one. The simple act of sitting required a moment's pause to let my knees settle into position. The joints protested the change in angle, sending sharp reminders up my legs that I was no longer the man who could spend days in the saddle without complaint. Red positioned himself nearby, close enough to offer comfort but far enough to avoid a kick from Bessie should she grow restless. "There's a good girl," I murmured, beginning the familiar rhythm of milking. My hands moved slowly but steadily, coaxing streams of warm milk into the wooden bucket. The motion itself was soothing, a meditation of repetition that connected me to countless generations of farmers who had performed this same simple task. But even this had grown harder over the years. My grip wasn't what it once was--fingers that had wielded Vulcanspine with precision now struggled to maintain pressure for extended periods. My shoulders protested the repetitive motion, the right one especially where an old wound had never quite healed properly. The muscles in my forearms burned with fatigue after just a few minutes of work. Bessie shifted restlessly, sensing my discomfort. Animals always knew when their humans were struggling, and she'd grown increasingly patient with my limitations as the years passed. Sometimes I wondered if she understood that we were all growing old together, all slowing down in harmony. The Mindreaver stirred then, as it sometimes did in quiet moments when my guard was down. Whispers of memory, faces of men I'd failed to save, villages that had burned because my fire had raged beyond control. The corruption that had spread through Ithek-touched lands, turning fertile fields to ash and clean water to poison. The weight of command decisions that had saved kingdoms but cost individual lives. I pushed the dark thoughts aside with practiced ease, the same mental discipline that had kept me sane through the war years. Those days were long past. I was a farmer now, a keeper of chickens and cows, a man whose greatest battle was against the daily deterioration of his aging body. The Scorch of Eryndor was dead, buried beneath years of peaceful routine and deliberate obscurity. "Nothing wrong with growing old, is there, Red?" I said, though I wasn't entirely convinced. The words felt like a prayer I wasn't sure I believed. "It's natural. Expected. Better than the alternative, as they say." Red's answering whine suggested he wasn't entirely convinced either. Or perhaps he was simply responding to the note of uncertainty in my voice that I'd tried to hide. With the milking finished, I struggled to my feet, muscles protesting the change in position like rusty hinges forced to move. The bucket felt heavier than it should, and I had to grip it with both hands to ensure I didn't spill our breakfast. The walk back to the cottage would test my already-tired legs, but there was no choice but to manage it. Red watched with patient understanding as I shuffled toward the cottage, moving at the pace my body demanded rather than the speed my mind might prefer. He understood limitation in a way that younger creatures couldn't--understood that strength was finite and had to be parceled out carefully throughout the day. The path from barn to cottage had never seemed longer. Each step was a negotiation between will and flesh, between the desire to move normally and the reality of joints that ground against each other with every motion. Halfway there, I had to pause to catch my breath, setting the bucket down and leaning heavily on my walking stick. The sun had climbed higher now, both Soli and Sola visible in the eastern sky, their combined light warming the morning air and painting the world in shades of gold and silver. On a younger man's day, this would have been the time for heavier work--tending the larger garden, repairing fences, perhaps riding to the village for supplies. For me, it meant rest and recovery from the simple task of gathering eggs and milk. I settled onto the wooden bench outside the cottage door, placing the bucket and basket carefully beside me with the exaggerated care of someone who couldn't afford to waste anything through clumsiness. The bench was another of my own constructions from those early years, built sturdy enough to support two people watching sunsets together. Now it held only me and my accumulated aches. Red joined me with his own careful movements, lowering himself with a sigh that perfectly matched my own feelings. We sat in comfortable silence, two old companions sharing the warmth of morning sun and the satisfaction of small tasks completed. The simple pleasure of sitting still, of not asking our bodies to perform any additional tasks, felt like luxury. "Peaceful, isn't it?" I said, scratching behind Red's ears with fingers that trembled slightly from the morning's exertions. His fur was softer now than it had been in his prime, and thinner, revealing the pink skin beneath in places. "No battles to fight, no impossible choices to make. Just chickens and cows and the slow passage of time." But even as I spoke the words, I wondered if peace was the same as contentment. There were moments--more frequent lately--when I felt like I was simply marking time, waiting for death with the patience of someone who had already lived his allotted span. The thought didn't frighten me, exactly, but it left me feeling oddly hollow, as if I were already fading away by degrees. * The Hiding The oak grove, Crygill Rowetha pressed herself against the massive trunk of the oldest oak, Izzy clutched tight against her chest. From here, she could see the entire village spread below, and what she saw made her stomach turn. Purple mist rose from every cottage now, twisting in the still air like smoke from funeral pyres. The things that had been her neighbors moved through the streets in coordinated patterns, searching. Their movements were too precise, too purposeful—like pieces on a game board controlled by a single mind. They were looking for her. She could feel it, the weight of their attention like cold fingers on her neck. "We have to be quiet, Izzy," she breathed into the kitten's fur. "We have to be so, so quiet." But even as she spoke, she could hear them calling her name. Not with the voices of people who loved her—Ma's warm tone that had sung lullabies, Da's gentle rumble that had told bedtime stories—but with something wearing their voices like masks. "Rowetha," the thing with Ma's face called from the cottage doorway. "Come home, little star. We have wonderful news to share." "Rowetha," echoed the Klisternok-thing from the river path. "Your friend Alyona is asking for you. She's eager to play." More voices joined the chorus—Mrs. Tibble promising fresh honey cakes, Henrik offering to teach her a new carving technique, Grandmother Morra saying she had stories to tell. A symphony of familiar tones twisted into something hungry and wrong. They knew she could hear them. They knew she was close. But they couldn't find her. Not yet. Izzy's purr vibrated against her chest—not the content rumble of a happy cat, but something deeper, more purposeful. The kitten's eyes glowed with faint silver light in the growing darkness, and Rowetha felt a strange warmth spread through her body. Hidden, a voice whispered in her mind—not words, but meaning flowing directly into her thoughts. Protected. Guardian watches. Rowetha didn't understand what was happening, but she trusted Izzy. The kitten had known something was wrong with Mr. Klisternok before anyone else. Maybe she knew how to keep them safe too. Below in the village, the purple-eyed things continued their search, but they passed by the oak grove without stopping, as if it simply wasn't there. Whatever power flowed through Izzy was hiding them both, wrapping them in a cloak of invisibility that even those hungry, seeking eyes couldn't penetrate. As night fell and the village lights turned from warm yellow to sickly purple, Rowetha held her breath and waited for dawn—for a chance to escape, to find help, to somehow save Bubbee and everyone else who'd been stolen by the thing that smiled with Mr. Klisternok's face. * The Approaching Storm Rawn's cottage, evening The storm clouds continued to build throughout the afternoon, and by evening, the first drops of rain had begun to fall. I secured the animals with movements that grew more labored as fatigue accumulated, brought in firewood with careful attention to my failing grip, and prepared for a night of wind and weather. Red settled by the hearth as thunder began to rumble in earnest, his instincts telling him to seek shelter and warmth. Neither of us knew that this would be the last ordinary evening of our old life together. That by morning, everything would have changed in ways I couldn't have imagined. That the peaceful farmer I'd become was about to be transformed back into something I'd thought was dead and buried. My bones are aching--an ache that reaches deep down into the marrow. The second storm of the day approaches, and I can tell it's going to be a whopper. There's an electric charge to the air. The hairs on my arms are standing on end, and the wind is howling. This is only the edge of the storm; the heart has yet to reach my quaint cottage. At my side lies my only companion these many years. As ancient as I, he whines at the maelstrom occurring outside these four walls. "Hush now, we've been through storms before," I said, reaching down to pat his grey head. "If it does take us, at least we go together." Red is all that remains of my family. My wife passed quietly in her sleep some ten years back. Our son passed long before that to a fever. After my service to the Seven Kingdoms during what they called the Sunding War, I had wished for a quiet life. We found an out-of-the-way area with a spot of land and farmed. We had been content. As I stare into the warm, glowing flames of the fireplace, I contemplate my long life. Sitting and remembering seems to be my only pastime nowadays. My life had been adventurous, full of triumphs and failures alike--even devastating loss on occasion. Full of choices that had to be made under the direst of circumstances. What I had been scared of most was the politics. Outside, the storm increased its intensity. Suddenly there came a knock at the door. "Red, is that the door?" I said. "Who would be out in this weather?" Red chuffed in response. Boom! Boom! Boom! "Confounded visitors in this mess," I muttered. Grunting, I rose to my feet and shuffled toward the door. I lifted the bar and turned the knob, and the door swung open. Before me stood a man of average stature with dark features. His gray eyes pierced into mine with an intensity that made me pause. "Yes, young man, what can I do for you?" I said. "So sorry, old master, but may I come out of this rain and find succor?" he asked. "Aye, I imagine you do. Alright, come in," I said. "I won't leave you out in this mess." He stepped inside, and I closed and barred the door behind him. "Sir, this is frightful weather. Thank you for your kindness," he said. "Are you hungry?" I asked. "Yes, sire, thank you." "Alright, let me fetch a bowl." * An Evening's Hospitality Rawn's cottage The stranger and I settled down to cheese, bread, and lamb stew. Red took his customary position at my feet, hoping for a dropped morsel or two. Then the strangest little squeak and squeal escaped Red's old gray backside--it was a right stinker. "Sorry, the dog is old and my only companion," I said apologetically. The stranger laughed. "It's his home. I am but a visitor." I laughed and smiled. I thought I might like this fellow. "What brings you out in this miserable weather?" I asked. "I was passing through when this storm struck. Yours is the only place I saw," he said. "It was providence." "Yes, indeed." We finished our fare, and I offered him some wine. "I'm old and achy--you may take the bed tonight. I've grown accustomed to sleeping in this old rocking chair by the fire to keep my old bones warm." "That is very kind of you. Thank you," he said. "Sleep well," I said. "Sleep well, sire," he responded. It was a tradition Alaina had started, insisting that we had room for one more at our table and a dry corner for anyone in need. She'd possessed an endless capacity for generosity that had shamed me during our early years together, before I learned to accept kindness as strength rather than weakness. Even now, a decade after her passing, I honored her memory by keeping an open door for travelers caught by weather or circumstance. The cottage grew quiet save for the storm raging outside and Red's occasional snore from his spot by the dying fire. I dozed fitfully in my chair, as had become my custom—the bed felt too large, too empty without Alaina's presence, and the chair allowed me to tend the fire through the night when my old bones protested too much to find sleep. But something about this storm felt different. More charged with purpose than mere weather should be. The lightning seemed to follow patterns, and the thunder carried echoes that reminded me unpleasantly of battle-cries from the war. I found myself checking the bar on the door more than once, though what I thought might be seeking entry, I couldn't say. * The Dawn of Change Crygill, the next morning By dawn, the entire village of Crygill would be transformed. Purple mist rose from every chimney, and the things that had once been her neighbors moved through the streets with coordinated purpose, still searching for the one who had escaped their net. But Rowetha remained hidden in the oak grove, wrapped in Izzy's protective magic, watching her world die and praying to gods who seemed very far away that someone, somewhere, would know how to make it right again. She didn't know that fifty miles to the south, that someone was about to be reborn. * The Transformation Begins Rawn's cottage, dawn I awoke to the glare of Unu's double suns pouring their light through my kitchen window. I rose on creaky knees, causing me to groan. I looked and saw I had awakened the stranger. "Care to breakfast with me?" I asked. "That would be great," he said. I tottered over to the fireplace to start preparing a meal. As I moved about the kitchen, I became aware of the stranger watching me with those piercing grey eyes--not in judgment, but with something that felt almost like... understanding. "You move like a soldier," he observed quietly. "Even with age, there's purpose in your steps. Economy of motion." I paused in my stirring of the porridge. Few people noticed such things. "Once upon a time, perhaps. Long ago." "Not so long that it's forgotten." He leaned forward slightly. "The Sunding War?" My hand stilled on the wooden spoon. "How did you--" "The way you positioned your chair last night. Back to the wall, clear view of the door. The sword by the hearth--that's no farmer's tool." His voice was gentle, not probing, as if he were simply stating facts he already knew. "Vulcanspine, if I'm not mistaken." I turned to face him fully, suspicious now. "Who are you?" "Someone who knows what it means to carry the weight of necessary choices," he said simply. "Someone who understands that the hardest battles are fought long after the war ends." There was something in his tone--a depth of weariness that matched my own. I found myself setting down the spoon and really looking at him. His face was unlined, but his eyes... his eyes held the same exhaustion I saw in my own mirror. I fried salted pork along with fresh eggs, broke out some cider and day-old bread. I set the table, and we sat down to eat. I flipped an occasional morsel to Red under the table as the stranger and I ate in companionable silence, enjoying the sounds of a spring morning. Birds were singing, and my old rooster cut loose with a crow. We finished eating, and the stranger stood. "Sir, you have shown a great deal of kindness to me. I wish to repay you. Take this tonic--it will help with the feebleness and aches of age," he said, producing a bottle wrapped in red cloth. "You owe me nothing, young man," I protested. "Rawnae Lupio, you are a good man. You deserve this simple gift," he said. Then he walked to the door, lifted the bar, opened it, and exited the cottage. Red moaned and trudged out the door after him. "Alright, I'm coming," I said to Red's retreating form. * The Mysterious Departure Rawn's cottage I walked out into the early morning glare of Soli and Sola, Unu's double suns. I looked around, but I could not see the stranger anywhere. "Strange—he couldn't have gotten too far. He was a pleasant young man, though," I said to myself. I inhaled the fresh, clean spring air. It smelled of recent rain and wildflowers. The morning bustled with the activities of nature. I looked at my hand, clutching a satiny red cloth wrapped around a bottle with clear liquid inside. Red trudged from around the side of the house. I waddled my way to my favorite oak tree to lean against it. Red and I had sat here many times over the years. We would sit in this spot for hours, watching the comings and goings of the local townsfolk and farmers—most of which were Tiffling folk. I lived in the Kingdom of Kleinae. This road connected Kleinae and Mannesket, so occasionally we would see humans as well. Alaina and I had chosen to live in Kleinae because the Tifflings were respectful and private folk. They never meddled in anyone's business, yet they were kind and friendly. I bent down and settled against the large oak tree. Red took his spot at my side, taking his sweet time to get comfortable. When he finally settled right against my hip, he let out a long sigh. I reached down to scratch his ears. "Well, fellow, another day arrives for us," I said. I started contemplating again, remembering a war that had nearly been the ending of all life on Unu. Remembering hundreds of acts of selfless heroism—men and women who had sacrificed themselves for the good of all. Red groaned. "Yes, old friend. I understand," I said. Red and I were at the stage of life where we were just waiting for the sweet release of death—the end of a full life, our final reprieve. Neither of us was what we once were. We were mere shells of the strength and vigorous bodies we had once possessed. "A tonic to remove the feebleness and aches of age, he said," I mused, examining the bottle. I reached down again, scratching along Red's neck. "You want me to take it, old boy?" Red groaned in response. "Well, if it kills me, that will remove the feebleness and aches of age too," I said with dark humor. Red whined. "Shh, stop, old boy. I was just making an observation." I continued my reflections. My Alaina had been gone now for ten years. I missed her daily. She had been a good, caring woman, though she was never the same after we lost our boy. I had never felt more helpless than when I watched him burn with fever in his bed. He was only ten summers at the time and should have had so many more. I examined the contents of the vial. Not a single thing about it seemed remarkable—clear as water, and when I pulled the cork, odorless. "Well, if it kills me, you'll have the whole place to yourself, Red," I said. Red whined again, then laid his head down and sighed. "Alright, old fellow—bottoms up!" I said, and quaffed the contents of the vial. Red raised his head and whined pitifully. My vision exploded into a rainbow of bright colors, and I felt a pain unlike any I had ever experienced in my life. I felt like my entire body had separated into the tiniest fragments of existence. I was simultaneously freezing and burning up. I was lightning and volcanic eruptions, the mightiest storm and the deepest abyss. I found myself begging to either die or pass out. Finally, a peaceful darkness encompassed me. And so began the transformation that would change everything—the corruption spreading north in places like Crygill, and the power that would be needed to fight it meeting in a moment that would reshape the fate of two worlds. The storm's wake had delivered its gifts: horror to some, hope to others, and change to all. In the oak grove of a dying village, a child and her Guardian cat waited for salvation. By an ancient oak in a peaceful kingdom, an old soldier was reborn as something that might provide it. The real war was about to begin.

Chapter: Ashes of Command The Orkael's mist clung to us like a shroud as we fled down Vaelhold's road—a faint scar through Mannesket's hills. Behind us, Eldhaven's mill burned, and Varkis's horn echoed in the dawn. Kalia's death burned in my gut like a brand. Her blood had seeped into the earth, her eyes gone glassy, a Talon's blade buried deep in her back. The weight of it pressed heavier than even the Lich Emperor's shadow during the Sunding War. I'd led then too—the Scorch of Eryndor, they called me. The General who burned enemy battalions to ash at Vyrn's Pass, sacrificing entire squads to break the Emperor's necromantic grip. Those hard choices still haunted me: leaving the wounded at Dusk Hollow, ordering the pyre at Sylvar's Bridge. We'd won victory, but the screams never faded. Like Alaina's screams. Now Kalia's fall was my failure, and the vial of Klisternok's vapor she'd died protecting—proof of Varkis's Ithek taint—was a weight I'd carry all the way to Theron's court. My side bled where Captain Drenn's Blazeclaw Rend had torn past Amicae's Tide's Caress. My Fire Essence ran weak from Overburn, Vulcanspine's runes dim and fading. Amicae's Mistveil Cloak concealed us as we moved, the Ithek orb and vial secured in her pack, both pulsing with violet light. Rowetha's tale of Crygill—her parents' violet eyes, their flesh oozing and corrupt—would be our blade against House Varkis. Lord Varren's greed and Lady Myrra's spies had funded the Emberwraith's Breach. Drenn's Talons served their coin, Klisternok was their pawn, and Rowetha's truth was their greatest fear. The girl clung to Izzy now, her sobs for Kalia echoing the loss of her grandmother Bubbee. Her courage reminded me of my own boy's spirit. Red limped beside us, his pearly teeth—Aeloria's gift—bared in a silent snarl, Fire Essence flaring around him. Trink buzzed overhead on singed wings, scouting the path ahead. His theft of the orb had proven his worth far beyond his usual swill-soaked jests at the Rusty Crown. Torren led our small band, Kalia's vial hidden in his cloak. His grief wore a soldier's mask, Sunding War scars pulled tight across his face. "Vaelhold's two days ahead," he growled, voice steady despite the graze on his arm. "Theron's steward will hear us, but Varkis patrols are thickening." I nodded, jaw set, using a General's grit to hide the ache. Kalia's pleading eyes haunted me, just like my men at Dusk Hollow—all of them begging for the life I couldn't give. The Mindreaver's whispers swelled in my head: Alaina's oozing face, Rowetha's parents clawing at their corrupted flesh, Kalia's blood mixing with the ash of my war's pyres. Guilt cut sharper than Vulcanspine ever could. "Rawn," Amicae said softly, her cowl pulled low to hide her scars. Fire-fear flickered in her eyes as she stayed near my warmth. "You're too quiet. Kalia... we couldn't save her." Her Aqua Lash coiled ready at her side, the orb's taint making her circlet tingle. "The vial's enough evidence for Theron, combined with Rowetha's testimony. Vaelar's spies—my own kin—will hunt us, but we're close now." I grunted, my side stinging with each step, eyes fixed on Rowetha's small form trudging ahead. "Kalia died for that vial," I said, voice rough, using a General's steel to mask the crack in my composure. "We don't waste her sacrifice. Theron will hear us, or Varkis burns." Inside, I felt like ash. Every death, from Sylvar's Bridge to Kalia, was the cost of my command. The Lich Emperor's skeletal grin, his necrotic fog choking the air at Vyrn's Pass—it had forced my fire to rage, squads left behind to hold the line, their screams the price of our victory. Kalia's sacrifice, like theirs, demanded that I lead forward, not break under the weight. Rowetha's voice trembled as she spoke, Izzy purring at her feet. "She was brave, sir. Like Ma and Da tried to be." Her tale of Crygill—Klisternok's vapor, her parents' Fleshreaver rasps—echoed the truth held in Kalia's vial. Her grief for both Bubbee and Kalia bound us together. "Will Theron save them?" Her wide brown eyes hit me like my own boy's fevered gaze. I knelt despite the burning in my side, keeping my General's mask firm. "We'll try, lass," I said, voice low and steady. "That vial Kalia died for—it's your truth. Theron will listen. Stay strong." Her nod, small but fierce, stirred my resolve. The Scorch's fire flickered within me despite the Overburn. The Mindreaver hissed, conjuring images of Kalia's fall, Alaina's plea, my men's funeral pyres. Burn, or be burned. Trink buzzed back on flickering wings. "Patrol ahead, mates!" he hissed, green eyes sharp with warning. "Varkis Talons, five of them, searching for us. Myrra's coin, I'll bet!" His grin hid the pain of his sister's loss to Varkis, fueling his loyalty to our cause. Like his theft of the orb, it proved the spark of worth beneath his rough exterior. Torren signaled a detour toward a ravine shadowed by pines, and we moved carefully. Red's growl rumbled low, his pearly teeth bared in readiness. Amicae's Tide's Caress eased my wound, though her touch trembled with her own fears. "Rawn, you're pushing too hard," she said, voice edged with steel. "Your fire's weak—don't let Kalia's death spark an Overburn." Her green eyes held mine steadily. Vaelar's threat—her brother's spies—loomed over us, but her resolve mirrored my own command. I gripped Vulcanspine tighter, my young body taut with a General's familiar choice—lead forward, despite the cost. "Form up," I barked, voice steady as Sunding War instincts kicked in. "Torren, flank left. Amicae, shield Rowetha. Trink, keep watch above." Red pressed close to my side, Fire Essence flaring bright, my mirror in battle. We skirted around the patrol, their hawk-etched leathers glinting in the dim light. One Talon's eyes flickered with violet corruption—the taint was spreading west. A twig snapped beneath someone's foot, and a Talon spun toward us, crossbow raised. I lunged forward, Scorchfang Slash igniting along Vulcanspine's blade. The flames were weak but true, severing his arm cleanly, ichor sizzling where it hit the ground. Amicae's Aqua Lash bound another Talon fast, while Torren's blade pierced straight through a third.
