Despite the scars, despite the decades that should have aged her, I knew that face. "Amicae," I breathed. She-elf from old memories, Red's mental voice carried surprise. Pack leader's old pack. She stood frozen, tears streaming down both the scarred and unscarred sides of her face. "You remember." "How could I forget?" The words came out rougher than I'd intended. My mind reeled—memories of the war flooding back. Amicae Draks, seventh daughter of House Draks, Water Weaver extraordinaire. We'd fought side by side during the darkest days of the Sunding War, her water magic complementing my fire. And we'd been more than companions. "But you... you haven't aged a day," I said, staring at her. "And those scars..." "Elven blood runs slow," she whispered. "And these marks are recent. Six moons past." Her hand rose unconsciously to touch the damaged flesh. "But you... you were old, Rawnae. Grey and bent and..." Her voice broke. "I came here to find the source of that magical surge, and instead I find you. Young again. As you were when..." "When we served together in the war," I finished quietly. "When we were both young and foolish and thought we could save the world." Old friend returns, Red settled beside me, his presence a steadying influence as emotions threatened to overwhelm me. Through our bond, I felt his simple acceptance: Pack grows. She nodded, unable to speak for a moment. I looked at her—really looked. The memories came flooding back, clean and sharp despite the Mindreaver's whispers trying to corrupt them. The night before Sylvar's Bridge when I couldn't sleep. Her steady presence when the weight of command threatened to crush me. The way she'd understood the terrible choices I had to make. "I thought you were dead," she said, her voice raw. "After the war ended, you just... vanished. I searched for years, Rawnae. Years. Then I heard rumors of a farmer named Rawn in Kleinae, but by the time I found you..." Understanding hit me like a physical blow. "I was married. To Alaina." She nodded, fresh tears spilling over. "You had a new life. A family. You looked... content. Peaceful. I couldn't... I wouldn't destroy that. So I stayed away." The weight of it nearly drove me to my knees. She'd found me. She'd seen me with Alaina, seen our happiness, and walked away rather than complicate my life. "You watched me grow old," I said, wonder and horror mingling in my voice. "From a distance. Making sure you were safe, that you were happy." She laughed bitterly. "Pathetic, wasn't it? An elf maiden pining after a human who'd moved on with his life." Not pathetic, Red's mental voice carried gentle correction. Loyal. Like pack. "Amicae..." "But now you're young again." She stepped closer, her eyes searching my face. "It's really you. The same eyes, the same stubborn set to your jaw when you're thinking too hard. But how? What happened to you?" The Stranger's Gift "A traveler," I said, still reeling from seeing her again. "During the storm last night. He gave me a potion, said it would help with the aches of age." I gestured helplessly at my transformed body. "It did more than that." Red pressed closer to my leg, and I realized he was trembling slightly. The transformation that had changed me was affecting him too—I could feel it through our new bond, a slow alteration of his very essence. "Magic like this... it's beyond anything I've ever seen. The power required..." She shook her head. "Who was he? This stranger?" "I don't know. Grey eyes, dark hair, average build. Called himself grateful for my hospitality." I paused, remembering. "He knew my full name. Called me 'Rawnae Lupio,' just as you did." Her eyes widened. "He knew who you were? Knew what you'd been?" "Seems so. Though how, I can't fathom." We stood in awkward silence, forty years of separation and unspoken conversations hanging between us like a blade. Red whined softly, pressing against my leg, sensing the tension but offering what comfort he could. Pack leader sad, his mental voice carried concern. She-elf sad too. Why sad when pack reunites? The innocence of the question nearly undid me. How could I explain to my loyal companion the complexity of human emotions, the weight of choices made and unmade? "Young lady," I said finally, falling back on old courtesy to fill the silence, "I find myself famished. Would you care to join me for an early lunch?" She glanced toward my cottage—the home I'd shared with Alaina, where I'd built a life she'd been too noble to interfere with. I saw the pain flicker across her features. "I... I should go. This is too much. Seeing you like this, after all these years..." "Please." The word came out more desperate than I intended. "Stay. We have much to discuss, and I..." I looked down at my alien hands. "I could use a familiar face. Even if it brings complicated memories." Yes, Red's tail thumped once against the ground in agreement. She-elf stays. Pack needs all family together. She hesitated, then nodded slowly. "Very well. But just for a little while." * The Darkening Grove The oak grove, Crygill - Late afternoon The moment came sooner than Rowetha had hoped but later than she'd feared. As the twin suns began their slow descent toward evening, casting longer shadows across the corrupted village below, she felt Izzy stir in her arms with renewed alertness. Time, the kitten's mental voice carried quiet urgency. Barrier weakest now, but bad-things also weakest. Must go. Must be brave. Rowetha's stomach clenched with terror that had nothing to do with hunger, though she was desperately thirsty and had eaten nothing all day. The thought of leaving the grove's protection, of stepping out into the open where those purple-eyed things could see her, made her legs feel like water. But she'd been watching the corrupted villagers' patterns all day, learning their movements. As the light changed, they did indeed seem to grow more sluggish. Their heads turned more slowly, their reactions seemed delayed, and several had begun to sway slightly as if fighting off drowsiness. Bad-things are day-hunters now, Izzy explained. Purple mist makes them strong in bright light, weak when suns are low. Night will change that—then they become different kind of strong. Must run during weakness time. "Which way?" Rowetha whispered, studying the perimeter of watchers. The kitten's eyes blazed silver as she extended her senses, reading the magical currents that flowed through the land. South path. Toward big river. Bad-things fewest there, and water will mask scent-trail. Rowetha nodded, though the thought of crossing the corrupted Orkael made her shudder. But Izzy was right—the corrupted villagers seemed to be avoiding the river, clustering more heavily around the northern and eastern approaches to the grove. Izzy goes first, the kitten continued. Small, fast, harder to see. Will draw attention. Sister-friend waits three heartbeats, then runs different direction. When bad-things chase Izzy, sister-friend circles back toward river path. "No!" Rowetha clutched the kitten tighter. "I won't let you sacrifice yourself for me." Not sacrifice, Izzy's mental voice carried a mixture of affection and exasperation. Clever hunting. Izzy faster than bad-things, can escape easy. Guardian magic protects from corruption-touch. But sister-friend needs head start to reach water safely. The plan made sense, but it still felt like abandonment. How could she let this brave little creature risk herself? Because Guardian protects pack, Izzy answered her unspoken thought. Is what Guardians do. Is what Guardians are for. Before Rowetha could protest further, Izzy was moving. The kitten slipped from her arms with fluid grace and bounded toward the grove's eastern edge, where three corrupted villagers stood watch. As she ran, her fur began to glow with that subtle silver light, making her clearly visible to the watchers. The effect was immediate. Purple eyes snapped to focus on the small, bright shape, and the three corrupted things began moving to intercept. Their movements were still sluggish, but purposeful, and Rowetha heard the Ma-thing's voice calling from across the grove: "There! The little beast! Catch it! Where the pet goes, the child follows!" More corrupted villagers converged on Izzy's position, but the kitten was already dancing away from them, staying just barely out of reach. Her silver glow pulsed in complex patterns, and Rowetha realized it was some kind of Guardian magic—confusing the purple-eyed things, making them focus only on catching her while forgetting about watching for other movement. Now! Izzy's mental voice rang with urgent command. Run, sister-friend! Run toward water! Rowetha ran. Her legs were cramped from sitting motionless all day, and terror made her clumsy, but desperation gave her speed. She burst from the grove's protection and sprinted toward the southern edge of the village, where a narrow path led down to the river. Behind her, she could hear the corrupted villagers crying out as they spotted her—but their voices were confused, distant, as if Izzy's Guardian magic was still affecting their ability to focus. The kitten had bought her precious seconds of confusion. The path to the river seemed endless, though it couldn't have been more than two hundred yards. Every step took her further from the grove's protection and deeper into the corruption's domain. The very air felt thick and oily, and with each breath she could taste the wrongness that had infected her home. But she could also smell water ahead—clean water, flowing fast, carrying away the taint instead of holding it. The Orkael might be darkened by corruption upstream, but here at the village's edge it still ran swift enough to cleanse itself. Almost there, she told herself. Almost safe. That's when she heard footsteps behind her. * Inside the Cottage Rawn's cottage The stew bubbled in the hearth, filling my cottage with a savory aroma that mingled uneasily with the storm's damp musk from last night. I ladled the vegetable-heavy concoction into two wooden bowls, my hands—youthful, unblemished, and still a stranger's—moving with unnerving speed and precision. Across the table, Amicae sat with her cowl partially lowered, revealing half her face while casting shadows over the worst of her scars. Her posture was rigid as a deer scenting a wolf, her gloved hands resting carefully in her lap. Red sprawled at my feet, his old bones creaking as he settled into position, oblivious to the tension coiling tighter than a bowstring but somehow sensing the emotional undercurrents through our new bond. I sat across from my guest and set a bowl before her, the clink of wood on wood sharp in the silence. "I'm Rawn," I said, though we both knew it was a pale echo of the truth. "Eat, lass. It ain't much, but it's hearty." My voice carried a young man's roughness, harsher than I'd intended. Her eyes, hidden beneath the cowl, flicked to the stew, then to me, their glint green as the Eldertree's heart. She didn't touch the spoon. Her hands, gloved in fine leather, remained in her lap, fingers twitching like they yearned for a weapon. "Thank you, sir," she murmured, her voice a melodic whisper like water over riverstones, but edged with caution. "Your hospitality is... familiar." She tilted her head, the cowl shifting, and I caught a glimpse of her jaw—smooth, then marred by a jagged line of puckered flesh. A spark from the hearth flared, its Fire Essence crackling, and she flinched, tugging the fabric tighter, her breath hitching as if burned anew. I looked away, busying myself with my bowl, though my stomach churned more from her pain than hunger. "You kept the cottage simple," she observed, studying the modest furnishings. "Just as you always preferred." "Alaina wanted it homey but not cluttered." Her name felt strange on my tongue with Amicae sitting here. "She had good taste." Good alpha female, Red's mental voice carried a warm memory. Kind hands. Smelled like bread and flowers. Amicae's smile was sad but genuine. "I'm sure she did. You always chose well." The comment hung between us, loaded with meaning I wasn't ready to unpack. "Tell me about her," Amicae said softly. "Your wife. Your family." So I did. I found myself speaking of Alaina—her laugh that could chase away the darkest war-dreams, her gentle way with wounded animals that reminded me why life was worth protecting, her patience with my nightmares when I'd wake screaming about burning villages. Of our son, bright and curious as a magpie, taken too young by fever while I held him helpless in my arms, watching his small body burn with heat I couldn't fight. Red settled deeper into his spot by the fire, his presence a comforting constant as I navigated painful memories. Through our bond, I felt his own grief—he'd loved them too, in his simple, devoted way. But there was also something else: a sense that this reunion with Amicae was necessary, that our small pack was incomplete without her. Amicae listened without jealousy, her expression soft with genuine sympathy for losses that might have caused another woman to feel relief or satisfaction. "She sounds wonderful," Amicae said when I finished. "I'm glad you were happy, Rawnae. Truly." "And you? All these years..." She pulled her cowl lower, shadowing her scars. "I wandered. Served my house when duty called, but mostly wandered. The seventh daughter has few obligations." Her voice turned bitter. "Until six moons ago, when duty found me at the Eldertree." "The scars," I said gently. Her face went distant, pain flickering in her green eyes like candleflame in wind. "Ithek Fire Weavers. They came in the night, speaking of rituals and feeding something called 'the Breach.' They... they didn't just burn my flesh. They burned pieces of my soul." Red's hackles rose slightly, a low growl rumbling in his throat as he sensed the darkness in her memories. Through our bond, I felt his instinctive hatred for anything that would harm a member of our pack. The Mindreaver stirred at her words, showing me twisted visions of her attackers' faces, feeding on her pain like a parasite. "I sought you after your Essence surge shook the air," she finished. "If anyone could face these monsters, it's the Scorch of Eryndor." The old title hit me like a blow. "That man's dead, Amicae. Has been for decades." "Has he?" She leaned forward, firelight playing across her scars. "Because the man I see before me looks remarkably like the young warrior who burned away the Lich Emperor's necromantic fog at Vyrn's Pass." "The Scorch burned villages," I said bluntly. "Innocent people died because I couldn't control it." Protected more than harmed, Red's mental voice carried firm disagreement. Saved pack. Saved all packs. "And now more innocents will die if you don't embrace what you once were." Her green eyes blazed with desperate intensity. "The Ithek taint grows, Rawnae. I've felt it, a rot in the Essence, spreading through the northern villages. They'll burn more than me if we don't stop them." I looked at Vulcanspine where it leaned against the hearth, its runes warming slightly as if responding to my thoughts. The familiar weight called to me, but it also reminded me of all the blood on these hands. Sword remembers, Red's mental voice carried certainty. Sword wants to protect again. "I'm no hero, Amicae. Never was. Just a soldier who learned to burn hot enough to end things quickly." "Then be that soldier again," she whispered. "Not for glory. Not for kingdoms. For the people who'll suffer if we don't act." * River's Edge The Orkael River, Crygill The footsteps behind Rowetha were gaining ground, but they weren't the smooth gliding of the fully corrupted. These steps stumbled and lurched, as if whoever followed was fighting against the purple mist's control. She reached the riverbank as the sun's light grew dimmer, both Soli and Sola sinking toward evening. The Orkael ran dark but swift here, and she could see that the water itself seemed to resist the corruption—purple tendrils of mist recoiled when they touched the flowing current. Water cleanses, she thought desperately. Water always cleanses. "Wait!" The voice behind her was familiar but wrong—young Tam from the blacksmith's shop, but his words came out in a strange harmony of normal speech and something else underneath. "Please, Rowetha! Don't run!" She turned to see Tam stumbling down the path after her, but something was clearly wrong with his transformation. His eyes flickered between their normal brown and the purple glow of corruption, and his movements jerked like a marionette with tangled strings. "Tam?" she whispered. "I... I'm fighting it," he gasped, collapsing to his knees at the water's edge. "The purple stuff, it got in me, but I keep pushing it back. Been hiding in the forge—the fire burns it, hurts it. But it keeps trying to take over." Steam was rising from his skin, and she could see why. Purple veins pulsed under his flesh, but around each vein was a network of tiny burn marks, as if something inside him was literally fighting fire with fire. "The forge-fire," he continued, his voice fluctuating between his own and something alien. "It's not normal fire anymore. Ever since the storm, it burns different. Burns the purple stuff. But I can't stay there forever, and it's getting stronger." Rowetha's heart clenched with hope and terror in equal measure. If Tam could fight the corruption, maybe others could too. Maybe Bubbee, safe in his cellar, could resist whatever tried to change him. "How?" she asked. "How are you fighting it?" "Don't know exactly. But the forge-fire, it's not just heat now. It's... angry. Like it hates the purple stuff as much as I do." His eyes flickered to full purple for a moment, and his voice became layered with harmonics: "Come back to the village, little star. We have such gifts to show you." Then the brown returned, and Tam shook his head violently. "No! Get away from me, Rowetha! I can't hold it much longer!" Behind them, more corrupted villagers were approaching the river path, drawn by the sound of voices. The Ma-thing led them, its purple eyes blazing with frustration as it saw its prey so close to the water. "Cannot cross running water," it called in Ma's stolen voice. "Cannot follow where the current cleanses. But you cannot run forever, little star. And your friend there... he will be ours soon enough." Tam's form was already beginning to change, the brown in his eyes fading as whatever force let him resist finally weakened. "Go!" he gasped. "Cross the water! Find help! Tell them about the forge-fire—tell them some fires can fight back!" Rowetha looked at the dark water, then at Tam's desperate face, then at the approaching crowd of purple-eyed horrors. There was only one choice. She jumped. The Orkael was shockingly cold and deeper than she'd expected. The current caught her immediately, dragging her downstream and away from the village. She fought to keep her head above water, struggling against the powerful flow while behind her the corrupted villagers gathered at the bank, watching her escape with inhuman patience. Good, came Izzy's mental voice, weaker now but still present. Sister-friend crosses threshold. Water protects. Current carries away from danger. "Where are you?" Rowetha gasped between mouthfuls of river water. Safe. Escaped bad-things. Moving toward river downstream. Will meet when current brings sister-friend to slow waters. The river carried her past the village and into wilder country, where overhanging trees created a tunnel of darkness. As the twin suns sank lower, the water grew colder, but it also grew cleaner. The corruption that had tainted the river upstream was being diluted and cleansed by the swift current and natural purification. By the time she managed to drag herself out onto a sandy bank a mile downstream, full darkness was approaching and her lips were blue with cold. But she was alive, she was free, and somewhere in the darkness ahead, a small silver-eyed guardian was waiting for her. Behind her, the purple glow of corrupted Crygill painted the northern sky like a malevolent aurora. Ahead lay the unknown—dangers she couldn't imagine, but also the possibility of finding help, of saving Bubbee, of making the wrongness right again. She wrapped her arms around herself for warmth and began walking south, toward whatever hope the wider world might hold. * The Days That Followed Rawn's cottage Amicae didn't leave. The first morning after our reunion, I woke expecting to find her gone—returned to her wandering, taking our shared history with her. Instead, I found her sitting by the cold hearth, staring into the ashes with an expression I couldn't read. The cottage's crystal windows were beginning their slow transition from night-opacity to the greater transparency that would welcome the double-dawn. Red was curled near her feet, which surprised me. He'd always been polite to visitors but rarely offered such trust so quickly. She smells like old pack, his mental voice explained when he noticed my attention. Safe. Sad, but safe. "Couldn't sleep?" I asked, moving to rekindle the fire. My transformed body made the task effortless, but I still missed the familiar morning ache of old joints. "Too many memories," she said softly. "Being here, seeing you like this... it's like the past forty years were just a dream." I knew the feeling. Having her here made my cottage feel different, charged with echoes of who we'd been. But it also highlighted how foreign this young body felt, how disconnected I was from my own life. "You don't have to stay," I said, though the thought of her leaving made my chest tighten. "I know this is difficult." Should stay, Red's mental voice carried disagreement. Pack stronger together. "Where would I go?" She pulled her cloak tighter around her scarred neck. "I've been searching for purpose since the Eldertree. Maybe this is it—helping an old friend find his way again." Old friend. The careful distance in those words spoke volumes. The Simple Things Gone Wrong That first week was a series of small disasters that made me feel like a stranger in my own life. When I tried to milk Bessie, my grip was too strong, causing the old cow to kick and low in distress. The wooden bucket split in my hands like kindling—a bucket that had been crafted by a Tiffling woodworker using traditional techniques, its wood treated with gentle Earth Essence to make it nearly unbreakable under normal use. Red immediately moved to calm the agitated animal, his presence somehow soothing to all the farm creatures. The transformation affecting him had deepened his natural connection to the living world, making him something like a bridge between human concerns and animal instincts. Through our bond, I felt his understanding: the familiar scent and presence remained the same, even if the hands were different. "Your strength isn't the same," Amicae observed, helping me calm Bessie with gentle Water Essence that flowed in soothing spirals around the cow's legs. "This body has power you're not used to." "Forty years of knowing my limits," I muttered, staring at the destroyed bucket. Its destruction felt like a small violation of the harmony that pervaded everything in Kleinae—the careful balance between human needs and natural systems that made Tiffling communities so remarkably stable. Learning, Red's mental voice carried patient acceptance. Pack leader learning new body. Red learning too. She began teaching me control—not magical techniques, but simple awareness of my transformed physical capabilities. How to judge grip strength, how to move without the careful compensation for age I'd developed over decades. "Remember when you first learned to channel Fire Essence?" she asked as I practiced handling eggs without crushing them. These weren't ordinary eggs—the hens in Kleinae were all touched by Earth magic, producing shells that were both stronger and more nutritious than normal. "You burned through three practice dummies before you learned restraint." "I was twenty then. Young enough to adapt quickly." "You're young now too," she pointed out gently. "Your mind might be old, but your body is learning fresh. Give it time." But time felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Every moment in this stolen flesh felt like a betrayal of Alaina's memory, of my boy's short life. They'd grown old and died while I got to play at youth again. Red seemed to sense my emotional turmoil. He began staying closer, offering wordless comfort through our bond. More remarkably, I noticed changes in him too—his movements were becoming more fluid, his eyes brighter, as if proximity to my transformed state was slowly revitalizing his aging body. Pack leader's fire shares, he explained when I asked about it mentally. Red stronger. Feels good. The Market Incident When market day came, I debated whether to attempt it. Amicae settled the question by offering to accompany me. "Two are less threatening than one mysterious stranger," she said, pulling her cowl up to hide her scars. "And if there's trouble, I can help." Red insisted on coming too, despite his age making the journey taxing in the past. But now he moved with surprising vigor, his step lighter than it had been in years. Protect pack, his mental voice carried determination. Show village Red knows pack leader. The walk to Grenhall took half the usual time, my transformed body covering ground with effortless strides. The road itself was a marvel of Tiffling engineering—not paved with dead stone, but grown from living rock that had been coaxed upward by Earth Weavers generations ago. Grenhall's market square was a testament to Tiffling architectural philosophy—a space that had been coaxed from the living bedrock rather than built upon it. The ground itself formed natural amphitheater-style terraces where vendors could display their wares, while overhead, carefully trained vines created a canopy that provided shade without blocking the essential flow of Essence from above. The square buzzed with typical Tiffling activity until I appeared. Vendors tended stalls filled with Essence-touched goods—preserves that would never spoil thanks to Earth magic, tools that stayed sharp through embedded Air Essence, clothing woven from fibers that adapted to weather changes. Then the whispers started, heads turning, conversations dying into uncomfortable silence. But Red's presence seemed to help. Many of the villagers recognized him, and his obvious comfort with me went a long way toward easing their fears. "That's his dog, sure enough," I heard Marta whisper to another vendor. "Red knows him. Dogs always know." Good observation, Red's mental voice carried satisfaction. Humans trust dog judgment. Holdwick approached us, his weathered face creased with suspicion but less hostile than I'd feared. "You again. And you've brought... company." "My niece," I said quickly, hoping the lie would hold. "She's visiting from... distant parts." Amicae gave a small curtsy, keeping her cowl positioned to hide her scars. "Uncle Rawn speaks of you often, Master Holdwick. Your kindness to him over the years." Her musical voice and gracious manner seemed to ease some of Holdwick's suspicion, but he still studied my face with uncomfortable intensity. "Family resemblance is strong," he admitted grudgingly. "But this is still... unusual." Red chose that moment to perform one of his most endearing tricks—sitting up on his hind legs to beg for attention. It was something he'd done since he was a pup, a gesture so distinctly his that several villagers smiled despite their unease. Good strategy, I thought to him. Pack leader taught Red. Now Red teaches pack leader. Yet even in the ordinary rhythm of trade, something felt wrong. At Marta's stall, a basket of apples gleamed with unnatural luster. When one split under her hand, its flesh was veined black and reeking of rot. She muttered, cursing, and swept it away as if it were nothing more than bad luck. Across the square, a woman spoke of her husband's lingering cough, but as she did, a sulfur tang rose in the air—sharp, acrid, unmistakable to my heightened senses. No one else seemed to notice, yet it clung to me like smoke. The atmosphere remained tense, whispers following us wherever we went. Constable Brewis intercepted us at the edge of the square, his official staff in hand. "A word, if you please." The confrontation played out much as I'd feared—questions I couldn't answer honestly, suspicions I couldn't allay. But throughout the tense conversation, Red remained calm and alert at my side, his presence a visible testimony that I was still the man he'd always known. "Your... niece... she's Elven," Brewis observed. "Half-Elven," Amicae corrected smoothly. "My mother was human. From Mannesket." "And she just happened to arrive when you were... recovering from your illness?" "Family takes care of family," I said firmly. "Surely you understand that, Brewis." Red added his own commentary by moving to sit directly between Brewis and me—not aggressively, but with clear protective intent. Brewis noticed the gesture and seemed to relax slightly. "Your dog's loyal, I'll give you that. Always has been." "Loyalty earned over fifteen years," I said. "Some bonds don't break, no matter what changes come." He didn't look convinced, but couldn't find grounds for official action. We left under a cloud of suspicion that had only deepened with my return. * On the Road South Two days after escaping Crygill Rowetha had never been more than a few miles from her village, and the world beyond Crygill's borders seemed impossibly vast and dangerous. She followed the trade road south toward the larger settlements, staying hidden during the day and traveling mostly at night when fewer people were about. Izzy had found her again the morning after her escape from the river, appearing like a small silver shadow while she huddled in the ruins of an abandoned mill. The kitten was thinner than before, her fur damp and bedraggled, but her eyes still glowed with that protective intelligence. Sister-friend safe, Izzy's mental voice carried exhausted relief. Water-crossing worked. Bad-things cannot follow running water, cannot track scent through flowing current. "Are you hurt?" Rowetha asked, gathering the kitten close. Tired. Guardian magic costly. But regenerates with rest and sunlight. Izzy's purr was weaker than usual, but warming. And sister-friend's presence helps. Pack-bond strengthens Guardian power. They'd been traveling together for two days now, and Rowetha was learning things about the wider world that her sheltered village life had never taught her. The corruption from Crygill wasn't spreading randomly—it followed specific patterns, like water flowing downhill or fire following fuel. Streams and rivers that ran swift and clean seemed to resist the taint, which explained why the corrupted villagers couldn't cross running water. But standing pools, wells, and slow-moving waterways all showed signs of infection—an oily film on their surfaces and a sulfurous smell that made both her and Izzy gag. Plants told their own story. Healthy forests with deep, strong root systems showed resistance to the spreading blight. But fields and meadows, gardens and orchards—anywhere humans had shaped the land and weakened its natural defenses—the corruption took hold more easily. Bad-magic follows human-paths, Izzy explained as they skirted around a farm where purple mist leaked from the barn doors. Roads, trade routes, settlements. Uses human networks to spread faster than natural magic can adapt. That explained why they were seeing more and more refugees on the road. Families fleeing their homes with whatever they could carry, speaking in frightened whispers of neighbors who'd changed overnight, of livestock that had turned violent, of wells that bubbled with purple foam. Most of these refugees ignored a travel-stained girl with a kitten, but some tried to help. A Tiffling trader shared bread and cheese when he saw how thin she was growing. A human merchant's wife gave her a worn but warm cloak when the spring nights proved colder than expected. A group of shepherds warned her away from the northern roads, where they'd seen things moving that weren't quite animal or human anymore. Good people still exist, Izzy observed after a particularly kind family had shared their evening meal with them. Corruption spreads fear, but also brings out protective instincts. Pack-bonds grow stronger when pack is threatened. But not everyone was kind. Some refugees looked at her small size and obvious youth and saw opportunity rather than someone to help. Twice now, groups of desperate people had tried to rob her of what little she carried, assuming a child traveling alone would be easy prey. They'd learned otherwise when Izzy's eyes blazed silver and their would-be attackers found themselves overcome with paralyzing terror—not magical compulsion, but the sudden, bone-deep certainty that they faced something far more dangerous than a little girl and her pet cat. Guardian magic, Izzy explained after the second such incident. Not harm—just truth. Show them what they really face. Most humans afraid of things they cannot understand. Rowetha was beginning to understand that Izzy was far more than just a magical cat. The term "Guardian" clearly meant something specific, something important, but the kitten was reluctant to explain in detail. Time will teach, was all she would say. Sister-friend grows stronger every day. Will understand when ready. What Rowetha did understand was that they were moving toward something important. Not just fleeing the corruption, but being drawn toward some place or person who could help them fight back. South and west, Izzy's mental voice carried certainty whenever Rowetha asked about their destination. Following warm currents in the magic-flow. Something awakens there. Something that burns clean. The third day of their journey brought them to a crossroads where multiple trade routes converged. A large inn called the Golden Mare sat at the intersection, its courtyard bustling with travelers, merchants, and refugees. The building itself was clearly Tiffling-built—grown rather than constructed—with living wood walls and a roof garden that bloomed despite the early spring season. Here, Izzy said suddenly, her mental voice sharp with attention. This place important. Information here. Maybe allies. Rowetha looked at the crowded inn with apprehension. She had no money for a room or even a meal, and her travel-stained appearance would mark her immediately as just another refugee child. Trust, Izzy's mental voice carried gentle encouragement. Guardian magic opens doors when they need opening. Sister-friend has important story to tell. People here need to hear truth about northern corruption. Taking a deep breath for courage, Rowetha walked toward the inn's main entrance, trying to project confidence she didn't feel. Inside, she might find help, information, or even someone who knew how to fight the purple-eyed horrors that had stolen her family. Or she might find more danger than a twelve-year-old girl and her Guardian cat could handle. But staying on the road wouldn't save Bubbee or anyone else who needed saving. Time to be brave. * The Call to Action Rawn's cottage The next morning brought disturbing news, delivered in a way that showcased Kleinae's remarkable communication network. A Tiffling messenger arrived at dawn—young Pip, Brewis's grandson, but he wasn't alone. Perched on his shoulder was a crystal-bright hummingbird whose artificial nature was immediately apparent—a message sprite, one of the living constructs that carried urgent communications throughout the kingdom. The sprite's eyes glowed with stored Essence as it spoke in Brewis's own voice: "Refugees coming down from Crygill. Something's wrong there. People with purple eyes. Sick, they say. Or worse." Red was immediately alert, positioning himself between the messenger and the cottage door. Through our bond, I felt his assessment: Young male. Frightened. Brings bad news. Magic bird carries death-scent. "How many refugees?" Amicae asked, studying the message sprite with professional interest. Pip swallowed hard, clearly uncomfortable with his role as bearer of dire tidings. "Dozen so far. More expected. They're talking about... about family members who changed. Who stopped being people." His voice dropped to a whisper. "The message sprite says Constable's asking anyone with fighting experience to consider helping. Even..." He looked at me uncertainly. "Even people who might not be what they seem." The sprite's stored message continued: "The corruption spreads faster than natural disease. We need all the help we can get." Around us, the cottage itself seemed to respond to the news. The breathing stones in the walls subtly shifted their patterns, as if the dwelling's living elements could sense the wrongness that was approaching. Red's hackles rose as he caught scent of more than just the boy's fear. Through our enhanced bond, I felt his awareness of something deeper—a taint in the very air, corruption carried on winds that had blown over afflicted lands. Corruption smell, his mental voice carried certainty. Evil magic. Old enemy. "Tell Brewis we'll be there by midday," I said firmly. The message sprite's eyes flared brighter as it recorded my response, then took wing with a high, chiming sound that would guide it back to its creator. Pip nodded and ran off, clearly relieved to be away from us and whatever danger we represented. "The corruption has reached Crygill," Amicae said unnecessarily, but her words carried the weight of someone who had seen such taint before. "Then it's time." I reached for Vulcanspine, its familiar weight grounding me. The blade's runes responded to my touch, beginning their slow transition from dormant steel to awakened fire. "Time to see if an old warrior in borrowed flesh can still make a difference." Not borrowed, Red's mental voice carried certainty. Earned. Given for purpose. Pack leader ready. Around us, the cottage continued its morning routines, but there was a subtle shift in its rhythms. The living wood of the walls seemed to pulse with tension. The moss gardens on the roof oriented themselves differently, as if preparing for some fundamental change. Even the Essence flows through the building's magical infrastructure felt altered—still harmonious, but tuned to a martial frequency that hadn't been present since my warrior days. "We'll make a difference," Amicae corrected. "The Scorch of Eryndor and the Tide Walker, together again." I looked at her—scarred but determined, her green eyes blazing with purpose. Then at Red, whose aging body was showing new vitality, whose eyes held intelligence that went beyond normal canine awareness. Finally, I gazed around the cottage that had been my peaceful refuge but was now transforming itself into something like a war council chamber, its living elements preparing for battle. For the first time since my transformation, I felt like I knew exactly who I was supposed to be. "Aye, princess," I said, letting Fire Essence flow through Vulcanspine's runes. "Together." The blade ignited with steady flame, and the cottage's crystal windows immediately adjusted their transparency to contain the light while the breathing stones shifted to absorb any excess heat. Even my home understood that the Scorch of Eryndor had awakened, and it was adapting to serve a warrior once more. Red's approval hummed through our bond, warm and fierce. Whatever power had transformed me, it had also begun awakening something in my oldest friend. We would face this darkness as we always had—as pack, as family, as companions bound by loyalty stronger than any magic. Outside, the twin suns continued their eternal dance across Unu's sky, but their light now revealed a world at war with forces that threatened not just life, but the very nature of existence itself. Our quiet days were over. The fight for Unu's soul was about to begin. And somewhere to the north, a brave young girl and her Guardian companion were also heading toward their own confrontation with the spreading darkness. Though we didn't know it yet, our paths were converging—the old warrior reborn, the child who'd witnessed the corruption's true face, and the ancient powers that would be needed to save them all. The Mindreaver's whispers grew stronger as I contemplated the battles ahead, but Red's loyal presence and Amicae's determined smile pushed back the darkness. Whatever came next, we would face it together. Just as we always had.

Chapter 5: Before the Throne Three weeks before the Fall of Eldhaven The Mill of Sorrow The Gauntlet The ancient mill on the Dorn River's banks sat like a cancer on the landscape, its waterwheel turning with mechanical persistence even as possessed workers shambled through its corridors. Intelligence suggested Klisternok had established it as a distribution point—sending tainted grain to surrounding villages, weakening their spiritual defenses before the Emberwraith arrived to claim them. We'd gained an unexpected ally during our approach—Torren, a grizzled veteran we'd found defending the hamlet of Millhaven from a pack of Fleshreavers. When he saw me fighting, recognition dawned in his scarred face. "General Rawn?" he'd said after we'd burned the last corrupted villager to ash. "Dusk Hollow. You saved what was left of my squad when the Lich Emperor's wraiths overran our position." Another ghost from my past, another reminder of choices made in desperation. "You were just a boy then," I'd replied, remembering the terrified private who'd held the line when older men broke. "Thought my war was over," Torren had said, hefting his notched blade. "But if the Scorch of Eryndor is fighting again, then I'll follow. Same as before." Now, crouched in the pre-dawn mist outside the mill, we prepared for what we hoped would be a simple extraction. How wrong we were. Drenn's horn echoed across the Dorn River valley like the death-cry of some ancient beast, and I knew our mission had just become a war. The captain's reinforcements weren't distant patrols—they were the full Varkis regiment, thirty Talons strong, pouring from the mill's hidden chambers like ants from a kicked nest. "Ambush," I growled, Vulcanspine blazing as power surged through me. "They wanted us to find that vial." Amicae's scarred face went pale beneath her cowl. "A trap within a trap," she whispered, her essence coiling around us in protective tendrils. "They know we're here." The mill's ancient waterwheel groaned to a halt as corrupted workers—their eyes gleaming with the Emberwraith's hunger—shambled into formation. Behind them, Captain Drenn strode forward, his hawk-crested armor gleaming with Ithek runes. "Scorch of Eryndor!" he called, voice carrying across the morning mist. "Lord Varkis sends his regards. You should have stayed buried in your cottage, old man." "Cheerful fellow," Trink muttered, buzzing overhead with a stolen Varkis dagger glinting in his tiny hands. "I'm guessing he's not here for the continental breakfast." The Fire of Eryndor The first wave hit us like a breaking storm. Three Fleshreavers—former mill workers twisted by possession—lunged forward with inhuman speed, their faces still recognizable beneath the corruption. I met them with Scorchfang Slash, Vulcanspine's blade wreathed in flames that burned away their false flesh to reveal the writhing darkness beneath. "Remember what they were!" Amicae shouted, her Aqua Lash binding a fourth creature even as tears tracked down her scarred cheeks. "They were people once!" "They're people still," I replied grimly, driving my blade through the nearest Fleshreaver's heart. My flames didn't just burn—they purified, turning corruption back to clean ash. "That's why we're setting them free." But the real threat wasn't the possessed workers. It was Drenn and his Talons, advancing in perfect formation, their hawk-crested shields locked tight, crossbows aimed at our hearts. These weren't random cultists—they were Mannesket's finest, corrupted by gold and power rather than supernatural taint. "Blazeclaw Formation!" Drenn commanded, and his men's weapons ignited with stolen techniques. "Take the girl alive—Lord Varkis wants to question her about Crygill. Kill the rest." The crossbow bolts came like deadly rain. "Well, this is cozy," Trink called out, diving between the projectiles with insect-like agility. "Nothing like a warm Varkis welcome! Should I tell them we're here about their expired grain warranty?" Water and Fire United Amicae's Surging Aegis rose between us and the volley, crystalline barriers that turned steel points to harmless droplets. But I could see the strain on her face—holding back thirty bolts was pushing her abilities to their limits. "Can't hold them long!" she gasped, sweat beading on her forehead despite the morning chill. "Don't need long," I replied, feeling the old battle-fury rising in my chest. The Mindreaver's whispers grew louder, but I pushed them back with iron discipline. Red bounded forward, his mysterious power blazing around him like a miniature sun. Where those pearly teeth found flesh, Talons screamed and fell, their stolen techniques no match for whatever gift Aeloria had given my old companion. I followed in his wake, Vulcanspine singing as it carved through their formation. Pyroclasm Burst erupted from my blade, sending three Talons flying into the mill's burning wreckage. Emberfall Stance kept me moving, each step leaving smoldering footprints in the earth. But Drenn was ready for me. "Molten Parry!" he roared, his sword meeting mine in a shower of sparks that lit the dawn like falling stars. His technique was brutal but effective—all power, no finesse, the mark of a soldier who'd learned to fight dirty and survive. "You killed her for nothing," I snarled, pressing the attack with Cinderstrike Combo, three precise cuts that should have opened his throat. He caught two on his blade, took the third on his armor. "Kalia was a romantic fool," he spat back, his Blazeclaw Rend scoring my shoulder. "Like you. Believing in honor, in justice. Lord Varkis offers something better—power." The Turning Tide That's when Trink's sabotage paid off. The sprite had been busy while we fought, his tiny hands working at the mill's ancient mechanisms. With a grinding crash that shook the earth, the waterwheel's restraints snapped, sending the massive structure spinning free. Water from the diverted Dorn River came pouring through the mill in a torrent that swept away half the remaining Talons. "Ha!" Trink buzzed triumphantly, dodging a crossbow bolt by inches. "Let's see you swim in armor, you tin-plated bastards! Hope you packed your water wings!" Amicae seized the moment, her hands weaving patterns in the air as she channeled the river's fury. Tidal Surge lifted the floodwater higher, turning it into a weapon that crashed down on our enemies like Unu's own judgment. But the Fleshreavers were still coming, unaffected by the flood, their forms moving through the water like eels. Worse, the mill's destruction had released something else—a thick, oily mist that rose from the broken grain stores, carrying the sweet-sick smell of Klisternok vapor. "The corruption!" Rowetha cried from where Torren guarded her. "It's spreading!" I could feel it too—the wrongness in the air, the way it made my power flicker and dim. This wasn't just a trap; it was a contamination site, designed to corrupt everything it touched. "Well," Trink said, his usual grin slightly strained as he watched the mist rise, "this just keeps getting better. Anyone else miss the good old days when enemies just tried to stab you?" The Scorch Unleashed The Mindreaver's whispers reached a crescendo as the corruption pressed against my mind: Let it take you. Let it make you strong. You've failed them all anyway—Alaina, your boy, Kalia. What's one more failure? But then I looked at Rowetha, saw her clutching Izzy while Torren's blade held back the darkness, and something inside me snapped. Not rage. Not the berserker fury that had made me the Scorch of Eryndor. Purpose. "Amicae!" I shouted over the chaos. "Link with me!" She understood immediately, her Water Essence reaching out to merge with my Fire. The technique was theoretical—opposing forces should destroy each other, but in perfect balance, they could create something greater than either alone. Steam rose around us as our powers merged, not the scalding vapor of conflict but the purifying mist of harmony. Where the steam touched, corruption burned away, leaving only clean air and the sweet scent of rain-washed earth. "Impossible," Drenn breathed, his stolen fire guttering in the face of our combined might. "No," I said, stepping forward as the steam wreathed around me like armor. "Just something you'll never understand." Vulcanspine blazed brighter than ever before, but the flames were different now—silver-white instead of red, water-cooled but losing none of their heat. When I struck, it wasn't with the Scorch's crude brutality but with a precision born of perfect balance. Drenn's blade shattered like glass. Kalia's Sacrifice But victory came at a terrible price. In the chaos of the battle, while we were distracted by Drenn's regiment, Kalia had made her own desperate play. She'd seen the vial of concentrated Klisternok vapor—the physical proof we needed—sitting unguarded in the mill's office. I found her there as the last of the Talons fled, the precious evidence clutched in her cold fingers. Drenn's blade had found her back just as her mission succeeded, the captain's final act of spite before our combined power overwhelmed him. "She knew," Amicae said softly, her cowl pulled back to reveal the full extent of her scars in the morning light. "She knew this might be a trap, but she came anyway." Torren nodded grimly, his own scars from the Sunding War pale lines across his weathered face. "That's what rangers do. We hold the line so others don't have to." Rowetha approached slowly, Izzy purring in her arms. The kitten's Guardian magic had protected her from the worst of the corruption, but I could see the weight of what she'd witnessed in her young eyes. "Will there be more fighting?" she asked, her voice steady despite the tears on her cheeks. "Yes," I said, not softening the truth. She'd seen too much horror to be comforted with lies. "But Kalia's gift will help us end it sooner." Trink settled on my shoulder, his usual manic energy subdued. "She was brave, that one," he said quietly. "Reminded me of my sister—too good for this rotten world, but willing to fight for it anyway." His voice hardened. "Varkis will pay for both of them." The Royal Audience Our desperate flight from the mill led us through Eldhaven's defenses to the royal district, where surviving ranger contacts vouched for us. The palace guards recognized Vulcanspine—and the legend that came with it. The throne room was cold stone and stained glass, filtered sunlight painting fractured banners in gold and crimson. King Theron sat slouched on his throne, maps and scrolls scattered around him like the debris of failed campaigns. When he looked up and saw me, the years collapsed between us. "Rawn..." he breathed, rising slowly from his seat. "By the gods... they said you were dead. Burned in the Emberfall. Lost. Gone." "I was," I said quietly, feeling the weight of Vulcanspine at my side. "But the storm had other plans." He descended the steps with desperate, trembling hope—a man seeing a ghost made flesh. His hand gripped my shoulder, searching for truth in the solid warmth of my transformed body. "You still wear Vulcanspine," he said, voice thick with awe. "Still breathing, and younger than I remember. How...?" "There's little time for stories," I said, though my heart ached at seeing my old friend's aged face. "The taint is in your city, Theron. In your court. House Varkis feeds corruption to entire villages." I produced the vial from my cloak—Klisternok vapor pulsing with sickly light, still warm from Kalia's dying grasp. "This is the proof. But it cost us dearly to obtain." His face went pale as he studied the evidence. "And Varkis?" "His spies move freely through Eldhaven," Amicae said, stepping forward with her cowl pushed back, letting him see her scars. "They watch the rangers, poison the nobles, and fan flames beneath your throne." Theron's gaze drifted to the great window, where smoke still rose from the lower city. "I trusted Varkis too long. I thought his iron grip would hold the realm together. But now I see—he was keeping it still so it could rot beneath." "Then help us cut it out," I said. Tears gleamed in the king's eyes. "You don't know what it means to see you alive, Rawn. When I feel I've failed my people, my court, and the gods themselves—you return, not as a memory, but a sword." He drew himself up with royal determination that reminded me of the young prince I'd once served. "You'll have what help I can give. I'll summon the royal rangers, open the vaults, recall the old guard. And if Varkis resists—he'll face judgment." A page burst in, breathless and wide-eyed. "Your Majesty! The Thorned Fang has been sealed off—black mist rising from the sewers. Strange symbols burn on the stones." Theron's jaw clenched. "Then we move at once." He turned to me. "Don your mantle once more, Rawn. The kingdom still remembers the Blade of Emberfall. Let them know you walk again." "Lovely welcome party," Trink buzzed from where he perched on a chandelier. "Nothing says 'royal hospitality' like demonic fog in the gutters. Should I start charging extra for sewer tours?" Building the Resistance With royal backing and Kalia's hard-won evidence, our mission gained legitimacy and resources. The rangers who'd served with her—men and women who'd seen their brothers turn into monsters, who'd watched villages fall to the possessed—flocked to our banner. Trink, despite his unconventional introduction, proved invaluable with his knowledge of Varkis movements and his skill at acquiring enemy intelligence. The pixie's crude humor masked a sharp mind and burning need for vengeance. "Doing this for my sister," he'd explained when I asked why he risked so much, his usual grin replaced by something harder. "Lost her to their experiments. They turned her into something that weren't her anymore. Something that begged me to kill her." He'd brightened with forced cheer. "But hey, least now I get to return the favor! Nothing says 'family reunion' like a knife in the back!" Torren brought with him knowledge of the kingdom's defenses and a network of contacts among the veteran community. His presence legitimized our cause among the older soldiers who remembered the Sunding War. "Half the men who served under you are still alive," he'd reported during one of our planning sessions. "Most are farmers now, innkeepers, blacksmiths. But they remember. Say the word, and they'll take up arms again." The response was overwhelming. Within days of Theron's proclamation, veterans began arriving at the palace—scarred men and women who'd thought their fighting days were over. They came quietly, without fanfare, but their presence sent ripples through the capital. "Look at them," Trink observed as we watched a group of former sergeants drilling raw recruits in the palace courtyard. "Twenty years older, twice as many aches, but still ready to march into hell if you asked. Inspiring, really. Also slightly terrifying." The Growing Threat As summer turned toward autumn, we discovered the true scope of the threat facing the Seven Kingdoms. The failed ritual in Delfaen hadn't just released two entities—it had unleashed something that fed on human souls, growing stronger with each village it consumed. "Klisternok," Amicae said during one of our planning sessions, studying the Ithek orb Trink had stolen from a Varkis courier. The artifact pulsed with sickly light, showing us glimpses of the entity's spread through the realm. "He's not just creating random chaos—he's guiding the Emberwraith's path. Every soul it takes makes its next possession easier." The orb showed us towns under threat: Millhaven had gone silent three days ago. Thornbridge reported strange mists rising from their wells. Even the border settlements near Aethelwood were sending desperate pleas for aid. "My brother's involved," Amicae admitted quietly, her scarred face pale in the orb's glow. "Vaelar's been sending reports to someone. I can feel his essence signature helping guide the entity's movements." The admission cost her. Family betrayal always cut deepest, and I could see the Mindreaver feeding on her pain, whispering its poison about trust and loss. "Family betrayal cuts deepest," I said, thinking of my own losses—Alaina's death, my boy's fever, the soldiers I'd sacrificed for victory. "But we can't let it paralyze us." We learned that House Varkis wasn't just funding the Acolytes—they were directing the Emberwraith's spread for political gain. Lord Varren's greed and Lady Myrra's spy network had turned a noble house into something monstrous, using an ancient entity to eliminate rival settlements and consolidate power over the survivors. "Smart plan," Trink observed with dark admiration as we studied the corruption's spread on our maps. "Let the spooky monster do all the dirty work, then swoop in to 'save' the survivors. Nothing builds loyalty like gratitude! Course, it's also completely barking mad, but you can't have everything." The pattern was clear: villages with strategic resources or political importance were being systematically corrupted, their populations either possessed or fleeing to territories under Varkis control. It was conquest disguised as catastrophe, and it was working. "How long before they move on Eldhaven?" Theron asked during one of our war councils, his aged face grim in the candlelight. I studied the map, traced the spreading stain of corruption with my finger. "Weeks. Maybe days. They're tightening the noose, cutting off escape routes, isolating the capital." "Then we'd better move fast," Torren said, sharpening his blade with methodical precision. "Because once Eldhaven falls, the rest of the kingdom won't be far behind." Red growled low at my feet, those pearly teeth gleaming in the firelight. Even my old companion could sense what was coming—a final confrontation that would determine the fate of not just Mannesket, but all the Seven Kingdoms. The storm was building, and we stood at its heart, holding only a vial of poisoned vapor and the fading hope that it would be enough to turn the tide. But as Trink would say, with his characteristic blend of optimism and gallows humor: "Well, mates, at least if we all die horribly, we won't have to explain our expense reports to the king!" Some comfort, that.

Document.Title: Scene4
Document.Text: I felt so incredibly energetic. My movements were quick and painless. I was having a tough time coming to grips with the overwhelming changes my body had just gone through. I chopped the vegetables so quickly, it was a delight as I worked faster than I had in years to get a stew made for my guest and me. I glanced at the Elven maiden, who watched me silently prepare the meal. Her cowl hid her face, but not her womanly form. It had been a long time since I’d cared to notice such things. I scowled at myself for doing so now. You’re an old man! What do you think you’re doing? I thought. Soon, the stew was together, needing only to cook for a while. I sat across the table from my guest. “I’m Rawn,” I said. The stew bubbled in the hearth, filling my Kleinae cottage with a savory aroma that mingled uneasily with the storm’s damp musk from last night. I ladled the vegetable-heavy concoction into two wooden bowls, my hands—youthful, unblemished, and still a stranger’s—moving with a speed that unnerved me. Across the table, the Elven maiden sat, her cowl casting shadows over her face, her posture rigid as a deer scenting a wolf. Red sprawled at my feet, his old bones creaking as he sniffed for scraps, oblivious to the tension coiling tighter than a bowstring. I set a bowl before her, the clink of wood on wood sharp in the silence. “Eat, lass,” I said, my voice a young man’s, rougher than I meant. “It ain’t much, but it’s hearty.” Her eyes, hidden beneath the cowl, flicked to the stew, then to me, their glint green as the Eldertree’s heart. She didn’t touch the spoon. Her hands, gloved in fine leather, rested in her lap, fingers twitching like they yearned for a circlet—or a blade. “Thank ye, sir,” she murmured, her voice a melodic whisper, like water over riverstones, but edged with caution. “Your hospitality is… unexpected.” She tilted her head, the cowl shifting, and I caught a glimpse of her jaw, smooth, then marred by a jagged line of puckered flesh. A spark from the hearth flared, its Fire Essence crackling, and she flinched, tugging the fabric tighter, her breath hitching as if burned anew. I looked away, busying myself with my bowl, though my stomach churned more from her fear than hunger. “Storm brought all sorts o’ strays to my door,” I said, aiming for levity. “You’re the prettiest, mind, but don’t tell Red.” The dog huffed, tail thumping. Her lips twitched—a ghost of a smile—but her shoulders stayed hunched, guarding that hidden face. I took a spoonful of stew, the heat grounding me, though my new body thrummed with restless energy. “You said you heard screams,” I ventured, recalling her words under the oak. “Miles off, you claimed. Elven ears, eh? Or somethin’ else?” I kept my tone light, but my eyes searched hers, probing. She’d seen my transformation—multicolored mist spewing from my mouth, old flesh turning young. That wasn’t idle curiosity. Her gloved hand tightened on the table’s edge, knuckles whitening. “Elven ears, aye,” she said, voice low. “And a sensitivity to Essence. Your change was no small thing, sir. It shook the air, like a wound in the world. And… something darker stirs nearby, in villages like Crygill. A taint, like rot in the Essence.” Her gaze sharpened. “What was that potion? No alchemist’s brew could do such a thing.” I grunted, stirring my stew, the memory of cosmic pain—fire, ice, lightning—flashing through me. “A gift from a storm-sodden stranger,” I said, half-truth. “Said it’d ease my aches. Did more’n that, it seems.” I flexed my hand, the skin smooth, unscarred, a mockery of the decades I’d carried. “Didn’t ask for it, mind. Reckon I’d rather’ve stayed old and creaky.” Her head tilted, the cowl slipping to show a lock of silver hair, stark against her shadowed cheek. Her eyes flicked to Vulcanspine, leaning against the hearth, its runes glinting faintly. “You jest,” she said, almost accusingly. “Who’d choose age over youth? Power over frailty?” Her voice trembled, carrying a weight deeper than my flippant words—pain, loss, a mirror’s cruel judgment. I met her eyes, or tried to, through the cowl’s gloom. “An old man who’s seen too much,” I said, quieter. “Youth’s a burden when you’ve naught left to live for.” My thoughts drifted to Alaina, her laughter gone ten years, and my boy, fever-stolen. The Mindreaver’s whispers stirred, conjuring their faces—violet-eyed, flesh splitting, clawing at me as Fleshreavers. I shook it off, focusing on her. “You’re young, lass. What’s got you so skittish?” Her breath caught, and she leaned back, the cowl a shield. “Not so young,” she whispered. “And… I’ve seen my share of scars.” Her gloved hand brushed her hood, a reflex, then dropped. “You’re kind, sir, but you know not who I am. Nor I you… Or so I thought.” Her tone shifted, probing, testing a blade’s edge. I leaned forward, spoon forgotten. “Then speak plain, lass. You’re no mere wanderer, not with that voice, that poise. Aethelwood, I’d wager. A Weaver, too, by your talk of Essence.” Her flinch confirmed it. “I’m just Rawn, a farmer now, whatever I was before. But you saw somethin’ in me out there. What’s your game?” She hesitated, then pushed her bowl aside, untouched. “No game,” she said, voice steadying. “I sought the source of that Essence surge. I found… you.” Her eyes narrowed, lingering on Vulcanspine’s runes, then meeting mine with sudden certainty. “You wielded Fire in the war, didn’t you? Long ago. The Scorch of Eryndor.” Her words were a whisper, but they struck like thunder. I froze, the name of a ghost I’d buried. “That man’s dead,” I growled, guilt clawing up—burning villages, screams of the innocent. “Don’t dig up graves, lass,” Red whined, nudging my knee. I forced a breath, steadying. “How’d you know?” Her gloved hand trembled, but her voice held firm. “Tales of the Sunding War reached Aethelwood. A Fire Bladeweaver who turned battles with Vulcanspine. Few could wield such power.” Her gaze softened, almost pitying. “And fewer still would hide from it, as you do. Yet here you are, reborn by some divine hand.” I scowled, hating her truth. “And you? Hidin’ under that cowl like a thief. What’s your tale, princess?” The title slipped out, half-guess, half-taunt, but her sharp intake of breath told me I’d hit true. She stood abruptly, chair scraping, her cowl slipping to reveal half her face—beautiful, Elven-sharp, but marred by jagged burns snaking from cheek to neck. Her green eyes blazed with shame and fury, a demon laid bare: not just pain, but the world’s judgment. “Do not mock me,” she hissed, voice breaking. “You know nothing of my loss, General.” I rose, hands raised, guilt twisting my gut. “I ain’t mockin’, lass. I’m sorry.” My voice softened, seeing Alaina in her pain, my boy in her defiance. “Your scars ain’t what I see. It’s your strength. You’re here, fightin’ whatever haunts you.” I gestured to the table, the stew. “Sit. Tell me, or don’t. But you’re safe here.” Amicae stood frozen, her breath ragged, then sank back into the chair, tugging her cowl lower. “Safe,” she echoed, bitter. “No one is safe, not with Ithek Acolytes stirring, tainting the Essence in places like Crygill.” Her gaze met mine, raw, unguarded. “I am Amicae, seventh of House Draks. I seek those who burned my soul at the Eldertree. Will you help me, Rawn Mortisimus? Or will you run from your fire again?” The storm’s echo rumbled outside, and the Mindreaver’s whispers swelled, conjuring her scars as my failures, her attackers as my war’s victims. I gripped Vulcanspine’s hilt by the hearth and nodded. “I’ll help, princess. But fire burns all it touches. You sure you want that?” Her smile was grim, a warrior’s edge. “Let it burn.” She paused, her gloved hand trembling, then spoke, voice a whisper that cut deeper than any blade. “You deserve my tale, Rawn, if we’re to walk this path.” I leaned forward, spoon forgotten, Red’s soft whine the only sound save her words. “I’m listenin’,” I said, meeting her eyes through the cowl’s gloom, bracing for the weight of her pain. Amicae’s fingers brushed her cowl, a reflex, then stilled. “Six moons past, I walked the Eldertree’s glade, its Water Essence my solace. I am seventh-born, low in Aethelwood’s line, free to wander where my siblings scheme in court. The Eldertree, it… sang to me, its roots deep as Unu’s heart, feeding my Weaver’s craft.” Her voice softened, then hardened. “That night, they came—Fire Weavers, cloaked in shadow, their blades etched with Ithek runes. I sensed their Essence, foul, like ash in a stream, but I was too late.” Her hand clenched, leather creaking. “They bound me with flames that bit like vipers, laughing as I screamed. Their leader… his eyes gleamed red, not Elven, not human. He spoke of a ritual, of ‘feeding the Breach.’ They tore my circlet, burned my face, my neck—” Her voice broke, a sob swallowed. “They… took more than my skin, Rawn. They stole my soul, left me to die in the glade’s ash.” I gripped the table, knuckles white, seeing Alaina in her pain, my boy in her defiance. The Mindreaver’s whispers swelled, conjuring a vision: Amicae’s attackers, their faces melting into Fleshreavers, their laughter a gurgling rasp. I shook it off, bile rising. “How’d you survive, lass?” I asked, voice rough, guilt clawing at me for every fire I’d ever wielded. Her eyes glistened, but her jaw set. “The Eldertree’s Essence. It poured into me, a tide of life, knitting my wounds enough to crawl away. I hid, shamed, my face a ruin. My court… they’d pity me, or worse, use me. My brother Vaelar—” She stopped, voice catching, a flicker of betrayal in her eyes. “He’s a Fire Weaver, ambitious, whispering with those who court the Ithek. I cannot trust him, not now.” She tugged her cowl, hiding her scars. “I heard tales of a Fire Bladeweaver, the Scorch, who could burn armies. I sought you, Rawn, after your Essence surge shook the air. I thought… if anyone could face these monsters, it’s you.” Her voice dropped, fierce. “They’re tied to the Ithek, to a darkness spreading through Kleinae, tainting the land. I’ll kill them, or die trying. But I need your fire.” The hearth flared, its spark catching my eye, and Amicae flinched, her gloved hand rising as if to shield her scars. I cursed myself, damping the Fire Essence in my blood. “I’m no hero, Amicae,” I said, meeting her gaze. “The Scorch burned villages, innocents, to end a war. My fire’s a curse, same as your scars. But if it’s vengeance you want, I’ll wield it—for you.” Her eyes searched mine, raw with pain but alight with resolve. “Not just vengeance,” she whispered. “The Ithek’s taint grows, Rawn. I’ve felt it, a rot in the Essence, like a storm’s heart. They’ll burn more than me if we don’t stop them.” She leaned forward, voice steadying. “Will you face that darkness with me, Scorch of Eryndor?” The Mindreaver’s whispers hissed, conjuring my boy’s face, flesh oozing, begging me to die. I gripped Vulcanspine’s hilt, its runes warm under my touch. “Aye, princess,” I said, voice grim. “We’ll face it. But don’t expect me to thank Aeloria for draggin’ me back to this fight.” Her lips curved, a faint, broken smile. “Nor I, for surviving.” Outside, the storm’s rumble answered, a warning of the heart we’d hunt.

Chapter 6: Royal Justice Three weeks before the Fall of Eldhaven The Evidence The throne room had never felt so charged with possibility. King Theron sat forward on his throne, studying the vial of Klisternok vapor with the intensity of a man seeing his worst fears confirmed. "This came from Eldhaven's mill?" he asked, his weathered face grave. "From Captain Drenn's own hand," I confirmed, still feeling the ache where his blade had scored my ribs. "Kalia died getting it to us." Beside me, Amicae stood with her cowl lowered, her scars visible in the throne room's filtered light. The weeks since our reunion had changed her—she no longer hid from me, and increasingly, she didn't hide from the world either. "The corruption spreads through Varkis channels," she said, her voice carrying the authority of someone who'd traced the taint to its source. "Lord Varren's coin, Lady Myrra's spy network—they've been funding and protecting Ithek Acolytes for months." Theron rose from his throne, pacing to the great window that overlooked his troubled kingdom. "I trusted them. Varren seemed so concerned with stability, Myrra so devoted to intelligence gathering..." "They were gathering intelligence," I said grimly. "On where to place the next Breaches." A commotion in the antechamber drew our attention. The herald's voice rang out: "Lord Vaelar Draks of Aethelwood, seeking urgent audience!" My blood chilled, but Amicae's face lit with surprised joy. "Vaelar?" She turned to me, hope shining in her green eyes. "My brother—if he's here, it means Aethelwood has finally decided to act against the corruption." The throne room doors swung open, and Vaelar Draks entered with the fluid grace of Elven nobility. Tall and aristocratic, with silver hair and the same green eyes as his sister, he carried himself with the confident bearing of someone accustomed to command. Behind him came two Aethelwood guards, their leaf-green cloaks pristine despite what must have been a long journey. "Your Majesty," Vaelar said, offering a precise bow. "I come bearing grave news and evidence of treachery that reaches into the very heart of your court." Amicae stepped forward, her scarred face radiating happiness. "Brother." Vaelar's expression softened as he saw her, though I caught a flicker of something else—pity? calculation?—before it was replaced by sibling affection. "Amicae. When word reached Aethelwood that you were alive, working with..." His gaze shifted to me, studying my transformed face with keen intelligence. "The legendary Scorch of Eryndor. Father was most interested." "What evidence do you bring?" Theron asked, cutting through the family reunion. Vaelar gestured to his guards, who produced a bound and gagged man in Varkis colors. "Joren Varkis, Lord Varren's own son. We captured him leading Ithek Acolytes toward the Thornwood Grove—one of our most sacred sites." The prisoner struggled against his bonds, his eyes wide with fear. Dark stains on his clothing suggested he'd been in contact with corruption—or worse. "There's more," Vaelar continued, producing a collection of scrolls. "Correspondence between House Varkis and various Ithek cells. Payment schedules, targeting priorities, even plans for corrupting Aethelwood's Essence springs." Theron's face darkened as he examined the documents. "This is... comprehensive. Damning." I studied Vaelar more closely, my war-trained instincts noting details: the way he positioned himself with clear sightlines to all exits, how his hand rested near his sword hilt, the calculating way he observed everyone's reactions. "How did you come by this intelligence?" I asked. Vaelar smiled, and there was something almost predatory in the expression. "Let's say Lord Varren wasn't as careful with his communications as he believed. Aethelwood has many... resources... for gathering information." Amicae beamed at her brother. "I knew you'd come through. When I was attacked at the Eldertree, I thought..." She touched her scars unconsciously. "I thought maybe our house had abandoned me." "Never," Vaelar said firmly, moving to embrace her. "Family protects family, dear sister. Always." Royal Justice Within the hour, King Theron had mobilized his loyal guards. The evidence was overwhelming, the threat too great to ignore. Lord Varren and Lady Myrra were arrested in their quarters, their protests falling on deaf ears as the vials of corruption and incriminating correspondence painted a clear picture of treason. "Twenty years I've known them," Theron said as we watched the prisoners being led away in chains. "Sat at their tables, trusted their counsel. How does corruption run so deep without showing itself?" "Because it wears familiar faces," Amicae said softly, her hand finding mine. "Evil rarely announces itself—it creeps in through trust and comfort." Vaelar nodded sagely. "The corruption of Essence is subtle. It doesn't change someone overnight—it slowly erodes their moral foundation until they justify the unjustifiable." As the immediate crisis passed, Theron called for a celebration. "Tonight, we feast in honor of those who saved the kingdom from shadow. Tomorrow, we begin the work of rooting out every trace of this corruption." The Celebration The great hall buzzed with relief and celebration. Nobles who'd spent months walking on eggshells around Varkis agents now spoke freely, wine flowed like water, and for the first time in ages, hope filled the air. Trink had discovered the royal wine cellars. "Friends! Companions! Fellow heroes!" the dragonfly pixie announced, hovering unsteadily above the high table with a goblet nearly as large as himself. His iridescent vest was already stained with wine, and his speech carried the careful precision of the very drunk trying to appear sober. "Allow me to pro-pro-propound a toast!" he continued, nearly tumbling into a nobleman's soup. "To my dear friends who saved the kingdom! To the magnificent Scorch of Eryndor, whose flames burn so bright they could light a candle in a hurricane!" I buried my face in my hands as Trink gestured grandly with his goblet, splashing wine across several startled courtiers. "And to the beautiful, the talented, the absolutely spectacular Lady Amicae!" Trink spun in midair, nearly colliding with a chandelier. "Whose scars tell tales of heroism, and whose... whose other attributes tell tales of..." He hiccupped. "Where was I going with this?" "Nowhere good," Amicae muttered, though she was fighting a smile. Rowetha giggled from her seat beside me, Izzy purring contentedly in her lap. The girl had been quiet since Kalia's death, but seeing Trink's ridiculous antics was drawing her out of her shell. "And to little Rowetha!" Trink declared, attempting a midair bow that sent him spiraling toward the table. "Bravest little warrior in all the kingdoms! And her faithful cat Izzy, who has the prettiest little white paws I've ever—" "Trink," I warned as the pixie veered dangerously close to Rowetha's plate. "Right, right. Mustn't forget..." Trink straightened with exaggerated dignity. "To our newest ally, the noble and handsome Lord Vaelar!" All eyes turned to where Vaelar sat in conversation with several Elven diplomats. He raised his own goblet graciously, his smile polite but distant. "Such a distinguished gentleman," Trink continued, his voice carrying across the hall. "So very... observant. Notices everything, he does. Every little detail. Every conversation. Every..." The pixie paused, his wine-addled brain seeming to catch on something. "Every secret passage and guard rotation and..." "That's enough, Trink," Vaelar said smoothly, though his smile had grown slightly strained. "Perhaps you should moderate your consumption." "Moderate? MODERATE?" Trink's voice rose to a squeak. "Good sir, we've just saved the kingdom! This calls for IMmoderation! For excess! For..." He attempted another flourish and promptly crashed into a serving platter, emerging covered in pastry crumbs and looking supremely confused. The hall erupted in laughter, and even Theron was chuckling. Red, lying at my feet, raised his head and gave what could only be described as a canine grin. I noticed he'd been more energetic lately, his grey muzzle showing hints of the red-brown coloring of his youth. "Someone should cut him off," Amicae observed, watching Trink attempt to extract himself from the remains of a meat pie. "Someone should, yes," I agreed, making no move to do so. The pixie's antics were providing much-needed levity after weeks of tension and loss. Trink finally freed himself from the pastry debris and launched into an increasingly incoherent song about heroism, true love, and the questionable virtue of various noble houses. His voice was surprisingly melodic when he wasn't hiccupping mid-verse. "He's going to feel that tomorrow," Rowetha observed wisely. "If he remembers any of it," I said. As the evening wore on, I found myself relaxing for the first time in weeks. The evidence against House Varkis was overwhelming, the king was taking decisive action, and we had powerful allies in Aethelwood. For once, it seemed like we might actually be winning. A Moment Alone Later, as the celebration wound down, Amicae and I found ourselves on one of the palace's balconies, looking out over Eldhaven's twinkling lights. The night air was cool against my skin, carrying the scent of jasmine from the royal gardens. "Hard to believe it's over," she said softly, leaning against the stone railing. "Not over," I corrected.
