The parchment itself seemed wrong somehow, too pale and smooth, with text that appeared to shift slightly when viewed peripherally. "These were taken from captured Ithek command centers," Vaelar explained, though something about his casual access to such materials should have raised immediate questions. "They reveal the true scope of what we're facing." The papers showed invasion plans that spanned multiple continents, coordination between supernatural entities that suggested vast intelligence networks, and timelines that indicated decades of preparation. The scale was staggering—far beyond anything Blackwater's mining operations could have supported. "The Blackwater conspiracy was significant," Vaelar continued, his voice taking on a different quality that I couldn't quite identify, "but it was also expendable. A test of human defenses, a probe to identify weaknesses before the real assault begins." King Theron's face grew pale as he studied the documents, his hands trembling slightly as he turned pages that detailed the systematic conquest of human civilization. "You're saying the real invasion is yet to come?" "I'm saying it's already begun," Vaelar replied with sudden intensity. "What we've seen so far is merely reconnaissance in force. The true assault will come when the barriers between realms have been sufficiently weakened by preliminary operations." The implications were staggering. Everything we'd fought for, everyone we'd saved, might be meaningless against forces of this magnitude. But something about the revelation felt orchestrated, too perfectly timed to destroy our sense of accomplishment. "How long do we have?" I asked, my military instincts engaging with the strategic problem despite my growing unease. "Not long," Vaelar replied, and now I could identify what had changed in his voice—it carried less diplomatic deference and more absolute authority. "The ritual that will complete the invasion requires specific conditions—astronomical alignments, magical convergences, and..." He paused meaningfully. "Appropriate sacrifices." "What kind of sacrifices?" Amicae asked, though her voice suggested she already feared the answer. Vaelar's expression became grave, but there was something behind his eyes now that hadn't been there before—a kind of anticipation that didn't match his words. "A kingdom's ruler, willingly or otherwise. A legendary warrior whose Fire Essence can breach dimensional barriers. A Guardian-bearer whose power can anchor the transformation." His gaze settled on each of us in turn as he spoke, and I felt a chill run down my spine as I realized what he was describing wasn't theoretical—it was a shopping list. "You're talking about us," Rowetha whispered, her young voice carrying dawning horror. "I'm talking about the components the ritual requires," Vaelar corrected with what might have been gentle patience or cold calculation. "Which is why it's so important that we act quickly to disrupt their plans before they can gather the necessary elements." But something in his tone had fundamentally changed. The diplomatic warmth was still there, but underneath it was something that reminded me of the corrupt nobles I'd known during the war—the kind who spoke of necessary sacrifices and acceptable losses with the same casual tone used for discussing the weather. The Mask Falls "There's something else," Vaelar continued, and now his voice carried an entirely different quality—still cultured, still intelligent, but stripped of the deferential courtesy that had characterized all our previous interactions. "Something I haven't shared with you about how I've been gathering this intelligence." He stood and moved to the window, looking out at the peaceful courtyard below with an expression that had shifted from diplomatic concern to something much more calculating. When he turned back to face us, I saw that his diplomatic mask had been completely discarded. "You see, effective intelligence gathering requires... intimate knowledge of your enemies. Their methods, their motivations, their ultimate goals." His smile was different now—sharper, colder, carrying implications that made my hand drift instinctively toward Vulcanspine's hilt. "What are you saying?" King Theron asked, though something in his voice suggested he was beginning to understand that the ground had shifted beneath us all. Vaelar's laugh was cultured, almost musical, but it carried no warmth whatsoever. "I'm saying that I've been working with certain Ithek cells for months. Infiltrating them, gaining their trust, learning their methods." He paused, savoring the moment. "How else does one gather such detailed intelligence about invasion plans and ritual requirements?" "Infiltrating?" Amicae's voice carried dawning horror as the implications hit her. "You mean as a spy?" "I mean as a collaborator," Vaelar said with casual precision that hit like a physical blow. "The distinction is largely semantic at this point." Red growled low in his throat, his hackles rising as some primal instinct recognized the threat that had been hiding in plain sight. Through our bond, I felt his instinctive hatred for the corruption that had finally revealed itself in our midst. "You're one of them," I said quietly, my hand completing its movement to Vulcanspine's hilt. "I'm evolved," Vaelar corrected with what might have been pride. "The entities beyond the Breach showed me truths that go far beyond mortal comprehension. This world is ending whether we resist or not. The only choice is whether we guide that transformation or are consumed by it." The True Conspiracy As Vaelar spoke, the scope of his deception began to crystallize with horrifying clarity. Every convenient coincidence, every perfectly timed solution, every piece of evidence that had seemed too good to be true—it had all been orchestrated by someone with access to both supernatural intelligence and kingdom resources. "You arranged all of this," Amicae whispered, her scarred face pale with betrayal. "The evidence against Blackwater, the border attacks, the bureaucratic delays—" "Of course," Vaelar said with what appeared to be genuine pride in his accomplishment. "Though I must give credit where it's due—Blackwater truly was involved in questionable mining ventures, making them perfect scapegoats. And the Order of the Silver Dawn's bureaucratic inefficiency required no manipulation whatsoever." He gestured toward the maps that still showed Blackwater facilities and corruption sites. "The beauty of the plan was its foundation in partial truth. Blackwater was selling mining equipment to remote locations. They were financially desperate. They were dealing with mysterious partners who paid in advance and asked few questions." "But those mysterious partners were you," I realized. "Varkis agents, to be precise. We needed legitimate supply chains for our operations, and Blackwater provided exactly that—transportation, equipment, and plausible explanations for our presence in remote areas." His smile was coldly satisfied. "They never knew what they were really facilitating, which made their confusion and fear entirely genuine when questioned." The elegance of it was disturbing. Vaelar had created a conspiracy that implicated innocent people through their own legitimate but misguided business decisions, then positioned himself as the heroic investigator who exposed it. "And the Ithek border attacks?" Torren asked, his ranger's mind grasping the tactical implications. "Coordinated distractions," Vaelar confirmed with casual indifference to the lives lost. "Every time you drew close to inconvenient truths, a convenient crisis emerged that required immediate attention elsewhere. The Ithek proved surprisingly... cooperative in their timing." "You murdered people," Rowetha said, her young voice breaking with the weight of understanding. "All those soldiers, all those villagers—they died just to keep us from asking the right questions." "They died to serve a larger purpose," Vaelar replied with chilling matter-of-factness. "Transformation requires sacrifice, child. The old world cannot birth the new without considerable pain." The Bureaucratic Tool The full scope of his manipulation became clear as he continued his revelation, each detail adding to the picture of systematic deception that had fooled an entire kingdom. "The Order of the Silver Dawn required no manipulation," he said with something approaching fond amusement. "Their procedures were so perfectly designed to prevent rapid response that I occasionally wondered if they were secret allies. Commander Aldric's insistence on proper documentation actually accelerated our timeline by weeks." "You used their incompetence," I said with growing understanding. "I exploited their dedication to process over results," Vaelar corrected. "Every time evidence emerged that might threaten our operations, the Order's requirements for verification, documentation, and approval created delays that gave us time to complete our work and eliminate witnesses." He produced a scroll that appeared to contain detailed procedural flowcharts. "Did you know that investigating a noble house requires seventeen separate forms, each approved by different departments? Or that emergency military deployment requires committee approval that can take weeks to arrange?" The documentation was genuine—apparently Vaelar had studied the kingdom's bureaucracy with the same thoroughness he'd applied to supernatural invasion planning. "Furthermore," he continued, "the Order's focus on supernatural manifestations rather than mundane logistics meant they investigated corruption sites after they appeared while ignoring the supply chains that created them. They were literally designed to always arrive too late." "And when they did investigate," Amicae said with dawning comprehension, "you were there to help guide their attention toward convenient targets." "Blackwater was genuinely involved in our supply operations," Vaelar confirmed. "Providing evidence of their participation required no fabrication—merely selective presentation of facts. The Order's investigators found exactly what I needed them to find because that evidence actually existed." The manipulation was comprehensive and elegant. Vaelar had created situations where well-meaning people uncovered genuine evidence that pointed toward conclusions he wanted them to reach. The Border Coordination "The frontier attacks were perhaps my finest work," Vaelar continued with the pride of an artist discussing his masterpiece. "Ithek forces are surprisingly responsive to proper coordination when someone with appropriate authority provides strategic guidance." "You commanded them," I said, the final pieces falling into place. "I guided them," he corrected with apparent precision about the distinction. "Ithek entities respond to strength and intelligence rather than traditional military hierarchy. Demonstrate superior tactical planning, and they'll follow your leadership quite enthusiastically." He moved to the window again, gazing out at the peaceful courtyard where royal guards maintained their routines, unaware that their kingdom's trusted ally was its greatest enemy. "The timing was crucial," he explained conversationally. "Every major revelation about our operations had to be accompanied by immediate crises that demanded attention elsewhere. Border attacks, supply shortages, bureaucratic emergencies—anything that would split focus and prevent thorough investigation." "General Morrison's requests for reinforcement," Torren realized. "Those were real battles, real casualties." "Completely real," Vaelar confirmed with chilling indifference. "The frontier garrisons fought bravely against coordinated Ithek assaults. Their sacrifice served multiple purposes—eliminating potential witnesses, testing Ithek tactical capabilities, and creating justification for deploying Varkis forces to positions where they could be useful for future operations." The calculation was breathtaking in its callousness. Vaelar had orchestrated real battles with real casualties to serve as cover for political manipulation. "And when House Varkis forces deployed to 'reinforce' the frontier," I said, "they were actually coordinating with the attackers." "They were ensuring that Ithek operations proceeded according to schedule while appearing to defend against them," Vaelar clarified. "A delicate balance, but quite achievable with proper planning and communication." The Royal Betrayal King Theron had been listening to this revelation with growing horror, his face cycling through disbelief, rage, and finally a kind of numb shock that spoke of a worldview crumbling in real time. "You used my kingdom's resources," he said quietly, his voice carrying the particular hurt of someone betrayed by trusted allies. "My intelligence networks, my military communications, my supply lines—all to facilitate invasion by our enemies." "I used inefficient systems more effectively than their legitimate operators," Vaelar replied with cold precision. "Your kingdom's resources were wasted on bureaucratic procedures and political theater. I directed them toward purposes that actually accomplished strategic objectives." "Strategic objectives like destroying human civilization?" "Like evolving human civilization beyond its current limitations," Vaelar corrected with the patience of someone explaining obvious truths to a slow student. "The barriers between realms are weakening regardless of our actions. We can either guide that transformation or be overwhelmed by it." He turned from the window to face the king directly, his expression carrying absolute conviction. "Your choice, Your Majesty, is not between transformation and stability. Your choice is between controlled evolution and chaotic destruction." "That's not your choice to make," Theron said with quiet dignity. "Isn't it?" Vaelar asked with genuine curiosity. "You've been making choices for your subjects for decades—what they can own, where they can travel, how they can worship. How is guiding their evolution toward immortal perfection any different from taxation or military conscription?" The casual equation of genocide with governance revealed the depth of his moral corruption—or evolution, as he might phrase it. The Attack That's when he moved. The assault came without warning, but not without purpose. Corrupted fire erupted from Vaelar's hands—not the clean flame of normal Fire Essence, but something wrong, tainted, shot through with veins of sickly green light that hurt to look at directly. But he didn't attack me or Amicae or any of the obvious threats. Instead, the corrupted flame lashed out at the one target whose death would eliminate the kingdom's leadership and complete a crucial component of his ritual requirements. King Theron. The tainted fire took the king in the chest, burning through royal robes and flesh with equal ease. Theron's scream was cut short as corruption spread from the wound, turning his blood to poison and his breath to vapor. "NO!" I lunged forward, Vulcanspine blazing with clean fire as I tried to reach the king, but I was too far away. Theron collapsed, his body convulsing as Ithek taint consumed him from within. "Necessary," Vaelar said with the same tone he might use to comment on the weather. "He would never have understood the larger purpose. This way, his death serves the ritual that will remake this world into something worthy of survival." Amicae's Aqua Lash struck him with the force of a tidal wave, but he dispersed into smoke and shadow, reforming across the room with an expression of mild annoyance rather than concern. "Really, Princess. Such dramatics. After everything I've shown you, you still cling to the illusion that this world can be saved in its current form?" "You murdered the king!" she screamed, tears streaming down her scarred face as the reality of complete betrayal hit her. "I eliminated an obstacle," Vaelar replied with chilling indifference. "The ritual requires a ruler's willing sacrifice, and while his cooperation would have been preferable, his resistance simply meant we had to proceed without his consent." The Perfect Frame The study doors burst open as royal guards flooded in, weapons drawn, taking in the impossible scene: their king's smoking corpse, Vaelar standing calm among flickering flames, and our group with weapons drawn in what appeared to be the aftermath of regicide. "Seize them!" shouted Captain Blackwood, his voice carrying absolute authority born of decades of loyal service. "They've murdered the king!" "Thank the gods you arrived in time," Vaelar said, his appearance shifting back to that of the diplomatic ally we'd known, the corrupted flames disappearing as if they'd never existed. "They revealed themselves as deep-cover Ithek agents when His Majesty discovered their true nature." It was brilliantly executed. We were foreigners with known connections to supernatural forces and access to the king during his final moments. Vaelar was a trusted ally whose investigation had saved the kingdom from one conspiracy—why wouldn't he be believed when he exposed another? "That's not true!" Rowetha cried, but her young voice was lost in the clatter of crossbows being readied and orders being shouted. "The evidence is overwhelming," Vaelar continued with perfect sorrow, his diplomatic mask sliding back into place with practiced ease. "The corruption ran deeper than anyone suspected. Even the legendary Scorch of Eryndor was turned to shadow, using his reputation to gain access to our beloved king." The guards began advancing with the mechanical precision of soldiers following orders that made perfect sense given the evidence before them. In seconds we'd be overwhelmed, captured, executed for regicide while the true architect of the kingdom's destruction assumed power. "The ritual components are nearly complete," Vaelar added quietly, just loud enough for us to hear but not the guards. "A ruler's death—achieved. A legendary warrior, a Guardian-bearer, and a Water Weaver of royal blood—all conveniently gathered for collection." That's when Trink moved. The Sacrifice The dragonfly pixie had been silent through the confrontation, hovering near the window, apparently forgotten by everyone present. Perhaps that's why none of them anticipated his sacrifice. "You're not taking the little one," he said quietly, his voice carrying absolute determination despite his small size. Vaelar turned, distracted by the unexpected intervention. "What—" Trink launched himself like a tiny missile, buzzing straight at the traitor's face with speed that blurred his small form. His tiny hands clawed at Vaelar's eyes while his voice rose in desperate command. "Run! Take Rowetha and run! Don't let this monster complete his ritual!" Vaelar swatted at him with renewed corrupted fire, but Trink was too quick, too determined, too willing to trade his life for ours. He dodged the flames and drove his small form into Vaelar's throat with suicidal courage. "Annoying pest!" Vaelar snarled, finally catching the pixie in hands that burned with tainted magic. "Not a pest," Trink gasped, his small body already smoking from contact with corrupted power. "Just someone who knows what matters. Family. Friendship. People worth dying for." His eyes found Rowetha across the room, blazing with fierce love despite the agony consuming him. "Draw beautiful pictures of the world we saved, little artist. Remember us as we really were, not as monsters like him." Vaelar's grip tightened, and Trink's light went out forever. Fighting Retreat "TRINK!" Rowetha's scream pierced the air, but I was already moving, scooping her up while Vulcanspine carved through the nearest guards with desperate precision. Amicae's magic exploded outward, grief and rage giving her power terrible focus. Water barriers rose around us while her attacks sent soldiers flying like leaves before a hurricane. Red leaped at the guards with strength that belied his apparent age, his enhanced jaws and Fire Essence-touched claws making him a deadly opponent despite his size. "The window!" I shouted, seeing our only escape from what had become a slaughter. We crashed through the casement in a shower of glass and desperation, my Fire Essence cushioning our fall to the garden below while above us, Vaelar's voice rose in theatrical anguish: "Murderers! Traitors! They've killed our beloved king and fled like the cowards they are! Sound the alarm! Let no one aid these enemies of the realm!" The bells of Eldhaven began tolling news of royal death while guards poured from every building. The city that had celebrated us as heroes now hunted us as the worst of criminals, and the man responsible for both transformations watched from the broken window with satisfaction. We ran through streets that echoed with pursuit, following Red's enhanced senses toward the relative safety of the sewers. Behind us, torches and magical lights spread through the city as our hunters coordinated their search with military efficiency. Underground Networks The sewers beneath Eldhaven proved to be more extensive than any of us had expected, but even in the foul darkness, we could hear the sounds of systematic search above. Vaelar's frame was perfect—we were foreign agents who had murdered the king and fled, while he was the loyal ally who had exposed our treachery. "This way," Amicae gasped, leading us toward passages that connected to older tunnel systems. "There are routes that predate the current city—the guards won't know all of them." We plunged deeper into the underground networks that had served smugglers and refugees for generations, following paths that led away from our former sanctuary. The stench was overwhelming, but it was freedom—putrid, desperate freedom that beat execution. When we finally emerged into countryside hours later, we were exhausted, filthy, and branded as the worst traitors in the kingdom's history. The realm we'd tried to save now wanted us dead, the allies we'd trusted had betrayed us, and our friends were gone. "What now?" Amicae asked, her scarred face streaked with tears and grime. I looked at our shattered group—a heartbroken girl clutching a Guardian cat, a dog whose loyalty transcended nature itself, and the woman I loved despite the world collapsing around us. "Now we fight from the shadows," I said quietly, my voice carrying the weight of command I'd hoped never to bear again. "We expose Vaelar's true nature, we stop whatever ritual he's planning, and we save the kingdom whether it wants saving or not." "How?" Rowetha asked through her tears for Trink. "Everyone thinks we're the villains now. Everyone believes his lies." "Then we prove them wrong," I said simply. "We gather evidence, we find allies who'll listen to truth, and we stop the real enemy before his ritual succeeds." Red barked once—young, strong, and ready for whatever came next. Through our bond, I felt his simple understanding: the pack was what mattered, and the pack would survive. "Together," Amicae said, taking my hand. "Together," I agreed. Behind us, Eldhaven burned with search parties and false accusations. Ahead lay uncertainty, danger, and the desperate hope that truth would eventually triumph over even perfect lies. But we had each other, we had purpose, and we had Trink's sacrifice to honor. Sometimes, in the darkest hours, that was enough to kindle hope. Sometimes it had to be. The real war was just beginning, and now we knew exactly who our enemy was.

Document.Title: Scene1
Document.Text: Chapter 3: The Weight of Youth The storm’s damp musk lingered in my farmhouse, seeping into the warped boards like a curse that wouldn’t quit. Night had settled over Kleinae, the hearth’s embers casting a feeble glow across the room where Amicae sat, her cowl a shadow sharper than Vulcanspine. Red, my old hound, snored by the fire, his tail twitching as if chasing ghosts of better days. I leaned against the table, my hands—smooth, strong, not mine—flexing with a vigor that mocked my ninety years. The potion’s fire still burned in my veins, a gift from Aeloria I hadn’t asked for, turning my creaky bones to a lad’s frame. I felt like a thief in my own skin, every quick step a betrayal of Alaina’s grave, my boy’s fevered end. Amicae’s tale, spilled over uneaten stew, hung heavy. Six moons back, Ithek Acolytes—Fire Weavers with red-gleaming eyes—had burned her at the Eldertree, scarring her face and soul, their ritual babble of a “Breach” tied to some darkness tainting Kleinae. Her brother Vaelar, a Fire Weaver with court ambitions, might be tangled with them, a betrayal she couldn’t voice fully. She’d sought me, the Scorch of Eryndor, after my rebirth’s Essence surge shook the air, begging my fire to hunt her attackers. I’d agreed, my guilt seeing Alaina in her pain, but the Mindreaver’s whispers—violet-eyed visions of my family as Fleshreavers—gnawed at my resolve. I paced, boots scuffing the floor, my new body restless. “This youth’s a jest,” I growled, half to myself. “Aeloria’s got a cruel sense o’ humor, draggin’ me back to fight when I’d rather rest in dirt.” I glanced at Amicae, her green eyes catching the firelight, wary but steady. “You’re sure these Ithek bastards are worth chasin’? They’re not just your demons, princess—they’re stirrin’ somethin’ bigger.” Amicae’s gloved hand brushed her cowl, hiding her scars. “Bigger than you know,” she said, voice a melodic blade. “Their taint’s in the Essence, Rawn—a rot I felt in Aethelwood, now here. Villages fall silent, their folk… changed.” She leaned forward, her circlet glinting under the cowl. “The Eldertree’s power could trace them, but it’s a week’s trek, and Vaelar’s spies may watch. We need a plan, not just your fire.” I snorted, stopping by Vulcanspine, propped against the wall, its runes dull but warm. “Plan? I’m a farmer now, lass, not a general. Sunding War’s long gone, and my plans burned with it.” Guilt clawed up—villages razed, screams I’d ordered. The Mindreaver’s hiss conjured them, faces oozing, pleading. I shook it off, focusing on her. “What’s your next step, then? Storm Aethelwood and gut Vaelar?” Her flinch was sharp, her hand rising as if to shield her scars. “Not Vaelar,” she said, voice tight. “Not yet. He’s my blood, but… I don’t trust him. The Ithek Acolytes first—their leader, the red-eyed one. He spoke of a Breach, a gate to darkness. If we find him, we find their heart.” Her eyes met mine, fierce. “You burned armies, Scorch. Can you burn a cult?” I gripped Vulcanspine’s hilt, its weight grounding me, but the hearth’s spark flared, and Amicae recoiled, her breath hitching. Her fear of fire—my fire—cut deeper than her scars. “I can burn ‘em,” I said, softer. “But you’ll need to face my flames, princess. Can you do that?” She straightened, jaw set. “For vengeance, aye. And for Eryndor, if the taint spreads.” Her voice dropped, a confession. “I’m not whole, Rawn. The Eldertree saved me, but every mirror’s a wound. Help me end this, and I’ll face any fire.” I nodded, respect stirring despite my death wish. “We’ll need supplies, then. Food, cloaks—my farm’s got enough for a trek. Maybe hit the Eldertree, like you said, if we dodge Vaelar’s eyes.” I paused, the Mindreaver’s whispers hissing of Fleshreavers in Kleinae. “And if this taint’s as close as you say, we might meet trouble sooner.” Amicae’s eyes narrowed. “Trouble’s already here. I felt it last night, a ripple in the Essence, not just your rebirth. Something’s wrong in Kleinae, Rawn. We can’t stay long.” I was about to reply when Red’s hackles rose, a low growl rumbling from his throat. The storm’s wind howled, rattling the shutters, and a frantic knock pounded the door. I drew Vulcanspine, its runes flaring, and Amicae stood, her Aqua Lash coiling in her hand, water dripping from nowhere. “Who’s there?” I barked, stepping to the door, heart racing in this cursed young chest. A girl’s voice, high and trembling, answered. “Please, sir, help! They’re changin’—their eyes, they’re wrong!” I flung the door open, Vulcanspine ready, and a Tiffling lass, no older than twelve, stood shivering, clutching a raggedy doll. Her brown eyes were wide, tear-streaked, a black cat with white socks hissing at her feet. The storm’s rain soaked her, but her fear was deeper, raw as Amicae’s scars. Who’re you, girl?” I demanded, lowering my blade but not my guard. Amicae stepped beside me, cowl hiding her face, her hand poised, water swirling. “Rowetha,” she gasped, hugging the doll tighter. “From Crygill, by the Orkael. Somethin’ happened to my family, my village… I ran away.” Her cat yowled, fur bristling, and Amicae’s breath caught, her gaze snapping to me. “Inside,” I said, stepping aside. “You’re safe here.” Red huffed, sniffing the cat, who spat but held her ground. Rowetha stumbled in, dripping, her doll—Betsy, I’d later learn—clutched like a lifeline. Amicae knelt, cowl slipping to show a glimpse of her scars, but Rowetha didn’t flinch, lost in terror. “Tell us, child,” Amicae said, voice soft but urgent. “What happened in Crygill?” Rowetha’s lip quivered, her voice a shaky whisper. “It started yesterday. Mr. Klisternok, our neighbor, came to our house, actin’ strange. A black vapor came outta his mouth, into Ma and Da’s noses. They went still, eyes empty, then acted normal, but… wrong.” She hugged Betsy tighter, eyes distant. “After the occurrence yesterday, Ma and Da haven’t been quite themselves. Last night at dinner, they were unusually quiet and kept givin’ me and Bubbee odd, creepy looks. I slept in fits and starts all night. I awoke this mornin’ soakin’ wet. I was cleanin’ up—‘I don’t think I’m gettin’ sick,’ I said out loud. The house had a creepy quietness this morn.” She swallowed, voice breaking. “Then Ma screamed, her eyes turnin’ purple, like glowin’ violets. Da’s did too, and they lunged at Bubbee, their skin… splittin’, oozin’. Izzy—my cat—hissed, scratched ‘em, and I grabbed Bubbee and ran. Bubbee fell behind, and… I lost him in the storm.” Tears spilled, her cat rubbing her leg, eyes fixed on the dark outside. “I prayed to Anna Perenna, and I found your light, sir.” Amicae’s hand tightened, water dripping from her Aqua Lash. “Mindreaver,” she whispered, voice grim. “The taint I felt. It’s spreading, Rawn, in Crygill.” She looked at Rowetha, softer. “You’re brave, child. How far is Crygill? Are others changed?” Rowetha sniffled, wiping her eyes. “A day’s walk, miss. I saw others in the village, eyes glowin’, movin’ like… monsters. I don’t know who’s left.” Her gaze pleaded, hitting me like my boy’s fevered stare. I sheathed Vulcanspine, guilt and duty warring. “You’re safe here, Rowetha,” I said, voice rough. “Red, stand down.” I glanced at Amicae, her eyes blazing with purpose. “This changes things, princess. Crygill’s taint’s too close. Your Ithek hunt starts there, not the Eldertree.” Amicae rose, her Mistveil Cloak spell weaving fog around her hand, a reflex. “Then we go to Crygill at dawn,” she said, voice steel. “The red-eyed leader may be there, spreading this Mindreaver. We end it before it reaches Aethelwood.” She looked at Rowetha, gentle. “We’ll try to save your kin, child, but it’s dangerous. Stay close.” Rowetha nodded, clutching Betsy. “Thank you, miss, sir. Izzy—she knew somethin’ was wrong. She’ll help.” Izzy’s eyes gleamed, fixed on the door, where the storm’s howl masked a fouler rustle. I grabbed a cloak, my young body itching for action despite my dread. “Dawn it is,” I said, meeting Amicae’s gaze. “But if Fleshreavers are in Crygill, you’ll see fire like never before. Ready for that, princess?” Her flinch was brief, but her nod was firm. “For her,” she said, glancing at Rowetha, “and for me.” The Mindreaver’s whispers hissed, conjuring my boy’s oozing face, Amicae’s scars as burning villages, Rowetha’s parents clawing at her. I gripped Vulcanspine, vowing to burn that darkness first. Chapter: Shadows at the Threshold The dawn’s gray light bled through my farmhouse shutters, the storm’s damp musk thick as a grave’s breath. I’d slept on a wolf pelt by the hearth, giving Amicae and Rowetha my creaky bed, their soft breaths a fragile peace against Crygill’s taint. Red, my hound, stirred at my feet, his snores a comfort drowned by the Mindreaver’s whispers—Alaina’s face, oozing, my boy’s violet eyes clawing. Rowetha’s tale of her parents, turned monstrous by Klisternok’s black vapor, echoed Amicae’s scars, burned by Ithek Acolytes at the Eldertree. We’d planned to head west to Mannesket, seeking High King Theron’s aid, but the Varkis figure I’d glimpsed in the mist—his brooch gleaming—meant trouble had found us first. I’d finished morning chores, feeding chickens, goats, and cows, my young body too swift, Aeloria’s potion a curse of vigor. A violet-eyed rat in the barn, its acrid rot lingering, confirmed Amicae’s warning: the Mindreaver’s taint was in Kleinae. Now, inside, Amicae stirred porridge by the hearth, her cowl hiding scars, while Rowetha clutched Betsy, her doll, Izzy’s green eyes fixed on the door. My warning—“Somethin’s off outside”—had sharpened Amicae’s gaze, her whisper of “Mindreaver” chilling the air. A rustle beyond the shutters snapped Red’s head up, Izzy hissing. I peered through a crack, spotting a cloaked figure in the mist, Varkis colors bold, too tall for a Tiffling. Amicae’s breath caught. “Varkis,” she whispered, voice grim. “They’re tied to the Ithek, Rawn. They want Rowetha silenced—her tale exposes Crygill’s taint.” Rowetha whimpered, hugging Betsy, as I gripped Vulcanspine, my young body taut, guilt and duty burning. “Stay with the lass,” I told Amicae, stepping toward the door. “Let’s see what this bastard wants.” The Mindreaver’s whispers swelled, conjuring Alaina’s oozing face, Rowetha’s parents clawing, Amicae’s scars as my war’s ash. I’d burn that darkness, or it’d burn me. I flung the door open, Vulcanspine’s runes flaring, Red snarling at my side. The Varkis figure stood twenty paces off, mist curling around him, a longsword sheathed at his hip. Beside him, an Ithek Acolyte, cowled, eyes red as blood, clutched a rune-etched dagger, black vapor trailing his breath. Two Fleshreavers shambled behind, once Tifflings, now monstrosities—skin splitting, violet eyes glowing, claws dripping ichor. The storm’s rumble masked their rasps, but the rot stench choked me, Crygill’s plague come to my doorstep. “Rawn Mortisimus,” the Varkis man called, voice cold as iron. “The Scorch of Eryndor, reborn by divine jest. House Varkis offers amnesty for your war crimes—surrender the Tiffling girl, and walk free.” His eyes flicked to the cottage, where Amicae’s Mistveil Cloak spell wove fog, shielding Rowetha. “Burn your amnesty,” I growled, Vulcanspine steady. “Rowetha’s under my roof. You want her, you’ll bleed.” Guilt surged—villages I’d razed, screams I’d ordered—but Rowetha’s fear, like my boy’s, steeled me. The Mindreaver hissed, conjuring their faces, melting. The Ithek Acolyte stepped forward, vapor coiling. “The Breach hungers,” he rasped, voice a serpent’s hiss. “The girl’s blood will feed it, as the Elven whore’s did.” Amicae’s gasp cut the air, her Aqua Lash snapping from the doorway, water coiling like a noose. “Speak again, and you drown,” she hissed, cowl slipping, scars bared, her green eyes blazing. The Varkis man sneered, drawing his sword. “Kill them,” he barked, and the Fleshreavers lunged, claws raking. I unleashed Scorchfang Slash, Vulcanspine igniting, divine flames cleaving a Fleshreaver’s chest, its ichor sizzling. The stench—rot and charred flesh—hit hard, and Amicae recoiled, her lash binding the second Fleshreaver, but its claws grazed her shield, Essence Chill numbing her hands. “Rawn!” she cried, fog thickening. Rowetha screamed, “Izzy!” as her cat leapt, claws raking the Ithek Acolyte’s face, drawing blood. The Acolyte snarled, dagger slashing, but Izzy darted, and Rowetha threw Betsy, hitting his arm, buying time. I charged, Emberclad Ward flaring a heat shield, deflecting the Varkis man’s sword. “Get to the cart!” I roared, Red snapping at a Fleshreaver’s leg, slowing it. Amicae grabbed Rowetha, pulling her to the cart, supplies loaded from dawn. “We can’t hold them!” she shouted, her Surging Aegis shielding Rowetha as the Ithek Acolyte chanted, black vapor rising—Mindreaver’s breath. I spun, Ashen Purge burning the vapor, flames licking the cottage’s edge. Amicae flinched, fire too close to her scars, but her Tide’s Caress healed a claw wound on my arm, her touch trembling. The Varkis man laughed, sword slashing, grazing my side. “You’re no general now, Scorch,” he taunted, but I countered with Blazecharge, a fiery dash knocking him back, Overburn searing my chest. Red yelped, a Fleshreaver’s claw raking his flank, and Rowetha sobbed, “Red!” Izzy hissed, guarding her. “We run—west!” Amicae yelled, cutting the cart’s horse free, mounting with Rowetha and Izzy. I grabbed Red, blood slick, and leapt behind, urging the horse into a gallop. The Varkis man’s horn blared, more Fleshreavers rasping in the mist, but Amicae’s Mistveil Cloak cloaked us, fog swallowing the farmhouse. My side bled, Overburn throbbing, as we raced along the Orkael, Mannesket’s hills a faint promise. The Mindreaver’s whispers surged, conjuring my boy’s oozing face, Amicae’s attackers, Rowetha’s parents clawing. “Hold on, lasses,” I growled, Red panting in my arms, Izzy’s claws digging into Rowetha. The horn’s echo faded, but a violet glow pulsed behind, the Ithek’s taint trailing. We’d escaped—barely—but Mannesket’s court, Theron’s justice, held new dangers, and Crygill’s heart still burned. Chapter: Embers of Recovery The Orkael’s mist clung to us like a shroud as we rode west, Mannesket’s hills a faint promise beyond the storm’s gray veil. My side throbbed, the Varkis blade’s graze a burning reminder of yesterday’s ambush, my young body—Aeloria’s cursed gift—straining under the weight of guilt and duty. Red limped beside the horse, his flank clawed by a Fleshreaver, but his tail flicked, loyal as ever. Amicae rode ahead, her cowl hiding scars, her arm bandaged from the Ithek Acolyte’s dagger, her Tide’s Caress spell a faint glow healing us all. Rowetha clung to me, her doll Betsy lost, Izzy’s claws digging into her lap, her sobs quieter now but raw as the Crygill taint she’d fled—her parents’ violet eyes, flesh oozing, Klisternok’s black vapor the root. We’d barely escaped, Vulcanspine’s Ashen Purge burning the Mindreaver’s vapor, Amicae’s Mistveil Cloak cloaking our flight. The Varkis horn’s echo haunted me, their demand for Rowetha—“Surrender the girl, and walk free”—tied to their Ithek allies, who spoke of a “Breach” to feed with blood. My grip on Vulcanspine yesterday, stepping toward that Varkis bastard, had been a vow to burn that darkness, though the Mindreaver’s whispers—Alaina’s oozing face, Rowetha’s parents clawing, Amicae’s scars as my war’s ash—nearly broke me. Now, midmorning, we halted in a copse by the Orkael, its waters murmuring like the storm’s dying breath. I dismounted, wincing, and tied the horse to a willow. “Rest here,” I growled, voice rough, my young chest tight with Overburn from yesterday’s Blazecharge. Amicae slid down, her Tide’s Caress flaring to ease Red’s wound, then mine, her gloved hand trembling, fire’s memory in her flinch. Rowetha sat on a log, Izzy curling beside her, green eyes scanning the mist. “We’re alive,” Amicae said, voice a melodic whisper, sitting beside Rowetha. “But Mannesket’s no haven. Theron’s court is split—Varkis coin buys loyalty, and Vaelar’s spies, my kin, may hunt us.” Her green eyes met mine, fierce despite her scars’ shadow. “Rowetha’s tale exposes Crygill’s taint, but we need proof—an Ithek artifact, or the red-eyed leader’s head. Without it, Theron may turn us away.” Rowetha’s lip quivered, stroking Izzy. “What’s in Mannesket, miss? Can the king save Ma and Da?” Her voice, small but steady, hit me like my boy’s fevered plea, stirring the Mindreaver’s hiss: their faces, oozing, clawing. I knelt by her, side aching. “Theron’s a hard man, lass, but if anyone can rally against this taint, it’s him. Your tale’s our blade—keep it sharp.” I glanced at Amicae, her cowl low. “What’s our path, princess? Crygill’s lost, but the Ithek’s Breach is growin’. Eldertree after Mannesket?” Amicae nodded, her circlet glinting. “Mannesket first, for aid. Then the Eldertree, to trace the Ithek’s Essence. But Varkis tracks us, and the Mindreaver’s taint spreads—yesterday’s Fleshreavers weren’t the last.” Her voice dropped, raw. “Your fire, Rawn… it’s our edge, but it’s dim. I saw your Overburn. Can you wield it as you did in the war?” I scowled, standing, Vulcanspine’s weight heavy at my hip. “That Scorch is dead,” I muttered, guilt clawing—villages burned, screams I’d ordered. My new body thrummed, but my Fire Essence felt sluggish, decades of disuse dulling it. “I pushed too hard yesterday. Need to balance it, reconnect.” I stepped to a clearing, Red watching, and drew Vulcanspine, its runes faint. “Forms,” I said, voice grim. “Old Bladeweaver drills. Slow work, not a day’s fix.” I began the Emberflow Kata, Vulcanspine tracing arcs, my young muscles fluid but clumsy, like a lad with a new sword. The first form, Flame’s Ascent, sparked faint embers, warming my blood, but Overburn lingered, my side stinging. The second, Pyre’s Spiral, faltered, flames flickering, my focus split by the Mindreaver’s vision: Alaina, flesh splitting, begging me to die. I growled, pushing through, sweat beading, Red’s tail thumping encouragement. Amicae watched, her flinch subtle but clear, fire’s glow too close to her scars. “Careful,” she warned, voice tight, her Aqua Lash coiling reflexively. “Your Essence is raw. Overburn could cripple you.” Rowetha’s eyes widened, Izzy hissing, sensing my strain. “Gotta try,” I panted, shifting to Cinder’s Veil, a defensive form, flames weaving a faint shield. The Essence stirred, sluggish but alive, a spark of the Scorch I’d buried. I stopped, chest heaving, Vulcanspine lowered. “It’s comin’ back, princess, but slow. Won’t burn armies today.” Amicae’s gaze softened, almost pitying. “Time’s not our ally, Rawn. The Ithek’s Breach grows, and Varkis won’t stop. We need you whole.” She stood, her Tide’s Caress flaring again, easing my Overburn, her touch cool but haunted. “Rest now. We ride soon.” Rowetha offered a shy smile, holding out a strip of dried meat. “For you, sir. My da… he’d say keep your strength.” Her voice broke, but Izzy nudged her, purring, and I took the meat, nodding, my boy’s face in her courage. By noon, we rode on, the Orkael’s banks fading as Mannesket’s border loomed—a wooden palisade, Theron’s raven banner flapping. The first town, Eldhaven, sprawled just beyond, its smoke stacks curling like wary fingers. Guards in leather armor, pikes gleaming, eyed us at the gate. “State your business,” one grunted, his gaze lingering on Amicae’s cowl, Rowetha’s trembling hands. “Travelers seekin’ shelter,” I said, voice steady, Vulcanspine hidden under my cloak, Red at my side, blood crusted but alert. “Fleein’ trouble east.” The guard’s eyes narrowed, but he waved us through, muttering of Varkis patrols nearby. Eldhaven’s streets buzzed—merchants hawking bread, smiths hammering, children dodging carts—but an undercurrent of fear lingered, whispers of “strange folk” vanishing. Amicae’s voice dropped as we dismounted at an inn, the Rusty Crown. “Varkis spies are here,” she murmured, her Mistveil Cloak spell faint, shielding Rowetha. “And the taint… I feel it, faint, in the Essence. We’re not safe.” I gripped Vulcanspine’s hilt, my young body taut, the Mindreaver’s whispers hissing: Rowetha’s parents, violet-eyed, Amicae’s scars burning, Alaina’s plea. “Then we move quick,” I said, meeting her eyes. “Theron’s court next, or the Eldertree. But first, we heal—and I train.” Red nudged my leg, Izzy’s eyes on the shadows, as Eldhaven’s pulse hid a darker heart. Chapter: The Pixie’s Plunge Eldhaven’s midday bustle clung to us like the storm’s damp musk, the Rusty Crown inn a brief refuge after our market trek. Amicae had led us to a contact—a weaver named Lyssa, who loathed Varkis’s taxes and whispered of ranger allies—but her promise of aid came with a warning: Varkis spies prowled Eldhaven, and a taint, faint in the Essence, soured the market’s air. My side ached from the Varkis blade’s graze, though Amicae’s Tide’s Caress dulled it, her own arm bandaged from the Ithek Acolyte’s dagger. Rowetha, clutching Izzy, her cat, bore no scars but Crygill’s weight—her parents’ violet eyes, flesh oozing, Klisternok’s black vapor the root. Red, my hound, trotted beside, his new pearly teeth and clear eyes, a gift of Aeloria or the storm, mirroring my cursed youth. I’d trained at dawn, Emberflow Kata stirring my Fire Essence, but Overburn and decades of disuse left it weak, Vulcanspine’s runes flickering. Rorik, the tanner, had vowed ranger aid if we brought proof of the Ithek’s Breach, but the barmaid’s gurgling cough last night, her eyes flickering violet, hinted the Mindreaver’s taint was here. Amicae’s warning—Varkis’s sway in Theron’s court, Vaelar’s spies hunting us—kept us wary, Rowetha’s tale our blade against the Ithek’s darkness. We approached the Rusty Crown, its sign creaking, Eldhaven’s smoke curling like a wary beast. I opened the door for Amicae. She got halfway in the doorway and started up a cussing storm. I rushed around to see a Dragonfly pixie male clutching Amicae’s dress at her cleavage and spewing copious amounts of frothy swill down her appreciable cleavage. “You nasty little flyin’ creatin’! By the nine hells, get this maggot off me!” she screamed. I felt a tingle of arcane power begin to ramp up. I clutched her arm. “No, Amica!” I said. Then there appeared a sketchy-looking man reaching out his nasty hand toward Amicae’s chest. Red was there instantly, his rumbling so low you could feel it in your chest. Amicae was stompin’ and flailin’ around, tryin’ to throw the offendin’ up-chucker from her person. Finally, the flyin’ water spigot shut off. “Aaagh, you flyin’ rat! You despicable, repugnant little swine!” Again, a spark of the arcane tingled my scalp. “Amicae, no!” I said. (hiccup) “Oh, pardon me, madame, I sss-eem to have accidentally flown into your sweater puppies. I cc…commend you on their obedience trainin’. They do a marvelous job o’ sittin’ pretty.” Amicae turned an even darker shade of red. The pixie, wings buzzing, hovered unsteadily, his iridescent vest stained with ale, green eyes bleary but glinting mischief. No taller than a handspan, he was a rapscallion, reeking of swill and trouble. “Name’s Trink!” he slurred, bowing midair, nearly crashing into Rowetha, who yelped, Izzy hissing, claws swiping. The sketchy man—leather apron, reeking of fish—laughed, his hand still outstretched, until Red’s snarl froze him. I grabbed his wrist, my young grip iron, guilt and fury burning. “Back off, filth,” I growled, Vulcanspine’s hilt warm under my cloak. Amicae’s Aqua Lash coiled, water dripping, her scars hidden but her fury bared. “You’ve one breath to explain, pixie, ‘fore I drown you,” she hissed, voice a melodic blade, her cowl damp with swill, arcane spark fading but poised. The common room stilled, patrons eyeing us, some Varkis spies, I’d wager. Trink hiccupped, wings faltering. “No harm meant, lady! Jus’ celebratin’—pinched a Varkis purse, see?” He flashed a coin pouch, Varkis colors bold, then tucked it away, grinning. “Heard you lot talkin’ ‘bout Crygill, taint, Ithek. I know things—ranger routes, Varkis hideouts. Help me, I help you!” His gaze flicked to Rowetha, softening. “Poor lass, seen monsters, eh?” Rowetha clutched Izzy, eyes wide but curious. “You know Ma and Da’s sickness?” she asked, voice small, her Crygill tale—Klisternok’s vapor, violet eyes—stirring Trink’s nod. The Mindreaver’s whisper hissed, conjuring her parents, clawing, Alaina’s oozing face, Amicae’s scars as my war’s ash. I released the fishmonger, shoving him back. “Speak, Trink,” I said, Red’s rumble steady, his new teeth a threat. “What’s your game? Varkis bait, or just a drunk fool?” My Fire Essence flickered, weak from yesterday’s Overburn, but Vulcanspine’s weight grounded me. Trink landed on a table, swaying. “No bait! I’m a Varkis thorn—steal their coin, dodge their blades. Know a ranger, Kalia, hates ‘em too. She’s in Eldhaven tonight, at the Thorned Fang tavern. Wants Ithek dirt, like you.” He winked at Amicae, reckless. “Sorry ‘bout the dress, lady. Mighty fine—” “Enough!” Amicae snapped, her Tide’s Caress cleaning the swill, her face flushed, scars glimpsed by patrons before her cowl fell back. “You’ll lead us to Kalia, pixie, or my water’s your grave.” Her fire fear lingered, my presence a spark she dreaded, but her resolve held, eyes on Rowetha’s hope. I glanced at the fishmonger, now slinking off, likely to Varkis ears. “We’re watched,” I muttered, meeting Amicae’s gaze. “Kalia sounds good, but Trink’s a risk. Trust him, or ditch him?” Rowetha stroked Izzy, whispering, “He’s funny, sir, like Bubbee,” her lost brother’s memory a knife in my gut. Amicae’s voice was grim. “We need rangers. Trink comes, but one jest.” and “he’s leashed.” She glared at him, arcane tingling. “Move, pixie. Thorned Fang, now.” Trink saluted, wings buzzing, and I grabbed Vulcanspine, Red’s new strength at my side, his growl a warning to the room. We stepped into Eldhaven’s dusk, the market’s din fading, smoke curling like a noose. Trink darted ahead, unsteady but swift, leading us to the Thorned Fang. A barmaid’s cough echoed from the Rusty Crown, wet, gurgling, her eyes flickering violet. Amicae’s hand tightened, sensing the taint, and Red’s hackles rose, his pearly teeth bared. The Ithek’s Breach loomed, Varkis spies closing, and Kalia’s aid was our spark in Eldhaven’s darkening heart.

Document.Title: Novel Format
Document.Text:
NOVEL FORMAT About This Template By default, when compiled (File > Compile…), this project will generate a document in the standard manuscript format for novels. Settings are also provided to make it easy to compile to a paperback-style PDF for self-publishing or an ePub or Kindle ebook. How To Use This Template Inside the “Manuscript” folder, create a new folder for each chapter and title each folder with the name of the chapter. If you don’t intend to use chapter names, just use something descriptive that tells you what the chapter is about. (You do not need to—and indeed shouldn’t—title the folders “Chapter One” and so on, because chapter numbering will be taken care of automatically during the Compile process.) The first chapter folder has been created for you with the placeholder title “Chapter”. ⁠Note: The “Manuscript” folder is what we refer to in the documentation as the “Draft folder”. It’s just been renamed “Manuscript” in this template. Create a new text document for each scene inside the chapter folders. (Upon export, scenes will be separated with the “#” character for standard manuscript format and with a blank line for other formats.) Information about characters can be placed in the “Characters” folder, and information about locations can be placed in the “Places” folder. (These are just regular folders that have had custom icons assigned to them using the Documents > Change Icon feature.) Character and setting sketch sheets have been provided which can be used for filling out information about the people and places in your novel. These are located in the “Template Sheets” folder. You should not edit the documents in the “Template Sheets” folder directly unless you wish to change the templates (which you are free to do—you may wish to customise the sketch sheets or get rid of them entirely). To create a new character sheet, click on the Characters folder (or wherever you want to create your new character sheet) and from the Project menu, select New From Template > Character Sketch. This creates a new character sketch document for you to edit and fill in with your character details. You can create setting sketch sheets in the same way. Alternatively, you can just click “Add”, or hit Ctrl+N, with the Characters or Places folders selected. You can view character and setting sketches in the Inspector as follows: Select the “Bookmarks” pane in the Inspector. If necessary, click on “Document Bookmarks” at the top and switch to “Project Bookmarks”. Click on the dropdown arrow next to the “Characters” or “Places” folders in the Bookmarks list and choose the sheet you wish to view. A “Notes” folder has been provided for project-level notes. This has been set as the default Bookmarks folder in Project > Project Settings…, so that when you create a new document from the Bookmarks sidebar in a Quick Reference panel, the document will be added to this folder. Compiling Title pages and front matter are all provided in the “Front Matter” folder (between the “Places” and “Notes” folders). Different front matter is used for different formats.
Tip: You can open this document in a Quick Reference window and have it open alongside the compile dialog if you need to refer to these instructions while compiling. To compile to standard manuscript format Edit the “Title Page” document inside the Front Matter/Manuscript Format folder to ensure that it contains the correct information (by default it will use the Author Information supplied in File > Options…). Go to File > Compile… Next to “Compile for”, select “Print”, “PDF” or one of the rich text file formats such as RTF, Word or OpenOffice. Select either “Manuscript (Courier)” or “Manuscript (Times)” from the list of formats on the left (note that this list changes depending on the file format you have chosen). Ensure that the “Add front matter” button is ticked under the contents list on the right and that the “Manuscript Format” folder is selected in the pop-up button next to it. (This has already been set up for you.) Click on “Compile”. To compile to paperback PDF format Edit the front matter pages contained inside the Front Matter/Paperback folder. Go to File > Compile… Next to “Compile for”, select “PDF”. Select “Paperback (5.06" x 7.81")” from the list of formats on the left. Ensure that the “Add front matter” button is ticked under the contents list on the right and that the “Paperback” folder is selected in the pop-up button next to it. (This has already been set up for you.) Click on “Compile”. To compile to ebook format Edit or remove the dedication page contained inside the Front Matter/Ebook folder. Feel free to add any other front matter documents as required. Import a cover image. You can store this anywhere, but it makes sense to place it in the “Ebook” front matter folder. A placeholder cover image is already provided—you will want to delete that once you have imported your own. Be sure to check online for recommended image sizes and file format, because the recommendations are constantly changing. The sample cover image provided is 2,500 x 1,563 pixels. Go to File > Compile… From the “Compile for” menu, select one of the ebook formats. To create an ePub file, choose either of the “ePub Ebook” formats. To create a Kindle file, choose the “Kindle Ebook” format. Select “Ebook” from the list of formats on the left. If you have front matter documents other than the cover image, ensure that the “Add front matter” button is ticked under the contents list on the right and that the “Ebook” folder is selected in the pop-up button next to it. If you don’t have any front matter documents, you can un-tick “Add front matter”. (Un-ticking this does not affect the cover image.) Above the contents list in the rightmost header bar are six buttons. Click on each of them in turn to go through the various available settings, changing what you need. In particular: Fill in the metadata such as author name and book title. Ensure your cover image is selected and shown. Click on “Compile”. Making Changes There are various minor changes you can make to tweak this template so that it better suits your needs, as follows: Project and author name: The default book title and author name used in headers and elsewhere can be edited in the metadata pane of Compile (click the tag icon in the rightmost header bar of the Compile panel). Chapter subtitles: By default, Compile for this template is set up so that chapter numbers are added but not chapter titles. If you want to include the title of the chapter folder in Compile: Click on “Assign Section Layouts” at the bottom of the Compile panel. Select “Chapter Heading” in the list on the left. Select a different layout on the right, such as “Chapter Title”. (These layouts determine how each part of your manuscript looks in the compiled text.) Font: You can override the font used throughout a compiled document. At the top of the “Section Layouts” pane in the centre of the Compile panel, change the “Font” setting.
Note that fonts are not included in ebooks, however—e-readers choose their own fonts (and you would need to pay a hefty fee for a licence to embed a font in an ebook). The “Font” setting is therefore not available for ebook formats. Page size: The “Paperback” formats are set to create a PDF with a page size of 5.06" x 7.81" or 6" x 9", which are two of the formats you can use with Amazon’s CreateSpace and are popular sizes for trade paperbacks. You can create your own paperback format using a different page size as follows: Right-click on the “Paperback (5.06" x 7.81")” format in Compile’s “Formats” list. Select “Duplicate & Edit Format…”. The compile format designer window will open. In the “Format Name” text field at the top, change the name to something meaningful (e.g. “Paperback (5.5" x 8.5")”) and select “My Formats” as the “Save To” location. Select “Page Settings” in the sidebar. Click on the “Page Setup…” button. In the “Page Setup” panel that appears, ensure “Custom” is selected in the “Paper Size” menu and click the “…” button. Enter a new paper width and height. (Do not worry about setting the margins here; if you want to change these for the compile format, do so after the next step by clicking the “Margins” button just below “Page Setup…”.) Click “OK” and then click “OK” again. Click “Save” in the Compile panel. This will return you to the main Compile preview. You now have a variation of the paperback format using a different page size that you can use at any time with any project. Making your own special folders: The “Characters” and “Places” folders are just regular folders that have been set up in a particular way; you can create other folders that work in a similar manner. To create a folder with a custom icon, add a new folder, place it where you want it, ensure it is selected, and then go to Documents > Change Icon to choose a different icon. To create your own template sheets, create a new document inside the “Template Sheets” folder and set it up however you want (e.g. add text, change the title or icon, set default metadata and so on). Now this document will be available as the basis for creating new documents from the New From Template menu. To make it so that adding a new document to the folder created in (1) always results in a document based on the template set up in (2), select the folder and go to Documents > Default Template for Subdocuments, then choose the template document you created. Working with chapters instead of scenes: By default, this project is set up so that you write each scene as a separate text document. If you don’t like to break things up quite that much and would prefer to write an entire chapter in each text document, make the following changes: Rename the “Scene” document to use your chapter title and move it so that it is on the same level as the “Chapter” folder rather than being inside it. Create a new text document for each chapter. Go to Project > Project Settings… and select “Section Types”. Select the “Default Types by Structure” tab. Delete “Level 1 files” and “Level 2 files and deeper”. In the “Section Type” column, change the setting for “All files” to “Chapter”, then hit “OK”. This tells Scrivener that all your files contain chapter text, not scenes. Compile has already been set up to apply the right formatting to the section types defined here. Using a custom table of contents in ebooks: When exporting to ebook format, Scrivener automatically generates a table of contents. If you would like to customise what appears in the contents, follow these instructions: Create a document for your table of contents inside the Front Matter/Ebook folder. Name the document “Contents”. With the “Contents” document open in the editor, select Navigate > Editor > Lock in Place. The editor header will turn pink to indicate the lock, and you will be able to work in the binder without affecting the editor. In the binder, select the documents you would like to appear in the table of contents (hold down Ctrl to select more than one document). For a simple flat list, drag the selected documents into the editor and drop them in the empty “Contents” document. Alternatively, if you would like the table of contents indented to match the binder structure, select Edit > Copy Special > Copy Documents as Structured Link List and then click into the text of the “Contents” document and hit Ctrl+V or use Edit > Paste. The documents you wish to appear in the table of contents will now appear as a list of links. If you wish to centre the table of contents, select the text and centre it. In the Inspector, change the “Section Type” of the “Contents” document to “Table of Contents”. Remove the editor lock by toggling Navigate > Editor > Lock in Place or deselecting the option from the menu that appears when right-clicking the document icon in the editor header.
Now, when you compile, your custom “Contents” document will be used instead of the automatically-generated one. The titles in the links of the “Contents” document will automatically be updated to match those of the final compiled ebook. Sample Documents The “Sample Output” folder inside the Research folder contains examples of documents that have been created by compiling from this template. “Standard Manuscript” shows the results when compiling to “Manuscript (Courier)”, and “Paperback Novel” shows the results when compiling to “Paperback (5.06" x 7.81")” format with chapter subtitles included, as described in Making Changes, above. Final Note Scrivener project templates are flexible and are not intended to restrict you to a particular workflow. You can change, delete or move the files and folders contained in the template to suit how you work.
Like all templates in Scrivener, this project was originally created from the “Blank” template. We’ve simply added a few folders and set everything up in ways that should be useful to novel writers. Everything you can do with this project, you could equally do by creating a “Blank” project and setting it up yourself.
You can create your own templates by setting up a skeletal project with the files, folders and settings you would like to use for new projects and using File > Save As Template…

Document.Title: Scene5
Document.Text: After the occurence yesterday. Ma and Da haven’t been quite themselves. Last night at dinner they were unusually quiet and kept giving me and Bubee odd creepy looks. I slept last night in fits and starts all night. I awoke this morning soaking wet. I was cleaning up “I don’t think I am getting sick” I say out loud. The house had a creepy quietness this morn

Chapter 3: The Guardian's Call Three days after the Awakening Day One: Relearning Fire Rawn's cottage, dawn The morning after Amicae's revelation, I stood in the clearing behind my cottage, Vulcanspine trembling in my grip. These weren't my hands—too smooth, too steady, no liver spots or tremor of age. The sword felt different, too, lighter in a grasp that had grown stronger overnight. But more than that, I could sense something burning within my chest—my Fire Essence core, blazing like a newly stoked forge where before it had been barely glowing embers. "Your stance is wrong," Amicae observed from beneath the oak tree, her cowl pulled low but her scarred chin visible in the dawn light. "But more than that—your Center is fighting itself." I could feel what she meant. The metaphysical core within me—which had been a dimmed star for decades—now pulsed with the intensity of a solar flare, but my mind kept trying to contain it with an old man's careful restraint. The sensation was nauseating, like trying to swallow lightning while pretending it was warm milk. "I don't know this body," I said, the admission bitter as wormwood. "Forty years I spent learning my limits, teaching my Fire Essence to burn low and steady. And now..." I gestured helplessly at myself, and small flames leaked from my fingertips unbidden. "It's like trying to control a wildfire with a candle's discipline." The flames singed the grass at my feet, and Red whimpered, backing away from me. The sound cut deeper than any blade. My own dog, afraid of what I'd become. Pack leader burning too bright, his mental voice whispered, tinged with confusion and concern. Hurts to be close. But still pack leader. Amicae tilted her head, studying me with those keen green eyes. "Show me your Ember Coil. Let the serpent energy gather in your chest, then release it through Vulcanspine." I tried to follow her instruction, feeling for the coiling sensation she described. In my transformed Fire Essence core, power gathered like molten metal—but it was too much, too fast. The energy built beyond my ability to contain it, and when I attempted the sharp release, the power exploded outward in an uncontrolled inferno that sent flames roaring twenty feet in every direction. The blast knocked Amicae backward, her water magic barely managing to shield her from the worst of it. Red yelped and fled behind the cottage. Trees caught fire. The very air seemed to scream. "Stop!" Amicae shouted, her own power surging to contain the inferno. "Pull it back!" I tried to yank the power back in a desperate withdrawal, but my panicked attempt only made things worse. The flames died, but the sudden reversal felt like tearing my soul in half. I collapsed to my knees, blood running from my nose, my vision swimming with afterimages of fire. "Too much!" I gasped, clutching my chest where the Fire Essence core felt like a red-hot coal lodged beneath my ribs. "I used to fight for every spark, coax flame like nursing a dying ember. Now it's like... like trying to sip from a volcano that's trying to drown me." * Day One: The Journey Begins Fifty miles north, the ruins of Crygill Rowetha crouched in the burned-out shell of what had been the village bakery, watching purple-eyed things that wore familiar faces patrol the streets below. Three days had passed since her escape from the oak grove, three days of hiding in ruins and foraging for scraps while the corruption completed its hold on everything she'd ever loved. The village was utterly transformed now. Purple mist hung like a permanent fog over every building, and the things that had been her neighbors moved with mechanical precision, following patterns that spoke of a single controlling intelligence. They worked constantly—building something in the village square, a structure of twisted wood and blackened stone that hurt to look at directly. Sister-friend, Izzy's mental voice whispered urgently. Cannot stay. Bad-things growing stronger. Soon will sense us even here. The kitten was right. Each day, the corrupted villagers' patrols grew more systematic, their searches more thorough. Yesterday, they'd nearly found her hiding place in the mill ruins. This morning, they'd discovered the cache of supplies she'd hidden near the river. "I know," she whispered back, stroking Izzy's silver-marked fur. "But I can't leave Bubbee. What if he's still alive down there?" They both looked toward her family's cottage, where the root cellar doors remained closed. No purple mist leaked from that underground space, and sometimes—just sometimes—she thought she saw movement in the shadows below. Her little brother might still be hiding down there, still himself, still waiting for rescue. Root cellar has old protections, Izzy confirmed. Stone foundation touched by Guardian magic long ago. Keeps worst corruption out. But boy cannot stay hidden forever. Will need food, water, hope. "Then we have to find help," Rowetha said, though the words felt like betrayal. "Real help. Someone who can fight this thing and win." Izzy's eyes blazed with silver light as she extended her senses beyond the ruined bakery. South, she said with sudden certainty. Warm currents in the magic-flow. Something awakens there. Something that burns clean and bright. Fire that could cleanse purple poison. Below in the square, the corrupted villagers paused in their work, heads turning in unison toward some distant signal only they could hear. Then they resumed their construction with renewed urgency, the twisted structure growing visibly larger even as Rowetha watched. "What are they building?" she breathed. Gateway, Izzy's mental voice carried dark knowledge. Portal for bringing more corruption from the source. When finished, bad-things won't need to spread slowly anymore. Will pour through like flood through broken dam. The implication hit Rowetha like a physical blow. Crygill wasn't just another infected village—it was becoming a beachhead for invasion. "How long before it's finished?" Days, maybe less. Must find fire-that-burns-clean soon. Must bring help before gateway opens. Rowetha nodded, gathering her meager possessions. Her stomach cramped with hunger—she'd eaten nothing but berries and roots for days—but desperation gave her strength. Somewhere to the south, help existed. Izzy could sense it, and Guardian magic didn't lie. "I'm sorry, Bubbee," she whispered toward the cottage. "I'll come back for you. I promise." Then she and Izzy slipped from the bakery ruins and began their long journey toward whatever hope the wider world might hold. * Day Two: The First Crack Rawn's cottage I woke on the second day to find my cottage filled with smoke. Not from the hearth—from me. My transformed body was literally smoldering in my sleep, wisps of vapor rising from my skin like I was a banked fire. The sheets were singed, the mattress blackened. "This can't continue," I muttered, stumbling outside to find Amicae already waiting in the clearing. She looked like she hadn't slept either. "The smoke woke me," she said simply. "How do you feel?" "Like I'm burning alive from the inside." It wasn't metaphor. My Fire Essence core felt like it was trying to cook me from within, the energy having nowhere to go during sleep. "I dreamed I was a funeral pyre. Everyone I've ever loved was burning in my flames." The Mindreaver had been particularly active during the night, showing me visions of Alaina and my boy consumed by my uncontrolled fire, their screams mixing with the crackling of flames. Each vision had caused my Center to flare, adding real burns to the imagined ones. "We need to establish basic control before you incinerate yourself," Amicae said, moving to what she clearly considered a safe distance. "Try the Breath of Names. Speak to your sword, let it know you're still you." I drew Vulcanspine, noting how the runes along its blade flickered weakly, like dying embers. "Vulcanspine," I said aloud, feeling foolish. "Old friend. I know this is... difficult." The blade's glow strengthened slightly, but I could feel its confusion—forty years of steady, controlled fire suddenly replaced by this chaotic inferno. It was like asking a trained warhorse to accept a different rider. "Tell it who you are," Amicae encouraged. "Not who you were, but who you are now." I struggled with that. Who was I? An old soldier in stolen flesh? A young man with an elder's memories? "I'm..." The words stuck in my throat. "I don't know what I am anymore." Vulcanspine's glow dimmed further, responding to my uncertainty. The weapon needed confidence from its wielder, but confidence was the one thing I lacked. "Then we'll figure it out together," I finally said to the blade, and its runes pulsed once in acknowledgment. Red approached cautiously, his mental voice carrying both love and wariness: Pack leader different but same. Red learning too. Fire changes us both. I looked down at my oldest friend and saw that he was right. His coat was developing new depth of color, the grey giving way to rich auburn that reminded me of his youth. His eyes held a brightness that went beyond normal canine intelligence. "We're all changing, aren't we, old boy?" I murmured, reaching out carefully to scratch his ears. To my relief, he didn't pull away, though I felt him wince slightly at the heat radiating from my skin. Change together, he confirmed. Pack adapts. Pack survives. * Day Two: Other Villages The trade road south of Crygill The first refugee camp Rowetha discovered broke her heart all over again. Families huddled around inadequate fires, their possessions reduced to whatever they could carry, their faces marked by the same shock and disbelief she felt every morning when she woke up and remembered that her world was gone. But these weren't refugees from Crygill. These were survivors from places with names she'd never heard—Millhaven, Brook's End, Tanner's Ford. Villages that had fallen to the same purple poison that had stolen her family. "Started three weeks ago," an elderly woman was telling a circle of listeners as Rowetha crept close enough to hear. "Wells went bad first, then the livestock. By the time people started changing, it was too late to run." "Same in Millhaven," a younger man added, bouncing a fussing baby on his knee. "My brother was first to change. Woke up one morning with purple eyes, speaking in voices that weren't his own. Had to flee our own home when he started calling to the corruption, drawing it in like a beacon." Rowetha's blood ran cold. The corruption wasn't limited to Crygill—it was spreading across multiple villages simultaneously, following some vast pattern she couldn't comprehend. Big magic, Izzy's mental voice carried grim understanding. Not natural spread. Someone controlling it. Someone with power to coordinate corruption across great distances. "How many villages?" Rowetha whispered. A Tiffling trader overheard her quiet question. "Dozen, maybe more," he said, his earth-touched features creased with worry. "All along the northern borders. Started small—bad dreams, spoiled food, sick animals. Then it accelerated. Whole settlements going dark in a matter of days." "And the authorities?" an older human asked. "The King's Guard? The magical orders?" The trader shook his head grimly. "King Theron's dealing with political troubles in the capital. The magical orders are scattered, each one claiming the others aren't doing enough. Meanwhile, the corruption spreads faster than ravens can carry warnings." Explains why no help comes, Izzy observed. Human leaders think in terms of armies and battles. Don't understand enemy that corrupts from within, turns allies into weapons. Rowetha listened for another hour, learning about a crisis far larger than her twelve-year-old mind had been able to imagine. Crygill wasn't unique—it was part of a coordinated assault on civilization itself. But the most important thing she learned was this: several refugees mentioned rumors of a "flame that burns clean" somewhere to the south, a power that could purify corruption without destroying what lay beneath. There, Izzy said as they slipped away from the camp under cover of darkness. That is what we seek. Fire-that-heals instead of fire-that-destroys. "But how do we find it?" Rowetha asked. Izzy's silver eyes gleamed in the moonlight. Follow the warm currents. Guardian magic knows Guardian magic. Will lead us to the flame. They walked through the night, following paths only Izzy could sense, while behind them the refugee camp's fires dwindled to embers and ahead the unknown beckoned with the promise of hope. * Day Three: Water and Fire Rawn's cottage "Your problem isn't the strength of your flames," Amicae said on the third day, water swirling around her hands in elegant patterns. "It's that you're trying to force stillness when your Center wants to burn free. You're at war with yourself." She was right. Every instinct I'd developed over decades of careful magic use was now actively harmful. My body wanted to channel fire like a river in flood, but my mind kept trying to parcel it out like drops from an eyedropper. "Watch," she continued, and her own magic flowed differently—not the explosive power of fire, but the patient persistence of water finding its way through stone. Where my Fire Essence core blazed like a furnace, hers felt like a deep spring, cool and inexhaustible. "I can't find the balance," I muttered, wiping sweat from my brow. Even at rest, heat distortion shimmered around me now, making the air dance. My transformed body ran hot, metabolism racing like a horse that had forgotten how to walk. "Before, my Center was barely a glowing coal. I could control every spark because there was so little power to control." "The mistake is thinking you need less power," she said, extending her arm as water formed into precise spirals. "Instead, you need better channels. Your sword isn't just steel—it's an extension of your Fire Essence core. Let it carry the overflow instead of trying to dam it up inside." I looked down at Vulcanspine, seeing it with new eyes. The runes along its blade weren't just decoration—they were conduits, pathways for Fire Essence to flow from my core through the steel and into the world. But they were designed for my old power levels, not this overwhelming flood. "The channels are too small," I realized. "It's like trying to drain a lake through a straw." "Then we widen them," Amicae said. "Try the Star Descent. Raise the blade skyward and draw power down from above instead of forcing it up from within." I lifted Vulcanspine toward the twin suns, and something remarkable happened. Instead of the explosive release I'd been fighting, power flowed downward through the blade—controlled, purposeful, like channeling lightning through a rod. The flame that resulted was golden and steady, responding to my will rather than overwhelming it. For perhaps thirty seconds. Then the borrowed strength of the technique failed, and my natural power reasserted itself with volcanic force. The blade became a pillar of fire reaching toward the sky, and I screamed as the energy tore through channels not meant for such intensity. The backlash when it ended left me unconscious for an hour. When I woke, Amicae was sitting beside me, her Tide's Caress cooling burns I didn't remember receiving. "Better," she said dryly, "but you're still trying to be what you were instead of what you are." "I don't want to be this," I whispered, my voice raw from screaming. "I don't want this power. I don't want this body. I want to be old and weak and ready to die with dignity." "Why?" The simple question caught me off guard. "Because... because I've had my time. I fought my war, loved my wife, buried my son. I was done. At peace." "And now?" I stared at my hands—young, unmarked, crackling with barely contained fire. "Now I'm a weapon again. And I'm tired of being a weapon." Red approached slowly, his mental voice carrying gentle wisdom: Weapons destroy. Pack leaders protect. Same fire, different purpose. The distinction hit me like a revelation.
