Perhaps the problem wasn't that I'd become a weapon—perhaps it was that I'd forgotten how to be a guardian. * Day Three: Fellow Refugees A crossroads inn called the Wayward Swan The inn was packed beyond capacity, refugees filling every room and sleeping in the common areas. The proprietor, a kindly Tiffling woman named Marta, had stopped charging for food days ago when she realized the scope of the crisis. "Can't in good conscience take coin from folks fleeing for their lives," she told Rowetha as she ladled thin but nourishing soup into a wooden bowl. "Besides, my own niece might be among the missing. Village of Copperdale went dark four days ago." Rowetha accepted the soup gratefully, her first hot meal since fleeing Crygill. Around her, refugees shared their stories in hushed tones, each tale adding another piece to the horrifying puzzle. "The corruption follows trade routes," a merchant was saying, studying a hand-drawn map covered in red marks. "Look at the pattern—every affected village sits on a major road or river. It's using our own infrastructure against us." "Brilliant strategy," a former soldier added grimly. "Strike the communication and supply lines first. Isolate the communities so they can't organize a coordinated response." A young mother rocking a crying baby spoke up: "My husband stayed behind to help evacuate others. That was five days ago. No word since." Her voice broke. "The purple mist... it moves like it's alive. Seeks out the living and changes them." Learning much, Izzy observed from beneath Rowetha's cloak. Enemy is intelligent, patient, strategic. Not random plague but coordinated conquest. "Have you heard anything about resistance?" Rowetha asked quietly. "Anyone fighting back successfully?" The soldier's face brightened slightly. "Rumors, mostly. Talk of a fire-mage in the southern territories who can burn away corruption without harming what lies beneath. Some say he's a Guardian-blessed warrior, others claim he's one of the old war heroes reborn." "Probably just wishful thinking," the merchant said, though his tone suggested he desperately wanted to believe otherwise. "People always invent heroes when they're desperate enough." But Rowetha felt Izzy's excitement through their bond. Not wishful thinking, the kitten's mental voice carried certainty. Truth. Can sense it from here—burning star in the south, growing brighter each day. Power that matches the corruption but opposes it. "How far south?" Rowetha pressed. "Hard to say," the soldier replied. "Reports come from all over. But if it's real, if there's really someone out there who can fight this thing..." He shrugged helplessly. "Might be our only hope." That night, as Rowetha curled up in a corner of the common room with Izzy warm against her chest, she made her decision. Tomorrow, she would leave the relative safety of the refugee camps and head south. Somewhere out there, a flame burned that could cleanse corruption. She would find it, no matter how long the journey took. Good choice, Izzy's mental voice whispered as sleep finally claimed them both. Guardian's path is always toward the light. * Day Four: The Breaking Point Rawn's cottage Red wouldn't come near me anymore. That was the detail that finally broke me. For forty years, that dog had been my constant companion. Through Alaina's death, through the long loneliness afterward, through nights when the war memories came calling. He'd slept at my feet, shared my meals, been the one living thing that needed me in a world that had largely forgotten the Scorch of Eryndor. Now he cowered whenever I entered a room, whimpering at the heat distortion that followed me like a personal aurora. The smell of my transformed body—ozone and ash and something like burning copper—made him flee. I'd become alien to the one creature that had always known me. "He's just afraid," Amicae said when she found me sitting alone by the cottage, tears streaming down my face. "Animals sense magical changes more acutely than humans." "He's not afraid," I corrected, my voice breaking. "He's grieving. His master died three days ago, and now there's a stranger wearing his skin." I watched Red pace nervously at the edge of the clearing, wanting to approach but unable to overcome his instincts. "Look at him. He knows I'm not Rawn anymore." Still pack leader, Red's mental voice whispered hesitantly across our bond. But... different. Scary different. Fire burns too hot, too bright. Hurts to be close. "You are Rawn," Amicae insisted. "You're just—" "Different," I finished bitterly. "Changed. Transformed into something that frightens the one being who loved me unconditionally." I stood, and flames erupted involuntarily from my feet, scorching the ground. "Do you see? I can't even grieve without setting things on fire." The flames spread in a circle around me, creating a barrier that kept everything at bay—including comfort, including connection, including the dog that had been my anchor to humanity. "This is what I am now," I said, watching the fire reflect in Red's frightened eyes. "A walking conflagration. A man-shaped weapon that destroys everything it touches." Amicae stepped closer to the flames, testing their heat. "Then learn to control it." "I've been trying!" I exploded, and the fire roared higher. "Three days of meditation and breathing exercises and pretty words, and all I've managed is to slightly delay the inevitable incineration of everything around me. This power isn't meant for control—it's meant for war." "Then maybe," she said quietly, "war is what we need to prepare for." The words hung in the air between us, heavy with implication. In the distance, Red whined—not in fear this time, but in recognition. Even transformed, even burning, I was still the man who had chosen to fight when fighting was needed. Pack leader chooses to protect, his mental voice carried across the flames with desperate loyalty. Red follows, even if it hurts. Pack is pack. The simple declaration shattered something inside me. Here was a creature terrified by my very presence, yet still choosing loyalty over comfort. If Red could find the courage to stand by me despite his fear, perhaps I could find the courage to master this power despite mine. I forced the flames back, pulling the fire inward until only wisps of smoke rose from my skin. Red approached cautiously, his mental voice carrying both relief and determination: Learning to be near new fire. Will adapt. Pack adapts. When he pressed his warm flank against my leg, I wept—not from despair this time, but from the overwhelming gratitude that somewhere in this transformed world, love remained constant. * Day Four: Dismissed and Doubted The town of Millbrook The constable listened to Rowetha's story with the weary patience of a man who'd heard too many impossible tales that week. His office was cramped and cluttered, maps and reports covering every surface, evidence of a crisis spiraling beyond any local authority's ability to manage. "Purple-eyed villagers, you say," he repeated, making notes on a piece of parchment already crowded with similar accounts. "And they were building some kind of structure in the town square." "A gateway," Rowetha insisted, though she could see disbelief growing in his eyes. "My... my cat can sense these things. She says they're opening a portal to bring in more corruption." The constable's pen paused. "Your cat told you this?" Careful, Izzy warned from within Rowetha's cloak. Adult humans become suspicious when children speak of magic they cannot see. "I mean... it seemed like a gateway," Rowetha corrected hastily. "The way they were building it, the materials they were using. It didn't look like any normal structure." "Miss, you're the seventh refugee this week claiming to know special details about the corruption," the constable said, not unkindly but with obvious weariness. "Everyone's got theories about what it is, where it comes from, how to fight it. But theories don't help when whole villages are going dark faster than we can evacuate them." He gestured to the maps, where red X marks showed an alarming pattern of affected areas. "We're doing what we can—sending warnings to outlying settlements, organizing evacuation routes, stockpiling supplies for refugees. But magical gateways and talking animals?" He shook his head. "That's beyond what simple constables can handle." "Then who can handle it?" Rowetha demanded, frustration making her voice crack. "There has to be someone with the power to fight this thing!" "The King's mages, maybe. The magical orders. But they're all locked in debate about jurisdiction and proper procedures while people die." His voice carried the bitterness of someone who'd sent too many requests for help and received too few responses. "Politics and protocol matter more than lives, apparently." Rowetha left the constable's office feeling smaller and more alone than ever. The adult world seemed paralyzed by its own complexity, unable to act while children like her saw the truth with painful clarity. Human leaders fear to believe, Izzy observed as they walked through Millbrook's crowded streets. Truth too large for small minds to accept. Easier to pretend problem is normal, manageable. "But it's not manageable," Rowetha said. "By the time they figure that out, there won't be anything left to save." Which is why Guardian magic exists, Izzy's mental voice carried ancient authority. When human institutions fail, individual heroes must act. Fire-that-burns-clean exists for such times. They spent the night in Millbrook's refugee shelter, surrounded by families whose stories echoed Rowetha's own. But while others spoke of evacuation and hiding, Rowetha planned her next moves south. Somewhere ahead, power waited that could fight corruption on its own terms. She just had to be brave enough to find it. * Day Five: The Mindreaver's Challenge Rawn's cottage The nightmares came strongest on the fifth night, but now they carried new terrors. The Mindreaver didn't just whisper of past failures—it showed me my Fire Essence core consuming me from within, turning me into a living pyre that would burn everything I touched. But worse than the visions of destruction were the visions of isolation. I saw myself growing more powerful and more alone, my flames driving away everyone who tried to get close. I saw Red dying of old age while I remained frozen in this borrowed youth, watching everything I loved wither while I burned eternal and untouchable. I woke screaming, dark Fire Essence erupting involuntarily from my core—not golden flame but that terrible black fire that sought to devour spirit itself. The dark energy scorched the cottage walls with flames that burned cold, leaving marks that hurt to look at. But this time was different. This time, the black fire spread. It raced along the walls like a living thing, seeking to consume not just wood and stone but the memories embedded in them. I watched in horror as the marks Alaina had carved into the doorframe—measuring our boy's height as he grew—began to smolder and fade. "No!" I lurched from the bed, trying to beat out flames that existed more in spirit than substance. "Not those! Please, not those!" Amicae rushed in, her own power flowing in desperate patterns to contain my runaway magic. But the dark fire resisted her water, feeding on spiritual energy rather than physical fuel. It was Red who saved the memories—the old dog somehow finding the courage to leap between me and the doorframe, his own latent Fire Essence creating a barrier the dark flames couldn't cross. The effort cost him. Red collapsed, fur singed, breathing labored. But the height marks remained. Pack leader's memories safe, he gasped through our mental bond. Good memories stronger than bad fire. "I'm becoming something monstrous," I gasped, kneeling beside my injured companion. "This power... it doesn't just burn things. It burns meaning. Memory. Love itself." Amicae studied the otherworldly burns on the walls. "This isn't corruption," she said carefully. "This is your fear of your own strength. Dark flame manifests when Fire masters are afraid of burning others—it turns the flame inward, consuming what should be protected." "Because I am afraid!" I exploded, more black fire threatening to spill out. Red whimpered, and I forced myself to stillness. "Look at these hands—they could incinerate you with a thought. This core burns so hot it could crack stone just from proximity. What if I lose control around innocents? What if I become the very thing I once fought against?" "Then we teach you absolute control," she said firmly. "Not the crude restraint of age, but the precise mastery of a true Fire master." "And if I can't learn it?" I asked, stroking Red's singed fur with hands that still leaked smoke. "What if this power is simply too much for any mortal vessel to contain?" She was quiet for a long moment, studying the spiritual burns that marred my home. "Then we find another way," she said finally. "But we don't give up. Not while people need protecting." "People need protecting from me," I said bitterly. No, Red's mental voice carried firm disagreement despite his pain. People need protecting by you. Big difference. Pack leader protects pack—all packs. Despite everything, I laughed—a broken sound, but genuine. Even burned and exhausted, my oldest friend still believed in me more than I believed in myself. * Day Five: Signs and Portents The village of Riverside Rowetha arrived in Riverside to find a community in the grip of panic. Unlike the organized refugee camps she'd seen before, this was chaos—people loading wagons with desperate haste, arguments breaking out over supplies, children crying as families prepared to abandon their homes. "What's happening?" she asked a woman hurriedly packing preserves into a travel sack. "Wells went bad this morning," the woman replied without stopping her work. "All of them, all at once. Water turned dark and started bubbling purple foam. The livestock won't drink it, and anyone who tries gets violently sick." Rowetha felt ice in her veins. "How far is this from Crygill?" "Twenty miles, maybe twenty-five." The woman looked up from her packing, seeing something in Rowetha's expression that made her pause. "Why? You seen this before?" Cannot tell truth, Izzy warned. They would not believe. Would think child mad with grief. "I've heard similar stories from other refugees," Rowetha said carefully. "It seems to spread through the water systems first." "Aye, that's what we're thinking too. Which is why we're not waiting around to see what comes next." The woman hefted her pack with grim determination. "My sister's family lives in Millbrook. We'll head there, then further south if need be." Throughout the village, similar scenes played out. Families abandoning homes their ancestors had built, taking only what they could carry and praying they were moving fast enough to stay ahead of whatever darkness was spreading north to south. But not everyone was leaving. Near the village well, a small group of people had gathered around an elderly man who seemed to be arguing for staying. "Running won't solve anything," he was saying as Rowetha approached. "We make our stand here, fight for what's ours. These are our homes, our lands. We don't abandon them to some mysterious corruption." "And how exactly do we fight water that turns purple?" a younger man demanded. "How do we battle something that changes people from the inside out?" "With fire," the elder replied firmly. "Fire purifies everything. Always has. We burn out the corruption before it can take hold." Wrong fire, Izzy's mental voice carried urgent warning. Normal fire will spread corruption, not cleanse it. They will make things worse. Rowetha found herself speaking up: "Fire might not be enough. The corruption... it's not like normal disease. It changes how things work at their deepest level." The elder turned to study her with sharp eyes. "And what would a refugee child know about fighting corruption?" "I know that good people have tried fighting it with normal means," she said, thinking of Tam and his forge-fire resistance. "Sometimes it works, but only briefly. What you need is fire that burns differently—fire that can tell the difference between corruption and truth." "Girl's talking nonsense," someone muttered, but the elder continued to study her intently. "What kind of fire are you talking about?" he asked. Rowetha felt Izzy's encouragement through their bond. "The kind that burns in the south," she said. "Guardian fire. Fire that heals instead of just destroying." "Guardian fire?" The elder's eyebrows rose. "Haven't heard that term in forty years. Where did you learn about such things?" Careful, Izzy warned, but Rowetha sensed an opportunity for truth. "From someone who knows about old magic," she said simply. "The kind of magic that might be our only hope." The elder studied her for a long moment, then nodded slowly. "Then you'd better find this Guardian fire quickly, child. Because I don't think running south will save any of us much longer." As if to emphasize his point, a shout arose from the direction of the wells. Purple mist was beginning to rise from the corrupted water, forming shapes that looked almost like grasping hands. The evacuation of Riverside became a rout. * Day Six: The Punishment of Progress Rawn's cottage Amicae decided that if control wouldn't come through meditation, perhaps it could be beaten into me through exhaustion. She worked me until my young body trembled with fatigue, forcing me to channel the flame techniques over and over until my Fire Essence core felt scraped raw. "Ember Coil," she commanded, and I gathered the serpent energy for the hundredth time that morning. The strike went wide, flames carving a blackened scar across three trees. My aim was still erratic, my power levels unpredictable. "Again." This time the coil snapped back on itself, burning my own arm. I bit back a scream, but the smell of my own flesh cooking made my stomach turn. "Again." "I can't," I gasped, sweat pouring down my face despite the morning chill. "My core feels like it's been scraped with broken glass. I need rest." "The corruption won't wait for you to rest," she said coldly. "Neither will the people it's killing. Ember Coil. Now." The next attempt failed so spectacularly that it set her cloak on fire. She extinguished it with sharp, angry movements, her patience clearly wearing thin. "You're holding back," she accused. "Still trying to be gentle, still trying to control what can't be controlled." "I'm trying not to kill us both!" I snapped back. "This power wants to burn everything. If I stop holding back—" "Then stop being afraid of what you might do and start focusing on what you need to do!" Her voice cracked like a whip. "Every moment you waste in self-pity is another moment the darkness spreads. Another village that falls. Another child that dies because you're too scared of your own strength to wield it properly." The words hit like physical blows. "You think I don't know that? You think I don't feel the weight of every life that hangs in the balance while I fumble around like a child with a sword too heavy for him?" "Then prove it," she snarled. "Stop making excuses. Stop mourning what you were. Become what you need to be." I raised Vulcanspine with shaking hands, gathering power for another Ember Coil. This time, instead of trying to limit the flames, I embraced them. Let them flow through me like a river of molten gold, accepting their hunger instead of fighting it. The resulting strike carved through stone like it was parchment. "Better," Amicae said, but her tone remained harsh. "Only nine hundred more repetitions and you might be ready to face a real threat." Red watched from a safe distance, his mental voice carrying both concern and encouragement: Pack leader learning. Learning hurts, but necessary. Pack leader becoming stronger. "We're all learning, old boy," I muttered, preparing for the next repetition. "Whether we want to or not." * Day Six: The Long Road Between Riverside and Crosshaven The road south stretched endlessly before them, a dusty track that wound through hills covered in spring grass and wildflowers. Under other circumstances, it might have been a pleasant journey. Now it felt like a race against time itself. Rowetha's feet were blistered despite the sturdy boots she'd taken from an abandoned farmhouse. Her stomach cramped with hunger—they'd been living on foraged berries and stream water for two days now. But Izzy's certainty about their direction never wavered, the kitten's silver eyes always fixed on some distant point only she could perceive. Closer, Izzy reported as they crested another hill. Can feel the burning star more clearly now. Fire Essence so strong it warms the magical currents for miles around. "How much closer?" Rowetha asked, though she wasn't sure she wanted to know. Each day of travel felt like an eternity when she thought about Bubbee hiding in that root cellar, alone and afraid. Days still. Maybe week. But stronger each day—fire learning to burn clean. Soon will be ready for what comes. They encountered other travelers on the road, most heading south like themselves but for different reasons. Merchants fleeing disrupted trade routes. Families seeking relatives in distant towns. Soldiers carrying messages between increasingly isolated garrisons. But they also met some heading north, and their stories were the most disturbing of all. "Turned back at Miller's Ford," a trader told them, his wagon loaded with goods he couldn't deliver. "Bridge is out, but not from flood or storm. Locals say something ate through the stone supports. Left marks like acid burns, but no smell of acid." A group of King's messengers had an even worse tale: "Tried to reach the northern garrison at Copperdale. Found the fort empty, gates standing open. No bodies, no signs of battle. Just purple stains on the walls and the smell of something rotting that wasn't quite death." Corruption spreads faster than thought, Izzy observed. Soon will cut off all escape routes. People fleeing south will find corruption already waiting for them. That night, they camped with a family of Tiffling refugees whose stories made Rowetha's heart ache. The father spoke of watching his eldest son change overnight, purple veins spreading under his skin like infected roots. The mother wept as she described having to abandon her garden—three generations of carefully tended earth-magic that had fed the community for decades. "Worst part is not knowing if we're running toward safety or into another trap," the father said as they shared a meager dinner around a small fire. "Maps don't show corruption. Reports are days old by the time they reach us. Could be heading straight into the same nightmare we left behind." But their youngest daughter, a girl about Rowetha's age, had noticed something the adults missed. "The animals know," she said quietly. "Birds fly different patterns now. Deer avoid certain areas. Even insects won't go near corrupted places." Izzy's ears perked with interest. Child speaks truth. Natural creatures sense wrongness better than humans. Could use animal signs to avoid corrupted areas. "My cat notices things too," Rowetha said carefully. "Changes in the air, bad smells, places that feel wrong. Animals have senses we don't." The Tiffling girl smiled—the first genuine smile Rowetha had seen in days. "What's your cat's name?" "Izzy." "Can I pet her?" Izzy allowed the girl to stroke her fur, purring with unusual warmth. For a moment, both children could pretend they were just travelers on an ordinary journey, sharing the simple pleasure of a friendly animal. But when morning came, the families parted ways—the Tifflings heading toward their relatives in the east, Rowetha and Izzy continuing their determined march south toward whatever hope lay ahead. * Day Seven: The Cost of Power Rawn's cottage My body began to show the strain on the seventh day. Not the aches and pains of an old man—those were gone, replaced by something far worse. The constant channeling of overwhelming power was changing me in ways that went beyond the initial transformation. My skin had taken on a faint luminescence, veins visible beneath the surface like glowing threads. My hair, already darkened by youth, now held highlights of deep red that seemed to move independently of any breeze. When I breathed, small sparks sometimes escaped my lips. "You're burning yourself out," Amicae observed, studying these changes with clinical detachment. "Your human form isn't meant to channel this level of power for extended periods." "Then what am I supposed to do?" I asked, flexing fingers that left scorch marks on everything I touched. "This is what I am now. I can't dial it back to what's comfortable." "You adapt," she said simply. "Or you die. Those are your choices." The bluntness of it was almost refreshing after days of careful encouragement. "And if adaptation means becoming something that's no longer human?" "Then that's the price you pay for the power to save those who still are." I thought of Alaina, who had faced her final illness with grace and acceptance. She'd never asked for magical healing, never begged for the transformation that might have saved her. She'd accepted death as part of life's natural order. Now here I was, gifted with power beyond imagining, and all I could do was complain about the burden of it. "The Flood & Withdrawal technique," I said, returning to our training. "Show me how to use it without losing myself in the fire." The technique required me to send flames streaming outward in wide arcs, then pull all the heat back into Vulcanspine's core in a single breath. The transition between expansion and contraction had to be seamless, like breathing fire in and out. My first attempt nearly incinerated the training ground. The flood was magnificent—streams of golden fire that painted the air itself with light. But the withdrawal failed catastrophically, leaving me drained and half the forest on fire. "Focus on the return," Amicae instructed as she used her water magic to contain the blaze. "The outward flow is easy for someone with your power. It's bringing it back under control that requires skill." We worked the technique for hours, each failure teaching me something new about the relationship between expansion and restraint. Gradually, I began to understand that control wasn't about suppressing power—it was about directing it with purpose. Pack leader learning patience, Red observed from his watching spot. Fire wants to rush, but wise fire waits for right moment. "When did you become so philosophical?" I asked him during a rest break. Since bond with pack leader deepened, he replied seriously. Share thoughts now, share wisdom. Red's simple wisdom, pack leader's complex learning—both important. The bond between us had indeed grown stronger each day, allowing us to share not just thoughts but insights. His canine perspective on patience and loyalty was teaching me things about power that no human instructor could have provided. By evening, I could execute Flood & Withdrawal with something approaching competence. The flames went out precisely where I sent them and returned exactly when I called. It wasn't perfect, but it was progress. "Tomorrow we work on Star Descent," Amicae announced as we prepared for another night of careful rest. "Drawing solar power down from above requires perfect timing with the twin suns' positions." I nodded, too exhausted to worry about the new challenges ahead. Each day brought its own painful lessons, but each day also brought me closer to mastery. And somewhere in the distance, corruption spread while innocent people waited for help that might never come if I failed to learn fast enough. * Day Seven: Izzy's Growing Power A hilltop overlooking the Fernway Valley Izzy had been changing too, Rowetha realized as she watched the kitten extend her senses across the valley below. What had begun as simple animal intuition had evolved into something far more sophisticated—a form of magical awareness that seemed to grow stronger each day. Valley is clean, Izzy reported, her silver eyes blazing with effort. No corruption touch yet. But... wrongness approaches from north. Following river path. Will reach valley in perhaps three days. "Can you see it? The corruption itself?" Not see with eyes. Feel with Guardian sense. Like cold spot in warm water, like discord in harmonious song. Wrongness that hurts to perceive directly. Rowetha had learned to trust Izzy's perceptions completely. Three times now, the kitten had guided them away from areas that looked perfectly safe but felt wrong to her magical senses. Later, other refugees had confirmed that those very areas had been overrun by corruption. But Izzy's abilities were costing her. Each use of her Guardian magic left the kitten exhausted, and she was growing thinner despite Rowetha's efforts to find food for both of them. "You're pushing yourself too hard," Rowetha said, gathering Izzy into her arms. "We can rest for a day, let you recover your strength." No time, Izzy replied firmly. Corruption spreads faster now. Gateway in Crygill grows stronger—can feel it pulling power from other places, accelerating the spread. Must reach fire-that-burns-clean soon. They descended into the valley as afternoon light began to fade. The village of Fernway sat peacefully beside its river, smoke rising from chimneys, children playing in the streets. It looked exactly like Crygill had looked seven days ago—normal, safe, innocent. Enjoy the peace while it lasts, Izzy's mental voice carried sadness. This place has three days of normal life remaining. After that... Rowetha didn't need her to finish the thought. She'd seen what "after that" looked like. In the village marketplace, they overheard fragments of conversation that confirmed what Izzy had sensed. Traders spoke of delays on the northern routes. A messenger mentioned that several villages had "gone quiet" with no explanation. Local authorities were beginning to implement "precautionary measures" without really understanding what they were preparing for. Humans sense wrongness but cannot name it, Izzy observed. Fear grows in their hearts but their minds reject what their instincts know. That night, they slept in a barn owned by a kindly farmer who took pity on a travel-worn girl and her cat. As Rowetha lay in the hay listening to Izzy's purr, she felt the weight of terrible knowledge. Tomorrow they would continue south toward hope, but behind them, this peaceful village was living through its last normal night. Unless the fire-that-burns-clean could learn to burn bright enough, fast enough, to make a difference. Will be ready, Izzy assured her, as if reading her thoughts. Can feel fire-bearer's progress. Power growing stronger, control growing surer. Soon will be weapon worthy of the war that comes. "What kind of war?" Rowetha whispered. War for the soul of the world, Izzy replied simply. War between corruption and cleansing, between despair and hope. Guardian and Fire-bearer together—perhaps strong enough to tip the balance. In the distance, thunder rumbled despite the clear skies. Not weather, Rowetha realized, but something else entirely. The sound of a world beginning to crack along lines that had been drawn long before she was born. The real battle was coming, and she was racing toward it as fast as her twelve-year-old legs could carry her.
